key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Dirichlet-form theory gives, outside a set N of zero capacity, a Markov transition kernel p_t(x,dy) whose action on L^2 functions has quasi-continuous versions on a closed nest. Proposition 2.7 shows that for each t>0 the semigroup also has an L^2 density p_t(x,y) with respect to ν for ν-a.e. x. Integrating these densities in time gives a ν⊗ν-a.e. density ρ_λ(x,y)=∫_0^∞ exp(-λt)p_t(x,y)dt for the resolvent, hence absolute continuity for ν-a.e. x. If B is ν-null, then R_λ 1_B=0 ν-a.e.; the quasi-continuous version is continuous on each set in the nest and vanishes on a dense subset there, so it vanishes for all x outside N. The kernel is set identically zero on N, giving absolute continuity for all x in the stated sense.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The closed Dirichlet form (E,D(E)) on L^2(ν), where ν is the bounded-density Gibbs perturbation of Brownian bridge law described above, is quasi-regular.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The graph norms of E and E_0 are equivalent because dν/dμ is bounded above and below. Quasi-regularity is stable under such equivalence of Dirichlet-form norms. The Gaussian Dirichlet form E_0 is quasi-regular because it is associated with the continuous Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic heat equation. Therefore E is quasi-regular.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (u_t)_{t≥0} be the Markov process associated with (E,D(E)). Almost surely, for Lebesgue-a.e. time t, the path θ↦u_t(θ) has a family of local times ℓ^a_{t,r}, indexed by r∈[0,1] and a∈R, which is jointly continuous in (r,a) and satisfies ∫_0^r g(u_t(s))ds=∫_R g(a)ℓ^a_{t,r}da for bounded Borel g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Under μ, the coordinate path is a Brownian bridge, hence a continuous semimartingale in the spatial parameter and has jointly continuous local times. Since ν is equivalent to μ, the same path property holds ν-a.s. Let S be the full-ν-measure set of paths with such local times. By Proposition 2.7, for every positive time the transition law of u_t from a fixed nonexceptional initial point is absolutely continuous with respect to ν, so P_x(u_t∈S^c)=0 for a.e. t. Integrating over t shows that the process spends zero Lebesgue time in S^c almost surely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let β be a standard Brownian bridge on [0,1] with law μ, let ρ(x)=exp(-F(x)) with F(x)=∫_0^1 f(x_r)dr, and let f(da) denote the signed Stieltjes measure associated with the bounded-variation function f. For every h∈D(A) and every φ∈C_b^1(H), E[ρ(β) ∂_h φ(β)] = E[ρ(β) φ(β)(-⟨h'',β⟩ + ∫_{R×[0,1]} f(da) h_r ℓ^a(dr))], where ℓ^a(r) is the Brownian bridge local time accumulated over [0,r] and ∂_h is the directional derivative in direction h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the occupation-time formula to express F(β+εh) in terms of the Brownian bridge local times and the Stieltjes measure f(da). Differentiating at ε=0 gives the local-time expression for the derivative of F along h, with the sign convention displayed in the paper. Then apply the Cameron-Martin formula for Brownian bridge shifts by εh: E[ρ(β)φ(β+εh)] equals E[ρ(β-εh)φ(β) exp(-ε⟨h'',β⟩-ε^2∥h∥^2/2)]. Differentiating at ε=0 yields the integration-by-parts formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let U_h(x)=⟨x,h⟩ for h∈C_c^2((0,1)). In the Fukushima decomposition U_h(u_t)-U_h(u_0)=M^{[U_h]}_t+N^{[U_h]}_t for the Markov process associated with E, there is an exceptional set N such that for every initial point x∈H∖N, P_x-a.s. for all t≥0, N^{[U_h]}_t = 1/2∫_0^t ⟨h'',u_s⟩ds + 1/2∫_{]0,t]×[0,1]}∫_R f(da) h'_r ℓ^a_{s,r} ds dr. The local times are understood through the identity -∫_0^1∫_R h'_r ϕ(a)ℓ^a_{s,r}da dr = ∫_0^1 h_r ϕ(u_s(r))dr for bounded continuous ϕ, whenever this notation is used.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Fukushima decomposition to U_h∈D(E). Proposition 3.4 identifies the distributional divergence of the gradient direction h, hence the Revuz measure of the zero-energy additive functional N^{[U_h]} is 1/2 times the signed measure Σ_h(dw)=(⟨w,h''⟩-∫_{R×[0,1]} f(da)h_r dℓ^a_r)ν(dw). The signed measure is handled by Jordan decomposition and finite-energy estimates: the local-time term is in L^2(ν) because f has bounded variation, h is bounded, and Brownian bridge local times have finite moments. Measures of finite energy are smooth, so each positive component has a unique positive continuous additive functional. Truncating the density and using monotone convergence shows that the PCAF is the time integral of the corresponding path functional. Finally, integration by parts in the spatial variable r converts ∫ h_r dℓ^a_r into -∫ h'_r ℓ^a_r dr, producing the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the martingale additive functional M^{[U_h]} in the Fukushima decomposition of U_h(x)=⟨x,h⟩, there exists a Brownian sheet W(t,θ), t≥0 and θ∈[0,1], such that M^{[U_h]}_t=∫_0^t∫_0^1 h_θ W(ds,dθ) for every h∈H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the carré-du-champ formula for the Revuz measure of the quadratic variation of a martingale additive functional: ∫ f dμ_{⟨M^{[U]}⟩}=2E(Uf,U)-E(U^2,f) for bounded test f in the form domain. For U_h(x)=⟨x,h⟩ this gives μ_{⟨M^{[U_h]}⟩}=∥h∥_H^2 ν, hence ⟨M^{[U_h]}⟩_t=∥h∥_H^2 t. Polarization gives cross-variations ⟨M^{[U_{h_1}]},M^{[U_{h_2}]}⟩_t=t⟨h_1,h_2⟩. Levy's characterization then identifies the family as an isonormal cylindrical Brownian motion over H. Taking h=1_{[0,θ]} defines W(t,θ), and the general integral representation follows by isometry.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Dirichlet form (E,D(E)) is quasi-regular, and the Markov process associated with it is a weak solution, in the sense of Definition 1.1 of the paper, of the singular SPDE ∂u/∂t = 1/2 ∂^2u/∂θ^2 - 1/2 ∫_R f(da) ∂_θ ℓ^a_{t,θ} + white noise, with u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0. The process is ν-symmetric and therefore ν is an invariant measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Quasi-regularity is Lemma 3.2, which gives an associated Markov process. Apply Fukushima decomposition to all linear functionals U_h(x)=⟨x,h⟩ with h∈C_c^2((0,1)). Proposition 3.5 identifies the zero-energy part as the deterministic heat drift plus the local-time drift; Proposition 3.6 identifies the martingale part as integration against a Brownian sheet. This is exactly the weak formulation against test functions h. The required space-time continuity is first obtained in stationarity from Lemma 6.1, using Sobolev moment estimates for Brownian bridge under ν, and then extended to nonstationary initial points outside an exceptional set using the absolute-continuity property of Proposition 2.5 and the Markov property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.1, 4.2, and 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f_n be smooth bounded approximations of the bounded-variation f as in Section 4, define F_n(x)=∫_0^1 f_n(x_r)dr and ν_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}exp(-F_n(x))μ(dx). The L^2(ν_n) and L^2(ν) norms are uniformly equivalent because all densities are bounded above and below. The forms E_n(φ,ψ)=1/2∫⟨∇φ,∇ψ⟩dν_n on K=Exp_A(H) are closable in L^2(ν_n). With the natural identification maps Φ_n:L^2(ν)→L^2(ν_n), the Hilbert spaces L^2(ν_n) converge to L^2(ν) in the sense of Kuwae-Shioya; moreover, in this bounded-density situation, strong convergence in that sense is equivalent to convergence in L^2(ν), and weak convergence is equivalent to ordinary weak convergence in L^2(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniform norm equivalence follows immediately from boundedness of f_n and f. Closability of E_n is the same argument as Lemma 2.4, comparing to the Gaussian form E_0. For convergence of Hilbert spaces, dominated convergence gives ∥Φ_n u∥_{L^2(ν_n)}→∥u∥_{L^2(ν)} because dν_n/dμ→dν/dμ a.s. and uniformly bounded. Strong convergence in Kuwae-Shioya's definition reduces to L^2(ν) convergence by norm equivalence. For weak convergence, one direction is tested against v_n=v ρ ρ_n^{-1}, where ρ=dν/dμ and ρ_n=dν_n/dμ, while the converse uses w_n=v_n ρ_n ρ^{-1}; dominated convergence and the strong-convergence characterization complete the equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.6 (Kuwae-Shioya, quoted)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a sequence of closed quadratic forms E_n on Hilbert spaces H_n converging to a Hilbert space H in the Kuwae-Shioya sense, Mosco convergence of E_n to a closed form E is equivalent to strong convergence of the associated resolvent operators.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Kuwae and Shioya and uses it as a black box. Its role in the paper is to convert variational Mosco convergence of Dirichlet forms with changing reference measures into strong convergence of semigroups and resolvents, which is then used for convergence of stationary processes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the smooth approximating potentials f_n of Section 4 and the associated closed Dirichlet forms (E_n,D(E_n)) on L^2(ν_n), the forms E_n Mosco-converge to E on L^2(ν), in the varying-Hilbert-space sense of Kuwae-Shioya. In the proof the smooth approximations are used so that the measures f_n'(a)da converge weakly to the Stieltjes measure f(da).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mosco II is immediate: for u∈D(E), take u_n=u under the natural identification and use dominated convergence of the densities to get E_n(u,u)→E(u,u); for u∉D(E), both sides are infinite. For Mosco I, take u_n weakly convergent to u. If the liminf of E_n(u_n,u_n) is infinite there is nothing to prove; otherwise the uniform equivalence of E_n with E_0 gives bounded E_0 energy, and compactness of D(E_0) into L^2(μ), plus norm equivalence, yields strong L^2 convergence along a subsequence. For test functions v in the exponential core, use the integration-by-parts formula for E and the analogous smooth formula for E_n. The terms involving Tr(D^2v) and the Gaussian drift pass to the limit by strong L^2 convergence and density convergence; the nonlinear terms pass by the occupation-time formula and weak convergence f_n'(a)da→f(da). Thus E(u,v)=lim E_n(u_n,v). The inequality E_n(u_n,u_n)^{1/2}≥E_n(u_n,v)/E_n(v,v)^{1/2}, followed by convergence and taking the supremum over the core, gives E(u,u)≤liminf E_n(u_n,u_n). If u∉D(E), bounded liminf would force u∈D(E) by lower semicontinuity, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P^n_{ν_n} be the law of the stationary solution of the smooth reaction-diffusion SPDE ∂u_n/∂t=1/2 ∂^2u_n/∂θ^2 - 1/2 f_n'(u_n)+white noise with Dirichlet boundary conditions and invariant measure ν_n. Let P_ν be the law of the stationary Markov process associated with E. Then P^n_{ν_n} converges weakly to P_ν in C([0,T]×[0,1]) for every T>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Finite-dimensional distributions follow from Mosco convergence and Theorem 4.6: the semigroups P_t^n converge strongly to P_t, and repeated use of the Markov property gives convergence of expectations of products f_1(X_{t_1})...f_m(X_{t_m}) for bounded continuous cylinder functions. Tightness in C([0,T];C^θ([0,1])) is obtained uniformly in n. Since ν_n is uniformly equivalent to Brownian bridge law, Brownian bridge Sobolev estimates give sup_n∫∥x∥_{W^{η,p}}^pν_n(dx)<∞ for η<1/2 and suitable p. Lemma 6.1 then gives uniform temporal Hölder-moment estimates in a spatial Hölder norm, and Kolmogorov's criterion yields tightness. Combining finite-dimensional convergence and tightness gives weak convergence in C([0,T]×[0,1]).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the one-dimensional skew Brownian motion X_t=X_0+B_t+βL_t^0 with |β|<1, define α∈R by (1-exp(-α))/(1+exp(-α))=β. Then X is associated with the Dirichlet form D(u)=1/2∫_R (u'(x))^2 exp(-α 1_{(-∞,0]}(x)) dx in L^2(exp(-α 1_{(-∞,0]})dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The weighted form is equivalent to the standard Brownian Dirichlet form, hence is closable and regular. An integration-by-parts formula for the discontinuous weight produces a boundary term at 0: ∫φ' exp(-α1_{(-∞,0]})dx=-(1-exp(-α))φ(0), equivalently a local-time coefficient 2(1-exp(-α))/(1+exp(-α)) after normalization by the symmetric speed measure. Fukushima decomposition then shows the associated Hunt process solves the skew Brownian SDE for quasi-every initial point. The explicit transition density of skew Brownian motion gives absolute continuity, allowing extension to every initial point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume f has the finite-jump form f(y)=f_0(y)+Σ_{j=1}^k α_j 1_{y≤y_j}, with f_0 smooth and bounded. Let H_n be the space of functions constant on dyadic intervals of length 2^{-n}, let μ_n be the law of the projection P_nβ of a Brownian bridge, and define π_n(dx)=Z_n^{-1}exp(-2^{-n}Σ_i f(x(i))) μ_n(dx). The finite-dimensional form Λ_n(u,v)=1/2∫⟨∇u,∇v⟩_{H_n} dπ_n is a regular Dirichlet form in L^2(π_n). Its associated Markov process is a weak solution of the interacting skew SDE dX_i=1/2((A_nX)_i-f_0'(X_i))dt + Σ_j ((1-exp(-α_j2^{-n}))/(1+exp(-α_j2^{-n}))) dℓ_t^{i,y_j} + dw_t^i, and this solution is unique in law.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Regularity follows because Λ_n is equivalent to a finite-dimensional Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form. An integration-by-parts formula for π_n contains the smooth drift term, the Gaussian linear drift A_n, and surface measures π_n(dx;x(i)=y_j) coming from jumps of f. Fukushima decomposition identifies the bounded-variation additive functional with these Revuz measures. Tanaka's formula for |X_i-y_j| identifies the surface-measure PCAF with the local time ℓ_t^{i,y_j} of the coordinate process. For uniqueness in law, use Girsanov to remove the bounded smooth drift 1/2(A_nX-f_0'(X)); under the changed measure the coordinates are independent skew Brownian motions with the displayed skew parameters. Harrison-Shepp pathwise uniqueness for skew Brownian motion gives uniqueness in law, and absolute continuity of skew Brownian transition kernels removes exceptional initial points.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the finite-dimensional approximation setting, the Hilbert spaces L^2(π_n) converge to L^2(ν) in the Kuwae-Shioya sense. The identification map Φ_n:L^2(ν)→L^2(π_n) is defined by conditional expectation with respect to the dyadic σ-field generated by the projected Brownian bridge values.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let β be the Brownian bridge and β_n=P_nβ. For φ∈L^2(μ), define Φ_nφ as E[φ(β) | σ(β_{i2^{-n}}, i=1,...,2^n)], viewed as a function of β_n. This martingale converges to φ in L^2(μ). The weights exp(-F_n(β_n)) converge a.s. to exp(-F(β)): f_n or the finite-dimensional sampling converges pointwise outside the finite discontinuity set of f, and Brownian bridge level sets at fixed levels have zero Lebesgue measure. Uniform boundedness of the weights gives uniform integrability, so ∥Φ_nφ∥_{L^2(π_n)}→∥φ∥_{L^2(ν)}. Since all densities are uniformly comparable, the same map works for L^2(ν) and L^2(π_n).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let u_n∈L^2(π_n) converge weakly to u∈L^2(ν) in the varying-Hilbert-space sense, and assume liminf_n Λ_n(u_n,u_n)<∞. Then there exists a subsequence such that u_n∘P_n converges to u in L^2(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Pass to a subsequence with uniformly bounded Λ_n(u_n,u_n). Formula (5.4) identifies Λ_n(u_n,u_n) with the Gaussian gradient energy of u_n∘P_n up to uniformly bounded density factors, so E_0(u_n∘P_n,u_n∘P_n) is uniformly bounded. By compactness of D(E_0) into L^2(μ), a further subsequence converges strongly in L^2(ν). The definition of the identification maps Φ_n and weak convergence force the limit to be u, giving the claimed subsequential strong convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the finite-dimensional forms Λ_n associated with a potential f=f_0+Σ_j α_j1_{y≤y_j} as in Theorem 5.2, Λ_n Mosco-converges to the infinite-dimensional form E on L^2(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mosco II is verified on the core Exp_A(H): for v in the core, take v_n=v restricted to H_n. The conditional-expectation identification shows v_n→v strongly, and dominated convergence plus P_n∇v→∇v yields Λ_n(v_n,v_n)→E(v,v). The paper uses the Andres-von Renesse variant in which this core recovery sequence suffices. For Mosco I, take u_n weakly convergent to u with bounded liminf energy. Lemma 5.7 gives strong L^2 convergence along a subsequence. The proof introduces smooth monotone regularizations of the jump potential and auxiliary forms with measures bounded by π_n. For v_n=v|_{H_n}, the dual inequality Λ_n(u_n,u_n)≥(tildeΛ_n(u_n,v_n))^2/tildeΛ_n(v_n,v_n) is used. Integration by parts reduces convergence of tildeΛ_n(u_n,v_n) to convergence of drift terms. Smooth terms converge by strong L^2 convergence and dominated convergence. Jump terms are approximated by nρ'(n(β-y)); Brownian bridge modulus estimates and standard Brownian local-time continuity estimates show these approximations converge in L^2 to the local-time term in E. The discrete Gaussian drift term involving A_nP_nh is handled by showing the linear functionals g↦∫g∘P_n⟨·,A_nP_nh⟩dμ are uniformly bounded and converge weakly to g↦∫g⟨·,Ah⟩dμ. Supremizing over core test functions gives the Mosco liminf inequality; if u were outside D(E), bounded liminf would contradict lower semicontinuity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q^n_{π_n} be the law of the stationary finite-dimensional process associated with Λ_n and invariant measure π_n. Then Q^n_{π_n} converges weakly to P_ν, the stationary infinite-dimensional process associated with E, in C([0,T];H^{-1}(0,1)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define the isometry J:H^{-1}(0,1)→L^2(0,1) by Jx(θ)=⟨x,1_{[0,θ]}⟩. The laws of JX_t^n satisfy the spatial Sobolev moment condition of Lemma 6.1 uniformly, because π_n is a bounded perturbation of projected Brownian bridge law and J maps into H^1. Lemma 6.1 gives tightness of the transformed laws in C([0,T]×[0,1]), hence tightness of Q^n_{π_n} in C([0,T];H^{-1}). Finite-dimensional distributions converge by Mosco convergence of Λ_n and the strong semigroup convergence; the Markov property reduces products at multiple times to iterated semigroups, and multiplication by bounded continuous functions preserves strong convergence in the varying-Hilbert-space sense. Tightness plus finite-dimensional convergence proves weak convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let γ be a probability measure on H and let (D,D(D)) be a Dirichlet form in L^2(γ) with C_b^1(H) as a core and D(u,v)=1/2∫⟨∇u,∇v⟩dγ on the core. Let (X_t) be the stationary Markov process associated with D. Suppose there exist η∈(0,1), ζ>0, and p>1 such that ζ>1/(1+2η/3), p>max{2/(1-ζ), 1/(η-(3/2)(1-ζ)/ζ)}, and ∫_H ∥x∥_{W^{η,p}(0,1)}^p γ(dx)<∞. Then there exist θ∈(0,1), ξ>1, and K>0, depending only on η,ζ,p and the moment bound, such that E[∥X_t-X_s∥_{C^θ([0,1])}^p]≤K|t-s|^ξ for all s,t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First obtain temporal increments in H^{-1}(0,1). The Lyons-Zheng decomposition writes ⟨h,X_t-X_0⟩ as one half of a forward martingale minus one half of a reversed martingale, both with quadratic variation t∥h∥^2. Burkholder-Davis-Gundy applied to the sine basis of H gives (E∥X_t-X_s∥_{H^{-1}}^p)^{1/p}≤C|t-s|^{1/2}. Stationarity and the assumed γ-moment give a uniform bound on (E∥X_t-X_s∥_{W^{η,p}}^p)^{1/p}. Interpolate between H^{-1} and W^{η,p}: with α=ζη-(1-ζ)>0 and 1/d=ζ/p+(1-ζ)/2, the parameter assumptions ensure αd>1 and ξ=p(1-ζ)/2>1. The Sobolev embedding W^{α,d}(0,1)⊂C^θ([0,1]) for some θ>0 then yields the displayed Hölder-norm moment estimate.

proof_techniques.name: Gibbs measure and symmetric Dirichlet form for a singular drift
proof_techniques.description: The paper starts from a bounded bounded-variation potential f and defines F(x)=∫_0^1 f(x_r)dr and ν(dx)=Z^{-1}exp(-F(x))μ(dx), where μ is Brownian bridge law. The process is then constructed from the symmetric gradient Dirichlet form E(φ,ψ)=1/2∫⟨∇φ,∇ψ⟩dν. For smooth f, the associated SPDE is the reaction-diffusion equation with drift -1/2 f'(u); for discontinuous f, the derivative f(da) enters through spatial local times, producing the skew/local-time SPDE. Because the form is symmetric in L^2(ν), ν is automatically reversible and invariant for the constructed Markov process.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The drift δ_0(u) can be viewed formally as -1/2 f'(u) for a Heaviside-type bounded potential with a jump at 0. This suggests a concrete candidate invariant measure for the original SPDE and a way to verify invariance by identifying the ABLM solution with the corresponding Dirichlet-form process or with limits of smooth reversible approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Bounded-density transfer from Brownian bridge Ornstein-Uhlenbeck theory
proof_techniques.description: Since f is bounded, ν is equivalent to Brownian bridge law μ with density bounded above and below. The paper repeatedly transfers analytic properties of the Gaussian Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Dirichlet form to the perturbed non-log-concave form using this norm equivalence. This gives closability, quasi-regularity, compactness of the form domain embedding, comparison of eigenvalues by the minimax principle, and Hilbert-Schmidt bounds for the perturbed semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the δ_0 drift is represented by a bounded jump potential, the same bounded-density comparison should be available. It can supply compactness and transition-density information even though the potential is non-convex and standard monotonicity or log-concavity arguments are unavailable.

proof_techniques.name: Absolute continuity of the resolvent without strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper notes that strong Feller is not available, but proves a weaker absolute-continuity property. First, Hilbert-Schmidt bounds give L^2 transition densities p_t(x,y) for ν-a.e. x. Second, quasi-regular Dirichlet-form theory gives quasi-continuous versions of resolvent potentials outside an exceptional set. Combining these, if B is ν-null then R_λ1_B vanishes ν-a.e. and hence, by quasi-continuity on a nest, vanishes for all nonexceptional starting points; the kernel is defined arbitrarily on the exceptional set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness of invariant measures, a common route is to combine absolute continuity with some form of irreducibility or full support. This paper supplies the absolute-continuity half in a singular, non-strong-Feller setting; a solver could look for an additional positivity/irreducibility argument for the δ_0 equation.

proof_techniques.name: Integration by parts using Brownian bridge local times
proof_techniques.description: The core analytic identity is an integration-by-parts formula for the weighted Brownian bridge measure exp(-F)μ. The derivative of F along a Cameron-Martin direction h is expressed through the occupation-time formula and the Brownian bridge local times ℓ^a(r), while the Gaussian shift contributes the term -⟨h'',β⟩. This converts the formal distributional derivative f'(u) into a rigorous local-time term involving the Stieltjes measure f(da).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a drift δ_0(u), the relevant potential has a jump at 0, so the corresponding integration-by-parts formula should involve Brownian bridge local time at level 0. This is the main calculation needed to identify the generator and to verify that a proposed Gibbs measure is infinitesimally invariant.

proof_techniques.name: Fukushima decomposition and Revuz-measure identification of SPDE terms
proof_techniques.description: After constructing the symmetric Markov process from E, the paper applies Fukushima decomposition to linear observables U_h(x)=⟨x,h⟩. The zero-energy additive functional is identified by computing its Revuz measure through the integration-by-parts formula, and then representing the corresponding smooth measures as time integrals of path functionals. The martingale additive functional is identified as a cylindrical Brownian motion by computing its quadratic variation through the carré-du-champ formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust way to prove that a Dirichlet-form process solves a singular SPDE without pointwise drift. It could be used to compare the ABLM weak mild solution with a reversible Dirichlet-form solution for the δ_0 potential.

proof_techniques.name: Mosco convergence of regularized singular equations
proof_techniques.description: The paper approximates the bounded-variation potential f by smooth bounded f_n and studies the reversible smooth SPDEs with invariant measures ν_n∝exp(-∫f_n)dμ. It proves Mosco convergence E_n→E using integration by parts, compactness of the Gaussian form domain, and convergence of the distributional derivatives f_n'(a)da to f(da). Kuwae-Shioya's theorem then turns Mosco convergence into strong resolvent and semigroup convergence, which gives convergence of stationary laws.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already assumes convergence of smooth approximations with drift G_{1/n}. One possible strategy is to identify invariant measures for the approximations, show they converge to the Gibbs measure for the jump potential, and pass invariance or ergodic properties to the limiting semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Stationary tightness via Lyons-Zheng and Sobolev interpolation
proof_techniques.description: The a priori estimate in Lemma 6.1 obtains time regularity of stationary processes associated with gradient Dirichlet forms without using explicit SPDE estimates. Lyons-Zheng decomposition gives H^{-1} increments of order |t-s|^{1/2}; stationarity gives uniform spatial W^{η,p} bounds from the invariant measure; interpolation and Sobolev embedding yield temporal Hölder estimates in a spatial Hölder norm. This is used for tightness of both smooth and finite-dimensional stationary approximations.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one needs compactness of invariant or stationary laws for δ_0 approximations on C_0([0,1]), this estimate provides a model route: prove uniform Brownian-bridge-type Sobolev moments under candidate invariant measures, then use Dirichlet-form martingale decomposition for time regularity.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-dimensional skew Brownian approximations
proof_techniques.description: For potentials with finitely many jumps, the finite-dimensional projections are systems of interacting skew Brownian motions. The jump of the potential produces coordinate local times with explicit skew parameters. The paper proves regularity of the finite-dimensional Dirichlet forms, identifies the SDEs by integration by parts and Tanaka formula, and proves uniqueness in law by a Girsanov transform to independent skew Brownian motions plus Harrison-Shepp uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Finite-dimensional approximations may be useful for proving irreducibility, support properties, or uniqueness of invariant measures at the approximate level before passing to the limit. The explicit skew Brownian structure is especially relevant when the δ_0 drift is encoded by a jump potential.

proof_techniques.name: Core-recovery Mosco convergence for changing finite-dimensional spaces
proof_techniques.description: In the finite-dimensional approximation, the paper uses an Andres-von Renesse variant of Mosco convergence: instead of constructing recovery sequences for every domain element directly, it suffices to construct them on a core Exp_A(H). Restrictions of exponential cylinder functions to H_n are the recovery sequence. The liminf inequality is obtained through compactness and duality against this core.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of invariant-measure uniqueness or invariance uses Galerkin approximations, this gives a practical method for proving semigroup convergence without needing dense explicit approximations of every infinite-dimensional Sobolev function.

other_useful_info: The introduction explicitly says that for general bounded-variation f, uniqueness of weak solutions to the singular SPDE seems out of reach; the constructed process is called canonical because it is associated with the explicit Dirichlet form and obtained as a limit of natural regularizations and discretizations. The authors also emphasize that the invariant Gibbs measure is generally non-log-concave, and that strong Feller is not proved; Proposition 2.5 is presented as a weaker substitute. For smooth f, the paper states that equation (1.6) is equivalent by occupation times to the reaction-diffusion SPDE ∂u/∂t=1/2∂^2u/∂θ^2 -1/2 f'(u)+white noise, which is the key bridge between distributional value-drifts and the local-time formulation. Since f is bounded, ν is equivalent to Brownian bridge law, so all Brownian-bridge almost sure path properties used in the paper, including continuity, local times, and Sobolev moment bounds, transfer to ν. External tools cited and used include Fukushima-Oshima-Takeda and Ma-Rockner for Dirichlet forms, Da Prato-Zabczyk for the Gaussian stochastic heat equation, Kuwae-Shioya and Kolesnikov for Mosco convergence with varying Hilbert spaces, Andres-von Renesse for a core version of Mosco convergence, and Harrison-Shepp for skew Brownian uniqueness.
why_relevant: This paper studies a closely related stochastic heat equation with a singular skew/local-time drift and gives an explicit Gibbs-type invariant measure plus Dirichlet-form and approximation constructions. It is one of the most directly relevant sources for identifying possible invariant measures and proving uniqueness for the δ0-drift equation.

title: Small Ball Probabilities and a Support Theorem for the Stochastic Heat Equation
overall_summary: The paper proves upper and lower small-ball probability estimates for a vector-valued stochastic heat equation on the circle with bounded drift, Lipschitz multiplicative noise, and uniformly elliptic noise coefficient. The main small-ball exponent is ε^{-6} for the event that the whole space-time profile remains within an ε-ball over a time interval of length T, and the paper also proves a support theorem showing positive probability of staying close to smooth predictable target paths. The core proof combines Girsanov transforms, parabolic scaling, a Markov decomposition into time blocks of length order ε^4 and spatial mesh order ε^2, Gaussian approximation by freezing coefficients, stochastic convolution chaining estimates, and the Gaussian correlation inequality. For the original problem, the most useful outputs are the support theorem philosophy and the local stochastic-convolution/small-ball estimates, which are standard ingredients for proving irreducibility of Markov semigroups.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1 — Small-ball estimates for the stochastic heat equation on the circle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d ≥ 1 and J ≥ 1. Let u(t,x) ∈ R^d be the mild solution on the circle [0,J] with endpoints identified of partial_t u(t,x) = 1/2 partial_xx u(t,x) + g(t,x,u(t,x)) + σ(t,x,u(t,x)) Wdot(t,x), with u(0,x) ≡ 0. Here Wdot is d-dimensional space-time white noise, g: R_+ × [0,J] × R^d → R^d is uniformly bounded, and σ: R_+ × [0,J] × R^d → M_d(R) is symmetric matrix-valued. Assume σ is Lipschitz in u with constant D, meaning |σ(t,x,u)-σ(t,x,v)| ≤ D|u-v| for all t,x,u,v, and uniformly elliptic, meaning there are constants a,b > 0 such that a ≤ <y,σ(t,x,u)y> ≤ b for every unit y ∈ R^d and all t,x,u. Then: (a) there is D0 = D0(J,a,b) > 0 and positive constants C0,C1,C2,C3 depending only on d,a,b, and ε0 also depending on sup_{t,x,u}|g(t,x,u)|, such that if D < D0, then for all 0 < ε < ε0 and T > 0, C0 exp(-C1 T J / ε^6) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)| ≤ ε) ≤ C2 exp(-C3 T J / ε^6). (b) For every D and every 0 < δ < 1, there are positive constants C0,C1,C2,C3 depending only on d,a,b, and ε0 also depending on J,D,δ and sup|g|, such that for all 0 < ε < ε0 and T > 0, C0 exp(-C1 T J^{1+δ/2} / ε^{6+δ}) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)| ≤ ε) ≤ C2 exp(-C3 T J / ((1+J D^2) ε^6)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is reduced first to the case g ≡ 0 by the white-noise Girsanov lemma. If the equation without drift is rewritten as an equation with drift g and shifted noise Wdot - σ^{-1}g, then σ^{-1}g is uniformly bounded by ellipticity and boundedness of g; Cauchy-Schwarz and the second-moment bound for the Radon-Nikodym derivative transfer small-ball upper bounds. Reversing the shift transfers lower bounds. Next the problem is reduced to J = 1 by the parabolic scaling v(t,z)=J^{-1/2}u(J^2t,Jz); the rescaled coefficient has the same ellipticity constants but Lipschitz constant J^{1/2}D. For J=1 and g=0 the proof fixes time points t_n = n c0 ε^4 and spatial points x_j = j c1 ε^2, with c0 small and c1^2 = θ c0 chosen using constants from the stochastic convolution tail and covariance estimates. It defines upper-bound events F_n requiring |u(t_n,x_j)|≤ε at all mesh points and lower-bound events A_n requiring |u|≤ε over a time slab and |u(t_{n+1},·)|≤ε/3 at the slab endpoint. Proposition 2.1 gives a one-block upper estimate P(F_n | past) ≤ C exp(-c/((1+D^2)ε^2)) and, for D small, a one-block lower estimate P(A_n | past) ≥ C exp(-c ε^{-2}). Iterating over about T/(c0ε^4) blocks via the Markov property gives the exponent T ε^{-6}. For arbitrary D in the lower bound, the paper uses a shorter time step so that the coefficient-freezing error is negligible compared with the Gaussian lower bound, yielding the weaker exponent ε^{-(6+δ)}. Scaling back from J=1 gives the J factors and the factor 1+J D^2 in the upper bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2 — Support theorem around smooth predictable paths
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same stochastic heat equation setting as Theorem 1.1, let h be a predictable R^d-valued function on R_+ × [0,J] such that, almost surely, h, partial_t h, and partial_xx h are uniformly bounded by a deterministic constant H; this is the paper's class PC^2_b. Let u0 and h be as in this class, and assume sup_{x∈[0,J]} |u0(x)-h(0,x)| < ε/2 almost surely. Then: (a) there is D0 = D0(J,a,b)>0 and constants C0,C1,C2,C3 depending only on d,a,b, and ε0 also depending on H and sup|g|, such that if D<D0 and 0<ε<ε0, T>0, then C0 exp(-C1 T J / ε^6) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)-h(t,x)| ≤ ε) ≤ C2 exp(-C3 T J / ε^6). (b) For every D and 0<δ<1, there are constants C0,C1,C2,C3 depending only on d,a,b, and ε0 also depending on J,D,δ,H and sup|g|, such that for all 0<ε<ε0 and T>0, C0 exp(-C1 T J^{1+δ/2} / ε^{6+δ}) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,J]} |u(t,x)-h(t,x)| ≤ ε) ≤ C2 exp(-C3 T J / ((1+J D^2) ε^6)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper derives this directly from Theorem 1.1 by subtracting the target path and the initial discrepancy. Let H_op = 1/2 partial_xx - partial_t be the heat operator and h0(x)=h(0,x). Define w(t,x)=u(t,x)-u0(x)-h(t,x)+h0(x). Then w(0,x)=0. The transformed drift and diffusion are written as g1(t,x,w) = -g(t,x,u) - H_op u0(x) - H_op h(t,x) + H_op h0(x) and σ1(t,x,w)=σ(t,x,u). The boundedness assumptions on h, u0 and g make g1 uniformly bounded, while σ1 has the same ellipticity constants and the same Lipschitz constant in the transformed variable. Thus w falls under Theorem 1.1. Since sup_x |u0(x)-h0(x)|≤ε/2, smallness of w implies closeness of u to h, and applying the small-ball estimates to w, with harmless changes in numerical ε constants, gives the displayed support estimates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 — Girsanov theorem for bounded predictable shifts of white noise
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W be d-dimensional space-time white noise on [0,∞)×[0,J], adapted to a filtration F_t. Let f(s,x,ω) be an R^d-valued predictable function uniformly bounded by M>0. For fixed t, let P_t be the restriction of P to F_t and define Q_t by dQ_t/dP_t = exp(Z_t^{(1)} - 1/2 Z_t^{(2)}), where Z_t^{(1)} = ∫_0^t ∫_0^J f(s,x) · W(dx ds) and Z_t^{(2)} = ∫_0^t ∫_0^J |f(s,x)|^2 dx ds. Then under Q_t, the shifted generalized field Wdot^f(s,x)=Wdot(s,x)-f(s,x), for 0≤s≤t and x∈[0,J], is again d-dimensional white noise. Moreover 1 ≤ E[(dQ_t/dP_t)^2] ≤ exp(M^2 t J).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The white-noise Girsanov assertion is quoted from Allouba's SPDE Girsanov theorem, with the vector-valued extension immediate componentwise. For the second moment, use that dQ_t/dP_t has expectation one. Replacing f by 2f in the exponential martingale identity gives E exp(2 Z_t^{(1)} - 2 Z_t^{(2)}) = 1. Since 0≤Z_t^{(2)}≤M^2 t J, one has exp(Z_t^{(2)})≤exp(M^2 t J). Therefore E[(dQ_t/dP_t)^2] = E[exp(2Z_t^{(1)}-Z_t^{(2)})] = E[exp(2Z_t^{(1)}-2Z_t^{(2)}) exp(Z_t^{(2)})] ≤ exp(M^2 t J). The lower bound follows from Jensen or from E[dQ_t/dP_t]=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2 — Parabolic scaling from the circle of length J to the unit circle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume g≡0. Let u solve the mild stochastic heat equation on the circle [0,J] with coefficient σ and initial profile u0. Define v(t,z)=J^{-1/2}u(J^2t,Jz) for t≥0 and z∈[0,1], and v0(z)=J^{-1/2}u0(Jz). Let W_{J^2,J}(dy ds)=J^{-3/2} W(Jdy, J^2ds), which has the same law as white noise. Then v is the mild solution on the unit circle [0,1] of partial_t v(t,x)=1/2 partial_xx v(t,x)+σ^{(J)}(t,x,v(t,x)) Wdot_{J^2,J}(t,x), v(0,x)=v0(x), where σ^{(J)}(t,x,u)=σ(J^2t,Jx,J^{1/2}u). The heat kernels satisfy G^{(1)}(J^{-2}t,J^{-1}x)=J G^{(J)}(t,x). The rescaled σ^{(J)} has the same ellipticity constants as σ, while its Lipschitz constant in u is J^{1/2}D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from the mild form for u on [0,J]. Substitute s=J^2r and y=Jw, multiply by J^{-1/2}, and use the heat-kernel scaling G^{(1)}(J^{-2}t,J^{-1}x)=J G^{(J)}(t,x). For the stochastic integral, use the distributional identity J^{-3/2}W(Jdw,J^2dr) has the law of white noise on [0,1]. The resulting integral equation is precisely the mild formulation for v with the coefficient σ(J^2r,Jw,J^{1/2}v(r,w)). Ellipticity is unchanged because σ is evaluated at transformed arguments but not multiplied; the Lipschitz constant gains the factor J^{1/2} from the argument J^{1/2}u.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 — One-block upper and lower estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Work after the reductions g≡0 and J=1, with u0≡0, and assume σ is uniformly elliptic with constants a,b and Lipschitz in u with constant D. Let K1,K2 be the constants from the local stochastic convolution bound of Lemma 3.4. Choose c0 as in equation (2.10) of the paper and define t_n=n c0 ε^4. Choose c1 by c1^2=θ c0, where θ is large enough as in equation (2.11), and define x_j=j c1 ε^2. Let I_n=[t_n,t_{n+1}], let n2 be the first integer with x_{n2}>1, and write p_{ij}=(t_i,x_j). Define A_{-1}=Ω and, for n≥0, A_n={|u(t_{n+1},x)|≤ε/3 for all x∈[0,1], and |u(t,x)|≤ε for all t∈I_n and x∈[0,1]}. Define F_{-1}=Ω and, for n≥0, F_n={|u(p_{nj})|≤ε for all j≤n2-2}. Then: (a) for every D>0 there are ε0=ε0(a,b,D)>0 and constants C4,C5>0 depending only on a,b such that for 0<ε<ε0 and n≥1, P(F_n | intersection_{k=0}^{n-1} F_k) ≤ C4 exp(-C5 / ((1+D^2)ε^2)). (b) There are D0=D0(a,b)>0 and ε0,C6,C7 depending only on a,b such that, if D<D0 and 0<ε<ε0, then for every n≥0, P(A_n | intersection_{k=-1}^{n-1} A_k) ≥ C6 exp(-C7 ε^{-2}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper bound, the paper first treats the Gaussian case D=0, so σ is independent of u. It is enough to consider the first coordinate u1 and events requiring |u1(p_{nj})|≤ε. By the Markov property, one studies one time step from an arbitrary deterministic initial profile satisfying |u0(x_j)|≤ε at the grid points. Write u1(p_{1j}) as a deterministic heat-flow term plus Gaussian stochastic convolution values N1(p_{1j}). Let G_{j-1} be the sigma-field generated by earlier values N1(p_{10}),...,N1(p_{1,j-1}). The conditional distribution of u1(p_{1j}) given G_{j-1} is Gaussian. Lemmas 4.1 and 4.2 show that its conditional variance is bounded below by c ε^2 uniformly in j; hence Gaussian anti-concentration gives P(|u1(p_{1j})|≤ε | G_{j-1})≤η<1. Multiplying over about ε^{-2} spatial grid points yields exp(-c ε^{-2}). For general D, the solution is compared with a clipped equation using f_ε(z)=z for |z|≤ε and f_ε(z)=ε z/|z| for |z|>ε, then with a frozen-coefficient Gaussian equation in which σ(t,x,f_ε(v(t,x))) is replaced by σ(t,x,f_ε(u0(x))). The difference stochastic convolution has coefficient bounded by 2Dε by Lipschitz continuity. Lemma 3.4 and Remark 3.1 bound the probability that this error exceeds ε at a grid point by exp(-c/(D^2ε^2)); a union over ε^{-2} grid points is absorbed into exp(-c/((1+D^2)ε^2)). For the lower bound, again begin with D=0 and deterministic initial profile sup_x |u0(x)|≤ε/3. A Girsanov change of measure with drift -σ^{-1}(s,y)G_su0(y)/t1 makes the solution under the new measure equal to (1-t/t1)G_tu0 plus a centered stochastic convolution, so the deterministic part is bounded by ε/3 and vanishes at t1. Lemma 3.4 gives positive probability that the convolution is ≤ε/6 on one spatial interval of length c0ε^2; the Gaussian correlation inequality multiplies this lower bound over order ε^{-2} intervals. The Radon-Nikodym second-moment estimate transfers the lower bound back to P, producing exp(-Cε^{-2}). For small nonzero D, freeze σ at u0 to get a Gaussian process u_g satisfying the preceding lower bound. On the event that u_g remains small and the difference convolution remains ≤ε/6, the original solution satisfies A_0. Up to the stopping time when u-u0 moves by more than 2ε, the difference coefficient is bounded by 2Dε, so Remark 3.1 makes the error probability exponentially small. Choosing D<D0 makes this error smaller than the Gaussian lower bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 — L2 heat-kernel increment estimates on the unit circle
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G(t,x) be the heat kernel on the unit circle [0,1] with endpoints identified, G(t,x)=sum_{n∈Z}(2πt)^{-1/2} exp(-(x+n)^2/(2t)). There is C0>0 such that for all relevant times in [0,1] and all x,y∈[0,1], the following estimates hold: ∫_0^t ∫_0^1 [G(r,x-z)-G(r,y-z)]^2 dz dr ≤ C0 |x-y|; for 0≤s<t≤1, ∫_s^t ∫_0^1 G^2(t-r,z) dz dr ≤ C0 |t-s|^{1/2}; and ∫_0^s ∫_0^1 [G(t-r,z)-G(s-r,z)]^2 dz dr ≤ C0 |t-s|^{1/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Fourier series G(t,x)=sum_{k∈Z} exp(-(2πk)^2t/2) exp(i2πkx). Orthogonality in L^2([0,1]) converts the squared spatial increment into a sum over k of exp(-(2πk)^2r)|1-exp(i2πk(x-y))|^2. Integrating in r gives factors of order k^{-2}; using |1-e^{iξ}|≤2∧|ξ| and splitting the k-sum at k≈|x-y|^{-1} gives the |x-y| bound. For the time increment over [s,t], orthogonality gives sums of ∫_0^{t-s} exp(-(2πk)^2r)dr, and splitting at k≈|t-s|^{-1/2} yields |t-s|^{1/2}. The final estimate is analogous after writing the difference of Fourier multipliers as 1-exp(-(2πk)^2(t-s)/2) and applying the same split.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 — Gaussian upper comparison for the periodic heat kernel
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For x∈[0,1], define x_* = x if 0≤x≤1/2 and x_*=x-1 if 1/2<x≤1, so |x_*| is the distance to 0 on the unit circle. There is C_G>0 such that for every t≤1 and x∈[0,1], G(t,x) ≤ C_G (2πt)^{-1/2} exp(-x_*^2/(2t)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since the heat kernel is periodic, write G(t,x)=G(t,x_*). In the image sum, the nearest images k=0,±1 are controlled by a fixed multiple of the Gaussian exp(-x_*^2/(2t)). For |k|≥2 one has -(x_*+k)^2 ≤ -x_*^2 - k^2/2, so the remaining terms are bounded by the same Gaussian factor times the summable series sum_{|k|≥2} exp(-k^2/(4t)), which is uniformly bounded for t≤1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3 — Tail bounds for spatial and temporal increments of the stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the reduced setting J=1 and g≡0, let N(t,x)=∫_0^t ∫_0^1 G(t-s,x-y) σ(s,y,u(s,y)) W(dy ds) be the stochastic convolution term in the mild solution. If the upper ellipticity constant for σ is b, then there are constants kappa1,kappa2>0 depending only on the dimension d such that for all 0<s<t<1, x,y∈[0,1], and λ>0, P(|N(t,x)-N(t,y)|>λ) ≤ kappa1 exp(-kappa2 λ^2/(b^2 |x-y|)), and P(|N(t,x)-N(s,x)|>λ) ≤ kappa1 exp(-kappa2 λ^2/(b^2 |t-s|^{1/2})).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to prove the estimates componentwise and then union bound over d. For a fixed component, the spatial increment can be represented as the terminal value of a continuous martingale in the integration upper limit q, with integrand [G(t-r,x-z)-G(t-r,y-z)]σ(r,z,u(r,z)). The quadratic variation is bounded by b^2 times the first L2 heat-kernel increment estimate of Lemma 3.1, hence by C b^2 |x-y|. By the Dambis-Dubins-Schwarz time-change theorem the martingale is a Brownian motion stopped by this time, and the reflection principle gives the Gaussian tail. The time increment is split into the new noise over [s,t] and the difference of kernels over [0,s]; Lemma 3.1 bounds the total quadratic variation by C b^2 |t-s|^{1/2}, and the same martingale tail argument applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 and Remark 3.1 — Local supremum tail for the stochastic convolution on parabolic boxes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N be the stochastic convolution from Lemma 3.3. There exist constants K1,K2>0 depending only on d such that for every α,λ,ε>0, P(sup_{0≤t≤αε^4, x∈[0,ε^2]} |N(t,x)| > λ ε) ≤ K1/(1∧sqrt(α)) exp(-K2 λ^2/(b^2 sqrt(α))), where b is the upper ellipticity constant. Remark 3.1 states that if the coefficient appearing in the stochastic convolution is instead bounded pointwise by B ε, then the same chaining proof gives the corresponding bound with the exponent denominator multiplied by B^2 ε^2, as written in the paper in the form K1/(1∧sqrt(α)) exp(-K2 λ^2/(B^2 b^2 ε^2 sqrt(α))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For α≥1, introduce anisotropic dyadic grids G_n={(j/2^{2n},k/2^n): 0≤j≤αε^4 2^{2n}, 0≤k≤ε^2 2^n}. Let n0=ceil(log_2(α^{-1/2}ε^{-2})), so for n<n0 the grid is essentially a single point. For n≥n0, define events A(n,λ) that all nearest-neighbor increments on G_n are at most λKε 2^{-(n-n0)/4}, with K chosen so that 4 sum_{n≥0}2^{-n/4} K=1. Lemma 3.3 and a union bound over at most order 2^{3(n-n0)} nearest-neighbor pairs give P(A(n,λ)^c) ≤ C 2^{3(n-n0)} exp(-c λ^2 2^{(n-n0)/2}/(b^2 sqrt(α))). Summing over n yields P(intersection A(n,λ)^c) bounded by the stated exponential. Any grid point can be connected to the origin by a chain using at most four nearest-neighbor increments at each scale, so on the good event all grid values are bounded by λε; density and continuity extend to the whole rectangle. For 0<α<1, partition [0,ε^2] into about α^{-1/2} intervals of length sqrt(α)ε^2 and apply the α=1-scale bound on each, with the rescaled threshold λ/α^{1/4}; the union bound gives the prefactor 1/sqrt(α) and exponent λ^2/sqrt(α). The remark follows by repeating the same proof with the smaller quadratic-variation bound coming from the coefficient size Bε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1 — Variance and covariance bounds for Gaussian grid variables
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the reduced setting J=1, g≡0, and in the Gaussian case D=0 so that σ is independent of u, let t1=c0ε^4, x_k=k c1ε^2, and p_{1k}=(t1,x_k). Let N1 be the first coordinate of the stochastic convolution. Then the random variables N1(p_{1k}) are centered Gaussian. There are constants C8,C9,C10 depending only on the ellipticity constants a,b such that C8 ε^2 ≤ Var(N1(p_{1k})) ≤ C9 ε^2, and Cov(N1(p_{1k}),N1(p_{1k'})) ≤ C10 t1 sup_{0≤t≤2t1} t^{-1/2} exp(-|x_k-x_{k'}|_*^2/(2t)), where |·|_* is circular distance to 0. If 0<|x_k-x_{k'}|≤1/2 and θ is chosen as in equation (2.11), then Cov(N1(p_{1k}),N1(p_{1k'})) ≤ C10 ε^2 exp(-θ(k-k')^2/8).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Gaussianity and zero mean follow from deterministic σ and the stochastic integral. Itô isometry and the heat-kernel semigroup property give a covariance bounded by C b^2 ∫_0^{t1} G(2t1-2s,x_k-x_{k'}) ds. Lemma 3.2 bounds this by C t1 sup_{0≤t≤2t1} t^{-1/2} exp(-|x_k-x_{k'}|_*^2/(2t)). The upper variance bound follows similarly by integrating G(2r,0), giving order ε^2 because t1=c0ε^4 and ∫_0^{t1} r^{-1/2}dr is order ε^2. The lower variance bound uses the lower ellipticity of σ, giving the same order from below. For off-diagonal points with circular distance at most 1/2, insert |x_k-x_{k'}|=c1ε^2|k-k'| and t1=c0ε^4. Since c1^2=θc0 and θ is chosen large relative to log(1/(2c0)), optimization over t≤2t1 yields the exponential decay exp(-θ(k-k')^2/8).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.2 — Small total regression coefficients for Gaussian grid variables
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Gaussian setting of Lemma 4.1, let G_{j-1} be the sigma-field generated by N1(p_{10}),...,N1(p_{1,j-1}). Write the conditional expectation of N1(p_{1j}) given G_{j-1} as X=sum_{k=0}^{j-1} β_k^{(j)} N1(p_{1k}). If θ is chosen as in equation (2.11), then sum_{k=0}^{j-1} |β_k^{(j)}| ≤ 1/2 for every 0≤j≤n2-2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let Y=N1(p_{1j})-X. Since Y is the Gaussian residual, it is independent of G_{j-1}, hence orthogonal to each N1(p_{1l}) for l<j. Therefore the covariance vector y with entries Cov(N1(p_{1j}),N1(p_{1l})) satisfies y=Sβ, where S is the covariance matrix of the previous variables. Thus β=S^{-1}y. Factor S=DTD, where D is diagonal with entries the standard deviations of N1(p_{1k}) and T is the correlation matrix. Lemma 4.1 gives lower variance bounds, so ||D^{-1}||_{1,1} is order ε^{-1}. The off-diagonal covariance decay in Lemma 4.1 and the choice of θ make the ℓ^1 matrix norm of A=I-T less than 1/3. Hence T^{-1}=(I-A)^{-1} has ||T^{-1}||_{1,1}≤3/2. The same off-diagonal decay bounds ||y||_1 by a small multiple of ε^2; the second inequality in equation (2.11) is chosen exactly so that ||β||_1≤||S^{-1}||_{1,1}||y||_1≤1/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3 — Gaussian correlation inequality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any centered Gaussian measure μ on R^d and any convex symmetric sets K,L⊂R^d, μ(K∩L) ≥ μ(K) μ(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Royen's proof of the Gaussian correlation inequality and the exposition by Latała and Matlak. It is used as a black box. In the proof of the lower small-ball estimate, the relevant convex symmetric sets are events that a centered Gaussian stochastic convolution remains within a fixed sup-norm ball on different spatial subintervals; the inequality allows a product lower bound without requiring independence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation (4.19) — Explicit one-block Gaussian lower bound after steering by Girsanov
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the reduced Gaussian setting J=1, g≡0, D=0, let t1=c0ε^4 and suppose the deterministic initial profile satisfies sup_x |u0(x)|≤ε/3. Define B0={|u(t1,x)|≤ε/6 for all x∈[0,1], and |u(t,x)|≤2ε/3 for all 0≤t≤t1 and x∈[0,1]}. With K1,K2 from Lemma 3.4 and ellipticity constants a,b, the paper obtains a lower bound of the form P(B0) ≥ exp(-1/(c0 a^2 ε^2)) exp((2/(c0ε^2)) log(1 - K1 exp(-K2/(36 b^2 sqrt(c0))))), after c0 is chosen so that the logarithm is well-defined. In particular P(B0) ≥ exp(-C ε^{-2}) for a constant C depending only on the ellipticity constants and dimension.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a new measure Q on F_{t1} by the exponential martingale with shift -σ^{-1}(s,y)G_su0(y)/t1, where G_su0 is the heat semigroup applied to the initial profile. Under Q, the shifted noise is white noise and the solution can be written as u(t,x)=(1-t/t1)G_tu0(x)+Ntilde(t,x). The deterministic term is bounded by ε/3 for t≤t1 and is zero at t=t1. Lemma 3.4 applied on a spatial interval of length c0ε^2 gives Q(sup on that interval |Ntilde|≤ε/6) ≥ 1-K1 exp(-K2/(36b^2 sqrt(c0))). Covering [0,1] by order 1/(c0ε^2) such intervals and applying the Gaussian correlation inequality yields the product lower bound under Q. The second moment of dQ/dP is bounded by exp(1/(c0a^2ε^2)). Finally Cauchy-Schwarz gives Q(B0) ≤ [E(dQ/dP)^2]^{1/2} P(B0)^{1/2}, which rearranges to the displayed lower bound.

proof_techniques.name: Bounded-drift removal by Girsanov
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly removes or inserts a uniformly bounded drift by shifting the driving white noise. If σ is uniformly elliptic and g is bounded, then f=σ^{-1}g is bounded, so the density exp(∫f·dW - 1/2∫|f|^2) has a controlled second moment. This permits transfer of probability estimates between the equation with drift and the equation without drift using Cauchy-Schwarz, in both upper-bound and lower-bound directions by choosing the sign of the shift.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For approximating equations with smooth drift G_{1/n}(u), this suggests comparing to an additive stochastic heat equation by a white-noise Girsanov transform, at least for fixed n or after localization. The main obstacle in the original problem is that the limiting Dirac drift is not a bounded function, so any use would need localization, approximation, or a replacement involving local time.

proof_techniques.name: Parabolic block decomposition with ε^4 time scale and ε^2 space scale
proof_techniques.description: The ε^{-6} small-ball exponent is obtained by splitting time into blocks of length c ε^4 and space into mesh intervals of length c ε^2. On each time block, the one-step probability of remaining small is of order exp(-Cε^{-2}), reflecting about ε^{-2} effective spatial degrees of freedom. Iterating over about T ε^{-4} time blocks by the Markov property yields exp(-C T ε^{-6}).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For irreducibility, one often needs only a positive probability of reaching or staying in a small ball over a fixed time, not sharp exponents. The same block structure could supply robust positive-probability estimates for the additive-noise heat equation with Dirichlet boundary conditions if analogous heat-kernel estimates are established.

proof_techniques.name: Chaining for stochastic convolution over anisotropic parabolic boxes
proof_techniques.description: The local supremum estimate for the stochastic convolution is proved using dyadic grids adapted to heat scaling: time mesh 2^{-2n} and space mesh 2^{-n}. Increment tails come from martingale time-change and L2 heat-kernel estimates. A chaining argument connects any grid point to the origin with only boundedly many increments per scale, and summing scale errors yields a Gaussian-type tail for the supremum on boxes [0,αε^4]×[0,ε^2].
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original SPDE has additive noise, so stochastic convolution estimates are simpler and should be available for the Dirichlet heat kernel. Such local sup estimates are useful for proving that the noise can remain small while a deterministic control or drift guides the solution.

proof_techniques.name: Freezing coefficients and controlling the Lipschitz error
proof_techniques.description: To handle multiplicative noise, the paper compares the true solution to a Gaussian equation in which σ(t,x,u(t,x)) is frozen at a clipped initial profile. The Lipschitz assumption implies the difference coefficient is bounded by order Dε as long as the profiles are in the relevant ε-ball. The resulting error is a stochastic convolution with small coefficient, and Remark 3.1 gives exponentially small probability that it disrupts the Gaussian estimate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original problem the noise coefficient is additive and constant, so this entire complication disappears. The method is still conceptually useful for approximating or localizing the singular drift: compare to a better-understood equation and show the error has small probability on the event of interest.

proof_techniques.name: Support theorem by subtracting a smooth target path
proof_techniques.description: The support theorem is obtained by transforming v=u-h, with an additional correction for the initial discrepancy. The transformed equation has zero initial condition, the same type of uniformly elliptic Lipschitz noise coefficient, and a new drift involving the heat operator applied to h and u0. If h and its required derivatives are bounded, the transformed drift is bounded, so the small-ball theorem applies.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness of invariant measures, this points to a route for topological irreducibility: choose a smooth path joining an initial condition to a desired neighborhood and prove the SPDE can track it with positive probability. In the original setting one would need to justify the analogous transformation despite the distributional drift or work first with the smooth approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Gaussian correlation inequality to multiply non-independent spatial events
proof_techniques.description: In the lower bound, the event that a centered Gaussian stochastic convolution is small on the whole circle is covered by events on many spatial subintervals. These events are convex and symmetric in the underlying Gaussian field but are not independent. Royen's Gaussian correlation inequality gives a lower bound for the intersection by the product of individual probabilities, enabling an exp(-Cε^{-2}) one-block lower bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For additive stochastic heat equations, small-noise events over spatial subintervals are Gaussian and convex-symmetric, so the same inequality can help prove positive support probabilities without requiring independence between subintervals.

proof_techniques.name: Conditional Gaussian anti-concentration from covariance decay
proof_techniques.description: For upper small-ball bounds, the paper studies Gaussian values at spatial grid points at a fixed time. It bounds variances below and covariances off the diagonal using heat-kernel decay. A regression calculation shows that the conditional variance of each new grid value given previous grid values remains at least order ε^2; hence the chance that it falls in an interval of radius ε is uniformly bounded away from one. Multiplying these conditional probabilities over ε^{-2} grid points gives the one-step upper estimate.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is less directly tied to uniqueness of invariant measures, but it can provide quantitative non-degeneracy or anti-concentration estimates for transition probabilities. Such estimates may help rule out degenerate invariant components or support arguments if strong Feller information is available.

proof_techniques.name: Domain-length scaling and tracking of constants
proof_techniques.description: The paper reduces estimates on a circle of length J to estimates on the unit circle through the scaling v(t,z)=J^{-1/2}u(J^2t,Jz). This preserves ellipticity but changes the Lipschitz constant to J^{1/2}D and rescales the small-ball radius to εJ^{-1/2}, explaining the J-dependence in the final estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is on a fixed interval [0,1], so global scaling is not essential. However, the same bookkeeping is useful if one localizes to subintervals or adapts the paper's periodic heat-kernel estimates to the Dirichlet heat kernel.

other_useful_info: Remark 1.1(a) notes that for the small-ball and support estimates, the assumptions on σ and g need only hold until the solution exits the relevant ε-ball, respectively the ε-ball around the target h. Remark 1.1(b) states that the small-Lipschitz threshold scales as D0(J,a,b)=D0(a,b)J^{-1/2}, and in the arbitrary-D lower bound ε0 can be chosen of order e0 (J D^2)^{-2/δ} with e0 depending on the other fixed parameters. Remark 1.1(c) observes that the existence of a sharp small-ball constant, i.e. the limit of ε^6 log P(sup|u|≤ε), is a difficult open-type question in general. The paper cites Bally-Millet-Sanz-Solé (1995) for a support theorem in Hölder topology for parabolic SPDEs, Allouba (1998) for SPDE Girsanov theorems, Walsh (1986) for stochastic integration with white noise, and Da Prato-Zabczyk for the Markov property. All estimates in the paper are for periodic boundary conditions on a circle; porting them to other one-dimensional boundary conditions would require analogous heat-kernel L2 increment estimates and Gaussian upper bounds.
why_relevant: Small-ball and support estimates for the additive stochastic heat equation are a key route to irreducibility of the Markov semigroup. Such irreducibility, combined with strong/asymptotic Feller properties, is a standard proof of uniqueness of invariant measures.

title: Harnack Inequalities and Ergodicity of Stochastic Reaction-Diffusion Equation in Lp
overall_summary: The paper proves dimension-free log-Harnack and power-Harnack inequalities for additive-noise stochastic reaction-diffusion equations on a bounded Lipschitz domain, formulated in L^p spaces for p ≥ 2, with dissipative polynomial-growth Nemytskii drift and possibly irregular but nondegenerate additive noise. The core method is coupling by change of measure: one couples two solutions by adding a singular-in-time feedback drift γ_t^{-1}(X_t−Y_t), proves a deterministic pathwise estimate forcing the two paths to meet at a prescribed time, and controls the Girsanov density by entropy and L^r moment bounds. These Harnack inequalities imply strong Feller-type regularity, gradient estimates, strict positivity of transition densities relative to an invariant measure, and at most one invariant probability measure. The paper also proves existence of an invariant measure via Krylov-Bogoliubov tightness, using time-averaged Sobolev estimates and compact embeddings. For the original problem, the most relevant content is the uniqueness mechanism: if an analogue of the paper's coupling/Harnack estimate can be established for the Dirac-drift SPDE or uniformly along smooth approximations, then uniqueness of invariant measures follows without a separate irreducibility proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Preliminary estimates (2.2), (2.3), (2.5), and (2.10)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let O be a bounded Lipschitz domain in R^d, let p ≥ 2, let A be the Dirichlet Laplacian on L^p(O), and let S_t be the analytic semigroup generated by A. If λ_1 > 0 is the first eigenvalue of −A, then for β ∈ (0,1), 1 ≤ s ≤ r ≤ ∞, and u ∈ L^s(O), the ultracontractivity estimate ∥S_t u∥_{β,r} ≤ C e^{−λ_1 t} t^{−(β/2 + d(r−s)/(2rs))} ∥u∥_s holds for t > 0. When p = 2, the paper uses the Poincare inequality ∥∇u∥_2 ≥ λ_1 ∥u∥_2 for u ∈ H_0^1(O). For β ∈ (0,d/p), the Sobolev-Slobodeckij embedding W^{β,p}(O) compactly embeds into L^p(O), where ∥φ∥_{β,p}^p = ∥φ∥_p^p + ∫_O∫_O |φ(ξ)−φ(η)|^p / |ξ−η|^{d+βp} dξ dη. Finally, if E is an M-type 2 Banach space and Φ is an adapted γ(U,E)-valued process in L^s(Ω;L^2(0,T;γ(U,E))) with s ≥ 2, then the stochastic integral satisfies E sup_{t∈[0,T]} ∥∫_0^t Φ_r dW_r∥_E^s ≤ C E(∫_0^T ∥Φ_r∥_{γ(U,E)}^2 dr)^{s/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These estimates are quoted as standard background rather than proved in the paper. The ultracontractivity estimate is used later to convert time averages of the drift F(X_t) into Sobolev W^{β,p} bounds for the deterministic convolution ∫_0^t S_{t-r}F(X_r)dr. Poincare is used in the pathwise contraction estimate when p = 2 to improve the dissipativity constant by λ_1. The compact embedding W^{β,p} ⊂ L^p is the compactness input in the Krylov-Bogoliubov tightness argument. The Banach-space Burkholder inequality is used to verify moment and continuity assumptions for stochastic convolutions driven by irregular additive noise.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Examples 2.1 and 2.2 (one-dimensional irregular additive noise)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the one-dimensional case O = (0,1), U = L^2(0,1), with Dirichlet eigenbasis e_k(x) = sqrt(2) sin(kπx) and eigenvalues λ_k = π^2 k^2, take G = (−A)^{θ/2} for θ < 1/2. Then G is a densely defined closed operator and belongs to the paper's class LS(U): for every t > 0 and r ≥ 2, S_tG extends to a γ-radonifying operator from U to L^r(O), with ∥S_tG∥_{γ(U,L^r)}^2 ≤ C ∑_{k≥1} e^{−2λ_k t} λ_k^θ. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic convolution W_A(t)=∫_0^t S_{t-r}G dW_r has a continuous version in C([0,∞);L^s(Ω;L^r(O))) for any s,r ≥ 2 and satisfies sup_{t≥0} E∥W_A(t)∥_r^s ≤ C(s)(∑_{k≥1} 1/(2λ_k^{1−θ}))^{s/2} < ∞. Moreover, if β_0 + θ < 1/2, then sup_{t≥0} E∥W_A(t)∥_{β_0,r}^s ≤ C(s)(∑_{k≥1} 1/λ_k^{1−(β_0+θ)})^{s/2} < ∞. For θ ∈ [0,1/2), G is invertible and G^{-1}=(−A)^{−θ/2} is bounded in the sense required by the paper's nondegeneracy assumption.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is the γ-radonifying characterization for L^r spaces of finite cotype: for Φ ∈ γ(U,L^r), ∥Φ∥_{γ(U,L^r)}^2 is equivalent to ∥∑_{k≥1}(Φe_k)^2∥_{r/2}. Applying this to Φ=S_tG gives ∥S_tG∥_{γ(U,L^r)}^2 ≤ C∥∑ e^{−2λ_k t}λ_k^θ e_k^2∥_{r/2} ≤ C∑ e^{−2λ_k t}λ_k^θ, using boundedness of the sine eigenfunctions. Integrating in time and applying the Banach-space Burkholder inequality yields the uniform moment estimate for W_A. Inserting an additional Sobolev factor (−A)^{β_0/2} gives the same estimate with λ_k^{β_0+θ}; convergence is equivalent to β_0+θ<1/2 in one dimension.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (well-posedness and pathwise contraction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the stochastic evolution equation dX_t = (AX_t + F(X_t))dt + GdW_t in L^p(O), p ≥ 2, where A is the Dirichlet Laplacian, W is a cylindrical Wiener process on U=L^2(O), G is an additive-noise operator for which the stochastic convolution W_A(t)=∫_0^t S_{t-r}GdW_r is defined, and F is the Nemytskii operator associated with a scalar function f. Assume that there exist L_f ∈ R, θ>0, L'_f>0, and q≥2 such that (f(ξ)−f(η))(ξ−η) ≤ L_f|ξ−η|^2 − θ|ξ−η|^q and |f(ξ)−f(η)| ≤ L'_f(1+|ξ|^{q−2}+|η|^{q−2})|ξ−η| for all ξ,η ∈ R. Assume also that W_A has a continuous version in L^{q+p−2}(O) and sup_{t≥0} E∥W_A(t)∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2}<∞. For every T>0 and x∈L^p(O), the equation has a unique mild solution X^x in C([0,T];L^p)∩L^{q+p−2}(0,T;L^{q+p−2}) almost surely, and the solution is Markov. If X^x and X^y are two solutions driven by the same noise, then for all t≥0, ∥X_t^x−X_t^y∥_p ≤ e^{−λt}∥x−y∥_p almost surely, where λ = −L_f + θ 1_{q=2,p≠2} + λ_1 1_{q≠2,p=2} + (λ_1+θ)1_{q=p=2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first writes X=Z+W_A, so that Z solves the random PDE ∂_t Z_t = ΔZ_t + F(Z_t+W_A(t)), Z_0=x. Local existence follows from the local Lipschitz bound on f. To prevent blow-up, test the random PDE against p|Z_t|^{p−2}Z_t. Integration by parts gives the coercive gradient term p(p−1)∫|Z_t|^{p−2}|∇Z_t|^2. The one-sided dissipativity condition gives p⟨|Z|^{p−2}Z,F(Z+W_A)−F(W_A)⟩ ≤ pL_f∥Z∥_p^p − pθ∥Z∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2}. The remaining term involving F(W_A) is controlled by Young's inequality, giving ∂_t∥Z_t∥_p^p plus coercive terms bounded by C(1+∥W_A(t)∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2}) + pL_f∥Z_t∥_p^p − pθ_1∥Z_t∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2}. Gronwall and the moment assumption on W_A yield global existence and the stated integrability. For continuous dependence, the difference D_t=X_t^x−X_t^y satisfies ∂_tD_t=AD_t+F(X_t^x)−F(X_t^y), with the noise cancelled. Testing by p|D_t|^{p−2}D_t gives an inequality for ∥D_t∥_p^p; applying the chain rule to ∥D_t∥_p^2 and using the q=2 dissipative term and/or Poincare when available gives ∂_t∥D_t∥_p^2 ≤ −2λ∥D_t∥_p^2. This yields the pathwise contraction estimate. Pathwise uniqueness follows immediately, and the Markov property is obtained by standard arguments for mild solutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 and estimate (3.15) (coupling, pathwise control, and Girsanov)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the hypotheses of Lemma 3.1 and the nondegeneracy condition that GG* is invertible on L^p and G^{-1}:=G*(GG*)^{-1}:L^p→U is bounded, with ∥G^{-1}∥_∞ = sup_{h≠0} ∥G^{-1}h∥_U/∥h∥_p < ∞. Fix T>0 and define γ_t = ∫_0^{T−t} e^{2λr}dr = (e^{2λ(T−t)}−1)/(2λ) for t∈[0,T], with the convention γ_t=T−t if λ=0. Then γ_t>0 on [0,T), γ_T=0, γ'_t+2λγ_t+1=0, and ∫_0^T γ_t^{−1}dt = ∫_0^T γ_t^{−2}dt = ∞. Let X solve the original equation from x∈L^p, and define Y on [0,T) from y∈L^p by dY_t=(AY_t+F(Y_t)+γ_t^{−1}(X_t−Y_t))dt+GdW_t. For s<T define v_t=G^{-1}(X_t−Y_t)/γ_t, W̃_t=W_t+∫_0^t v_rdr, and M_t=exp(−∫_0^t(v_r,dW_r)_U − 1/2∫_0^t∥v_r∥_U^2dr). Then for every s<T, ∥X_s−Y_s∥_p^2/γ_s + ∫_0^s ∥X_t−Y_t∥_p^2/γ_t^2 dt ≤ ∥x−y∥_p^2/γ_0 almost surely. Consequently E exp(1/2∫_0^s∥v_t∥_U^2dt) ≤ exp(∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(2γ_0)), so Q_s:=M_sP is a probability measure on F_s and W̃ is a cylindrical Wiener process on [0,s] under Q_s.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The difference D_t=X_t−Y_t solves the deterministic random PDE ∂_tD_t=AD_t+F(X_t)−F(Y_t)−γ_t^{−1}D_t. Testing by p|D_t|^{p−2}D_t and using the same dissipativity and Poincare improvements as in Lemma 3.1 yields ∂_t∥D_t∥_p^2 ≤ −2λ∥D_t∥_p^2 − 2γ_t^{−1}∥D_t∥_p^2. Multiply by γ_t^{−1}; because γ'_t+2λγ_t+1=0, one obtains ∂_t(γ_t^{−1}∥D_t∥_p^2) ≤ −γ_t^{−2}∥D_t∥_p^2. Integrating from 0 to s proves the pathwise estimate. The boundedness of G^{-1} gives ∫_0^s∥v_t∥_U^2dt ≤ ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∫_0^s∥D_t∥_p^2/γ_t^2dt ≤ ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/γ_0, which is stronger than Novikov's condition. Hence M_s is a true density and the standard Girsanov theorem turns W̃ into a cylindrical Wiener process under Q_s.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3 (entropy bound for the Girsanov density)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses and notation of the coupling construction, for all x,y∈L^p and T>0 one has sup_{s∈[0,T)} E[M_s log M_s] ≤ λ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(e^{2λT}−1), with the constant interpreted by continuity when λ=0. Consequently M_T:=lim_{s↑T}M_s exists and (M_s)_{s∈[0,T]} is a uniformly integrable martingale under P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For fixed s<T, under Q_s=M_sP the process W̃ is Brownian and the equations for X and Y can be rewritten with W̃ as noise: dX_t=(AX_t+F(X_t)−γ_t^{−1}(X_t−Y_t))dt+GdW̃_t and dY_t=(AY_t+F(Y_t))dt+GdW̃_t. Since Q_s is equivalent to P on F_s, the pathwise estimate from Lemma 3.2 still holds Q_s-a.s. The density can be written as log M_s = −∫_0^s(v_r,dW̃_r)_U + 1/2∫_0^s∥v_r∥_U^2dr. The pathwise estimate bounds the finite-variation term by ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(2γ_0). Taking Q_s-expectation kills the stochastic integral, giving E[M_s log M_s]=E_{Q_s}logM_s ≤ ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(2γ_0). Since 1/(2γ_0)=λ/(e^{2λT}−1), this is the stated bound. Uniform entropy boundedness implies uniform integrability and martingale convergence up to T.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4 (L^r moment bound for the Girsanov density)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses and notation of the coupling construction, for every exponent r>1 and all x,y∈L^p, sup_{t∈[0,T]} E[M_t^{r/(r−1)}] ≤ exp(rλ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/((r−1)^2(e^{2λT}−1))), again with the λ=0 case understood by continuity.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Work under Q_t=M_tP and use the representation M_t^{1/(r−1)} = exp(−(r−1)^{−1}∫_0^t(v_s,dW̃_s)_U + (2(r−1))^{−1}∫_0^t∥v_s∥^2ds). Split this into an exponential martingale with integrand −v/(r−1) and a deterministic exponential factor exp(r/(2(r−1)^2)∫_0^t∥v_s∥^2ds). The pathwise estimate bounds the latter by exp(r∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(2(r−1)^2γ_0)). Novikov holds for the rescaled integrand because the same pathwise bound controls ∫∥v∥^2. Thus the exponential martingale has Q_t-expectation one, and converting E[M_t^{r/(r−1)}] to E_{Q_t}[M_t^{1/(r−1)}] gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1 / Theorem 2.1 (log-Harnack and power-Harnack inequalities)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the dissipativity and growth hypotheses on f, the stochastic convolution moment/continuity hypothesis, and the nondegeneracy hypothesis on G^{-1} stated above. Let P_tφ(x)=Eφ(X_t^x) be the Markov semigroup of the L^p-valued mild solution. For every T>0, r>1, x,y∈L^p, and φ∈B_b^+(L^p), the log-Harnack inequality P_T logφ(y) ≤ log P_Tφ(x) + λ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(e^{2λT}−1) holds, and the power-Harnack inequality (P_Tφ(y))^r ≤ P_T(φ^r)(x) exp(rλ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/((r−1)(e^{2λT}−1))) holds. The paper states as part of Theorem 2.1 that consequently P_t admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the coupling from Lemma 3.2 and the limiting density Q=M_TP from Lemma 3.3. First prove that X_T=Y_T Q-a.s. Let τ=inf{t∈[0,T]:X_t=Y_t}. If τ>T, continuity of X−Y on [0,T] gives inf_{t∈[0,T]}∥X_t−Y_t∥_p^2>0, while the divergence of ∫_0^Tγ_t^{−2}dt forces ∫_0^T∥X_t−Y_t∥_p^2/γ_t^2dt=∞. This contradicts the pathwise estimate, which is valid Q-a.s.; hence τ≤T and pathwise uniqueness gives X_T=Y_T. Under Q, Y is a solution of the original equation started at y and driven by W̃; under P, X is the original solution started at x. Therefore this is a successful coupling by change of measure. The standard entropy inequality gives P_T logφ(y)=E_Q logφ(Y_T)=E[M_T logφ(X_T)] ≤ log Eφ(X_T)+E[M_TlogM_T], and Lemma 3.3 supplies the entropy term. Similarly, Hölder's inequality gives (P_Tφ(y))^r ≤ P_Tφ^r(x)(E M_T^{r/(r−1)})^{r−1}; Lemma 3.4 gives the stated exponential constant. The uniqueness of invariant measure is then invoked as a standard consequence of the log-Harnack framework.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.1 (gradient estimate, strong Feller consequence, and uniqueness of invariant measure)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the same assumptions as Theorem 4.1, define |∇φ|(x)=limsup_{y→x}|φ(y)−φ(x)|/∥y−x∥_p for bounded measurable φ. For every T>0, x∈L^p, and φ∈B_b(L^p), |∇P_Tφ|(x) ≤ sqrt(2λ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2/(e^{2λT}−1)) sqrt(P_Tφ^2(x)−(P_Tφ(x))^2). Consequently, P_t admits at most one invariant probability measure. If an invariant probability measure exists, then the transition kernel P_T(x,·) has a density relative to it, and that density is strictly positive.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites standard consequences of the log-Harnack inequality, specifically Wang's gradient-estimate and uniqueness results. The gradient bound follows by applying the log-Harnack inequality to perturbations of a bounded test function and optimizing the resulting entropy/variance inequality as y approaches x. The same log-Harnack inequality implies strong Feller regularization and mutual absolute-continuity/positivity properties for transition kernels relative to invariant measures. These properties imply that two invariant probability measures cannot be mutually singular and, in fact, at most one invariant probability measure can exist.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.2 (total variation estimate)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Corollary 4.1, the paper records that there exist positive constants C and T_0 such that ∥Law(X_t^x)−Law(X_t^y)∥_{TV} ≤ C e^{−λt}∥x−y∥_p for all t≥T_0 and x,y∈L^p. Here ∥μ−ν∥_{TV}=sup_{∥φ∥_∞≤1}|∫φdμ−∫φdν|.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The remark is a consequence of combining short-time smoothing from the gradient estimate with the pathwise contraction estimate. After running two solutions with the same noise up to time t−T_0, Lemma 3.1 gives ∥X_{t−T_0}^x−X_{t−T_0}^y∥_p ≤ e^{−λ(t−T_0)}∥x−y∥_p. Applying the gradient/strong-Feller bound for the remaining time T_0 converts this distance between starting points into a total variation bound between the laws at time t. Absorbing e^{λT_0} and the short-time Lipschitz constant into C gives the displayed estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.2 / Theorem 2.2 (existence, full support, and ergodicity of invariant measure)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the dissipativity/growth hypotheses on f and the stochastic convolution moment hypothesis. Also assume the Sobolev regularity condition that for some β_0∈(0,1), sup_{t≥0}E∥W_A(t)∥_{β_0,p}<∞. Suppose q>2 and d < 2p(q+p−2)/((p−1)(q−2)). Then the Markov semigroup P_t has an invariant probability measure μ on L^p with full support and μ(∥·∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2})<∞. If, in addition, the nondegeneracy condition on G^{-1} holds, then μ is the unique invariant probability measure and is ergodic. The statement in the paper places the nondegeneracy assumption only in the uniqueness clause, although the written proof of full support invokes the power-Harnack inequality.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The existence proof uses Krylov-Bogoliubov. Let μ_n=(1/n)∫_0^n δ_0P_tdt be the empirical measures starting from 0. Write X^0=Z^0+W_A. The a priori estimate from Lemma 3.1 with x=0, together with elementary inequalities comparing Z and X, gives θ_5∫_0^t∥X_r^0∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2}dr ≤ C∫_0^t(1+∥W_A(r)∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2})dr + 2^{p−1}∥W_A(t)∥_p^p. The uniform moment assumption on W_A yields sup_n μ_n(∥·∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2})<∞. Next, use the mild formula X_t^0=∫_0^tS_{t-r}F(X_r^0)dr+W_A(t). Ultracontractivity with source exponent s=(q+p−2)/(q−1) and target p gives ∥∫_0^tS_{t-r}F(X_r^0)dr∥_{β,p} bounded by an integral kernel e^{−λ_1(t−r)}(t−r)^{−α}(1+∥X_r^0∥_{q+p−2}^{q−1}), where α=β/2 + d(p−1)(q−2)/(2p(q+p−2)). The dimension condition allows β>0 small enough that α<1. Averaging in time, using Young's inequality, the previous L^{q+p−2} time-average bound, and the Sobolev moment bound on W_A gives sup_n μ_n(∥·∥_{β,p})<∞ for some β<β_0∧d/p. Since W^{β,p} compactly embeds into L^p, the measures μ_n are tight, and a subsequential weak limit is invariant. For full support, the proof applies the power-Harnack inequality with φ=1_Γ and integrates against μ to get absolute continuity of P_T(x,·) with respect to μ; if μ vanished on a ball around x_0, then P_T(x_0, that ball)=0 for all T>0, contradicting continuity in time of X_T^{x_0} at T=0. The integrability μ(∥·∥_{q+p−2}^{q+p−2})<∞ follows by integrating a finite-time version of the same coercive estimate against μ and using invariance. Under nondegeneracy, uniqueness follows from Corollary 4.1; uniqueness of an invariant probability measure implies ergodicity in the usual L^2(μ) mean sense.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 4.5 (irreducibility from full support and power-Harnack)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the conditions ensuring a unique invariant measure μ with full support on L^p, the semigroup P_t is irreducible: for every nonempty open set Γ⊂L^p, every x∈L^p, and every T>0, P_T1_Γ(x)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the power-Harnack inequality to φ=1_Γ: (P_T1_Γ(y))^r ≤ P_T1_Γ(x) exp(rλ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/((r−1)(e^{2λT}−1))). Since μ has full support, μ(Γ)>0. Invariance gives ∫P_T1_Γ(y)μ(dy)=μ(Γ)>0, so there exists y with P_T1_Γ(y)>0. The displayed inequality then forces P_T1_Γ(x)>0 for every x.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2 (L^s estimate for transition densities)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting where the invariant measure μ exists and the Harnack inequalities apply, let p_T(x,·) denote the density of P_T(x,·) with respect to μ. Then for every T>0, x∈L^p, and s>1, ∥p_T(x,·)∥_{L^s(μ)} ≤ (∫_{L^p} exp(−sλ∥G^{-1}∥_∞^2∥x−y∥_p^2/(e^{2λT}−1)) μ(dy))^{−(s−1)/s}. The paper states this under the invariant-measure assumptions of Theorem 4.2 or the alternative λ>0 existence route; the proof explicitly uses the power-Harnack inequality and hence the nondegenerate-noise framework.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let G_class={ψ∈B_b^+(L^p): μ(ψ^{s/(s−1)})≤1}. Apply the power-Harnack inequality with exponent s/(s−1) to φ∈G_class, rearrange, and integrate over y with respect to the invariant measure μ. Invariance gives μ(P_Tφ^{s/(s−1)})=μ(φ^{s/(s−1)})≤1.
