Therefore P_Tφ(x) is bounded by the displayed integral to the power −(s−1)/s. Finally use duality ∥p_T(x,·)∥_{L^s(μ)}=sup_{φ∈G_class}∫p_T(x,y)φ(y)μ(dy)=sup_{φ∈G_class}P_Tφ(x).

proof_techniques.name: Coupling by change of measure with singular terminal control
proof_techniques.description: The central construction fixes a terminal time T and introduces γ_t=∫_0^{T−t}e^{2λr}dr, so γ_T=0 and γ'_t+2λγ_t+1=0. Given a solution X from x, the coupled process Y from y solves the same SPDE but with an additional drift γ_t^{−1}(X_t−Y_t). This drift becomes singular as t approaches T and is designed to force X_T=Y_T. Because the added drift lies in the range of the additive noise through the bounded inverse G^{-1}, it can be absorbed into the noise by Girsanov: v_t=G^{-1}(X_t−Y_t)/γ_t, W̃_t=W_t+∫_0^t v_rdr.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirac-drift SPDE, this is the most direct route to uniqueness: establish the same forced coupling for either the limiting equation or the Gaussian-drift approximations, with additive white noise corresponding formally to a nondegenerate G. The key quantity to reproduce is the deterministic estimate controlling ∫∥X_t−Y_t∥^2/γ_t^2dt, since it simultaneously gives Novikov and terminal coupling.

proof_techniques.name: Pathwise L^p contraction from one-sided dissipativity
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses that when two solutions are driven by the same additive noise, their difference satisfies a deterministic random PDE. Testing this difference equation by p|D|^{p−2}D, using integration by parts and the one-sided dissipativity of f, yields a differential inequality for ∥D_t∥_p^2. In the coupling proof, the feedback term −γ_t^{−1}D_t adds exactly −2γ_t^{−1}∥D_t∥_p^2, and the identity for γ_t turns the estimate into a monotone inequality for γ_t^{−1}∥D_t∥_p^2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For smooth approximations G_{1/n} of δ_0, one can test whether a one-sided Lipschitz version of this estimate holds with q=2; constants may depend on n, but the form of the estimate identifies what must be uniform to pass to the limit. For a direct treatment of the distributional drift, an analogue would likely require an integration-by-parts/local-time interpretation of the drift contribution in the difference equation.

proof_techniques.name: Entropy and moment control of the Girsanov density via deterministic energy
proof_techniques.description: After the pathwise estimate bounds ∫_0^T∥v_t∥^2dt, the paper obtains both an entropy bound E[M_TlogM_T] and an L^{r/(r−1)} bound E[M_T^{r/(r−1)}]. These are not probabilistic estimates of the solution itself; they are deterministic consequences of the coupling energy inequality and boundedness of G^{-1}. The entropy bound feeds directly into log-Harnack, while the L^r bound feeds directly into power-Harnack.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can construct the same Girsanov shift for the original SPDE, proving just this energy bound is enough to get Harnack inequalities. In approximation approaches, these estimates indicate that uniform entropy or L^r control of the change-of-measure densities is the critical compactness requirement.

proof_techniques.name: Terminal meeting from divergence of the control kernel
proof_techniques.description: The control γ_t is chosen so that γ_t→0 and ∫_0^Tγ_t^{−2}dt=∞. The pathwise estimate says ∫_0^T∥X_t−Y_t∥^2/γ_t^2dt is finite. If the two paths had not met by T, continuity would imply ∥X_t−Y_t∥ is bounded below near T, forcing the integral to diverge. This contradiction proves X_T=Y_T under the changed measure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This argument only uses continuity of sample paths in the chosen state norm plus the energy estimate. It is therefore potentially portable to C_0([0,1]) if the solution has continuous paths there and if the coupling estimate can be obtained in a compatible norm such as L^2 or sup norm.

proof_techniques.name: Harnack inequalities as a substitute for separate strong Feller and irreducibility proofs
proof_techniques.description: Once the successful coupling by change of measure is established, the log-Harnack inequality follows from the entropy inequality, and the power-Harnack inequality follows from Hölder's inequality. The paper then invokes standard Wang-type consequences: gradient estimates, strong Feller regularization, positivity of densities with respect to invariant measures, and uniqueness of invariant probability measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem asks only for at most one invariant measure. Thus a full ergodic theorem is unnecessary: a log-Harnack inequality for the Dirac-drift semigroup, or a limiting log-Harnack inequality from approximations, would be enough to prove uniqueness by the same abstract implication.

proof_techniques.name: Krylov-Bogoliubov tightness through time-averaged Sobolev regularity
proof_techniques.description: For existence of invariant measures, the paper averages laws from the zero initial condition and proves uniform bounds first in L^{q+p−2} and then in W^{β,p}. The W^{β,p} estimate comes from applying heat semigroup ultracontractivity to the deterministic convolution of F(X_t), while the stochastic convolution supplies the remaining Sobolev regularity. Compact embedding W^{β,p}⊂L^p yields tightness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original question assumes a Markov semigroup and asks uniqueness, this compactness method could help if one also wants existence for the Dirac-drift equation on [0,1]. The one-dimensional Dirichlet heat kernel and space-time white noise fit the paper's stochastic convolution estimates particularly well.

proof_techniques.name: Full support and irreducibility from power-Harnack plus invariant measure support
proof_techniques.description: The paper shows that if an invariant measure has full support and power-Harnack holds, then every nonempty open set has positive transition probability from every initial condition. The argument is purely measure-theoretic: invariance and full support give some point y with P_T1_Γ(y)>0, and the Harnack inequality transfers positivity from y to any x.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an invariant measure for the Dirac-drift SPDE can be shown to have full support, power-Harnack would imply irreducibility automatically. This can complement strong Feller arguments if one chooses to prove uniqueness through the classical Doob-Khasminskii route rather than the direct log-Harnack uniqueness theorem.

other_useful_info: Remark 3.1 records a Wasserstein contraction: if two solutions start with laws μ_0^1 and μ_0^2 and are coupled by the same noise, then W_r(μ_t^1,μ_t^2) ≤ e^{−λt}W_r(μ_0^1,μ_0^2) for every r≥1. Remark 4.1 notes that previous uniqueness of invariant measures for the one-dimensional white-noise stochastic heat equation with Lipschitz coefficients in L^p(0,1) was known for p>4, and the paper's Harnack method fills the gap p∈(2,4] in the additive-noise case. Remark 4.4 says that when λ>0, the remote-start method from Da Prato-Zabczyk may give existence of an invariant measure without the dimension restriction used in Theorem 4.2. The paper cites Wang's monograph Harnack inequalities for stochastic partial differential equations, especially Theorem 1.1.1 for coupling-by-change-of-measure Harnack inequalities, Proposition 1.3.8 for gradient estimates from log-Harnack, and Theorem 1.4.1 for uniqueness/positivity consequences. The one-dimensional example G=I, corresponding to θ=0 in G=(−A)^{θ/2}, verifies the paper's stochastic convolution regularity and nondegeneracy assumptions for additive space-time white noise on (0,1).
why_relevant: Harnack inequalities are another standard route to strong Feller properties, irreducibility, and uniqueness of invariant measures for stochastic reaction-diffusion equations. The techniques may be adaptable to smooth δ0-approximations or limiting arguments.

title: Well-posedness of stochastic heat equation with distributional drift and skew stochastic heat equation
overall_summary: The paper develops a solution theory for one-dimensional stochastic heat equations of the form ∂_t u = 1/2 ∂_{xx} u + b(u) + dot W when the drift b is a distribution in a Besov space B^β_q, including finite measures and in particular b = κδ_0. The main results are weak existence for β - 1/q > -3/2, strong existence and uniqueness in a natural drift-regularity class for β - 1/q ≥ -1 and β > -1, and unconditional strong well-posedness for finite non-negative measure drifts; the skew stochastic heat equation b = κδ_0 is covered. The core technique is a family of stochastic sewing lemmas, combined with Besov heat-kernel regularization estimates, tightness of smooth approximations, a Gyöngy-Krylov/Yamada-Watanabe argument, and a Davie-type critical uniqueness argument. For the original invariant-measure problem, the most useful contributions are the approximation theorem by smooth drifts, the comparison principle, the a priori C^{3/4}-type drift estimates for positive measure drifts, and the warning that the paper itself excludes Dirichlet boundary conditions because the uniform local nondeterminism estimate degenerates near the boundary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Assumption 2.1, Definitions 2.2-2.4: paper setting, distributional mild solution, and class V(κ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix T0 > 0. The paper treats three heat-kernel settings: D = R with the free heat kernel g_t(x-y), D = [0,1] with periodic heat kernel p^{per}, and D = [0,1] with Neumann heat kernel p^{Neu}. The notation p denotes the corresponding kernel and P_t φ(x) = ∫_D p_t(x,y) φ(y) dy is the heat semigroup. Let W be space-time white noise and V_t(x) = ∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy). For b in the Besov space B^β_q(R), a sequence b_n of smooth bounded functions converges to b in B^{β-}_q if sup_n ∥b_n∥_{B^β_q} < ∞ and ∥b_n - b∥_{B^{β'}_q} → 0 for every β' < β. A measurable adapted process u on (0,T0] × D is a solution with bounded measurable initial condition u0 if there is a process K such that u_t(x) = P_t u0(x) + K_t(x) + V_t(x) a.s.; for every smooth bounded b_n converging to b in B^{β-}_q, the quantities ∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr converge to K_t(x) uniformly in t ∈ [0,T0] and locally uniformly in x in probability; and u is a.s. continuous on (0,T0] × D. A solution belongs to V(κ), κ ∈ [0,1], if all pointwise moments are uniformly finite and, for every m ≥ 2, sup_{0<s≤t≤T0,x∈D} ∥u_t(x) - V_t(x) - P_{t-s}(u_s - V_s)(x)∥_{L^m} / |t-s|^κ < ∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions rather than proved results. The point is that when b is an actual Hölder or bounded continuous function, choosing b_n converging uniformly recovers the usual mild formulation u_t = P_t u0 + ∫_0^t P_{t-r} b(u_r) dr + V_t. When b is distributional, the nonlinear drift is defined only as the limit K of smooth drift convolutions. The class V(κ) measures the time-regularity of this drift term after factoring out the stochastic convolution V and the heat flow.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate (2.5): uniform local nondeterminism of the stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's three settings D = R, D = [0,1] periodic, and D = [0,1] Neumann, the stochastic convolution V_t(x) = ∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy) satisfies, for every 0 ≤ s ≤ t ≤ T0 and x ∈ D, the lower bound ∥V_t(x) - E[V_t(x) | F_s]∥_{L^2} ≥ π^{-1/4} |t-s|^{1/4}. The estimate is uniform in x in these three settings.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Conditioning on F_s leaves exactly the future-noise increment ∫_s^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy). Its variance is ∫_s^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)^2 dy dr = ∫_s^t p_{2(t-r)}(x,x) dr. Lemma C.1 gives a uniform lower bound p_a(x,x) ≥ (2πa)^{-1/2}, and integrating a^{-1/2} over a ∈ (0,t-s) gives a constant times |t-s|^{1/2}; taking the square root yields the |t-s|^{1/4} bound. The paper explicitly notes that this uniformity fails for Dirichlet boundary conditions because the lower bound degenerates as x approaches the boundary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6: weak and strong well-posedness for Besov distributional drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (D,p) be one of the paper's three settings: R/free, [0,1]/periodic, or [0,1]/Neumann. Let β ∈ R, q ∈ [1,∞], b ∈ B^β_q(R), and u0 ∈ B(D), the bounded measurable functions on D. If β - 1/q > -3/2, then the stochastic heat equation ∂_t u = 1/2 ∂_{xx}u + b(u) + dot W has a weak solution in the sense of Definition 2.3, and this solution is in V(κ) for every κ ∈ (0, 1 + (β∧0)/4 - 1/(4q)] except κ = 1. If β - 1/q ≥ -1 and β > -1, then in the class V(3/4) there is a unique strong solution.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is organized through the propositions in Section 3. First reduce to q ≥ 2 and β < 0 using Besov embeddings. For existence, approximate b by bounded continuous or smooth b_n and solve the classical mild equations. Proposition 3.2 gives uniform a priori bounds on the drift parts u^n - V in a conditional C^{1+β/4-1/(4q)}-type seminorm. Lemma 5.4 and Proposition 3.3 give tightness of the drift-plus-noise fields in C_uc([0,T0]×D). Proposition 3.4 identifies any limit as a solution to the distributional equation by decomposing K^{bar b_n;u} - K into a fixed-smooth continuity term, a Besov drift-approximation term, and a convergence term. Strong uniqueness in the critical range β - 1/q ≥ -1, β > -1 is Proposition 3.6: compare two solutions driven by the same noise, freeze the drift at the left endpoint, use critical stochastic sewing to control the residual, and close a Davie-type logarithmic Grönwall argument. Strong existence follows by applying the Gyöngy-Krylov criterion to arbitrary pairs of approximation subsequences: any joint weak limit consists of two solutions driven by the same white noise, uniqueness forces the two limits to coincide, and hence the full approximation sequence converges in probability to a strong solution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.8 and Corollary 2.9: finite non-negative measure drifts and skew stochastic heat equation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let b be a finite non-negative Radon measure on R. In any of the paper's three heat-kernel settings and for every bounded initial condition u0, the stochastic heat equation with drift b has a unique strong solution in the sense of Definition 2.3. Consequently, the skew stochastic heat equation, namely the same equation with b = κ δ_0 for κ ∈ R, has a unique strong solution for every bounded measurable initial condition u0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.6 already gives existence and uniqueness in V(3/4) for measure drifts, since finite measures are covered by the Besov assumptions. The extra issue is to show that the auxiliary assumption u ∈ V(3/4) is automatic. This is Proposition 3.8. For non-negative b, choose smooth non-negative b_n approximating b. The approximating drift terms K^n_t(x) = ∫_0^t ∫ p_{t-r}(x,y) b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr are non-negative and converge to K. Positivity implies the heat-flow monotonicity P_{T-t}K_t(x) ≥ P_{T-s}K_s(x), so λ^T_{s,t}(x) = P_{T-t}K_t(x) - P_{T-s}K_s(x) is a random control. Applying the stochastic sewing lemma with random controls to the frozen approximating drift yields K^n_t(x)-P_{t-s}K^n_s(x) ≤ C(K_t(x)-P_{t-s}K_s(x))(t-s)^{1/2} + L^n_{s,t}(x), with ∥L^n_{s,t}(x)∥_{L^m} ≤ C|t-s|^{3/4}. Passing to the limit and absorbing the first term on short intervals gives the V(3/4) estimate. The corollary for κδ_0 is stated immediately after Theorem 2.8; κ ≥ 0 is direct, and the paper records the conclusion for all real κ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10: convergence and stability of smooth approximations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume β ∈ R, q ∈ [1,∞], β - 1/q ≥ -1, β > -1, b ∈ B^β_q(R), and u0 ∈ B(D). Let b_n be bounded continuous functions converging to b in B^{β-}_q, and let u^n_0 ∈ B(D) converge uniformly to u0. If u^n is the strong mild solution with drift b_n and initial condition u^n_0, then there exists a measurable u on [0,T0]×D×Ω such that for every N > 0, sup_{t∈(0,T0], x∈D, |x|≤N} |u^n_t(x)-u_t(x)| → 0 in probability; u is a strong solution to the distributional equation with drift b and initial condition u0; and for every m ≥ 1, sup_{0<s<t≤T0,x∈D} ess sup_ω E[|u_t(x)-V_t(x)-P_{t-s}(u_s-V_s)(x)|^m | F_s]^{1/m} / |t-s|^{3/4} < ∞. In particular u ∈ V(3/4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the Gyöngy-Krylov lemma. Take two arbitrary subsequences of the approximations. Proposition 3.3 gives tightness of the centered fields u^n - P_t u^n_0 together with V. After Skorokhod representation, Lemma B.2 reconstructs a white noise from the limiting stochastic convolution and ensures the two limiting fields are adapted to a common filtration. Proposition 3.4 identifies both limits as distributional solutions and provides the conditional regularity estimate. Proposition 3.6 then gives equality of the two limits. Since every pair of subsequences has a further joint subsequence whose two components coincide a.s., Gyöngy-Krylov implies convergence in probability of the whole sequence. Uniform convergence of the deterministic initial heat flows supplies the convergence of u^n rather than just u^n - P_t u^n_0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.11: comparison principle for distributional drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let β ∈ R, q ∈ [1,∞], β - 1/q ≥ -1, and β > -1. Let b' and b'' be elements of B^β_q(R), and let u'_0,u''_0 ∈ B(D). Let u' and u'' be the corresponding solutions. If u'_0(x) ≤ u''_0(x) for almost every x ∈ D and b' ≼ b'', meaning that b'' - b' is a non-negative Radon measure equivalently <b',φ> ≤ <b'',φ> for every non-negative test function φ, then almost surely u'(t,x) ≤ u''(t,x) for all t > 0 and x ∈ D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Mollify both drifts by the heat kernel on R: b'_n = G_{1/n}b' and b''_n = G_{1/n}b''. Heat convolution preserves the distributional order, so b'_n(x) ≤ b''_n(x) pointwise. For smooth bounded drifts, the standard SPDE comparison principle gives u'^n(t,x) ≤ u''^n(t,x) for all t,x. Lemma A.3 gives b'_n → b' and b''_n → b'' in B^{β-}_q. Theorem 2.10 permits passage to the limit in probability, and continuity of the limits upgrades pointwise fixed-(t,x) inequalities to simultaneous inequalities over all t > 0 and x ∈ D.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2: uniform conditional a priori estimate for smooth or continuous approximations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the paper's standing reduction β < 0, q ∈ [2,∞], and β - 1/q > -3/2, let f:R→R be bounded continuous with f ∈ B^β_q(R), let η ∈ B(D), and let u^{η;f} solve the classical mild equation with initial condition η and drift f. For every m ∈ [2,∞), there is C = C(β,q,m,T0), independent of f and η, such that [u^{η;f}-V]_{C^{1+β/4-1/(4q),0}L^m,∞([0,T0])} ≤ C∥f∥_{B^β_q}. Here [ψ]_{C^{τ,0}L^m,∞} is the supremum over 0≤s≤t≤T0 and x∈D of ∥∥ψ_t(x)-P_{t-s}ψ_s(x)∥_{L^m|F_s}∥_{L^∞} / |t-s|^τ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For bounded f the seminorm is initially finite with exponent at most one. Apply Lemma 5.2(i) to the drift increment ψ_t-P_{t-s}ψ_s = ∫_s^t P_{t-r} f(V_r+ψ_r) dr, taking γ = β - 1/q, p = ∞, and τ = 1 + β/4 - 1/(4q), using the embedding B^β_q into B^{β-1/q}_∞. This yields a local inequality of the form [ψ]_{τ;[S,S+ℓ]} ≤ C∥f∥ + C∥f∥[ψ]_{τ;[S,S+ℓ]} ℓ^{3/4+β/4-1/(4q)}. Since β - 1/q > -3/2, the exponent of ℓ is positive, so choose ℓ small enough to absorb the second term. To extend to [0,T0], subdivide any interval into pieces of length at most ℓ and use the telescoping identity ψ_t-P_{t-s}ψ_s = Σ_k P_{t-s_k}(ψ_{s_k}-P_{s_k-s_{k-1}}ψ_{s_{k-1}}) together with heat-semigroup contraction in the conditional norms.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 3.3-3.4 and Corollary 3.5: tightness, stability, and joint weak-limit construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the standing assumptions β < 0, q ∈ [2,∞], β - 1/q > -3/2, let b'_n and b''_n be bounded continuous functions converging to b in B^{β-}_q, and let u'_{0,n},u''_{0,n} converge uniformly to u0. If u'_n,u''_n solve the corresponding classical equations, and e u'_n = u'_n - P_t u'_{0,n}, e u''_n = u''_n - P_t u''_{0,n}, then Proposition 3.3 says that some subsequence of (e u'_n,e u''_n,V) converges weakly in [C_uc([0,T0]×D)]^3. Proposition 3.4 says that if b_n→b in B^{β-}_q, u^n_0→u0 uniformly, V^n has the same law as V, u^n solves the classical equation driven by V^n, and (e u^n,V^n) converges in probability in [C_uc]^2 to (e u,V), then u = e u + P_tu0 is a distributional solution and satisfies [u-V]_{C^{1+β/4-1/(4q),0}L^m,∞([0,T0])} ≤ C sup_n ∥b_n∥_{B^β_q}. Corollary 3.5 combines these facts: after a Skorokhod representation of any tight subsequential limit, there is a filtered probability space carrying a common white noise and two adapted weak solutions v',v'' of the limiting equation, with both satisfying the same conditional regularity bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For tightness, write u^n - P_tu^n_0 = K^n + V. Lemma 5.4 gives a Kolmogorov-type modulus for K^n: for every δ∈(0,1), |K^n(t,x1)-K^n(s,x2)| ≤ H_n(1+|x1|+|x2|)(|x1-x2|^δ+|t-s|^{δ/2}) with E H_n uniformly bounded in terms of the Besov norms. Arzela-Ascoli and Prokhorov give tightness. For stability, define K = e u - V and verify Definition 2.3. For a test approximation bar b_n, decompose |K^{bar b_n;u}-K| into: the difference between u and u^k for fixed smooth bar b_n, controlled by uniform convergence; the difference between bar b_n and b_k evaluated along u^k, controlled by Lemma 5.4 and convergence in B^{β'}_q for β'<β; and K^{b_k;u^k}-K, controlled by convergence of (e u^k,V^k). Regularity passes to the limit by Fatou from Proposition 3.2. Corollary 3.5 uses Skorokhod representation, then Lemma B.2 to reconstruct a white noise from the limiting stochastic convolution and prove that the limiting processes are adapted to a common filtration.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6: uniqueness criterion for two solutions driven by the same noise
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume β < 0, q ∈ [2,∞], β - 1/q ≥ -1, and β > -1. Let u be a solution of the distributional SPDE with initial condition u0. Let v be a measurable process satisfying the representation and continuity parts of Definition 2.3 and the weaker approximation condition that there exists one smooth approximating sequence b_n→b in B^{β-}_q for which ∫_0^t P_{t-r} b_n(v_r) dr converges pointwise in probability to its drift term. Suppose u and v are adapted to the same filtration, both belong to V(3/4), and for some m ≥ 2, [u-V]_{C^{3/4,0}L^m,∞([0,T0])} < ∞. Then u = v almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set ψ = u - V, φ = v - V, and z = u - v. For smooth approximations b_n define frozen increments H^{n,ψ}_{s,t}(x) = ∫_s^t ∫ p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}ψ_s(y))dy dr, and similarly H^{n,φ}. Lemma 5.5 shows H^{n,ψ} and H^{n,φ} converge in probability to H^ψ and H^φ, with ∥H^ψ_{s,t}-H^φ_{s,t}∥_{L^m} ≤ C∥z_s∥_{BL^m}(t-s)^{1/2} and also ≤ C(t-s)^{3/4}. Define the residual R_{s,t} = (ψ_t-P_{t-s}ψ_s-H^ψ_{s,t}) - (φ_t-P_{t-s}φ_s-H^φ_{s,t}). Lemma 5.7, proved by the critical stochastic sewing lemma, gives for some δ>0 and every τ>1/2 the estimates ∥z_t-P_{t-s}z_s∥_{BL^m} and ∥R_{s,t}∥_{BL^m} bounded by C(∥z∥_{C^0L^m([s,t])}+∥R∥_{C^τL^m([s,t])})(t-s)^{1/2+δ} plus C∥z∥_{C^0L^m([s,t])}|log∥z∥_{C^0L^m([s,t])}|(t-s). On sufficiently short intervals the R term is absorbed, yielding a closed inequality for z. Continuity of t↦∥z_t∥_{BL^m} follows from the equation and heat-continuity estimates. If z were nonzero, define decreasing hitting times t_k where ∥z_{t_k}∥_{BL^m}=2^{-k}; the inequality forces t_k-t_{k+1} ≥ c/(1+k), whose sum diverges, contradicting boundedness of the time interval. Hence z≡0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8: positive measure drifts automatically satisfy V(3/4)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If b is a finite non-negative Radon measure, then every solution of the distributional stochastic heat equation with drift b belongs to V(3/4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose smooth bounded non-negative b_n converging to b in B^{0-}_1 with uniformly bounded B^0_1 norm. For a solution u, define K^n_t(x)=∫_0^t∫p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr; by the definition of solution, K^n→K locally uniformly in probability along subsequences. Positivity implies the deterministic inequality P_{T-t}K^n_t(x) - P_{T-s}K^n_s(x) = ∫_s^t∫p_{T-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr ≥ 0, and after passing to the limit P_{T-t}K_t(x) ≥ P_{T-s}K_s(x). Thus λ^T_{s,t}(x)=P_{T-t}K_t(x)-P_{T-s}K_s(x) is a random control. Define frozen approximating increments A^{T,n}_{s,t}(x)=∫_s^t∫p_{T-r}(x,y)b_n(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}(u_s-V_s)(y))dy dr. The conditional mean of δA is bounded by C|t-s|^{1/2}λ^T_{s,t}(x), while the martingale part has order |t-s|^{3/4}; these are exactly the hypotheses of Theorem 4.7. The resulting estimate, after setting T=t and passing n→∞, is K_t-P_{t-s}K_s ≤ C(K_t-P_{t-s}K_s)(t-s)^{1/2}+L_{s,t} with ∥L_{s,t}∥_{L^m}≤C|t-s|^{3/4}. Absorb the first term on short intervals and patch intervals to obtain the V(3/4) seminorm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2: main regularization estimates for ∫P f(V+ψ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:R→R be bounded continuous and in B^γ_p(R). Let ψ:[0,T]×D×Ω→R be measurable and adapted. Fix m∈[2,∞), n∈[m,∞], p∈[n,∞], τ∈(0,1), and γ∈(-2+1/p,0), with γ - 1/p + 4τ > 1. Then there is C depending on γ,p,τ,m, and where relevant δ, but not on T, such that: (i) for every S∈[0,T], sup_x ∥∥∫_S^T∫_D p_{T-r}(x,y)f(V_r(y)+ψ_r(y))dy dr∥_{L^m|F_S}∥_{L^n} ≤ C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}(T-S)^{1+γ/4-1/(4p)} + C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}[ψ]_{C^{τ,0}L^m,n([S,T])}(T-S)^{3/4+γ/4-1/(4p)+τ}; (ii) for every δ∈(0,1) and x1,x2∈D, the L^m norm of the same integral with p_{T-r}(x1,y)-p_{T-r}(x2,y) in place of p_{T-r}(x,y) is at most C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}|x1-x2|^δ times (T-S)^{1-δ/2+(γ-1/p)/4} + [ψ]_{C^{τ,0}L^m([S,T])}(T-S)^{3/4-δ/2+γ/4-1/(4p)+τ}; (iii) for every δ∈(0,1) and bar T>0, the L^m norm of the integral with p_{T+bar T-r}(x,y)-p_{T-r}(x,y) is bounded by the same expression multiplied by bar T^{δ/2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to Lemma 6.3, a stochastic-sewing estimate with a deterministic kernel X_r(y) satisfying ∫_D |X_r(y)|dy ≤ Γ_X(T-r)^{-ν}. For smooth f, set A_{s,t}=∫_s^t∫X_r(y)f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}ψ_s(y))dy dr and A_t=∫_S^t∫X_r(y)f(V_r(y)+ψ_r(y))dy dr. The increment δA is controlled by the difference between ψ_u and P_{u-s}ψ_s. Conditional Gaussian estimates for V, in particular Lemma C.4, give factors (r-u)^{γ/4-1/(4p)}; Besov translation estimates give the dependence on the C^{τ,0} seminorm of ψ. These verify the stochastic sewing hypotheses and yield the first estimate for general X. Taking X=p gives (i); taking X=p(x1,.)-p(x2,.) and using heat-kernel L^1 Hölder bounds gives (ii); taking X=p_{T+bar T-r}-p_{T-r} and the heat-kernel time-increment bound gives (iii). For bounded continuous f, approximate by heat mollifications G_{1/k}f and pass by dominated convergence while using uniform Besov bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Corollary 6.2: frozen-path Besov regularization and first/second difference estimates
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 5.1 states that if f∈B^γ_p is bounded, m∈[2,∞), p∈[m,∞], γ∈(-2,0), 0≤s≤t≤T, and κ:D×Ω→R is B(D)⊗F_s-measurable with sup_x∥κ(x)∥_{L^m}<∞, then sup_x ∥∫_s^t∫_D p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}κ(y))dy dr∥_{L^m} ≤ C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}(t-s)^{1+γ/4-1/(4p)}. Corollary 6.2 gives stronger forms. If X_r(y) satisfies ∫|X_r(y)|dy ≤ Γ_X(T-r)^{-ν} with ν<1/2, the same frozen estimate holds conditionally with Γ_X(T-s)^{-ν}. If λ∈(0,1] and γ>-2+λ, then replacing κ by two frozen fields κ1,κ2 gives a difference bound of order ∥κ1-κ2∥_{BL^m}^λ (t-s)^{1+(γ-λ)/4-1/(4p)}. If λ,λ1,λ2∈(0,1], γ>-2+λ and γ>-2+λ1+λ2, a second-order difference involving κ1,κ2 frozen at F_s and κ3,κ4 frozen at F_u is bounded by a product term involving ∥κ1-κ2∥^{λ1}, a conditional ∥κ1-κ3∥^{λ2}, and a remainder involving ∥κ1-κ2-κ3+κ4∥^λ, with the corresponding time powers shown in formula (6.11).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 6.1 is the engine. For A_{s,t}=E_s∫_s^t∫X_r(y)h(V_r(y),r,y)dy dr, with h F_S-measurable in ω and uniformly B^γ_p in the spatial argument, conditional Gaussian smoothing gives ∥E_u h(V_r)∥ ≤ C∥h∥_{B^γ_p}(r-u)^{γ/4}(u-S)^{-1/(2p)}(r-S)^{1/(4p)}. This verifies the stochastic sewing increment estimates, and the Riemann sums converge by martingale orthogonality. To get the frozen estimates, take h(z,r,y)=f(z+P_{r-s}κ(y)); Besov translation invariance gives the same norm as f. For first differences use ∥f(·+a)-f(·+b)∥_{B^{γ-λ}_p} ≤ C|a-b|^λ∥f∥_{B^γ_p}. For second differences use the Besov two-increment estimate ∥f(·+a1)-f(·+a2)-f(·+a3)+f(·+a3+a2-a1)∥_{B^{γ-λ1-λ2}_p} ≤ C|a1-a2|^{λ1}|a1-a3|^{λ2}∥f∥_{B^γ_p}, then apply heat-semigroup contraction to the random frozen fields.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1: stochastic sewing lemma with singularities and conditional norms
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 0≤S<T, m∈[2,∞), n∈[m,∞], and A_{s,t} be F_t-measurable for (s,t)∈Δ_{S,T}. Write δA_{s,u,t}=A_{s,t}-A_{s,u}-A_{u,t}. Suppose that for u=(s+t)/2, ∥E_s δA_{s,u,t}∥_{L^n} ≤ Γ1(u-S)^{-α1}(T-u)^{-β1}|t-s|^{1+ε1} with α1,β1∈[0,1), ε1>0, and ∥∥δA_{s,u,t}∥_{L^m|F_S}∥_{L^n} ≤ Γ2(u-S)^{-α2}(T-u)^{-β2}|t-s|^{1/2+ε2} with α2,β2∈[0,1/2), ε2>0. Assume there is a process A_t such that Riemann sums Σ A_{t_i,t_{i+1}} over partitions of [S,t] converge in probability to A_t. Then, with η^{(α,β)}_{S,T}(s,t)=∫_s^t(r-S)^{-α}(T-r)^{-β}dr, ∥∥A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}∥_{L^m|F_S}∥_{L^n} ≤ CΓ2(η^{(2α2,2β2)}_{S,T}(s,t))^{1/2}|t-s|^{ε2}+CΓ1η^{(α1,β1)}_{S,T}(s,t)|t-s|^{ε1}, and ∥E_S[A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}]∥_{L^n} ≤ CΓ1η^{(α1,β1)}_{S,T}(s,t)|t-s|^{ε1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use dyadic partitions π^k of [s,t]. Let A^k_{s,t}=Σ_i A_{t_i^k,t_{i+1}^k}. The dyadic refinement difference is A^{k+1}_{s,t}-A^k_{s,t}=Σ_i δA_{t_i^k,u_i^k,t_{i+1}^k}. Decompose this into I^k, the sum of conditional expectations given F_{t_i^k}, and J^k, a martingale-difference sum. The I^k terms are bounded by summing the assumed conditional-mean estimate; the singular factors are integrated using elementary bounds like (B.6). The J^k terms are bounded by the conditional Burkholder-Davis-Gundy inequality and Minkowski, producing the square-root η term. Summing over k gives a Cauchy estimate between A^k and A^0=A_{s,t}. Passing k→∞ with the assumed Riemann-sum convergence and Fatou yields the conclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 4.5 and 4.7: critical stochastic sewing and stochastic sewing with random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 4.5 assumes the hypotheses of Theorem 4.1 with n=m and no singular factors α_i=β_i=0, and in addition assumes a borderline conditional mean estimate ∥E_sδA_{s,u,t}∥_{L^m} ≤ Γ3|t-s| + Γ4|t-s|^{1+ε4}. Then ∥A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}∥_{L^m} ≤ CΓ3(1+|log(Γ1T^{ε1}/Γ3)|)(t-s)+CΓ2(t-s)^{1/2+ε2}+CΓ4(t-s)^{1+ε4}. Theorem 4.7 assumes a random control λ on Δ_{S,T}, meaning λ(s,u)+λ(u,t)≤λ(s,t) a.s., and the conditional mean bound |E_uδA_{s,u,t}| ≤ Γ1|t-s|^{α1}λ(s,t)^{β1} with α1+β1>1, together with a martingale increment bound of the Theorem 4.1 type with no singularities. If Riemann sums converge to A_t, then there is a random error B_{s,t}, a functional of the midpoint increments, such that |A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}| ≤ CΓ1|t-s|^{α1}λ(s,t)^{β1}+B_{s,t} a.s. and ∥B_{s,t}∥_{L^m} ≤ CΓ2|t-s|^{1/2+ε2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Theorem 4.5, the dyadic decomposition from Theorem 4.1 gives two possible bounds on each refinement level: a summable supercritical bound involving Γ1 2^{-kε1}, and a critical bound Γ3(t-s)+Γ4 2^{-kε4}(t-s)^{1+ε4}. Split the dyadic sum at level k. For levels below k use the critical Γ3 estimate; for levels above k use the summable Γ1 estimate. Optimizing k so that 2^{-kε1}Γ1T^{ε1} is comparable to Γ3 yields the logarithmic factor. For Theorem 4.7, decompose dyadic refinements using conditional expectations at midpoints. The predictable part is bounded by Γ1|t-s|^{α1}Σ λ(t_i,t_{i+1})^{β1}; Hölder and superadditivity of λ give a summable factor 2^{-k(α1+β1-1)}λ(s,t)^{β1}. The martingale differences are estimated by BDG and summed into B_{s,t}. Passing to the Riemann-sum limit gives the asserted pathwise estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas A.2-A.3 and measure/Besov embeddings used for approximation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma A.2 gives Besov translation estimates: for a tempered distribution f, ∥f(a+·)∥_{B^γ_p}=∥f∥_{B^γ_p}; ∥f(a1+·)-f(a2+·)∥_{B^γ_p} ≤ C|a1-a2|^α∥f∥_{B^{γ+α}_p}; and the analogous second-order translation difference is bounded by C|a1-a2|^{α1}|a1-a3|^{α2}∥f∥_{B^{γ+α1+α2}_p}. Lemma A.3 says that heat mollification G_t satisfies, for f∈B^γ_p: if γ<0, ∥G_t f∥_{L^p} ≤ C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}t^{γ/2}; G_t f→f in B^{bar γ}_p for every bar γ<γ; sup_t∥G_t f∥_{B^γ_p}≤∥f∥_{B^γ_p}; and if γ-1/p<0, ∥G_t f∥_{C^1}≤C∥f∥_{B^γ_p}t^{(γ-1/p-1)/2}. The paper also uses the known embedding that finite signed measures belong to B^0_1, and Besov embeddings B^β_{q1} into B^{β-1/q1+1/q2}_{q2} for q1≤q2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The translation estimates are proved dyadic block by dyadic block. Mean-value estimates and Bernstein inequalities give ∥Δ_j(f(·+a1)-f(·+a2))∥_p ≤ C|a1-a2|2^j∥Δ_jf∥_p, while the trivial triangle inequality gives a zeroth-order bound; interpolation yields the fractional power. The second-order estimate is analogous, using two mean-value increments. The heat-mollification estimates use the Fourier support of dyadic blocks: for high frequencies, ∥G_tΔ_jf∥_p≤Ce^{-ct2^{2j}}∥Δ_jf∥_p, and summing over j gives the t^{γ/2} smoothing. Convergence G_t f→f in lower Besov regularity follows from ∥G_tΔ_jf-Δ_jf∥_p≤C(t2^{2j})^ε∥Δ_jf∥_p. The C^1 estimate combines heat smoothing, the L^∞ Besov embedding, and standard heat-kernel derivative bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma B.2: reconstructing white noise from a limiting stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde V be a measurable random field on a filtered probability space with the same law as the stochastic convolution V. Then on the same space there exists a white noise tilde W such that tilde V_t(x)=∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) tilde W(dr,dy). Moreover the natural filtrations agree: F^{tilde W}_t = F^{tilde V}_t. If an additional filtration tilde F_t contains F^{tilde V}_t and, for every s≤t and test function φ, ∫_D(tilde V_t(x)-P_{t-s}tilde V_s(x))φ(x)dx is independent of tilde F_s, then tilde W is a tilde F_t-white noise.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define tilde W_t(φ)=<tilde V_t,φ> - 1/2∫_0^t<tilde V_s,φ''>ds for test functions φ. Since tilde V has the law of V, these linear functionals have the finite-dimensional laws of white-noise integrals and extend by density to L^2(D). The distributional stochastic heat equation representation is equivalent to the mild stochastic convolution representation, giving tilde V as the convolution of tilde W. The equality of filtrations follows because tilde V is obtained by integrating tilde W forward, while tilde W_t(φ) is explicitly reconstructed from tilde V up to time t. For the enlarged filtration, approximate tilde W_t(φ)-tilde W_s(φ) by sums of independent heat-smoothed increments ∫(tilde V_{s_i}-P_{s_i-s_{i-1}}tilde V_{s_{i-1}})ψ_i and use the assumed independence plus L^2 convergence.

proof_techniques.name: Distributional drift defined by universal smooth approximation
proof_techniques.description: The paper avoids assigning a pointwise meaning to b(u) when b is a distribution. Instead it defines the drift term K as the limit, in probability and locally uniformly in space-time, of the classical mild drift convolutions obtained from every smooth bounded approximating sequence b_n→b in B^{β-}_q. A key part of the theory is showing that, in the well-posedness regimes, this limit is independent of the approximating sequence and coincides with the limit of constructable smooth solutions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem already assumes this solution notion and approximation by G_{1/n}. This framework suggests proving semigroup or invariant-measure facts first for smooth Gaussian drifts G_{1/n} and then passing to the δ_0 limit using Theorem 2.10-type convergence.

proof_techniques.name: Stochastic sewing as regularization by space-time white noise
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly estimates time integrals of singular functions of V+ψ by treating left-endpoint frozen approximations as a two-parameter process A_{s,t}. Stochastic sewing converts conditional Gaussian smoothing and martingale increment bounds into estimates for the full nonlinear integral. This yields powers such as 1+γ/4-1/(4p), reflecting the Hurst-1/4 behavior of the stochastic heat equation at fixed spatial points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Any attempt to prove continuity of transition probabilities, tightness of stationary laws, or stability under approximation for the Dirichlet δ_0 equation can try to reuse these estimates, assuming the Dirichlet analogue of the local nondeterminism input is available.

proof_techniques.name: Smooth approximation plus Gyöngy-Krylov/Yamada-Watanabe upgrade
proof_techniques.description: The existence proof first solves equations with bounded continuous drifts, proves tightness of the centered fields, identifies all weak limits as distributional solutions, and then uses pathwise uniqueness to force all subsequential limits to agree. The Gyöngy-Krylov criterion then gives convergence in probability of the entire approximation sequence, producing a strong solution.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant measures, one can try to analyze invariant or long-time laws for the smooth approximating semigroups and use tightness plus uniqueness of subsequential limits to identify any limiting stationary law of the singular equation.

proof_techniques.name: Comparison by mollification of ordered distributions
proof_techniques.description: The comparison principle is obtained by mollifying ordered Besov distributions b'≼b'', using the classical comparison theorem for smooth reaction terms, and passing to the limit with the approximation theorem. The order b'≼b'' is exactly distributional positivity of b''-b'.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since δ_0 is a non-negative measure, this gives monotone comparisons with equations having smaller or larger ordered drifts, which may support coupling, domination, or tightness arguments for invariant measures.

proof_techniques.name: Positive-measure random control for automatic drift regularity
proof_techniques.description: For non-negative measure drifts, positivity of the approximating drift makes P_{T-t}K_t - P_{T-s}K_s non-negative and superadditive in time. The paper treats this quantity as a random control in a stochastic sewing lemma, obtaining the V(3/4) drift regularity for every solution without assuming it a priori.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original drift δ_0 is positive. This mechanism is likely the most relevant a priori estimate for stationary or invariant-law arguments, because it gives uniform control of the singular drift term in the same regularity class used for well-posedness.

proof_techniques.name: Critical Davie-Grönwall uniqueness with logarithmic loss
proof_techniques.description: At the critical Besov index β-1/q=-1, the sewing estimate produces a borderline term of size ∥z∥ |log∥z∥| (t-s). The paper closes uniqueness by considering hitting times at levels 2^{-k}; the logarithmic term forces time gaps of order 1/k, whose divergent sum contradicts finite time if the difference ever becomes positive.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one attempts a coupling proof showing that two stationary solutions driven by the same noise must coincide, this hitting-level/logarithmic Grönwall argument is a ready-made pathwise uniqueness mechanism.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-limit preservation of the driving white noise
proof_techniques.description: When applying Skorokhod representation to stochastic convolutions, the paper reconstructs the white noise from the limiting convolution using the distributional heat equation. Independence of future increments is checked through heat-smoothed increments of the limiting V.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If stationary approximations are taken to a weak limit, this method can identify the limit as a genuine weak solution driven by white noise rather than just as an abstract random field.

proof_techniques.name: Besov heat mollification calculus
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically uses heat mollification G_t b to approximate distributions in lower Besov regularity while keeping the B^β_q norm bounded, and uses Besov translation inequalities to quantify how f(V+a)-f(V+b) behaves when f is distributional. These estimates are the analytic backbone of the stochastic sewing bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original approximants G_{1/n}δ_0 are exactly of this form. The uniform Besov norm control and convergence in B^{β-}_q are the inputs needed to justify passing from smooth SPDEs to the δ_0 SPDE.

proof_techniques.name: Awareness of boundary dependence through local nondeterminism
proof_techniques.description: The authors deliberately avoid Dirichlet boundary conditions because their proof uses a lower bound on the conditional variance of V_t(x) uniformly in x, and the corresponding Dirichlet stochastic convolution has variance degenerating near the boundary. They state that the Dirichlet case should be adaptable but would introduce additional technical difficulties.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes the needed Dirichlet extension. Any proof porting paper estimates should explicitly check which uses require uniform-in-x bounds and whether boundary localization or weighted estimates are needed.

other_useful_info: The paper itself proves no invariant-measure, ergodicity, strong Feller, or irreducibility theorem. It cites Bounebache-Zambotti [BZ14] for a Dirichlet-form construction of weak solutions to the skew stochastic heat equation, Gyöngy-Pardoux [GP93a, GP93b] for comparison and regularization of SPDEs with rough but functional drifts, and Butkovsky-Mytnik [BM19] for path-by-path uniqueness of stochastic heat equations with bounded drift. Theorem 2.12, not central to invariant measures, shows that certain rescaled smooth reaction-diffusion SPDEs converge to SPDEs with homogeneous distributional drifts; in the absolutely integrable case this gives a scaling limit to the skew stochastic heat equation with δ_0 coefficient equal to the integral of the reaction term. The paper notes that finite signed measures lie in B^0_1 and hence are included in the Besov framework via embeddings; this is the basic reason δ_0 is in the critical well-posedness class.
why_relevant: This is the core paper defining and proving well-posedness/approximation for stochastic heat equations with distributional drift, including b = δ0. Its approximation framework is directly needed for transferring irreducibility, Feller, or invariant-measure arguments from smooth drifts to the singular limit.

title: A Support Theorem for Parabolic Stochastic PDEs with Nondegenerate Hölder Diffusion Coefficients
overall_summary: The paper proves small-ball probability estimates and a full-support theorem for one-dimensional parabolic stochastic heat equations on [0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, uniformly bounded drift g, and uniformly elliptic diffusion coefficient σ that is only Hölder continuous in the solution variable. The main lower small-ball bound holds for every probabilistic weak solution when the Hölder exponent α is merely positive; a two-sided estimate is proved under α>3/4, matching available strong well-posedness theory. The proof reduces bounded drift and nonzero target paths by Girsanov and path subtraction, compares the nonlinear equation to a Gaussian equation with frozen diffusion coefficient, controls the comparison error using sharp local stochastic-integral estimates reflecting 1:2:4 stochastic heat scaling, and iterates one-slab estimates by the Markov property. For the original invariant-measure problem, the most relevant content is the full-support and small-ball machinery for nondegenerate additive or uniformly elliptic noise, especially as a possible route to topological irreducibility for regularized equations or singular-drift limits if analogous Dirichlet-boundary and singular-drift estimates are available.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the real-valued SPDE on [0,∞) × [0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, ∂_t u(t,x) = (1/2)∂_x^2 u(t,x) + g(t,x,u(t,x)) + σ(t,x,u(t,x)) dot W(t,x), u(0,x)=u0(x). Let PC_b^2 denote predictable fields whose value, time derivative, and second space derivative are almost surely bounded by a fixed constant. Assume u0 and h are in PC_b^2 and sup_{x∈[0,1]} |u0(x)-h(0,x)| < ε/2. Assume that for some D,c_min,c_max>0 and α∈(3/4,1], for all t≥0, x∈[0,1], and r,s∈R one has |σ(t,x,r)-σ(t,x,s)| ≤ D|r-s|^α and c_min ≤ |σ(t,x,r)| ≤ c_max, and also sup_{t,x,r} |g(t,x,r)| < ∞. Then for every β>2−α there are positive constants A0,A1,A2,A3 and ε0, with the dependence specified in the theorem in terms of β,c_min,c_max and sup|g|, such that for every 0<ε<ε0, A0 exp(−A1 T / ε^(2+4β)) ≤ P(sup_{0≤t≤T, x∈[0,1]} |u(t,x)-h(t,x)| ≤ ε) ≤ A2 exp(−A3 T / ((1+D^2) ε^(4+2α))). If instead only α>0 is assumed, then the lower bound still holds. Moreover, there exists D0>0 depending only on c_min and c_max such that, when D<D0, the estimates can be taken with the endpoint value β=2−α.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first reduces to the case g=0 and h=0. For g, write the equation as the driftless equation driven by dot W + σ^{-1}g and use Girsanov; boundedness of g and the lower ellipticity c_min give an exponential martingale with finite second moment, and Cauchy-Schwarz compares event probabilities under the drifted and driftless laws. For h, set H=∂_t−(1/2)∂_x^2 and define w(t,x)=u(t,x)−u0(x)−h(t,x)+h(0,x); then w has zero initial condition and solves the same type of equation with modified diffusion σ1(t,x,w)=σ(t,x,u) and modified drift g1(t,x,w)=g(t,x,u)−H u0(x)−Hh(t,x)+Hh(0,x), which remains bounded because u0,h∈PC_b^2. After these reductions, the proof uses two quoted Gaussian/small-scale estimates: Proposition 2.1 for stochastic integrals over parabolic boxes and Proposition 2.3 for the Gaussian equation whose σ does not depend on u. For the upper bound, the proof needs α>3/4 to solve an auxiliary SPDE strongly. It defines the truncation f_ε(z)=z for |z|<ε and f_ε(z)=ε z/|z| for |z|>ε, solves v with coefficient σ(t,x,f_ε(v)), and observes that v=u until u exits the ε-tube. It then compares v with a Gaussian process v_g whose diffusion coefficient is frozen at σ(t,x,f_ε(u0(x))). The difference D=v−v_g is a stochastic convolution with integrand bounded by D(2ε)^α by Hölder continuity of σ. On one time slab and at the spatial grid points p_{1j}, the event {|v(p_{1j})|≤ε} is contained in {|v_g(p_{1j})|≤2ε} ∪ {|D(p_{1j})|≥ε}. Hence the one-slab small-ball probability is bounded by a Gaussian grid small-ball probability, controlled by Proposition 2.3, plus a union bound over error probabilities, controlled by Proposition 2.1 and the bound D(2ε)^α on the stochastic-integral coefficient. This gives a one-slab upper estimate of order exp(−c/((1+D^2)ε^(2α))). Iterating over about T/ε^4 slabs using the Markov property yields the exponent ε^(−4−2α). For the lower bound, choose a finer time mesh with step c0 ε^(4β), where β>1 and α+β>2. Starting from sup_x |u0(x)|≤ε/3, freeze the coefficient at u0 and let u_g be the corresponding Gaussian process. Let τ be the first time sup_x |u(t,x)−u0(x)|>2ε and define a stopped error convolution tilde D using u(s∧τ). If u_g stays within 2ε/3 on the slab and ends within ε/6, and if sup|tilde D|≤ε/6, then u stays in the required ε-tube and ends within ε/3. Proposition 2.3 gives the Gaussian event probability at least C exp(−C/ε^2), because the refined time slab is shorter than the original parabolic slab. The error term is bounded by covering space with O(ε^−2) intervals and applying Proposition 2.1 with time-scale parameter ε^(4β−4) and coefficient size D(2ε)^α; this gives a probability bounded by an exponentially smaller term of order exp(−c/ε^(2(α+β−1))) up to polynomial factors. Since α+β>2, this is negligible relative to exp(−C/ε^2) for small ε, yielding a one-slab lower bound C exp(−C/ε^2). Markov iteration over about T/ε^(4β) refined slabs gives the lower exponent ε^(−2−4β). The final small-D assertion comes from keeping the D-dependence in the error estimate and allowing the borderline α+β=2 when D is sufficiently small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume α∈(0,1] and the hypotheses of the SPDE setting in Theorem 1.1 that are needed for the lower support estimate. Let μ be the probability law on C([0,T] × [0,1]) of any possible weak solution to the SPDE. Define Ω_{u0}([0,T] × [0,1]) = {w∈C([0,T] × [0,1]) : w(0,x)=u0(x) for all x∈[0,1]}. Then the topological support of μ, with C([0,T] × [0,1]) endowed with the supremum norm topology, is exactly Ω_{u0}([0,T] × [0,1]).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion Supp μ ⊆ Ω_{u0} is immediate from continuity of sample paths and the deterministic initial condition. For the reverse inclusion, Theorem 1.1 gives positive probability for every sufficiently small supremum-norm tube around every admissible smooth bounded target path h with the correct initial value up to the ε/2 tolerance. Smooth PC_b^2 paths are dense in the continuous path space with fixed initial condition in the supremum norm, so for any w∈Ω_{u0} and any open supremum ball around w one chooses such an h inside a smaller ball and applies the lower bound. Thus every nonempty open neighborhood in Ω_{u0} has positive μ-mass.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 drift and target-path reduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the SPDE of Theorem 1.1 with bounded drift g and uniformly elliptic σ, one may compare the law with drift g to the driftless law by Girsanov. If P_t is the original law up to time t and Q_t is defined by the exponential martingale with stochastic integral ∫_0^t∫_0^1 σ^{-1}g(s,x,u(s,x)) W(ds dx) and compensator (1/2)∫_0^t∫_0^1 (σ^{-1}g(s,x,u(s,x)))^2 dx ds, then dot W + σ^{-1}g is a space-time white noise under Q_t. For the event A={sup_{s∈[0,T],y∈[0,1]} |u(s,y)−h(s,y)|<ε}, Cauchy-Schwarz gives Q_T(A) ≤ P_T(A)^(1/2) (E_P[(dQ_T/dP_T)^2])^(1/2) ≤ M P_T(A)^(1/2), where M depends only on T, the ellipticity lower bound, and sup|g|. The same comparison with P and Q interchanged, replacing g by −g, is used for upper bounds. A separate deterministic shift w(t,x)=u(t,x)−u0(x)−h(t,x)+h(0,x) converts estimates around h and u0 into estimates around zero initial data and zero target, at the cost of replacing g by a bounded modified drift involving H=∂_t−(1/2)∂_x^2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Uniform ellipticity gives |σ^{-1}g|≤c_min^−1 sup|g|, so Novikov-type integrability and a finite second moment of the Girsanov density are immediate on finite time intervals. Under the changed measure the shifted noise is white noise, so the equation with drift is represented as a driftless equation under the new law. The displayed Cauchy-Schwarz inequality transfers lower bounds from Q to P and upper bounds from P to Q, up to changing constants. For the path shift, direct substitution into the SPDE gives ∂_t w=(1/2)∂_x^2 w+g1(t,x,w)+σ1(t,x,w)dot W with g1=g−Hu0−Hh+Hh(0), and boundedness of u0,h and their indicated derivatives ensures g1 is bounded, so the preceding drift reduction applies again.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1, quoted from Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G(t,x)=Σ_{n∈Z}(2πt)^−1/2 exp(−(x+n)^2/(2t)) be the heat kernel used in the paper. For a predictable integrand σ(s,y,u(s,y)) satisfying sup_{s,y}|σ(s,y,u(s,y))|≤C, define the stochastic convolution N(t,x)=∫_0^t∫_0^1 G(t−s,x−y) σ(s,y,u(s,y)) W(dy ds). There exist universal constants K1,K2 such that for all a,λ,ε>0, P(sup_{0≤t≤a ε^4, x∈[0,ε^2]} |N(t,x)| > λ ε) ≤ K1/(1∧sqrt(a)) exp(−K2 λ^2/(C^2 sqrt(a))).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not prove this estimate; it quotes it from [1, Proposition 3.4 and Remark 3.1]. It is used as a precise small-scale large-deviation estimate for stochastic heat convolutions, encoding the 1:2:4 scaling that on a time interval of length a ε^4 and a spatial interval of length ε^2 the stochastic convolution has typical size ε. In the present paper the estimate is applied to comparison-error convolutions whose integrand is bounded by a Hölder coefficient difference, for example C=D(2ε)^α, and then combined with spatial union bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The estimate in Proposition 2.1 remains valid, with the same form of constants, if the spatial supremum over x∈[0,ε^2] is replaced by x∈[(k−1)ε^2,kε^2] for any positive integer k satisfying kε^2≤1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper explains that the proof in [1] only uses a modulus-of-continuity estimate for the stochastic convolution that is independent of the spatial location of the interval, together with a path of grid points from the initial corner to the point (t,x). For a shifted interval one starts the grid path at (0,(k−1)ε^2) instead of (0,0), so the same bound applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3, quoted from Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1]
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Use the parabolic mesh t_i=i c0 ε^4 and x_j=j c1 ε^2, with c0 small and c1^2=θ c0 as fixed in Section 2.2, and write I_n=[t_n,t_{n+1}], p_{ij}=(t_i,x_j), n1=min{n≥1:t_n>T}, and n2=min{n≥1:x_n>1}. Define A_n={|u(t_{n+1},x)|≤ε/3 for all x∈[0,1], and |u(t,x)|≤ε for all t∈I_n and x∈[0,1]} and F_n={|u(p_{nj})|<ε for all j≤n2−2}. Under the assumptions of Theorem 1.1, with additionally g=0, u0≡0, and σ(t,x,u) independent of u, there are constants ε0,C4,C5>0 depending only on the ellipticity bounds such that for 0<ε<ε0, P(F_n | ∩_{k=0}^{n−1} F_k) ≤ C4 exp(−C5 ε^−2). Also there are constants C6,C7>0 depending only on the ellipticity bounds such that for 0<ε<ε0, P(A_n | ∩_{k=0}^{n−1} A_k) ≥ C6 exp(−C7 ε^−2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted from [1, Proposition 2.1] and is not reproved in the paper. It supplies the Gaussian one-slab input for both sides of Theorem 1.1. The upper estimate says that, conditionally on previous grid-time smallness, the probability of being small at all grid points on the next time layer is at most exponentially small with exponent ε^−2. The lower estimate says that, conditionally on previous slab smallness, the Gaussian equation has at least exponentially small probability exp(−Cε^−2) to remain in an ε-tube on the next slab and end in an ε/3-tube. Han applies these estimates to frozen-coefficient Gaussian comparison processes such as u_g and v_g.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Derived one-slab nonlinear comparison estimates in Sections 2.3 and 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the reduced setting g=0 and h=0, the proof derives the following one-slab estimates. For the upper bound, with the parabolic time mesh I_{n−1}=[t_{n−1},t_n] of length c0 ε^4 and B_n={|u(t,x)|≤ε for all t∈I_{n−1}, x∈[0,1]}, the paper obtains for small ε an estimate of the form P(B_n | ∩_{j=1}^{n−1} B_j) ≤ exp(−c/((1+D^2) ε^(2α))), after constants are adjusted. For the lower bound, with the refined time mesh hat t_n=n c0 ε^(4β), hat I_n=[hat t_n,hat t_{n+1}], and hat A_n={|u(hat t_{n+1},x)|≤ε/3 for all x∈[0,1], and |u(t,x)|≤ε for all t∈hat I_n, x∈[0,1]}, if β>1 and α+β>2 then for small ε one has P(hat A_n | ∩_{j=0}^{n−1} hat A_j) ≥ C exp(−C′/ε^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the upper estimate, the truncated process v with coefficient σ(t,x,f_ε(v)) agrees with u as long as u remains in the ε-tube. Freezing the coefficient at the initial profile gives a Gaussian process v_g. At each spatial grid point, smallness of v implies either smallness of v_g at twice the radius or largeness of the difference D=v−v_g. Proposition 2.3 controls the first alternative over all grid points, while Proposition 2.1 and the Hölder bound |σ(f_ε(v))−σ(f_ε(u0))|≤D(2ε)^α control the second after a union bound. For the lower estimate, the process is compared with a Gaussian process u_g with coefficient frozen at u0, and the error is stopped at the first time u moves more than 2ε from u0. If the Gaussian process stays small and the stopped error is at most ε/6, then u satisfies hat A_0. Proposition 2.3 gives the Gaussian probability at least exp(−C/ε^2). Proposition 2.1 on the refined time scale, together with spatial covering and the bound D(2ε)^α on the stopped coefficient difference, makes the error probability exponentially smaller when α+β>2. The Markov property then makes these estimates conditional for every slab.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov removal of bounded drift under uniform ellipticity
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the fact that when |σ| is bounded below and g is uniformly bounded, the drift term can be absorbed into a shift of the white noise: g+σ dot W = σ(dot W+σ^−1 g). The exponential martingale for σ^−1 g has controlled moments, so probabilities of path events under the drifted and driftless equations are comparable via Cauchy-Schwarz, with constants depending on finite time, the ellipticity lower bound, and sup|g|.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original additive-noise equation this is directly relevant to smooth bounded drift approximations of the singular drift, although the bounds for G_{1/n} are not uniform in n. A singular δ0 drift would require an additional argument replacing bounded Girsanov control by an integrability or limiting/local-time estimate.

proof_techniques.name: Subtract a smooth target path to reduce support around h to support around zero
proof_techniques.description: Given a smooth enough predictable target h and initial profile u0, the paper defines w=u−u0−h+h(0). The transformed process has zero initial condition and target zero, while the price is a modified drift involving Hh and Hu0, where H=∂_t−(1/2)∂_x^2. Because h and u0 are PC_b^2, the modified drift remains bounded and is then removed by the Girsanov step.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an analogous support theorem is sought for the Dirichlet problem, this suggests choosing smooth bridges h between an initial profile and a target endpoint, respecting the boundary, and reducing the question to a small-ball estimate around zero.

proof_techniques.name: Freeze the diffusion coefficient and compare to a Gaussian stochastic heat equation
proof_techniques.description: The nonlinear equation is compared on one time slab with a Gaussian equation whose diffusion coefficient is frozen at the initial profile and therefore no longer depends on the evolving solution. The difference is a stochastic convolution whose integrand is the coefficient difference. Hölder continuity of σ gives a deterministic bound on this integrand as long as the solution is localized in an ε-tube, enabling Gaussian small-ball estimates for the frozen process plus stochastic-convolution tail estimates for the error.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original additive-noise problem σ is constant, so this comparison error would vanish; the remaining difficulty lies in the singular drift rather than multiplicative noise. This makes the paper's Gaussian small-ball and path-support ideas especially plausible for additive-noise regularizations.

proof_techniques.name: Use sharp local 1:2:4 stochastic heat scaling estimates
proof_techniques.description: The key stochastic-integral estimate controls the supremum of a stochastic heat convolution on boxes with time length a ε^4 and spatial length ε^2, showing Gaussian tails at amplitude ε. The paper combines this with spatial covering to control the supremum over the whole interval [0,1]. This is the probabilistic substitute for a deterministic Stroock-Varadhan control problem, which may be ill-posed when coefficients are merely Hölder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Dirichlet boundary conditions, an analogous local estimate for the Dirichlet heat kernel would provide the basic small-ball ingredient needed to prove transition probabilities charge neighborhoods of arbitrary continuous paths.

proof_techniques.name: Refine the time mesh to handle Hölder coefficient errors
proof_techniques.description: When σ is only α-Hölder, the coefficient-freezing error is of size ε^α rather than ε. To make this error negligible in the lower bound, the paper uses a shorter time step c0 ε^(4β) instead of the natural c0 ε^4. In Proposition 2.1 this introduces the parameter ε^(4β−4), producing an error exponent ε^(−2(α+β−1)); choosing α+β>2 makes this exponent stronger than the Gaussian small-ball exponent ε^−2.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original equation has additive noise and does not need this Hölder-diffusion refinement, the general idea of changing the time scale to dominate approximation errors may be useful when passing from regularized drifts G_{1/n} to the δ0 drift.

proof_techniques.name: Markov iteration of one-slab small-ball estimates
proof_techniques.description: After proving uniform one-slab estimates conditional on the past, the paper multiplies them over many slabs using the Markov property. A one-slab lower bound of order exp(−Cε^−2) over about T/ε^(4β) refined slabs yields exp(−CT/ε^(2+4β)); a one-slab upper bound with exponent ε^(−2α) over about T/ε^4 slabs yields exp(−CT/ε^(4+2α)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant-measure questions, this type of estimate can be used to establish finite-time irreducibility: choose a path from the starting state to a desired neighborhood and show the transition kernel assigns it positive probability.

proof_techniques.name: Path-space full support implies endpoint support
proof_techniques.description: The corollary turns positive probability of small tubes around smooth paths into full support of the entire law on C([0,T]×[0,1]). Once one has path-space full support, endpoint support follows by selecting continuous paths with prescribed terminal profile and considering the endpoint map.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the same path-space support statement can be proved for the SPDE with δ0 drift and Dirichlet boundary, then the Markov semigroup would be topologically irreducible on C_0([0,1]), a standard ingredient in uniqueness of invariant measures when combined with a suitable strong Feller, asymptotic strong Feller, or coupling property.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that for additive noise σ=Id and drift g that is not too singular, one can often remove the drift by Girsanov and obtain full support from the full support of white noise; it cites Ling-Riedel-Scheutzow [12] for a related finite-dimensional SDE example with singular drift. Remark 1.3 says the α>3/4 assumption is tied to available strong well-posedness results for Hölder σ; the lower bound itself only needs α>0, while the upper-bound proof requires solving an auxiliary SPDE with the same Hölder regularity on the same probability space. Remark 1.4 says the arguments extend from [0,1] to finite intervals [0,J], and at least the lower bound extends to vector-valued solutions; the paper itself uses Neumann boundary conditions and does not prove a Dirichlet-boundary version. The main external input for Gaussian small-ball estimates is Athreya-Joseph-Mueller [1], Small ball probabilities and a support theorem for the stochastic heat equation, Annals of Probability 2021, which the paper uses as a black box through Proposition 2.1 and Proposition 2.3.
why_relevant: This paper extends support theorems to rougher coefficients and bounded drifts. Its full-support estimates are relevant because the target equation is obtained as a singular drift limit and one may need robust support/irreducibility arguments.

title: Invariant measures for nonlinear SPDE's: uniqueness and stability
overall_summary: Maslowski and Seidler survey criteria for uniqueness, ergodicity, and asymptotic stability of invariant measures for stochastic evolution equations in infinite-dimensional spaces, especially SPDEs. The paper distinguishes strong asymptotic stability, meaning convergence of transition laws in total variation, from weak asymptotic stability, meaning narrow convergence of measures; either form of global stability implies uniqueness of an invariant probability measure. The most directly useful route for the original problem is the abstract Doob-type criterion: on a Polish state space, if positive-time transition probabilities are mutually equivalent, then any invariant probability measure is unique and the transition laws converge to it in total variation; strong Feller plus irreducibility is a standard way to obtain this equivalence. The paper also records several mechanisms for proving strong Feller/irreducibility for semilinear SPDEs with additive noise, including Girsanov comparison with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, support/approximate controllability arguments, Kolmogorov-equation smoothing, and Bismut-Elworthy derivative formulae. As alternatives when total-variation methods are unavailable, it reviews Lyapunov and dissipativity/remote-start methods yielding weak convergence and uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1 (linear Gaussian strong asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a real separable Hilbert space, let A: Dom(A) ⊆ H → H generate a strongly continuous semigroup (e^{At})_{t≥0}, and let W_t be a Wiener process on H with incremental covariance operator Q ∈ L(H), possibly cylindrical. Assume that for some T>0, ∫_0^T ||e^{At} Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2 dt < ∞, so the linear stochastic equation dZ_t = A Z_t dt + dW_t has the mild solution Z_t = e^{At}x + ∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)} dW_r and transition law P_t(x,·)=N(e^{At}x,Q_t), where Q_t=∫_0^t e^{Ar}Qe^{A*r}dr. In the proposition the paper assumes, for simplicity, Q>0. Let K be a linear subspace of H such that for every x∈K there is t_0(x)≥0 with e^{At}x ∈ Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for all t>t_0(x), and ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}x|| → 0 as t→∞. Then for all x,y∈K, the total variation distance |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| tends to 0 as t→∞. If, in addition, sup_{t≥0} Tr Q_t < ∞, so that the linear equation has invariant Gaussian measure μ_* = N(0,Q_∞) with Q_∞=lim_{t→∞}Q_t, and if μ_*(K)=1, then |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 for every x∈K. Consequently μ_* is the only invariant probability measure concentrated on K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this proposition is proved by direct computation using the Cameron-Martin formula for Gaussian measures. Since P_t(x,·) and P_t(y,·) are Gaussian measures with the same covariance Q_t and means e^{At}x and e^{At}y, their mutual absolute continuity is governed by whether e^{At}(x-y) lies in the Cameron-Martin space Im(Q_t^{1/2}). Under the range hypothesis, the Radon-Nikodym derivative is the usual Cameron-Martin exponential with shift norm ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}(x-y)||. The second hypothesis forces this shift norm to vanish as t→∞, which implies convergence of the translated Gaussian measures in total variation. For convergence to μ_*, use invariance μ_*P_t=μ_* and μ_*(K)=1: formally, |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| = |||P_t(x,·)-∫_K P_t(y,·) μ_*(dy)||| ≤ ∫_K |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| μ_*(dy), and dominated convergence gives the limit 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2 (linear range criterion for uniqueness and total-variation convergence)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same linear Hilbert-space setting as Proposition 1, assume ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2 dt < ∞ for some T>0 and sup_{t≥0} Tr Q_t < ∞, so that μ_*=N(0,Q_∞) is an invariant Gaussian measure. Suppose that for every t>0, Im(e^{At}) ⊆ Im(Q_t^{1/2}). Then |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 as t→∞ for every x∈H. In particular, μ_* is the only invariant probability measure for the linear equation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper presents this as a corollary of the abstract equivalence theorem later stated as Theorem 4. The inclusion Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) is exactly the Cameron-Martin/Hájek-Feldman criterion ensuring that the Gaussian transition measures P_t(x,·)=N(e^{At}x,Q_t) are mutually equivalent for positive times. Once positive-time transition probabilities are equivalent and an invariant probability μ_* exists, Theorem 4 yields total-variation convergence of P_t(x,·) to μ_* and uniqueness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3 (spectral check for parabolic linear equations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let A be self-adjoint, negative, and have compact resolvent on H. Let {e_j}_{j≥1} be an orthonormal basis such that Ae_j=-α_j e_j, with 0<α_j→∞. Suppose Qe_j=λ_j e_j, where 0<λ_j≤λ_0<∞. Then the mild solution and invariant Gaussian measure for dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t exist if ∑_{j=1}^∞ λ_j/α_j < ∞. The range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) from Proposition 2 is equivalent, in this diagonal setting, to boundedness for every t>0 of the sequence { (α_i/λ_i) exp(-2α_i t) }_{i∈N}. In particular, for the heat equation ∂_t u = ∂_ξ^2 u + η on (0,1) with Dirichlet boundary conditions, H=L^2(0,1), A=∂_ξ^2, and space-time white noise corresponding to Q=I, the criterion is satisfied.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the diagonal basis, Q_t is diagonal with eigenvalues q_{j,t}=λ_j(1-exp(-2α_jt))/(2α_j). Thus Tr Q_∞ = ∑_j λ_j/(2α_j), giving the summability criterion for existence of the invariant Gaussian law. The inclusion Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) reduces to a coordinatewise boundedness condition: applying e^{At} multiplies the j-th coordinate by exp(-α_jt), while Q_t^{1/2} multiplies by q_{j,t}^{1/2}. Hence the relevant ratio is exp(-2α_jt)/q_{j,t}, equivalent for fixed t>0 to (α_j/λ_j)exp(-2α_jt), up to harmless constants and the factor 1-exp(-2α_jt). For Q=I and the one-dimensional Dirichlet Laplacian, α_j grows quadratically, so ∑1/α_j<∞ and α_j exp(-2α_jt) is bounded for each t>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4 (abstract equivalence criterion: ergodic theorem and strong asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ((X_t)_{t≥0},(P^x)_{x∈H}) be a Markov process in a Polish space H with transition probability function P_t(x,·), t≥0, x∈H. Assume it has an invariant probability measure μ_*. Assume further that all positive-time transition probabilities P_t(x,·), with t>0 and x∈H, are mutually equivalent. Then: (i) for each bounded Borel function φ:H→R and every x∈H, lim_{T→∞} (1/T)∫_0^T φ(X_t)dt = ∫_H φ dμ_* holds P^x-a.s.; (ii) for every x∈H, |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*||| → 0 as t→∞. In particular, μ_* is the unique invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The review cites this result as obtained independently by Stettner and Seidler and uses it as an abstract black box. The mechanism is the classical Doob/Khasminskii principle: mutual equivalence of positive-time kernels implies that null sets for one transition law are null for all, and, after averaging against the invariant measure, the transition probabilities are equivalent to μ_* at positive times. This eliminates nontrivial invariant classes and gives ergodicity of μ_*. The strong form of Doob's theorem for Markov processes on Polish spaces then yields total-variation convergence of P_t(x,·) to μ_* for every initial state, and the pointwise ergodic theorem follows for bounded Borel observables. Uniqueness is immediate: if ν were another invariant probability measure, then νP_t=ν, while P_t(x,·)→μ_* in total variation for every x, forcing ν=∫P_t(x,·)ν(dx)→μ_*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5 (Girsanov criterion for semilinear additive equations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear Hilbert-space stochastic evolution equation dX_t = AX_t dt + f(X_t)dt + dW_t, where A and W are as in the linear equation and f:H→Im(Q^{1/2}) satisfies the Lipschitz-type condition ||Q^{-1/2}(f(x)-f(y))|| ≤ K||x-y|| for all x,y∈H and some K<∞. Let P_t be the transition function of this nonlinear equation and let ~P_t be the transition function of the linear equation dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t. Assume ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2dt<∞ for some T>0 and assume the linear range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for every t>0. Then for every t>0 and x∈H, P_t(x,·) and ~P_t(x,·) are equivalent measures. If, moreover, the nonlinear equation has an invariant probability measure μ_*, then μ_* is strongly asymptotically stable: |||P_t(x,·)-μ_*|||→0 for every x∈H; in particular it is unique.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The range condition gives mutual equivalence of the linear Gaussian transition measures by the Cameron-Martin/Hájek-Feldman theorem. The condition f(H)⊆Im(Q^{1/2}) with the displayed Lipschitz bound is exactly what is needed to apply the infinite-dimensional Girsanov theorem. The Girsanov density over a time interval [0,T] has the form exp{ ∫_0^T ⟨Q^{-1/2}f(Z_t),dW_t⟩ - (1/2)∫_0^T ||Q^{-1/2}f(Z_t)||^2dt }, where Z is the linear solution. Thus the nonlinear path law is absolutely continuous with respect to the linear path law on finite time intervals, and the same holds for time-t marginals; applying the argument in the reverse direction gives equivalence. Therefore all nonlinear transition probabilities are mutually equivalent, and Theorem 4 gives strong asymptotic stability and uniqueness whenever an invariant probability exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6 (strong Feller plus irreducibility implies equivalence of transition probabilities)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Polish space and let P_t(x,·) be the transition function of a Markov process on H. The process is called strong Feller in the paper if, for each t>0 and each Borel set Γ⊆H, the function x↦P_t(x,Γ) is continuous. It is called irreducible if P_t(x,U)>0 for each t>0, each x∈H, and each nonempty open set U⊆H. If the process is strong Feller and irreducible, then the measures P_t(x,·), for all t>0 and x∈H, are mutually equivalent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the standard support-continuity argument. Suppose P_t(x,A)=0 for a Borel set A. To compare with P_s(y,A), choose r with 0<r<min{s,t}. By Chapman-Kolmogorov, 0=P_t(x,A)=∫_H P_r(z,A)P_{t-r}(x,dz). The function g(z)=P_r(z,A) is nonnegative and continuous by the strong Feller property. If g(z_0)>0 for some z_0, then by continuity g is positive on a nonempty open neighborhood U of z_0; irreducibility gives P_{t-r}(x,U)>0, contradicting the integral being zero. Hence P_r(z,A)=0 for all z. Applying Chapman-Kolmogorov again, P_s(y,A)=∫_H P_r(z,A)P_{s-r}(y,dz)=0. Thus any null set for one positive-time transition probability is null for all, proving mutual equivalence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7 (support/approximate-controllability criterion for irreducibility)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear additive equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t in a Hilbert space H, where A and W are as in the linear equation and f:H→H is Lipschitz continuous. For a fixed T>0 define the linear control operator K:L^2(0,T;H)→C_0([0,T];H) by (Kψ)(t)=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}Q^{1/2}ψ(r)dr, where C_0([0,T];H)={g∈C([0,T];H):g(0)=0}. If Im(K) is dense in C_0([0,T];H), then the transition probability at time T is irreducible in the sense that P_T(x,U)>0 for every x∈H and every nonempty open U⊆H. If this density condition holds for every T>0, then the transition probability function is irreducible.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the solution as X_t=u(t,x;~Z), where ~Z_t=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}dW_r is the linear stochastic convolution with zero initial condition, and u(·,x;φ) solves the deterministic integral equation u(t,x;φ)=e^{At}x+∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}f(u(r,x;φ))dr+φ(t). Since f is Lipschitz, φ↦u(·,x;φ) is continuous from C_0([0,T];H) to C([0,T];H). The law of the Gaussian process ~Z on C_0([0,T];H) has closed support equal to the closure of its reproducing kernel Hilbert space, which is the closure of Im(K); by assumption, the support is all of C_0([0,T];H). Given any target open set U at time T, choose a continuous path η with η(0)=x and η(T)∈U, and set φ(t)=η(t)-e^{At}x-∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}f(η(r))dr; then u(·,x;φ)=η. Continuity gives a neighborhood of φ whose images at time T lie in U, and the full support of ~Z gives positive probability for that neighborhood. Hence P_T(x,U)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8 (strong Feller property via the infinite-dimensional Kolmogorov equation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let the linear equation dZ_t=AZ_tdt+dW_t satisfy ∫_0^T ||e^{At}Q^{1/2}||_{HS}^2dt<∞ for some T>0 and sup_{t≥0}Tr Q_t<∞, and let μ=N(0,Q_∞) be its invariant Gaussian measure. Let T_t be the corresponding Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Markov semigroup on L^2(H,μ), and let P_t be the Markov semigroup of the semilinear additive equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t. Consider the mild backward Kolmogorov equation u(t,·)=T_tφ+∫_0^t T_{t-s}⟨f,Du(s,·)⟩ds. Assume f:H→H is bounded and continuous, assume the linear range condition Im(e^{At})⊆Im(Q_t^{1/2}) for t>0, and assume the stronger smoothing condition ∫_0^T ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}||_{L(H)}dt<∞ for some T>0. Then for every bounded Borel φ on H there exists a unique solution u to the mild Kolmogorov equation and u(t,·)∈C_b^1(H) for t>0. Moreover, u(t,x)=P_tφ(x)=E^xφ(X_t) for t>0 and x∈H whenever φ∈C_b(H). In particular, |||P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·)||| ≤ γ(t)||x-y|| for t>0 and x,y∈H, where γ(t)=sup{||DP_tφ(z)||: z∈H, φ∈C_b(H), |φ|≤1}<∞; hence the transition semigroup is strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Da Prato-Zabczyk and Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys for the proof. The key input is that the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup T_t is smoothing under the range and integrability condition involving Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}; this gives bounds on derivatives of T_tφ even when φ is merely bounded. One then solves the mild Kolmogorov equation by a fixed-point/variation-of-constants argument, using boundedness of f to control the perturbation term T_{t-s}⟨f,Du(s)⟩. Identification of the mild Kolmogorov solution with the Markov semigroup P_t follows first for sufficiently regular or continuous φ and then by approximation. The derivative bound implies |P_tφ(x)-P_tφ(y)|≤γ(t)||x-y|| for bounded test functions φ; taking the supremum over |φ|≤1 gives the displayed total-variation Lipschitz estimate and therefore the strong Feller property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9 (Elworthy/Bismut-type derivative formula)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the semilinear additive Hilbert-space equation dX_t=AX_tdt+f(X_t)dt+dW_t. Assume Q is boundedly invertible, f is Lipschitz continuous and Gateaux differentiable on H, and the Gateaux derivative is continuous as a map from H into L(H) endowed with the strong operator topology. Then for every t>0 and every bounded Borel function φ, P_tφ∈C^1(H), and for x,h∈H, ⟨DP_tφ(x),h⟩ = (1/t) E^x[ φ(X_t) ∫_0^t ⟨Q^{-1/2}X_s^h,dW_s⟩ ], where X_s^h is the L^2-directional derivative of the solution X_s with respect to the initial condition in direction h.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk for this formula. The derivative process X_s^h is obtained by differentiating the stochastic evolution equation with respect to the initial condition, yielding the linear variational equation driven by the derivative of f along the solution.
