A stochastic integration-by-parts or Bismut-Elworthy argument expresses the spatial derivative of the transition semigroup without differentiating φ: the derivative is transferred to a stochastic integral involving Q^{-1/2}X_s^h. Since Q^{-1/2} is bounded and the variational equation can be estimated in L^2, the right-hand side gives bounds on ||DP_tφ(x)|| in terms of ||φ||_∞. Such bounds imply strong Feller regularization by showing that P_t maps bounded Borel functions to continuous, indeed differentiable, functions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 10 (Lyapunov criterion for weak asymptotic stability)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space and consider dX_t={AX_t+f(X_t)}dt+σ(X_t)dW_t, where A:Dom(A)→H generates a C_0-semigroup, W is a Wiener process in another real separable Hilbert space U with nuclear covariance operator Q, and f:H→H and σ:H→L(U,H) are globally Lipschitz continuous. Let P_t be the associated transition semigroup. Suppose there exists V∈C^2(H;R) such that: (i) V(0)=0 and inf_{||y||≥r}V(y)>0 for every r>0; (ii) there are k<∞ and p>0 such that V(y)+||DV(y)||+||D^2V(y)|| ≤ k(1+||y||^p) for all y∈H; (iii) there exists a>0 such that for all x,y∈Dom(A), ⟨DV(x-y),Ax-Ay+f(x)-f(y)⟩ + (1/2)Tr{(σ(x)-σ(y))^*D^2V(x-y)(σ(x)-σ(y))Q} ≤ -aV(x-y). Then P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·) converges narrowly to 0 as t→∞ for every x,y∈H. If an invariant probability measure exists, then it is globally asymptotically stable for narrow convergence and therefore unique.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is a Lyapunov argument applied to the difference of two solutions driven by the same noise. For sufficiently regular approximations, Itô's formula applied to V(X_t^x-X_t^y) gives a drift term exactly of the form in condition (iii), hence d/dt E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]≤-a E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]. Gronwall's lemma yields exponential decay of E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)]. Condition (i) turns small V into small Hilbert norm, so the coupled solutions converge in probability as t→∞. The polynomial bounds in (ii) justify the Itô formula/approximation and integrability steps for mild solutions. For any bounded continuous φ, uniform continuity on large balls plus tightness estimates and convergence in probability give Eφ(X_t^x)-Eφ(X_t^y)→0, i.e. narrow convergence of P_t(x,·)-P_t(y,·) to zero. If μ is invariant, integrate the convergence against μ(dy) to obtain P_t(x,·)⇒μ, and uniqueness follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Quoted sufficient condition after Theorem 10 (norm-power Lyapunov function)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper quotes Corollary 2.8 of Maslowski [49]: in the setting of Theorem 10, the hypotheses of Theorem 10 are fulfilled with the natural choice V=||·||^p for a suitable p>0, provided the following hold. There exist β∈R and α≥0 such that ⟨Ax,x⟩≤β||x||^2 for all x∈Dom(A), ||Q^{1/2}(σ(x)-σ(y))^*(x-y)|| ≥ α||x-y||^2 for all x,y∈H, and β+Lip(f)+(1/2)Lip(σ)^2 Tr Q < α^2. Here Lip(·) denotes the Lipschitz constant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reproduce the proof, but the verification consists of substituting V(z)=||z||^p into the drift inequality of Theorem 10. The first displayed inequality controls the contribution of A to the derivative of ||X_t^x-X_t^y||^p. The Lipschitz constants of f and σ control the drift and diffusion growth terms. The lower bound involving Q^{1/2}(σ(x)-σ(y))^*(x-y) supplies a coercive noise contribution in the second-derivative term. The strict inequality β+Lip(f)+(1/2)Lip(σ)^2Tr Q<α^2 gives the negative coefficient required in Theorem 10(iii).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 11 (dissipativity/remote-start theorem for additive noise)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space and consider the additive-noise equation dX_t=(AX_t+f(X_t))dt+σ dW_t, where W is a standard cylindrical Wiener process in a Hilbert space U, σ∈L(U,H), and A:Dom(A)→H is a closed linear operator. For a Banach space E, write ∂||x||_E for the subdifferential of the norm. A map γ:Dom(γ)⊆E→E is dissipative if for every x,y∈Dom(γ) there is z^*∈∂||x-y||_E with z^*(γ(x)-γ(y))≤0; it is m-dissipative if Im(λI-γ)=E for some λ>0. If G⊆E is a subspace, γ_G denotes the part of γ on G. The theorem assumes the standard well-posedness/Feller hypotheses listed in the paper: (1) there exists η∈R such that A-ηI and f-ηI are m-dissipative on H; (2) there exists a reflexive Banach space K densely and continuously embedded in H such that (A-ηI)_K and (f-ηI)_K are m-dissipative on K; (3) Dom(f)⊇K and f maps bounded sets in K into bounded sets in H; (4) the stochastic convolution W_A(t)=∫_0^t e^{A(t-r)}σdW(r) is Dom(f_K)-valued, has paths continuous in H, and for every T>0, sup_{t∈[0,T]}{||W_A(t)||_K+||f(W_A(t))||_K}<∞ almost surely. In addition, suppose there are ω_1,ω_2∈R with ω=ω_1+ω_2>0 such that A+ω_1I and f+ω_2I are dissipative on H, and suppose sup_{t≥0} E[||W_A(t)||_H+||f(W_A(t))||_H] < ∞. Then the equation has a unique invariant probability measure μ, and P_t(y,·) converges narrowly to μ for every y∈H. Moreover, there exists L<∞ such that for every y∈H, t>0, and bounded Lipschitz g:H→R, |∫_H g dP_t(y,·)-∫_H g dμ| ≤ L(1+||y||)e^{-ωt/2}Lip(g).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper sketches the proof, known as the remote start method. Extend the driving cylindrical Wiener process to the whole real line by setting ~W(t)=W(t) for t≥0 and ~W(t)=Y(-t) for t<0, where Y is an independent standard cylindrical Wiener process. Let X(t;s,y), t≥s, denote the solution started from y at time s. First prove the a priori estimate E||X(t;s,y)||≤c+||y|| for s<0 and t≥s. To do this, subtract the stochastic convolution from time s: Ψ(t)=X(t;s,y)-∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r). Then Ψ solves pathwise dΨ/dt=AΨ+f(Ψ+∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r)), Ψ(s)=y. The dissipativity of A+ω_1I and f+ω_2I yields the Dini-derivative inequality d^-/dt ||Ψ(t)|| ≤ -ω||Ψ(t)|| + ||f(∫_s^t e^{A(t-r)}σd~W(r))||. The uniform expectation assumption on the convolution and f of the convolution gives the estimate. Next, for v<s<0 one obtains E||X(t;s,y)-X(t;v,y)|| ≤ e^{-ω(t-s)}(2||y||+c), t≥s. Hence {X(0;s,y):s≤0} is Cauchy in L^1(Ω;H) as s→-∞; let p be its limit and set μ=Law(p). By stationarity of the two-sided construction, P_t^*δ_y=Law(X(t;0,y))=Law(X(0;-t,y)) converges narrowly to Law(p)=μ. Since the Markov process is Feller, P_s^*μ=P_s^*(lim_{t→∞}P_t^*δ_y)=lim_{t→∞}P_{t+s}^*δ_y=μ, so μ is invariant. The exponential estimate for bounded Lipschitz g follows from the same Cauchy estimate by comparing X(t;0,y) with the remote-start limit. Uniqueness follows because every initial law is attracted narrowly to μ.

proof_techniques.name: Doob-type uniqueness from equivalence of transition kernels
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses an abstract Markov-process theorem on Polish spaces: if an invariant probability measure exists and all positive-time transition probabilities are mutually absolutely continuous, then the invariant measure is unique, pointwise ergodic averages converge almost surely, and transition laws converge in total variation to the invariant measure. This avoids constructing the invariant measure explicitly and reduces uniqueness to a positivity/equivalence property of the semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The state space C_0([0,1]) is Polish, so this criterion can be applied directly if one proves mutual equivalence of P_t(u_0,·) for t>0. A practical route suggested by the paper is to prove strong Feller and irreducibility for the SPDE semigroup and then invoke Lemma 6 plus Theorem 4.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller plus irreducibility implies kernel equivalence
proof_techniques.description: The paper records the standard argument that strong Feller regularity turns hitting probabilities of Borel sets into continuous functions, while irreducibility prevents a nonnegative continuous hitting probability from being positive somewhere but integrating to zero from another point. This proves that all positive-time transition probabilities share the same null sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirichlet stochastic heat equation with additive space-time white noise, irreducibility is often accessible via support of the stochastic convolution, and strong Feller may be attacked through smoothing of the nondegenerate noise or through approximation by the smooth drifts G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Gaussian support and approximate controllability for irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: For semilinear additive equations, write the solution as a deterministic continuous map of the stochastic convolution. The law of the stochastic convolution has support equal to the closure of its reproducing kernel Hilbert space; if the corresponding deterministic control operator has dense range in the path space, then every deterministic controlled path can be approximated with positive probability, yielding irreducibility.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The additive Dirichlet heat noise has a rich stochastic convolution. A solver can try to formulate a support theorem in C([0,T],C_0([0,1])) for the heat convolution and combine it with continuity of the solution map, perhaps first for the G_{1/n}-regularized equations and then through the assumed approximation result.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov comparison with the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process
proof_techniques.description: When the nonlinear drift f takes values in Im(Q^{1/2}) and Q^{-1/2}f satisfies suitable Lipschitz/growth bounds, the semilinear additive equation is absolutely continuous with respect to the linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation on finite time intervals. If the linear transition laws are mutually equivalent, the nonlinear transition laws are too, and uniqueness follows from the abstract equivalence criterion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For each smooth approximation G_{1/n}, the drift is a bounded Lipschitz Nemytskii-type term, and with space-time white noise one has the formally nondegenerate covariance Q=I in an L^2 setting. This suggests proving uniqueness for approximating semigroups via Girsanov and investigating whether equivalence or enough positivity survives the singular limit.

proof_techniques.name: Kolmogorov-equation smoothing criterion for strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper describes a method based on mild solutions of the infinite-dimensional backward Kolmogorov equation. Under a nondegeneracy/smoothing condition such as ∫_0^T ||Q_t^{-1/2}e^{At}||dt<∞ and bounded continuous drift, the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup regularizes enough to solve the Kolmogorov equation with C_b^1 spatial regularity. The derivative bound yields a Lipschitz estimate in total variation for transition kernels.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The one-dimensional Dirichlet heat semigroup with space-time white noise satisfies the spectral nondegeneracy pattern in Example 3. This technique is a candidate for proving strong Feller for regularized equations and possibly for the limiting distributional-drift equation if the ABLM approximation theory gives sufficient convergence of semigroups.

proof_techniques.name: Bismut-Elworthy derivative formula
proof_techniques.description: Instead of solving a Kolmogorov PDE, the paper records a derivative formula for P_tφ in terms of a stochastic integral involving the variational derivative of the solution with respect to its initial condition. The formula differentiates the semigroup without differentiating φ and can establish strong Feller estimates when Q^{-1/2} is bounded and the variational equation is controlled.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For smooth G_{1/n} drifts one may derive variational equations and Bismut-type gradient estimates. Uniform estimates in n, if available, would be a possible route to strong Feller regularity of the limiting semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Lyapunov stability of synchronized solutions
proof_techniques.description: The paper reviews a method in which two solutions are driven by the same noise and a C^2 Lyapunov function V of their difference is shown, via Itô's formula, to have negative drift. This yields decay of E[V(X_t^x-X_t^y)], hence narrow convergence of transition laws from different initial conditions. If an invariant measure exists, this asymptotic coupling in law implies uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a uniqueness route that does not require strong Feller or irreducibility. It would require identifying a Lyapunov functional for the difference of two solutions to the singular-drift SPDE or to its approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Dissipativity and remote start
proof_techniques.description: The remote-start method constructs solutions on the whole real line and starts them at times s→-∞. Dissipativity gives exponential contraction between solutions started at different remote times, making X(0;s,y) Cauchy in L^1. The limiting random variable defines an invariant law, and the same contraction gives convergence toward it and uniqueness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if the drift plus Dirichlet Laplacian can be placed into an m-dissipative framework or if the regularized drifts satisfy a uniform dissipativity estimate. It proves weak uniqueness/stability and does not rely on total-variation smoothing.

proof_techniques.name: Spectral verification for additive heat noise
proof_techniques.description: For diagonal parabolic operators, the paper reduces the key Gaussian range condition to an explicit sequence bound involving eigenvalues of A and Q. In the one-dimensional Dirichlet heat equation with Q=I, the summability and range conditions needed for strong Gaussian mixing are satisfied.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The linear part of the original SPDE is exactly the one-dimensional Dirichlet heat operator with space-time white noise. This supplies a ready-made nondegeneracy check for any argument comparing the nonlinear dynamics to the linear Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process.

other_useful_info: The paper defines strong asymptotic stability as convergence in total variation and weak asymptotic stability as narrow convergence against bounded continuous functions; both imply uniqueness of an invariant measure. It explicitly says that existence of invariant measures is not treated in the review. Section 2 notes that SPDEs with additive noise and drift terms having a potential may have explicitly describable invariant measures, citing Marcus [45-47], Kozlov [40], and Freidlin [25]; this pointer may be relevant for distributional drifts that are formal derivatives of bounded potentials. The paper also emphasizes that in infinite dimensions strong total-variation convergence can fail for degenerate noise even when a unique invariant measure exists and transition laws converge narrowly, so weak-stability methods can be essential. For multiplicative-noise equations, the paper cites an analogous dissipativity theorem in which the key condition involves Yosida approximations A_n=nA(nI-A)^{-1}: ⟨A_n(x-y)+f(x)-f(y),x-y⟩+||σ(x)-σ(y)||_{HS}^2 ≤ -ϖ||x-y||^2. Main related references identified by the review include Da Prato-Zabczyk [18,20] for stochastic equations and ergodicity in infinite dimensions, Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys [8] and Gatarek-Goldys [28] for Kolmogorov/invariant-measure methods, Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk [11] and Peszat-Zabczyk [60] for strong Feller derivative methods, Stettner [63] and Seidler [61] for the abstract equivalence theorem, and Mueller [59] for coupling methods for heat equations with noise.
why_relevant: This review collects uniqueness and stability criteria for invariant measures of nonlinear SPDEs, including strong Feller, irreducibility, recurrence, and convergence methods. It is useful for choosing the right abstract theorem for the present Markov semigroup.

title: Ergodic behaviour of stochastic parabolic equations
overall_summary: Seidler studies homogeneous Markov processes in separable Banach spaces, especially strong Feller irreducible processes with continuous sample paths, and proves infinite-dimensional analogues of the classical Khasminskii-Maruyama-Tanaka recurrence dichotomy. The paper shows that, under suitable small-time path-control assumptions, existence of a recurrent compact set yields a unique σ-finite invariant measure, Harris recurrence, ratio ergodic theorems, and total-variation convergence of transition probabilities; conversely, nonrecurrent compact sets cannot carry finite invariant mass. A particularly useful shortcut is Proposition 2.5: if the transition probabilities at positive times are all mutually equivalent and any finite invariant measure exists, then the process is Harris recurrent and transition laws from arbitrary initial distributions coalesce in total variation, implying uniqueness of invariant probabilities. The core techniques are Foguel's resolvent construction of a σ-finite invariant measure, embedded Markov chains on recurrent compact sets, Chacon-Ornstein ratio ergodic theory, and tail σ-algebra triviality for Harris recurrent skeleton chains. The paper also gives verification schemes for stochastic parabolic equations, showing how strong Feller, irreducibility, and the small-time condition can be checked from smoothing and moment estimates.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standing framework and assumptions in Section 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper works with a homogeneous Markov process X = (Ω, F, (F_t), (X_t), P_x) taking values in a separable Banach space H with norm ||·|| and Borel σ-algebra B(H). Its transition function is P(t,x,A), and P_t f(x) = ∫_H f(y) P(t,x,dy) for bounded Borel f. A measure μ on B(H) is invariant if P_t^* μ = μ for every t > 0. For a Borel set A, the first hitting time is τ_A = inf{t > 0 : X_t ∈ A}; A is recurrent if P_x{τ_A < ∞} = 1 for every x ∈ H. The main assumptions are: (A1) X has continuous sample paths; (A2) X is strong Feller, meaning P_t maps bounded Borel functions into bounded continuous functions for every t > 0; (A3) X is irreducible, meaning P(t,x,U) > 0 for every t > 0, x ∈ H, and nonempty open U ⊂ H; (A4) for every compact K ⊂ H and every η > 0, lim_{r down to 0} sup_{y ∈ K} P_y{sup_{0 ≤ t ≤ r} ||X_t - y|| ≥ η} = 0; and (A5) for every x ∈ H and ε,η > 0 there exist an open neighborhood V of x and T > 0 such that sup_{y ∈ V} P_y{sup_{0 ≤ t ≤ T} ||X_t - y|| ≥ η} ≤ ε. The paper also uses condition (E): all transition probabilities P(t,x,·), t > 0, x ∈ H, are mutually equivalent. It notes that (A2) and (A3) imply (E). A reference measure φ is fixed as φ = P(1,0,·); under (A2) and (A3), all P(t,x,·) are equivalent to φ, and if an invariant measure exists then it is equivalent to φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions and standard observations used throughout. The strong Feller and irreducibility implication to equivalence of transition probabilities is cited as classical: irreducibility gives positivity on nonempty open sets, while the strong Feller property prevents singular exceptional sets from depending on the starting point. The small-time assumptions (A4) and (A5) are path-continuity/tightness conditions used to transfer recurrence from hitting events to positive occupation times and to prove continuity of stopped semigroups. The paper later remarks that the Banach linear structure is not essential and that analogous statements hold in Polish, or more generally separable metrizable Radon, state spaces.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1, transient compact sets and finite invariant measures
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a homogeneous Markov process in a separable Banach space H satisfying (A1) continuous paths, (A2) strong Feller, (A3) irreducibility, and (A4) compact-uniform small-time control. Let K ⊂ H be compact and not recurrent, i.e. there is some starting point x for which P_x{τ_K < ∞} < 1, with τ_K = inf{t > 0 : X_t ∈ K}. Then sup_{x ∈ H} ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,K) dt < ∞. Consequently, if μ is any finite invariant measure for X, then μ(K) = 0. If additionally φ(K) > 0, where φ = P(1,0,·), then for every x ∈ H and every compact L ⊂ H, P_x{sup{t ≥ 0 : X_t ∈ L} < ∞} = 1 and lim_{t→∞} P(t,x,L) = 0. In particular, if a finite invariant measure exists, then some compact set must be recurrent, because finite Borel measures on H are Radon.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key input is Lemma 4.1, which gives continuity of x ↦ P_x{τ_K = ∞} off K. From nonrecurrence choose a point with positive probability of never hitting K; by continuity choose an open set V and α > 0 such that inf_{z ∈ V} P_z{τ_K = ∞} ≥ α. Fix T > 0. For y ∈ K, the Markov property gives P_y{∫_0^∞ 1_K(X_t) dt < T} ≥ P_y{X_T ∈ V and after time T never hits K} ≥ α P(T,y,V). By strong Feller and irreducibility, y ↦ P(T,y,V) is continuous and strictly positive on compact K, so q := α inf_{y ∈ K} P(T,y,V) > 0. Let χ = inf{r ≥ 0 : ∫_0^r 1_K(X_s) ds = T}. On {χ < ∞}, path continuity gives X_χ ∈ K. Applying the strong Markov property at χ and iterating yields sup_y P_y{∫_0^∞ 1_K(X_t) dt > kT} ≤ (1 - q)^k. Summing the tail probabilities gives the uniform bound sup_x ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,K)dt = sup_x E_x ∫_0^∞ 1_K(X_t)dt < ∞. If μ is finite invariant, then μ(K) = T^{-1} ∫_0^T μ(K)dt = ∫_H [T^{-1}∫_0^T P(t,y,K)dt] μ(dy), and dominated convergence using the uniform occupation bound gives μ(K)=0. For the last assertion, define h(x)=∫_0^∞ P(s,x,K)ds; this is bounded and strictly positive if φ(K)>0 by equivalence of transition probabilities. Its superlevel sets H_m={h>1/m} cover compact L. For the first hitting time σ(k) of L after time k, optional/strong Markov estimates give m^{-1} P_x{σ(k)<∞} ≤ E_x[1_{σ(k)<∞} h(X_{σ(k)})] ≤ ∫_k^∞ P(v,x,K)dv → 0. Hence after some finite random time the path never visits L, and then P(t,x,L)→0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2, recurrent compact set implies unique σ-finite invariant measure, Harris recurrence, and total-variation mixing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a homogeneous Markov process in a separable Banach space H satisfying (A1) continuous paths, (A2) strong Feller, (A3) irreducibility, and (A4) compact-uniform small-time control. Assume there exists a recurrent compact set K ⊂ H. Then: (i) there exists a σ-finite invariant measure μ for X; μ is unique up to multiplication by a positive constant and is Radon. (ii) For η > 0 let n_η = inf{t > η : X_t ∈ K}. If there exists δ > 0 such that sup_{x ∈ K} E_x n_δ < ∞, then μ(H) < ∞. (iii) X is Harris recurrent relative to μ: for every x ∈ H and every Borel A with μ(A) > 0, P_x{∫_0^∞ 1_A(X_s) ds = ∞} = 1. (iv) If (A_t) and (B_t) are additive functionals, (B_t) is nonnegative, E_μ |A_1| < ∞, and 0 < E_μ B_1 < ∞, then for every x ∈ H, P_x{lim_{t→∞} A_t/B_t = E_μ A_1 / E_μ B_1} = 1; also lim_{t→∞} E_x A_t / E_x B_t = E_μ A_1 / E_μ B_1 for μ-almost every x. (v) For any probability measures π and ρ on B(H), lim_{t→∞} ||P_t^*π - P_t^*ρ|| = 0 in total variation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is assembled from Section 5. First, (A2) and (A3) imply equivalence (E) of all positive-time transition laws. Foguel's theorem gives a σ-finite measure subinvariant for the resolvent. Lemma 5.3 shows that this subinvariant measure becomes invariant and unique if one has an infinite-occupation condition (C): there is a set V of finite μ-measure such that P_x{∫_0^∞ 1_V(X_s)ds=∞}=1 for all x. Proposition 5.4 proves this occupation condition from the recurrent compact K: every nonempty open set is occupied for infinite Lebesgue time almost surely. Lemma 5.2 supplies local finiteness and Radon regularity of the invariant measure. The finiteness criterion (ii) is proved by Proposition 5.9: define an embedded Markov chain by returns to K after a positive delay; it is a Feller chain on compact K, has an invariant probability π, and b(A)=E_π∫_0^{n_δ}1_A(X_s)ds is an invariant measure with b(H)=E_π n_δ <∞ under the displayed bound. Uniqueness of σ-finite invariant measures then forces μ to be finite. Harris recurrence and the ratio limit theorem are consequences of Proposition 5.5 and Corollary 5.6, which apply Chacon-Ornstein ratio ergodic theory to the shift on path space under P_μ. Finally, Proposition 5.7 proves total-variation coalescence of all transition laws by showing that the one-step skeleton is aperiodic Harris recurrent, hence has trivial tail σ-algebra, and then lifting tail triviality from the skeleton to the continuous-time process.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.3, consequences for finite and infinite invariant measure
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 2.2, let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure. If μ(H)=∞, then for every β ∈ L^1(μ) and every x ∈ H, lim_{T→∞} T^{-1}∫_0^T β(X_s)ds = 0 P_x-almost surely. Also, for every f ∈ L^1(μ) ∩ B(H), lim_{t→∞} P_t f(x)=0 for every x ∈ H; in particular, if D is Borel with μ(D)<∞, then P(t,x,D)→0 for every x. If μ(H)<∞ and μ^* = μ/μ(H), then μ^* is an invariant probability, and for every ψ ∈ L^1(μ) and every x ∈ H, lim_{T→∞} T^{-1}∫_0^T ψ(X_s)ds = ∫_H ψ dμ^* P_x-almost surely. Moreover, for every probability measure π, lim_{t→∞} ||P_t^*π - μ^*|| = 0; in particular P(t,x,·)→μ^* in total variation for every x ∈ H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The almost-sure time-average statements follow by applying the ratio ergodic theorem in Theorem 2.2(iv) to additive functionals A_t=∫_0^t β(X_s)ds and B_t=t in the finite case, and by comparing A_t to occupation of finite-measure sets in the infinite case. For μ(H)=∞, choose a finite-measure set M with μ(M) large relative to ||β||_1 and apply the ratio theorem to ∫β and ∫1_M; because ∫_0^T1_M(X_s)ds ≤ T, the ordinary time average is bounded by a ratio whose limit can be made arbitrarily small. The total-variation convergence in the finite case is Theorem 2.2(v) with ρ=μ^*. The pointwise convergence P_t f(x)→0 for f∈L^1(μ)∩B(H) in the infinite case is obtained from the same recurrent Markov-process limit theorem cited in the proof of Corollary 5.8.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4, recurrence of neighborhoods yields a recurrent compact set
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a homogeneous Markov process in a separable Banach space H satisfying (A1) continuous paths, (A2) strong Feller, (A3) irreducibility, and (A5) local small-time control: for every x ∈ H and ε,η > 0 there exist a neighborhood V of x and T > 0 with sup_{y∈V}P_y{sup_{0≤t≤T}||X_t-y||≥η}≤ε. Suppose there is a point x_0 ∈ H having a local base consisting of recurrent neighborhoods. Then there exists a recurrent compact set K ⊂ H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof observes that the earlier arguments really need the infinite-occupation condition (C), not directly a recurrent compact. Let μ be the Radon resolvent-subinvariant measure obtained in the Foguel construction. Choose an open neighborhood V of x_0 with μ(V)<∞. Pick a smaller open C around x_0 with positive distance η from H\V. By (A5), there is q>0 and a neighborhood M of x_0, chosen from the recurrent local base and lying in C, such that sup_{y∈M}P_y{sup_{0≤t≤q}||X_t-y||≥η}<1/2; hence from M the process stays in V for at least time q with probability at least 1/2. Let τ be the first hit of M and σ the first exit from V. Define successive cycles τ_n, σ_n by alternating hits of M and exits from V. Conditional probabilities of the events {σ_n-τ_n>q} are bounded below on the event τ_n<∞, so the generalized Borel-Cantelli lemma gives infinitely many such long sojourns in V unless the process never exits V after some cycle; in either case ∫_0^∞1_V(X_s)ds=∞. Thus condition (C) holds. The ratio/Harris recurrence machinery then gives Harris recurrence for all positive μ-measure sets. Since μ is Radon, it charges some compact set positively, and Harris recurrence makes such a compact recurrent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5, finite invariant measure plus equivalent transition laws implies Harris recurrence and total-variation coalescence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a homogeneous Markov process in a separable Banach space H satisfying (A1) continuous sample paths. Assume that all measures P(t,x,·), t > 0, x ∈ H, are mutually equivalent. If there exists a finite invariant measure for X, then X is Harris recurrent. In particular, the ratio limit theorem for additive functionals stated in Theorem 2.2(iv) and the total-variation coalescence statement in Theorem 2.2(v) hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: At the end of Section 5 the proof is reduced to the general condition (C) used in Propositions 5.5 and 5.7. If μ is a finite invariant measure, simply take V=H in condition (C): μ(V)<∞ and ∫_0^∞1_H(X_s)ds=∞ identically. The equivalence of all transition probabilities is exactly condition (E). Therefore Proposition 5.5 gives the ratio ergodic theorem, Corollary 5.6 gives Harris recurrence, and Proposition 5.7 gives lim_{t→∞}||P_t^*π-P_t^*ρ||=0 for arbitrary probability measures π and ρ. A direct uniqueness consequence is that two invariant probabilities π and ρ must satisfy ||π-ρ||=||P_t^*π-P_t^*ρ||→0, hence π=ρ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1, continuity of a killed semigroup
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous sample paths, (A2) strong Feller, and (A4) compact-uniform small-time control. Let F ⊂ H be closed and let τ = inf{t > 0 : X_t ∈ F}. For every bounded Borel function g on H and every t > 0, the function J(x)=E_x[1_{τ>t} g(X_t)] is continuous on H\F. The paper remarks that the same argument gives continuity on H\F of x ↦ P_x{τ=∞}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix t>0 and write, for s∈(0,t), J(x)=J_1(s,x)-J_2(s,x), where J_1(s,x)=E_x[θ_s(1_{τ>t-s}g(X_{t-s}))]=E_x G_s(X_s) with G_s(y)=E_y[1_{τ>t-s}g(X_{t-s})], and J_2(s,x)=E_x[1_{τ≤s} θ_s(1_{τ>t-s}g(X_{t-s}))]. By strong Feller, x↦J_1(s,x)=P_sG_s(x) is continuous for each s>0. To handle J_2 uniformly near a point w∈H\F, suppose w_n→w and let C={w}∪{w_n:n≥1}; C is compact and has positive distance from F after discarding none because w∉F and w_n→w in H\F. If τ≤s starting from y∈C, then the path must move by at least a fixed positive distance before time s. Assumption (A4) makes sup_{y∈C}P_y{τ≤s} arbitrarily small by choosing s small. Thus J_2(s,w) and J_2(s,w_n) are uniformly small, while J_1(s,w_n)→J_1(s,w). This contradicts any assumed discontinuity of J and proves the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1, recurrent compact set forces recurrence of all positive-reference-measure Borel sets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous sample paths, (A2) strong Feller, and (E) mutual equivalence of all positive-time transition probabilities. Assume the filtration satisfies the usual conditions. If there exists a recurrent compact set K ⊂ H, then every Borel set B with φ(B)>0, where φ is any fixed transition probability equivalent to all P(t,x,·), is recurrent: P_x{τ_B<∞}=1 for all x ∈ H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let m=τ_B. Since φ(B)>0 and transition laws are equivalent, P(1,y,B)>0 for every y∈H. Strong Feller makes y↦P(1,y,B) continuous, so compactness of K gives q:=inf_{y∈K}P(1,y,B)>0. Define successive returns to K separated by a time gap: τ_1=τ_K and τ_{n+1}=inf{t>τ_n+2:X_t∈K}. Recurrent compactness gives τ_n<∞ and X_{τ_n}∈K almost surely. Let A_n={τ_n≤m≤τ_n+1}=θ_{τ_n}{m≤1}. By the strong Markov property, P_x(A_n | F_{τ_n}) ≥ q on the relevant event. Hence ∑_n P_x(A_n | F_{τ_n})=∞ almost surely. The generalized Borel-Cantelli lemma implies that some A_n occurs almost surely, hence m<∞. The paper notes that the usual filtration assumptions are needed only to ensure Borel hitting times are stopping times; without them the same proof applies to closed positive-measure sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.2, subinvariant measures for strong Feller kernels are Radon under a finite positive test set
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a strong Feller Markov kernel on a separable Banach space H, meaning x↦Γ(x,A) is bounded continuous for every Borel A. Assume all probability measures Γ(x,·), x∈H, are mutually equivalent. Let γ be a nonzero measure with Γ^*γ≤γ. If there is a Borel set R such that 0<Γ^*γ(R)≤γ(R)<∞, then γ is locally finite, σ-finite, and Radon.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since Γ^*γ(R)=∫Γ(y,R)γ(dy)>0, choose y_0 with Γ(y_0,R)>0. Mutual equivalence implies Γ(x,R)>0 for any prescribed x. By strong Feller, y↦Γ(y,R) is continuous, so for δ=Γ(x,R)>0 there is a neighborhood V of x with Γ(y,R)≥δ/2 on V. Then γ(R)≥∫_V Γ(y,R)γ(dy)≥(δ/2)γ(V), so γ(V)<∞. Thus every point has a finite-measure neighborhood; separability gives σ-finiteness. The paper then invokes standard measure-theoretic regularity results: locally finite Borel measures on separable Banach/Radon spaces are Radon, or equivalently restrictions to finite-measure open sets are Radon and these pieces patch together.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3, Foguel resolvent construction of a σ-finite invariant measure
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous paths and (E) mutual equivalence of positive-time transition laws. Define the resolvent kernel U(x,A)=∫_0^∞ e^{-t}P(t,x,A)dt. Under (E), X is φ-irreducible, and Foguel's theorem gives a σ-finite nonzero measure μ with U^*μ≤μ. If for this μ there is a Borel set Q with μ(Q)<∞ and inf_{x∈H}∫_0^∞P(t,x,Q)dt=∞, then μ is invariant for X. Consequently, under these hypotheses there exists a σ-finite invariant measure, and it is unique up to multiplication by a positive constant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Foguel's theorem supplies a σ-finite resolvent-subinvariant measure μ because irreducibility in the resolvent sense follows from equivalence of transition probabilities. Let ν=μ-U^*μ, a nonnegative measure. For k≥1, summing powers of U gives ∫∑_{n=1}^k U^n(x,Q) ν(dx)=U^*μ(Q)-(U^{k+1})^*μ(Q)≤μ(Q)<∞. The divergence assumption on occupation of Q forces the integrand ∑_{n≥1}U^n(x,Q) to be infinite everywhere, hence ν=0. Thus U^*μ=μ. A standard resolvent-semigroup argument then yields P_t^*μ=μ for all t>0. Uniqueness of σ-finite invariant measures is cited from a standard proposition: for irreducible Markov processes with equivalent transition probabilities, two σ-finite invariant measures must be scalar multiples.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Condition (C) and Proposition 5.4, recurrent compact implies infinite occupation of every nonempty open set
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Condition (C) used in the paper is: for the σ-finite invariant measure μ constructed by the resolvent method, there exists a Borel set V with μ(V)<∞ such that P_x{∫_0^∞1_V(X_t)dt=∞}=1 for every x∈H. Proposition 5.4 states that if X satisfies (A1) continuous paths, (A2) strong Feller, (A3) irreducibility, and (A4) compact-uniform small-time control, and if there exists a recurrent compact set K, then for every nonempty open U⊂H and every x∈H, P_x{∫_0^∞1_U(X_t)dt=∞}=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a nonempty open U, irreducibility gives φ(U)>0. Since φ is Radon, choose a compact M⊂U with φ(M)>0. By Lemma 5.1, M is recurrent. Let η=dist(M,H\U)>0. Assumption (A4) gives q>0 such that sup_{y∈M}P_y{sup_{0≤t≤q}||X_t-y||≥η}<1/2, so starting from M the process remains inside U for time q with probability at least 1/2. Let τ be the first hitting time of M and σ the first hitting time of H\U. Define alternating stopping times τ_1=τ, σ_n=τ_n+θ_{τ_n}σ, and τ_{n+1}=σ_n+θ_{σ_n}τ. On each cycle, the event Q_n={σ_n-τ_n>q} has conditional probability at least 1/2. Since M is recurrent, the cycles continue almost surely, and the generalized Borel-Cantelli lemma yields infinitely many Q_n. Each Q_n contributes at least q units of occupation time in U, so the total occupation time of U is infinite. This verifies condition (C) by choosing a nonempty open set of finite μ-measure, whose existence follows from local finiteness/Radon regularity via Lemma 5.2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5, ratio ergodic theorem for additive functionals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous paths, (E) equivalence of all positive-time transition laws, and condition (C). Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure for X. Let (A_t) and (B_t) be additive functionals, with B_t nonnegative, E_μ|A_1|<∞, and 0<E_μB_1<∞. Then for every x∈H, P_x{lim_{t→∞}A_t/B_t=E_μA_1/E_μB_1}=1. Moreover, lim_{t→∞}E_xA_t/E_xB_t=E_μA_1/E_μB_1 for μ-almost every x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let P_μ be the σ-finite path measure obtained by integrating P_x against μ. Since μ is invariant, P_μ is invariant under the time-one shift θ_1. The σ-algebra of θ_1-invariant sets is trivial under P_μ because of equivalence of transition probabilities: any invariant event corresponds to a Borel set that is either μ-null or μ-conull. Define T on L^1(P_μ) by Tξ=θ_1ξ. To apply Chacon-Ornstein, T must be conservative. Condition (C) gives a finite-measure set V and v=∫_0^1 1_V(X_s)ds∈L^1(P_μ) such that ∑_{n≥0}T^nv=∫_0^∞1_V(X_s)ds=∞ P_μ-a.e.; this is conservativity. The event that ∑_{n≥0}θ_1^nB_1=∞ is θ_1-invariant and has positive P_μ-measure because E_μB_1>0, hence is conull. Chacon-Ornstein, plus the Neveu-Chacon identification of invariant conditional expectations, gives the discrete-time ratio limit for sums of θ_1^iA_1 and θ_1^iB_1, and additivity identifies these sums with A_n and B_n. The paper then invokes the standard continuous-time extension used by Azéma-Duflo-Revuz. To upgrade from μ-almost every initial state to every x, let C be the full P_μ-measure event of convergence; if N={x:P_x(C)<1}, then μ(N)=0, and for arbitrary y, equivalence gives P(1,y,N)=0, so P_y(C)=P_y(θ_1C)=∫P_z(C)P(1,y,dz)=1. For expectations, define the Markov operator S f(x)=∫f(z)P(1,x,dz) on L^1(μ), use h(x)=∫_0^1P(s,x,V)ds to show S is conservative, apply Chacon-Ornstein to f(x)=E_xA_1 and g(x)=E_xB_1, and pass from integer to continuous time as before.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.6, Harris recurrence and ergodic averages under condition (C)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the assumptions of Proposition 5.5, X is Harris recurrent: for every Borel A with μ(A)>0 and every x∈H, P_x{∫_0^∞1_A(X_s)ds=∞}=1. If μ(H)=∞, then for every β∈L^1(μ), T^{-1}∫_0^Tβ(X_s)ds→0 P_x-a.s. for every x. If μ(H)<∞ and μ^*=μ/μ(H), then for every ψ∈L^1(μ), T^{-1}∫_0^Tψ(X_s)ds→∫ψ dμ^* P_x-a.s. for every x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Harris recurrence, suppose some A with μ(A)>0 has finite total occupation with positive probability from z. Let V be the finite-measure set from condition (C). On that event, ∫_0^T1_A(X_s)ds divided by ∫_0^T1_V(X_s)ds tends to 0, while Proposition 5.5 applied to A_t=∫_0^t1_A(X_s)ds and B_t=∫_0^t1_V(X_s)ds forces the limit to be μ(A)/μ(V)>0, contradiction. In the infinite-measure case, reduce to β≥0 and choose a finite-measure set M with μ(M) so large that ||β||_1/μ(M) is small; the ratio theorem for β versus 1_M bounds the ordinary time average because ∫_0^T1_M(X_s)ds≤T. In the finite-measure case, use B_t=t and A_t=∫_0^tψ(X_s)ds in the ratio theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7, tail triviality and total-variation convergence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous paths, (E) equivalence of positive-time transition laws, and condition (C). Then for any probability measures π and ρ on H, lim_{t→∞}||P_t^*π-P_t^*ρ||=0 in total variation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider the skeleton chain X_n with one-step kernel P(1,x,dy). Equivalence of the transition laws makes this chain aperiodic. For any Borel B with 0<μ(B)<∞, let R={∑_{n≥1}1_B(X_n)=∞}. This event is θ_1-invariant. Conservativity from condition (C) implies the relevant shift operator cannot have finite total visits to B on a set of full measure; if P_μ(R)=0, then taking expectations gives 0=E_μ∑_{n≥1}1_B(X_n)=∑_{n≥1}μ(B), impossible. Thus R is P_μ-conull, and equivalence upgrades recurrence from μ-a.e. starting point to every starting point. Hence the skeleton is aperiodic Harris recurrent. By the Jamison-Orey theorem, its tail σ-algebra is trivial, and in particular ||P(n,x,·)-P(n,y,·)||→0 for all x,y. To transfer this to continuous time, let S_tail=∩_{t≥0}σ{X_r:r≥t}. For A in this tail σ-algebra, write A=θ_tA_t with A_t∈F_t, so P_x(A)=P(t,x,A_t). The skeleton convergence implies P_x(A)=P_y(A) for all x,y. Martingale convergence of E_x[1_A | F_n] then forces this common value to be 0 or 1, so the continuous-time tail σ-algebra is trivial. Standard Markov-process theory identifies tail triviality with total-variation convergence of transition probabilities from arbitrary initial probability measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.8, finite invariant measure gives convergence to the invariant probability; infinite invariant measure gives escape from finite-measure sets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the assumptions of Proposition 5.7, let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure. If μ(H)<∞ and μ^*=μ/μ(H), then μ^* is an invariant probability and for every probability measure π, ||P_t^*π-μ^*||→0. If μ(H)=∞, then for every f∈L^1(μ)∩B(H) and every x∈H, P_t f(x)→0; in particular, for every Borel D with μ(D)<∞, P(t,x,D)→0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the finite case, Proposition 5.7 is applied with ρ=μ^*, which is invariant. In the infinite case, the paper cites the standard Markov-process limit theorem of Jain and notes that the proof of Theorem 2.7 in that reference applies with the established recurrence and tail-triviality properties. Taking f=1_D gives decay of transition probabilities of finite-μ sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9, embedded return chain construction of an invariant measure and a positive-recurrence criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume X satisfies (A1) continuous paths, (A2) strong Feller, and (E) equivalence of positive-time transition laws. Let K⊂H be a recurrent compact set. For η>0 define n_η=inf{t>η:X_t∈K}. Then there exists a σ-finite invariant measure b for X. If there exists δ>0 such that sup_{x∈K}E_x n_δ<∞, then b(H)<∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix δ>0 and write n=n_δ. Define return times n_1=n and n_{k+1}=n_k+θ_{n_k}n. Since K is recurrent, X(n_k)∈K and n_k<∞ almost surely. The embedded process X(n_k) is a homogeneous Markov chain on compact K with transition kernel Q(x,A)=E_x1_A(X_n). It is Feller: for f∈C_b(K), extend f to F∈C_b(H), let τ be the first hitting time of K and ψ(y)=E_yF(X_τ). Then E_xf(X_n)=E_xψ(X_δ), and the strong Feller property at time δ gives continuity on K. A Feller chain on compact K has an invariant probability π. Define b(A)=E_π∫_0^n1_A(X_s)ds. For bounded nonnegative f and t>0, invariance is checked by the identities ∫P_tf db=E_π∫_0^n f(X_{t+s})ds=E_π∫_t^{t+n}f(X_s)ds, then splitting the last integral into [0,n] plus two boundary pieces and using invariance of π for the embedded chain to cancel the boundary terms. Hence b is invariant. If sup_{x∈K}E_x n<∞, then b(H)=E_πn<∞. Without this bound, the paper shows b is still σ-finite: after possibly choosing K with positive φ-measure, b(K)<∞, and since ∫P(1,y,K)b(dy)=b(K)>0, Lemma 5.2 yields σ-finiteness and Radon regularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.4 and Remark 2.2, alternative Khasminskii-type finiteness condition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In addition to the condition sup_{x∈K}E_x n_δ<∞, the paper records another sufficient condition for the invariant measure to be finite. Let K be a recurrent compact set, let V be a bounded open set with V⊃K, let σ be the first hitting time of H\V, let τ be the first hitting time of K, and define ρ=σ+θ_στ, the first return to K after first leaving V. If sup_{x∈K}E_xρ<∞, then the invariant measure is finite. This is the infinite-dimensional analogue of a Khasminskii condition.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same embedded-chain construction as in Proposition 5.9, replacing n_δ by ρ. The only extra point is to show that the embedded kernel remains Feller. If f∈C_b(K) and ψ(y)=E_yf(X_τ), then E_xf(X_ρ)=E_xψ(X_σ). Lemma 4.1 implies continuity of x↦E_xψ(X_{σ∧t}) on V for fixed t, and the uniform expectation bound sup_{x∈K}E_xρ<∞ lets one pass to t=∞ uniformly on K. Once the embedded chain is Feller on compact K, it has an invariant probability and the occupation measure over one cycle has finite total mass by the displayed expectation bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.1, verification of assumptions for Lipschitz stochastic evolution equations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space, A generate a C_0 semigroup S(t), and W be a standard cylindrical Wiener process in H. Consider dX=(AX+f(X))dt+b(X)dW, where f:H→H and b:H→L(H) are globally Lipschitz, every b(z) is invertible with sup_z||b(z)^{-1}||<∞, and there exists r∈(0,1) such that ∫_0^T t^{-r}||S(t)||_{HS}^2dt<∞ for every T>0. Then the mild solution defines a Markov process satisfying (A1), (A2), (A3), and (A5).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The strong Feller property and irreducibility are cited from Peszat-Zabczyk, while continuity of paths follows from Da Prato-Zabczyk under the Hilbert-Schmidt smoothing condition. To verify (A5), choose p>2/r. Standard estimates give sup_y E||X(t,y)||^p≤C(1+||y||^p), an estimate of order T(1+||y||^p) for the p-th moment of the stochastic convolution supremum on [0,T], and the same order for the deterministic nonlinear convolution. Hence E_y sup_{0≤t≤T}||X_t-y||^p≤C(sup_{0≤t≤T}||S(t)y-y||^p+T(1+||y||^p)). For y in a small neighborhood of a fixed x, strong continuity of S(t) and small T make the right side arbitrarily small, and Chebyshev's inequality gives (A5).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.3, reaction-diffusion systems with additive space-time white noise and Dirichlet boundary conditions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H=L^2((0,1);R^n), let A be the Dirichlet Laplacian acting componentwise with domain W^{2,2}∩W_0^{1,2}, and consider the stochastic reaction-diffusion system du=(Au+f(u))dt+dW, where W is a standard cylindrical Wiener process in H and f(v)(ξ)=(F_1(v(ξ)),…,F_n(v(ξ))) with F_i:R^n→R Lipschitz. Then the Markov process satisfies (A1)-(A4). If additionally the drift is of potential type, (F_1,…,F_n)=∇U for a function U:R^n→R bounded above, and U_bar(v)=∫_0^1U(v(ξ))dξ, then dμ=exp(U_bar(v)) dν is a finite invariant measure, where ν=N(0, -1/2 A^{-1}) is the Gaussian invariant measure of the linear equation. The paper notes that nonempty open sets in C_0([0,1];R^n) have positive μ-measure and are recurrent by Theorem 2.2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The verification of (A1)-(A4) is obtained by viewing the system as a special case of Example 3.1 with additive nondegenerate noise and the smoothing Dirichlet heat semigroup. The invariant Gibbs-type measure is cited from Zabczyk's result on symmetric solutions of semilinear stochastic equations; boundedness above of U makes exp(U_bar) integrable with respect to the Gaussian ν. Since the Gaussian ν is supported on continuous functions satisfying the boundary condition and charges every nonempty open set in that topology, μ has the same positivity property. Theorem 2.2 then turns positive invariant measure into Harris recurrence for those sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.4, bounded weakly continuous drift in Hilbert space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper considers dX=(AX+g(X))dt+dW in a separable Hilbert space H, where A generates a C_0 semigroup S(t), W is a cylindrical Wiener process with covariance Q, and g:H→H is bounded and weakly continuous. Under the covariance/smoothing assumptions sup_{t≥0}Tr Q_t<∞ for Q_t=∫_0^tS(r)QS(r)^*dr, suitable range and kernel conditions Rng S(t)⊂Rng Q_t^{1/2}, Ker Q_t^{1/2}={0}, an integrability condition on Q_t^{-1/2}S(t), and a Hilbert-Schmidt smoothing condition analogous to ∫_0^T t^{-r}||S(t)||_{HS}^2dt<∞, the martingale solution is unique in law, has continuous paths, and the resulting Markov process is strong Feller, irreducible, and satisfies (A5).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence of martingale solutions with continuous paths is cited from Gatarek-Goldys; uniqueness in law, strong Feller property, and irreducibility are cited from Chojnowska-Michalik and Goldys under the stated covariance nondegeneracy and smoothing assumptions. The verification of (A5) is the same as in Example 3.1: boundedness of g controls the deterministic convolution, and the stochastic convolution is controlled by the trace/Hilbert-Schmidt assumptions, so short-time deviations from the initial point are uniformly small on a sufficiently small neighborhood.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Examples 3.5 and 3.6, stochastic heat equations in C_0 with polynomial drift
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper recalls Maslowski-type conditions for stochastic evolution equations in a Banach space E continuously embedded in a Hilbert space H, with additive noise and locally Lipschitz drift satisfying a dissipative subdifferential estimate. Under assumptions ensuring an E-valued continuous stochastic convolution, irreducibility, strong Feller property, and small-time estimates, the resulting Markov process in E satisfies (A1)-(A4). A concrete example is the one-dimensional stochastic heat equation on (0,π) with Dirichlet boundary conditions, ∂_t u=∂_x^2u+P(u)+noise, where P is an odd-degree polynomial with negative leading coefficient, H=L^2(0,π), E=C_0([0,π]), and the covariance is diagonal in the sine basis. Under the displayed spectral condition (3.21) on the covariance eigenvalues and either trace-class noise or the identity covariance case with known stochastic convolution estimates, the C_0-valued Markov process satisfies (A1)-(A4).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the abstract Banach-space setting, the mild solution is written as X(t,y)=S(t)y+∫_0^tS(t-s)f(X(s,y))ds+Z(t), and v_y(t)=X(t,y)-S(t)y-Z(t) is controlled by the dissipativity inequality (Ax+f(x+y),x^*)≤a(||y||_E). This gives ||v_y(t)||_E≤∫_0^t a(||S(s)y+Z(s)||_E)ds. Together with small-time continuity of S(t)y and of the stochastic convolution Z in E, this proves (A4). Irreducibility and strong Feller are cited from Maslowski under the range and finite-dimensional approximation assumptions. For the polynomial heat equation, the negative leading coefficient gives the dissipativity estimate with a polynomial a(r), and the cited stochastic convolution bounds verify the required E-continuity and small-time moment conditions.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness via total-variation coalescence from equivalent transition laws
proof_techniques.description: A central device is Proposition 2.5 combined with Proposition 5.7. If all positive-time transition probabilities P(t,x,·) are mutually equivalent and there is a finite invariant measure, then condition (C) holds trivially with V=H. The path-space shift is conservative, the skeleton chain is Harris recurrent and aperiodic, its tail σ-algebra is trivial, and therefore transition laws from any two initial probability distributions converge to one another in total variation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an at-most-one-invariant-probability statement, one can assume two invariant probabilities exist and use total-variation coalescence to force equality. Thus the key hypothesis to try to prove for the SPDE semigroup is mutual absolute continuity of positive-time transition laws, or strong Feller plus irreducibility, which the paper notes implies it.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller plus irreducibility gives compact-uniform lower bounds
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the following pattern: irreducibility gives P(t,x,U)>0 pointwise for nonempty open U, and strong Feller makes x↦P(t,x,U) continuous. On a compact set K, this yields a strictly positive lower bound inf_{x∈K}P(t,x,U)>0. This lower bound is then inserted into Markov-property estimates at hitting or return times.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the SPDE is shown strong Feller and irreducible on C_0([0,1]), then any compact set of initial profiles has a uniform positive chance to enter a chosen open set after fixed positive time. This is the exact input used for uniqueness and recurrence arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Nonrecurrent compact occupation estimate
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.1 proves that a nonrecurrent compact set K has uniformly finite expected total occupation time. The proof finds an open set from which K can be avoided forever with uniformly positive probability, then uses successive blocks of occupation time and the strong Markov property to get a geometric tail bound for ∫1_K(X_t)dt.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If working under the full strong Feller/irreducible/small-time framework, this gives a dichotomy: any finite invariant measure must charge some recurrent compact set. Since invariant probabilities on C_0 are Radon, this can reduce uniqueness to the recurrent compact case.

proof_techniques.name: Foguel resolvent measure plus infinite occupation condition
proof_techniques.description: Rather than constructing an invariant measure directly, the paper first obtains a σ-finite measure subinvariant for the resolvent U from Foguel's theorem. If there is any finite-measure set Q whose expected total occupation is infinite from every starting point, then the subinvariant defect μ-U^*μ must vanish, because otherwise integrating the divergent occupation over the defect measure would contradict finiteness of μ(Q). This turns a resolvent-subinvariant measure into an invariant one.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness this may not be needed if an invariant probability is assumed, but it is useful if one wants to prove a full dichotomy or identify recurrence properties of any invariant measure for the SPDE.

proof_techniques.name: Infinite occupation of open sets from recurrent compact sets
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.4 converts recurrence of a compact K into infinite Lebesgue-time occupation of every nonempty open set U. The argument chooses a compact M⊂U of positive reference measure, proves M is recurrent, and uses small-time path control to ensure that each visit to M has a fixed positive chance to remain in U for a fixed positive duration. Conditional Borel-Cantelli then yields infinitely many such sojourns.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a recurrent compact can be located for the SPDE, this method can promote mere hitting recurrence into occupation recurrence, which is what the ergodic and uniqueness machinery needs.

proof_techniques.name: Embedded Markov chain on a recurrent compact
proof_techniques.description: For a recurrent compact K, the process sampled at successive delayed returns to K forms a Markov chain on compact K. Strong Feller properties of the original semigroup imply the embedded kernel is Feller. A Feller chain on compact K has an invariant probability π, and integrating the original process over one return cycle under π gives an invariant measure for the continuous-time process.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a route to proving existence or finiteness of invariant measures for SPDEs if one can show expected return times to a compact set are uniformly bounded. It can also give a positive recurrence criterion independent of explicit invariant measure formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Chacon-Ornstein ratio ergodic theorem for additive functionals
proof_techniques.description: The paper applies Chacon-Ornstein to the time-one shift on path space under the σ-finite invariant path measure P_μ. Conservativity comes from infinite occupation of a finite-measure set. Triviality of the invariant σ-algebra follows from equivalence of transition probabilities. This yields almost-sure limits for ratios A_t/B_t of additive functionals and, as a corollary, Harris recurrence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Once an invariant probability or finite invariant measure is present, taking B_t=t gives ergodic averages; taking A_t and B_t as occupation times gives recurrence of positive-measure sets. This may support stronger conclusions than mere uniqueness for the SPDE.

proof_techniques.name: Tail σ-algebra triviality via Harris recurrent skeleton
proof_techniques.description: The total-variation convergence proof samples the continuous-time process at integer times. Under equivalence of transition probabilities and recurrence, the skeleton chain is aperiodic Harris recurrent, so known Markov-chain results give triviality of its tail σ-algebra and convergence of P(n,x,·) and P(n,y,·). The paper then lifts tail triviality to continuous time by representing tail events as shifted finite-time events and using martingale convergence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the SPDE semigroup, this technique would show not just uniqueness but convergence of transition probabilities from different initial conditions, provided the equivalence/recurrence hypotheses can be verified.

proof_techniques.name: Small-time path control to prove continuity of stopped kernels
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.1 shows how (A4) and strong Feller imply continuity of x↦E_x[1_{τ>t}g(X_t)] away from a closed target set. The semigroup is split at a small time s: the unstopped part is continuous by strong Feller, and the error from hitting the closed set before s is uniformly small on compact neighborhoods by small-time path control.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For SPDEs with continuous C_0-valued paths, proving an analogue of (A4) from stochastic convolution estimates can unlock stopped-process continuity and embedded-chain Feller properties.

proof_techniques.name: SPDE verification through smoothing and stochastic convolution estimates
proof_techniques.description: In Section 3, Seidler verifies the abstract assumptions for several stochastic parabolic equations. The typical estimate bounds E_y sup_{0≤t≤T}||X_t-y||^p by a semigroup continuity term plus small deterministic and stochastic convolution terms. Nondegenerate additive or multiplicative noise and semigroup smoothing are used to cite strong Feller and irreducibility results.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the stochastic heat equation on C_0([0,1]), analogous heat-kernel smoothing and additive space-time white noise estimates are natural tools for proving continuous paths, small-time control, and possibly irreducibility/strong Feller for approximating equations or the limiting singular-drift equation.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly remarks that its results do not essentially use the linear structure of H and remain valid for Polish, or more generally separable metrizable Radon, state spaces. Remark 4.2 says Theorem 2.1 remains valid without irreducibility (A3) if one assumes directly that all positive-time transition probabilities are equivalent. The introductory discussion emphasizes that strong Feller alone or irreducibility alone is not enough in infinite dimensions; Maslowski constructed irreducible linear stochastic evolution equations with many invariant probabilities when strong Feller fails. The paper cites Peszat-Zabczyk for strong Feller and irreducibility of Hilbert-space diffusions, Chojnowska-Michalik and Goldys for bounded weak drifts, Maslowski for equations with unbounded polynomial drift, Khasminskii and Maruyama-Tanaka for classical recurrent diffusion methods, Foguel for subinvariant resolvent measures, and Jamison-Orey for Harris recurrent Markov chains. Remark 2.1 notes that Theorem 2.2(v) implies lim_{t→∞}||P(t,x,·)-P(t,y,·)||=0 for all x,y and hence |P_tf(x)-P_tf(y)|→0 for all bounded Borel f. Remark 2.2 records a Khasminskii-style finite invariant measure criterion based on uniformly bounded expected return to K after first leaving a bounded open neighborhood.
why_relevant: This paper studies strong Feller irreducible Markov processes in Banach spaces and applies the results to stochastic parabolic equations. It contains exactly the type of uniqueness-of-invariant-measure criteria needed for the SPDE semigroup.

title: Asymptotic properties of stochastic semilinear equations by the method of lower measures
overall_summary: The paper develops and applies the method of lower measures, also called the l-condition, to prove total variation convergence of Markov evolution systems and, in the time-homogeneous case, uniqueness and mixing of invariant measures. The abstract mechanism is: prove that all sufficiently late transition laws asymptotically dominate a common nonzero finite measure, then total variation distances between any two evolved probability measures vanish. The paper then verifies this mechanism for Hilbert-space semilinear stochastic equations with additive cylindrical noise by combining recurrence into a ball, a one-step minorization on that ball, Girsanov comparison with an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, and a lower bound on Gaussian transition densities. For the original SPDE with singular δ0 drift, the most directly portable content is the abstract lower-measure criterion and the recurrence-plus-minorization template; the Hilbert-space density estimates suggest a possible route through smooth drift approximations and uniform one-step lower bounds, although the paper itself treats regular H-valued drifts satisfying restrictive summability and exponential-integrability assumptions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.4 (lower measures imply merging and, in the homogeneous case, invariant mixing)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,F) be a measurable space. Let M be the finite signed measures on F, M_+ the finite nonnegative measures, P the probability measures, and ∥ν∥ the total variation norm of a signed measure ν, with ν^- its negative variation. A Markov operator is a linear map S:M→M such that S(P)⊂P. Let Σ={S_{s,t}:0≤s≤t<∞} be an evolution system of Markov operators, meaning S_{u,t}S_{s,u}=S_{s,t} for 0≤s≤u≤t. A family {μ_s:s≥0}⊂M_+ is a system of lower measures for Σ if inf_{s≥0} μ_s(X)>0 and, for every fixed s≥0 and every ν∈P, ∥(S_{s,t}ν−μ_s)^-∥→0 as t→∞. If such a system of lower measures exists, then for every s≥0 and every ν_1,ν_2∈P one has ∥S_{s,t}ν_1−S_{s,t}ν_2∥→0 as t→∞. If Σ is homogeneous, meaning S_{s,t}=S_{s+h,t+h} for all h≥0 and S_t:=S_{0,t}, then existence of a lower measure is equivalent to existence of an invariant probability measure μ* with S_t μ*=μ* for all t≥0 and ∥S_tν−μ*∥→0 as t→∞ for every ν∈P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Maslowski [27], based on Lasota-Yorke type lower-measure arguments, rather than reproving it. The underlying mechanism is that the l-condition gives a common nonzero asymptotic submeasure in all evolved laws S_{s,t}ν. Removing the common part from two evolved probability measures yields a strict loss of total variation mass, and iteration forces the distance between any two evolved probabilities to vanish. In the homogeneous case, convergence of S_tν for all ν gives an invariant limit by the semigroup property; conversely, if an invariant probability attracts all initial laws in total variation then it itself supplies a lower measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.5 (quantitative lower-measure contraction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Σ={S_{s,t}:0≤s≤t<∞} be an evolution system of Markov operators on a measurable space (X,F). Let {μ_s:s≥0}⊂M_+ be finite nonnegative measures with λ:=inf_{s≥0} μ_s(X)>0. Fix ν_1,ν_2∈P, ε∈[0,1/2], and α∈(0,1/2). Assume there is a set B∈F such that (i) S_{σ,t}ν_1(X∖B)≤ε and S_{σ,t}ν_2(X∖B)≤ε for every 0≤σ≤t<∞, and (ii) there exists τ≥0 such that for every probability μ with μ(B)=1 and every σ≥0, ∥(S_{σ,σ+τ}μ−μ_σ)^-∥<λα. Put q_α:=1−(1−2α)λ, so q_α∈(0,1). Then for every s≥0, every n∈N, and every t≥s+nτ, ∥S_{s,t}ν_1−S_{s,t}ν_2∥≤q_α^n∥ν_1−ν_2∥+12ε∑_{i=0}^n q_α^i≤q_α^n∥ν_1−ν_2∥+12ε/(1−q_α).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is an induction on n. Suppose the estimate has been obtained up to time s+nτ. Let ν_i^n:=S_{s,s+nτ}ν_i. Normalize the restrictions of ν_i^n to B by μ_i(A)=c_iν_i^n(A∩B), where c_i=ν_i^n(B)^{-1}. Assumption (i) and ε≤1/2 give ∥ν_i^n−μ_i∥≤2(ε+ε/(1−ε))≤6ε. Let μ:=μ_1−μ_2 and η:=∥μ^+∥=∥μ^-∥=∥μ∥/2. If η>0, then η^{-1}μ^+ and η^{-1}μ^- are probabilities supported on B. Assumption (ii) gives ∥(S_{s+nτ,s+(n+1)τ}η^{-1}μ^±−μ_{s+nτ})^-∥<λα. Since each Sη^{-1}μ^± has total mass 1 and μ_{s+nτ}(X)≥λ, the total variation identity ∥ρ−m∥=ρ(X)−m(X)+2∥(ρ−m)^-∥ for nonnegative measures yields ∥Sη^{-1}μ^±−μ_{s+nτ}∥≤1−λ+2λα=q_α. Therefore ∥S_{s+nτ,s+(n+1)τ}μ∥≤q_α∥μ∥. Combining this contraction with the two 6ε errors from replacing ν_i^n by μ_i and using the induction hypothesis gives the next-step bound. Finally, every Markov operator has total variation operator norm at most one on signed measures, so the estimate at s+nτ extends to all later t≥s+nτ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.7 (recurrence plus one-step minorization gives lower measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P(s,x,t,A), 0≤s≤t<∞, x∈X, A∈F, be the transition function of a nonhomogeneous Markov process on a measurable space (X,F). Define the adjoint Markov evolution system P^*_{s,t}ν(A):=∫_X P(s,x,t,A)ν(dx). Assume that for every s≥0 there exist β(s)>0, a finite nonnegative measure μhat_s∈M_+, and a measurable set B(s)∈F such that (i) for every x∈X there is t_0=t_0(s,x) with P(s,x,t,B(s))≥β(s) for all t≥t_0, (ii) for every A∈F, inf{P(t,x,t+1,A):x∈B(s), t≥s}≥μhat_s(A), and (iii) inf_{s≥0} β(s)μhat_s(X)>0. Then {β(s)μhat_s:s≥0} is a system of lower measures for {P^*_{s,t}}. If β, B, and μhat can be chosen independent of s, then β μhat is a lower measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For t≥s and A∈F, use the Chapman-Kolmogorov formula: P^*_{s,t+1}ν(A)=∫_X∫_X P(t,y,t+1,A)P(s,x,t,dy)ν(dx). Restrict the inner integral to y∈B(s) and apply the one-step lower bound (ii) to get P^*_{s,t+1}ν(A)≥μhat_s(A)∫_X P(s,x,t,B(s))ν(dx). By (i) and Fatou's lemma, the liminf of the scalar factor is at least β(s) for each initial probability ν. Hence P^*_{s,t+1}ν asymptotically dominates β(s)μhat_s in the sense that ∥(P^*_{s,t+1}ν−β(s)μhat_s)^-∥→0. Condition (iii) gives the uniform positive mass required in the definition of a system of lower measures.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Conditioned Ornstein-Uhlenbeck bridge representation (equations 2.7-2.9)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Section 2, H has an orthonormal eigenbasis {e_n} for a negative self-adjoint operator A with Ae_n=−λ_ne_n, λ_n>0. Let W be a cylindrical Wiener process with scalar components w_n(t)=⟨W_t,e_n⟩. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation is dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r. For fixed t≥0 and endpoints x,y∈H, write x_n=⟨x,e_n⟩ and y_n=⟨y,e_n⟩. The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process conditioned to go from x at time t to y at time t+1, denoted Zhat(s)=Z^{t,x}_{t+1,y}(s), s∈[t,t+1], is represented by Zhat(s)=∑_{n≥1} Zhat_n(s)e_n, where Y_n(s):=((1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)})/e^{−λ_n(t+1−s)})∫_t^s e^{−λ_n(t+1−u)}(1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−u)})^{-1}dw_n(u), and Zhat_n(s)=e^{−λ_n(s−t)}(1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)})(1−e^{−2λ_n})^{-1}x_n+e^{λ_n(t+1−s)}(e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)}−e^{−2λ_n})(1−e^{−2λ_n})^{-1}y_n+Y_n(s).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this formula from Simão [34]. Later, in the proof of Lemma 3.2, the same bridge is characterized by the scalar stochastic differential equation dZhat_n(s)=dw_n(s)−λ_nZhat_n(s)ds−2λ_n(e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)}Zhat_n(s)−e^{−λ_n(t+1−s)}y_n)(1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)})^{-1}ds with Zhat_n(t)=x_n. Solving this linear equation gives the displayed formula and ensures Zhat(t)=x and Zhat(t+1)=y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2 (uniform lower bound on nonlinear transition density relative to OU)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space, let A be self-adjoint, negative, and have an orthonormal eigenbasis {e_n} with Ae_n=−λ_ne_n, λ_n≥λ_0>0, and ∑_n λ_n^{-1}<∞. Let W be cylindrical Brownian motion on H. Let X solve dX_r=[AX_r+f(r,X_r)]dr+dW_r and let P(t,x,t+1,·) be its transition law over one unit of time. Let Q(1,x,·) be the corresponding one-step transition law of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck equation dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r, and set γ:=Q(1,0,·). For f_n(r,z):=⟨f(r,z),e_n⟩ define δ_n:=sup{|f_n(r,z)|:r≥0,z∈H}. Assume (A1) δ_n<∞ for all n and ∑_n δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞; (A2) there exists α>0 such that E exp{α|f(s,Z^{t,x}(s))|^2}<∞ for all t≥0, s∈[t,t+1], x∈H, where Z^{t,x} is the OU process started from x at t; and (A3) there exist β>1 and functions v_1,v_2:H→R_+, bounded on bounded sets, such that E exp{β∫_t^{t+1}|f(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds}≤v_1(x)v_2(y) for every t≥0 and x,y∈H, where Zhat is the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1. Let D:={y∈H:∑_n δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|<∞}. Then for every bounded set B⊂H, P(t,x,t+1,·) is absolutely continuous with respect to Q(1,x,·) for every t≥0 and x∈H, and there exists a bounded measurable h:H→R_+, depending on B, such that h(y)>0 for y∈D and inf{dP(t,x,t+1,·)/dQ(1,x,·)(y):x∈B,t≥0}≥h(y) for γ-almost every y∈H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The absolute continuity is obtained from Girsanov's theorem and the integrability hypotheses. Lemma 3.2 gives an explicit conditional density formula on D: dP/dQ(y)=E exp{φ(t,x,y)}, where φ is the L^1-limit of sums involving the drift coordinates along the pinned OU bridge. Lemma 3.1 supplies exponential estimates and uniform integrability for the terms in φ. In the final proof, for all m the stochastic-exponential part satisfies E|∑_{n≤m}∫ f_n d w_n−(1/2)∑_{n≤m}∫|f_n|^2ds|≤2E exp{∫|f(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds}≤2v_1(x)v_2(y), using (A3). The deterministic endpoint and bridge-noise correction terms are controlled by Lemma 3.1(ii),(iii). For x in a fixed bounded B, v_1(x) is uniformly bounded, giving E|φ(t,x,y)|≤c_B[1+v_2(y)+∑_n(δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|+δ_ne^{−λ_n})], which is finite for y∈D. Jensen's inequality then yields E exp{φ(t,x,y)}≥exp{Eφ(t,x,y)}≥exp{−E|φ(t,x,y)|}. Defining h(y)=exp{−g(y)} on D, with g the displayed upper bound, and h(y)=0 outside D, gives a bounded measurable lower bound that is strictly positive on D.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3 (uniform Cameron-Martin lower bound for OU kernels from bounded sets)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting where A is self-adjoint negative with eigenvalues −λ_n and ∑_n λ_n^{-1}<∞, let S(t) be the semigroup generated by A. Let Q(1,x,·) be the law at time 1 of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process dZ_r=AZ_rdr+dW_r started from x, and let γ:=Q(1,0,·). For every bounded set B⊂H there exist constants κ_1,κ_2>0 such that dQ(1,x,·)/dγ(y)≥κ_1 exp{−κ_2|y|} for every x∈B and γ-almost every y∈H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The OU laws are Gaussian: Q(1,x,·)=N(S(1)x,Q_1) and γ=N(0,Q_1), where Q_1=∫_0^1S(2r)dr and Q_1e_n=(1−e^{−2λ_n})(2λ_n)^{-1}e_n. Range(S(1)) is contained in Range(Q_1), and the closed graph theorem gives Q_1^{-1}S(1)∈L(H). The Cameron-Martin formula yields dQ(1,x,·)/dγ(y)=exp{⟨Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x,Q_1^{-1/2}y⟩−(1/2)|Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x|^2}. This is bounded below by exp{−|Q_1^{-1}S(1)x||y|−(1/2)|Q_1^{-1/2}S(1)x|^2}. Since x ranges over a bounded set, the two coefficients are bounded uniformly, producing κ_1 and κ_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.4 (dissipativity gives moment bounds, tightness, and entrance into a ball)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X solve dX_t=[AX_t+f(t,X_t)]dt+dW_t in a separable Hilbert space H under the Section 2 assumptions on A and W. Assume f satisfies (A4): for every x∈H, t↦f(t,x) is continuous, and for every r>0 there is K_r>0 such that |f(t,x)−f(t,y)|≤K_r|x−y| for t∈[0,r] and x,y in the open ball B_r={z∈H:|z|<r}. Assume also that there are measurable functions ω:R_+→R_+ and a:R_+^2→R_+, with a bounded on bounded sets and a(t,·) increasing, such that ⟨Ax+f(t,x+y),x⟩≤−ω(t)|x|^2+a(t,|y|)|x| for all t≥0, x∈D(A), y∈H; that inf_{σ≥0}∫_σ^{σ+T}ω(λ)dλ→∞ as T→∞; and that M:=sup_{t≥s≥0}∫_s^t exp{−∫_r^tω(λ)dλ} E a(r,|φ(r)|)dr<∞, where φ(r):=∫_s^rS(r−u)dW_u is the stochastic convolution. Put m:=sup_{t≥s≥0}E|∫_s^tS(t−r)dW_r|, which is finite under ∑λ_n^{-1}<∞. Then (i) E|X^{s,x}(t)|≤m+|x|exp{−∫_s^tω(λ)dλ}+M for every 0≤s≤t and x∈H. Moreover, (ii) for every ε>0 and R_1>0 there is R_0>0 such that P(σ,x,t,H∖B_{R_0})<ε for all x∈B_{R_1} and 0≤σ≤t, and (iii) for any beta_tilde,L>0 satisfying (1−beta_tilde)L>m+M and every R_2>0 there is T>0 such that P(σ,x,σ+t,B_L)≥beta_tilde for all x∈B_{R_2}, σ≥0, and t≥T.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write the mild solution as X(t)=v(t,s,x,φ)+φ(t), where φ is the stochastic convolution and v solves the deterministic random integral equation v(t)=S(t−s)x+∫_s^tS(t−r)f(r,v(r)+ψ(r))dr for a fixed path ψ. Approximate v by strong solutions v_n using the resolvent R(n)=n(nI−A)^{-1}: v_n(t)=R(n)S(t−s)x+∫_s^tR(n)S(t−r)f(r,v(r)+ψ(r))dr. Then v_n→v uniformly on compact intervals and the residual δ_n(t):=dv_n/dt−Av_n−f(t,v_n+ψ) tends uniformly to zero. Differentiating |v_n(t)| and using the dissipativity inequality gives the left-derivative bound d^-|v_n(t)|/dt≤−ω(t)|v_n(t)|+a(t,|ψ(t)|)+|δ_n(t)|. Gronwall's inequality and the limit n→∞ yield |v(t)|≤|x|e^{−∫_s^tω}+∫_s^t e^{−∫_r^tω}a(r,|ψ(r)|)dr. Taking ψ=φ and expectations gives (i), including the stochastic convolution contribution m. Chebyshev's inequality gives the tightness statement (ii). For (iii), choose T so that the exponentially damped initial term is uniformly small for |x|≤R_2, using inf_σ∫_σ^{σ+T}ω→∞; then Chebyshev gives probability at least beta_tilde of being inside B_L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.5 (linear growth with small slope implies the dissipativity hypotheses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert-space setting of Proposition 2.4, suppose f satisfies (A4) and the linear growth bound |f(t,x)|≤k_1+k_2|x| for all (t,x)∈R_+×H, with k_1,k_2>0 and k_2<λ_0, where λ_0 is the lower bound in Ae_n=−λ_ne_n, λ_n≥λ_0. Then the dissipativity assumptions (2.16)-(2.18) of Proposition 2.4 hold, and therefore the moment, tightness, and ball-entrance conclusions of Proposition 2.4 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since ⟨Ax,x⟩≤−λ_0|x|^2 for x∈D(A), the growth bound gives ⟨Ax+f(t,x+y),x⟩≤−λ_0|x|^2+(k_1+k_2|x+y|)|x|≤(−λ_0+k_2)|x|^2+(k_1+k_2|y|)|x|. Thus Proposition 2.4 applies with constant ω=λ_0−k_2>0 and a(t,θ)=k_1+k_2θ. The remaining integrability condition (2.18) follows from the finiteness of m and the exponential damping produced by the positive constant ω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.6 (main Hilbert-space l-condition, total variation convergence, and invariant measure uniqueness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a separable Hilbert space, A self-adjoint negative with eigenbasis {e_n}, Ae_n=−λ_ne_n, λ_n≥λ_0>0, and ∑λ_n^{-1}<∞, and W a cylindrical Wiener process. Let X solve dX_t=[AX_t+f(t,X_t)]dt+dW_t, and let P^*_{s,t} be the adjoint transition operators on probability measures on H. Define δ_n:=sup{|⟨f(t,x),e_n⟩|:t≥0,x∈H}, γ:=Q(1,0,·) for the OU transition Q, and D:={y∈H:∑δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|<∞}. Assume f satisfies (A1) δ_n<∞ and ∑δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞; (A2) the one-point exponential integrability condition along OU paths; (A3) the bridge exponential integrability condition with some β>1 and v_1,v_2 bounded on bounded sets; (A4) local time-continuity and local Lipschitz continuity in x on bounded sets; the dissipativity and recurrence hypotheses (2.16)-(2.18) from Proposition 2.4; and γ(D)>0. Then for all s≥0 and all probability measures ν_1,ν_2 on H, ∥P^*_{s,t}ν_1−P^*_{s,t}ν_2∥→0 as t→∞. If f(t,x)=f(x) is autonomous, then the equation has a unique invariant probability measure μ* and ∥P^*_tν−μ*∥→0 for every probability ν on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose L>m+M, where m and M are the constants in Proposition 2.4, and choose b>0 with (1−b)L>m+M. Proposition 2.4(iii) gives the recurrence part of Proposition 1.7 with B(s)=B_L and β(s)=b, uniformly in s. For the one-step minorization, take x∈B_L and Γ∈B(H). Proposition 2.2 gives P(t,x,t+1,Γ)≥∫_Γh(y)Q(1,x,dy), where h does not depend on x∈B_L or t and h>0 on D. Proposition 2.3 gives Q(1,x,dy)≥κ_1e^{−κ_2|y|}γ(dy) uniformly over x∈B_L. Hence P(t,x,t+1,Γ)≥μhat(Γ), where μhat(Γ):=κ_1∫_Γh(y)e^{−κ_2|y|}γ(dy). Because γ(D)>0 and h>0 on D, μhat(H)>0. Thus all assumptions of Proposition 1.7 are satisfied with a lower measure independent of s. Theorem 1.4 then gives total variation convergence of all evolved probabilities. In the autonomous case, Theorem 1.4(b) yields existence of an invariant probability measure, uniqueness, and convergence to it. The paper also notes that since D is a Borel linear subspace of H, the Kallianpur zero-one law implies γ(D)=1 whenever γ(D)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.7 (bounded drift version of Theorem 2.6)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Theorem 2.6, assume |f| is bounded, f satisfies (A4), and with δ_n:=sup{|⟨f(t,x),e_n⟩|:t≥0,x∈H} one has ∑δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞ and γ({y∈H:∑δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|<∞})>0. Then all assumptions of Theorem 2.6 are satisfied, and hence its total variation convergence and autonomous invariant-measure conclusions hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The two displayed assumptions are exactly (A1) and γ(D)>0. If |f| is bounded, then the OU-path and OU-bridge exponential integrability assumptions (A2) and (A3) are immediate. Boundedness also gives the linear-growth condition of Corollary 2.5 with k_2=0, so the dissipativity assumptions (2.16)-(2.18) hold. Therefore Theorem 2.6 applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.8 (three concrete sufficient conditions for the coordinate summability assumptions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert setting of Theorem 2.6, suppose f satisfies (A4) and |f| is bounded. Let δ_n:=sup{|⟨f(t,x),e_n⟩|:t≥0,x∈H}. If at least one of the following holds, then the conclusions of Theorem 2.6 hold: (i) ∑_nδ_n^2<∞; (ii) f maps R_+×H boundedly into the fractional domain D_A^α of (−A)^α for some α>0 such that (−A)^{−α} is Hilbert-Schmidt; or (iii) ∑_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 2.7 it suffices to verify ∑δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞ and γ(D)>0. Under (i), Cauchy-Schwarz and ∑λ_n^{-1}<∞ give ∑δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞, while ∑δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|≤|y|(∑δ_n^2)^{1/2}, so D=H and γ(D)=1. Under (ii), ∑δ_n^2≤sup_{t,x}|(−A)^αf(t,x)|^2∑_n|(−A)^{−α}e_n|^2<∞, so (i) applies. Under (iii), boundedness of f gives sup_nδ_n≤sup|f| and hence ∑δ_nλ_n^{-1/2}<∞. Moreover, for y∈D_A^{1/4}, ∑δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|≤sup|f|(∑λ_n^{-1/2})^{1/2}|(−A)^{1/4}y|, so D contains D_A^{1/4}; the paper states that (iii) implies γ(D_A^{1/4})=1, hence γ(D)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.9 (quantitative choice of minorization time for Theorem 1.5)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Theorem 2.6, Proposition 2.4(i) and the lower measure μhat constructed in the proof of Theorem 2.6 can be combined with Theorem 1.5 to estimate convergence speed. The paper records the bound P(σ,x,t,H∖B_R)≤R^{-1}(m+|x|+M) for 0≤σ≤t, x∈H, R>0. Given ν_1,ν_2∈P and ε>0, it states that one can choose R>m+M such that P^*_{σ,t}ν_i(H∖B_R)<ε for i=1,2 and all 0≤σ≤t. For any probability μ supported in B_R, σ≥0, τ≥1, and Γ∈B(H), P^*_{σ,σ+τ}μ(Γ)≥inf{P(σ+τ−1,x,σ+τ,Γ):x∈B_R} P^*_{σ,σ+τ−1}μ(B_R). Tracing Theorem 2.6 with B_R gives the first factor at least μhat(Γ), and choosing beta_tilde<1−R^{-1}(m+M) gives P^*_{σ,σ+τ−1}μ(B_R)≥beta_tilde for τ=1+T, where T is chosen so that inf_{σ≥0}∫_σ^{σ+T}ω(λ)dλ>−log[1−beta_tilde−R^{-1}(m+M)]. Thus ∥(P^*_{σ,σ+τ}μ−beta_tilde μhat)^-∥=0 for all σ≥0 and all μ supported in B_R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The estimate is obtained by inserting a last one-step transition into P^*_{σ,σ+τ}μ and forcing the process to be in B_R at time σ+τ−1. The last step is minorized uniformly by the measure μhat from the proof of Theorem 2.6. The probability of being in B_R at the penultimate time is bounded below using the moment estimate in Proposition 2.4(i) and Chebyshev's inequality, with T chosen large enough to damp the initial condition.
