This verifies condition (ii) of Theorem 1.5 with an explicit τ, while the first displayed tail estimate supplies condition (i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1 (exponential estimates for terms in the pinned-OU density)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the Hilbert setting and hypotheses (A1)-(A3) of Proposition 2.2. Let f_n(s,x):=⟨f(s,x),e_n⟩. Fix t≥0, x,y∈H, and let Zhat be the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1 with coordinates Zhat_n and Gaussian fluctuation terms Y_n. Define a_n(s,x_n,y_n):=2λ_ne^{−λ_n(t+1−s)}(1−e^{−2λ_n})^{-1}(y_n−e^{−λ_n}x_n). Then: (i) there exist k>1 and c_k>0 such that for every m∈N and t≥0, E exp{k(∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))dw_n(s)−(1/2)∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds)}≤c_kv_1(x)v_2(y). (ii) For every x∈H, y∈D, k>0, and m∈N, E exp{k|∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))a_n(s,x_n,y_n)ds|}≤exp{2k∑_{n=1}^∞δ_n|y_n−e^{−λ_n}x_n|/(1−e^{−2λ_n})}<∞. (iii) For every k>0 there exists c'_k>0, depending only on k, such that E exp{k∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}δ_n[2λ_ne^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)}/(1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)})]|Y_n(s)|ds}≤c'_k for every m∈N and t≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says (i) follows from (A3) by the same argument as a standard exponential martingale estimate in Friedman, Stochastic Differential Equations and Applications, Chapter 7, Section 1. Part (ii) is a direct deterministic bound: use |f_n|≤δ_n and integrate the explicit coefficient a_n over s to get an upper bound by 2∑_{n≤m}δ_n|y_n−e^{−λ_n}x_n|/(1−e^{−2λ_n}); finiteness follows from y∈D and (A1). Part (iii) is stated to be completely analogous to Lemma 4.1 in Simão [33], using the explicit Gaussian representation of Y_n and the summability in (A1) to obtain a bound uniform in m and t.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2 (Girsanov formula conditioned on the OU endpoint)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume the Hilbert setting and hypotheses (A1)-(A3) of Proposition 2.2. For t≥0, x∈H, and y∈D, define φ(t,x,y) as the L^1-limit as m→∞ of ∑_{n=1}^mφ_n(t,x,y), where φ_n(t,x,y)=∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))dw_n(s)−(1/2)∫_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Zhat(s))|^2ds+∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))a_n(s,x_n,y_n)ds−2∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Zhat(s))λ_ne^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)}(1−e^{−2λ_n(t+1−s)})^{-1}Y_n(s)ds. Here Zhat is the OU bridge from x at t to y at t+1, Y_n is its fluctuation term, and a_n is as in Lemma 3.1. Then for every t≥0 and x∈H, dP(t,x,t+1,·)/dQ(1,x,·)(y)=E exp{φ(t,x,y)} for γ-almost every y∈D.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Girsanov's theorem gives the density of the nonlinear transition law with respect to the OU transition law as E(ρ_t | Z^{t,x}(t+1)=y), where ρ_t is the L^1-limit of finite-dimensional exponential martingales ρ_t^m=exp{∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}f_n(s,Z^{t,x}(s))dw_n(s)−(1/2)∑_{n=1}^m∫_t^{t+1}|f_n(s,Z^{t,x}(s))|^2ds}. Conditional expectations of ρ_t^m converge in L^1(Q(1,x,·)) and along a subsequence almost everywhere; Q(1,x,·) is equivalent to γ because the OU semigroup is strongly Feller in this setting. Conditioning the OU process on Z(t+1)=y replaces it by the pinned process Zhat. The stochastic differential equation for Zhat_n introduces extra drift terms; substituting it into the stochastic integral yields exactly the four terms φ_n. Lemma 3.1 gives uniform integrability of exp{∑_{n≤m}φ_n} and convergence to exp{φ}, so the finite-dimensional conditional density formula passes to the infinite-dimensional limit.

proof_techniques.name: Lower-measure l-condition for total variation merging
proof_techniques.description: The central abstract technique is to find a nonzero finite measure μ_s such that, for every initial probability ν, the negative part of S_{s,t}ν−μ_s vanishes in total variation as t→∞. This says that all sufficiently late transition laws contain essentially the same common piece of mass. Once such a common piece exists uniformly with positive mass, the Lasota-Yorke argument forces the total variation distance between any two evolved probability measures to go to zero. In the homogeneous case this yields a unique attracting invariant probability measure.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, it would be enough to prove an analogue on X=C_0([0,1]): every pair of transition laws eventually shares a common nonzero submeasure. This can bypass a direct strong Feller proof for the singular δ0 drift.

proof_techniques.name: Recurrence plus local one-step minorization
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 1.7 decomposes the l-condition into two verifiable ingredients: a recurrence or entrance estimate showing that from any starting point the process reaches a fixed set B with probability at least β at all sufficiently large times, and a uniform one-step minorization P(t,x,t+1,·)≥μhat(·) for all x∈B and all relevant t. The product β μhat is then a lower measure. This is a Harris-type small set argument formulated in the language of lower measures and total variation convergence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the δ0 SPDE, one can try to show that solutions enter a bounded subset of C_0([0,1]) or an L^2-ball with positive probability, and that from that bounded set the one-step transition kernels dominate a common measure. If successful, uniqueness of invariant measures follows immediately from Theorem 1.4.

proof_techniques.name: Girsanov comparison with a Gaussian reference process
proof_techniques.description: For additive-noise semilinear equations, the paper compares the nonlinear transition law P(t,x,t+1,·) to the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck transition law Q(1,x,·). Girsanov's theorem expresses the Radon-Nikodym derivative as the conditional expectation of an exponential martingale given the endpoint of the OU process. Conditioning on the endpoint turns the OU path into a pinned OU bridge; the bridge representation allows the density to be written as E exp{φ(t,x,y)}. Exponential estimates and Jensen's inequality then produce a strictly positive lower bound on this density on a Gaussian-positive set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For regularized drifts G_{1/n}(u), this suggests proving uniform-in-n lower bounds of transition densities relative to the stochastic heat equation without drift, then passing to the δ0 limit if the convergence of transition laws is strong enough on suitable test sets. The singular drift makes direct Girsanov delicate, but the approximation framework in the problem may make this route viable.

proof_techniques.name: Pinned Ornstein-Uhlenbeck bridge calculations
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses an explicit coordinate formula for the OU bridge between endpoints x and y over a unit time interval. This formula separates deterministic endpoint interpolation terms from Gaussian fluctuation terms Y_n. In the density proof, the bridge stochastic differential equation produces endpoint correction terms and singular-looking coefficients near the terminal time; Lemma 3.1 controls these terms using coordinate summability of the drift and Gaussian estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the one-dimensional Dirichlet stochastic heat equation, the OU bridge of the linear stochastic heat equation has an analogous spectral representation using sine eigenfunctions. This could be used to analyze endpoint-conditioned versions of approximating equations and to build a common lower bound for transition probabilities.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform Cameron-Martin lower bound from bounded initial sets
proof_techniques.description: After obtaining a lower bound for dP/dQ, the paper still needs a common reference measure independent of the initial point x in a bounded set. Proposition 2.3 supplies this by comparing Q(1,x,·) to γ=Q(1,0,·). The Cameron-Martin formula shows that the Gaussian density is bounded below by κ_1e^{−κ_2|y|} uniformly over x in a bounded set.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirichlet heat semigroup on [0,1], the same Gaussian-shift comparison should be available in an L^2 formulation. It can convert a lower bound relative to the linear transition from u_0 into a lower bound relative to a fixed Gaussian law.

proof_techniques.name: Dissipativity and stochastic convolution estimates for recurrence
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.4 proves recurrence estimates by writing the solution as a stochastic convolution plus a deterministic equation driven by the convolution. A dissipativity inequality for ⟨Ax+f(t,x+y),x⟩ gives a Gronwall bound on the deterministic part, while the stochastic convolution has uniformly bounded first moment because A^{-1} is nuclear. Chebyshev's inequality then gives boundedness in probability and positive-probability entrance into a fixed ball.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, the heat operator with Dirichlet boundary is dissipative and the drift δ0 is nonnegative but singular. One possible task is to derive an analogue of the moment or Lyapunov estimate for the ABLM24 mild solution, perhaps first for G_{1/n} approximations and then in the limit.

proof_techniques.name: Quantitative convergence by local l-condition
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 1.5 refines the lower-measure method by allowing a small tail error ε outside a set B and a local lower-measure condition only for measures supported in B. The proof normalizes restrictions to B, contracts their signed difference using the common lower measure, and carries the tail error through an induction. This gives an explicit geometric term plus an ε-dependent residual.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one can estimate the probability that the δ0 SPDE lies outside a good bounded set and obtain a local minorization time τ, Theorem 1.5 gives not only uniqueness but a route to quantitative mixing estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-dimensional truncation and uniform integrability of exponential martingales
proof_techniques.description: The infinite-dimensional Girsanov density is obtained by truncating to the first m eigen-directions, proving estimates uniformly in m, and passing to an L^1 limit. Lemma 3.1 provides uniform exponential bounds for the stochastic exponential and the pinned-bridge correction terms, ensuring uniform integrability. This avoids formal manipulation of infinite sums of stochastic integrals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For approximating the singular drift, a similar spectral truncation strategy may help justify density comparisons or lower bounds at finite dimension before taking limits in both truncation and drift regularization.

other_useful_info: Remark 2.1 shows that assumption (A1) can be quite restrictive for Nemytskii drifts f(x)(ξ)=F(x(ξ)) in H=L^2(D): under a mild condition on a test function g, (A1) forces F to be bounded. This warns that the Hilbert-space theorem does not automatically cover rough pointwise drifts, and certainly not the distributional δ0 drift, without additional approximation or smoothing arguments. The paper notes that D={y:∑δ_n|⟨y,e_n⟩|<∞} is a Borel linear subspace, so γ(D)>0 implies γ(D)=1 by Kallianpur's zero-one law. The examples indicate where the Section 2 theorem is naturally applicable: fourth-order parabolic equations with bounded Nemytskii drift work because λ_n grows like n^4 and ∑λ_n^{-1/2}<∞, while second-order heat equations require smoothing in the drift, such as an integral kernel mapping into H^1, to satisfy the coordinate summability assumptions. The paper cites Da Prato-Elworthy-Zabczyk, Maslowski, Chojnowska-Michalik-Goldys, Peszat-Zabczyk, and others for strong Feller and irreducibility approaches, but its lower-measure method is presented as an alternative route to ergodicity and mixing.
why_relevant: The lower-measure method gives an alternative route to uniqueness and mixing of invariant measures for stochastic semilinear equations. This is useful if the δ0 drift obstructs a clean strong Feller proof.

title: Ergodic properties of recurrent solutions of stochastic evolution equations
overall_summary: Maslowski and Seidler prove recurrence-based ergodic and uniqueness results for semilinear stochastic evolution equations dX = (AX + f(X))dt + dW in a Hilbert space H, under hypotheses ensuring that solutions actually evolve continuously in a smaller Banach space E compactly embedded into H, and that the transition semigroup is strong Feller and irreducible. The central method is Khasminskii's embedded-cycle construction: choose a bounded recurrent set, build a Markov chain from successive returns to its boundary after excursions, prove the chain is strong Feller and irreducible, and use expected occupation measures over one cycle to construct invariant measures for the continuous-time process. The main dichotomy is that, under assumptions A1-A6, a recurrent process has a unique σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar, while a nonrecurrent process has no finite invariant measure; hence in this framework there is at most one invariant probability measure. The paper is useful for the original problem because it gives a route to uniqueness of invariant measures that can avoid directly identifying the invariant measure: verify an appropriate strong Feller property, support/irreducibility, compact smoothing, and recurrence or use the recurrent/nonrecurrent dichotomy. Its Example 3.10 is especially relevant as it checks the abstract assumptions for one-dimensional stochastic reaction-diffusion equations with Dirichlet boundary conditions and space-time white noise, though only for globally Lipschitz drift.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Standing framework and assumptions A1-A6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper studies the semilinear stochastic evolution equation dX(t) = (AX(t) + f(X(t)))dt + dW_t, X(0)=x, in a real separable Hilbert space H. Here A generates a strongly continuous semigroup S(t) on H; W is a cylindrical Wiener process with bounded self-adjoint strictly positive covariance operator Q; f maps a Banach space E into H; and E is a separable Banach space compactly embedded into H. Assumption A1 is ∫_0^T |S(t)Q^{1/2}|_{HS}^2 dt < ∞ for some T>0. Assumption A2 is that S restricted to E is a strongly continuous semigroup on E and the stochastic convolution Z(t)=∫_0^t S(t-r)dW_r has an E-valued E-continuous modification with the usual Markov independent-increment property Z(t+r)-S(r)Z(t). Assumption A3 says f:E→H is H-Lipschitz on bounded subsets of E and the deterministic mild equation u(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(u(r))dr+Φ(t), with Φ∈C_0([0,T],E), has a unique solution u in C((0,T],H)∩C((0,T],E), and in C([0,T],E) if x∈E. Assumption A4 is a uniform nonexplosion and continuity condition for this deterministic solution map: on bounded E-balls and bounded driving paths it remains uniformly bounded on bounded time intervals, and u(.,x_n,Φ_n)→u(.,x,Φ) in C_0([0,T],E) whenever x_n→x in E and Φ_n→Φ. Assumption A5 is H-strong Feller: for every t>0 and Borel A⊂H, P(t,x_n,A)→P(t,x,A) whenever x_n→x in H. Assumption A6 is full support of the stochastic convolution: for every T>0, the Gaussian law of Z on C_0([0,T],E) has support equal to all of C_0([0,T],E).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the paper's abstract framework rather than a theorem. The assumptions separate the roles needed later: A1-A3 give existence of mild solutions as X(t)=u(t,x,Z); A4 gives deterministic continuity and small-time control; A5 is used to pass continuity to hitting probabilities and embedded chains; A6 is used to steer the system along deterministic paths and obtain irreducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A3 in the standing framework. Then there exists a unique mild solution X to dX=(AX+f(X))dt+dW, meaning an H-continuous adapted process satisfying X(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(X(r))dr+Z(t). Moreover, the family of solutions with initial states x∈E defines a homogeneous Markov process on E, and therefore also on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The stochastic convolution Z is E-valued and E-continuous by A2. Given a realization of Z, A3 gives a unique deterministic mild solution u(.,x,Z) to the integral equation, so define X(t)=u(t,x,Z). The nonanticipativity and independent-increment property in A2 imply the Markov property. The result is cited from Zabczyk's theorem on such pathwise mild formulations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:E→H be globally Lipschitz. Suppose S(t) maps H boundedly into E for every t>0 and, for each T>0, there are c>0 and α∈(0,1) such that ||S(t)||_{L(H,E)} ≤ c t^{-α} for t∈(0,T). Then the deterministic well-posedness condition A3b and the uniform boundedness and continuous-dependence condition A4 hold.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For fixed x∈H and driving path Φ∈C_0([0,T],E), solve the deterministic mild equation by a contraction argument in the weighted Banach space of E-valued functions on (0,T] with norm sup_{0<t≤T} t^α ||Y(t)||. The map A(Y)(t)=S(t)x+∫_0^t S(t-r)f(Y(r))dr+Φ(t) maps this space into itself because ||S(t)||_{H→E}≤ct^{-α} and ∫_0^t (t-r)^{-α}r^{-α}dr has the needed behavior for α<1 after the paper's chosen weighting. Introducing an equivalent exponentially weighted norm sup e^{-bt}t^α||Y(t)|| and taking b large makes A a contraction. Uniform bounds on bounded sets and continuous dependence on x and Φ follow from the generalized Gronwall lemma applied to the inequality ||u(t,x_n,Φ_n)-u(t,x,Φ)|| ≤ K(||x_n-x||+||Φ_n-Φ|| + ∫_0^t (t-r)^{-α}||u(r,x_n,Φ_n)-u(r,x,Φ)||dr). Continuity at t=0 in H, and in E when x∈E, follows because the convolution term tends to zero using the same singular-kernel estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4. Let {e_i} be an orthonormal basis of H contained in D(A), let π_n be projection onto span{e_1,...,e_n}, set A_n=π_nAπ_n and S_n(t)=exp(tA_n). Define Q_t=∫_0^t S(r)QS*(r)dr, Q_{t,n}=∫_0^t S_n(r)π_nQπ_nS_n*(r)dr, B_t=Q_t^{-1/2}S(t), and B_{t,n}=Q_{t,n}^{-1/2}S_n(t)π_n. If the basis can be chosen so that for every T>0, sup_{0≤t≤T}|S_n(t)π_nx-S(t)x|→0 for all x∈H, sup_n sup_{0≤t≤T}|S_n(t)|_{L(H)}<∞, and sup_n |B_{t,n}|_{L(H)}+|B_t|_{L(H)}≤C t^{-β} for t∈(0,T] with some β∈(0,1), then the transition probabilities of X satisfy A5, the strong Feller property in H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: When f is extendable to a bounded globally Lipschitz map on H, the result follows from Da Prato and Zabczyk's smoothing estimates for the backward Kolmogorov equation associated with the Galerkin approximations. These estimates yield a total variation Lipschitz bound Var(P(t,x,.)-P(t,y,.))≤C_t|x-y|, with the singularity controlled by t^{-β}. For a merely locally Lipschitz f on bounded E-sets satisfying A3-A4, the paper indicates using truncation: replace f by bounded globally Lipschitz approximations agreeing with f on large E-balls, apply the estimate, and pass to the original equation using the nonexplosion and continuity controls.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A2. Define the deterministic control operator J:L^2(0,T;H)→C_0([0,T],H) by Jγ(t)=∫_0^t S(t-r)Q^{1/2}γ(r)dr. If either D1 holds, namely Q^{1/2}∈L(H,E) and the range of Q^{1/2} is dense in E, or D2 holds, namely S(t)∈L(H,E) for t>0 and ||S(t)||_{L(H,E)}≤h(t) on (0,T) for some h∈L^2(0,T), then A6 holds: the law of the stochastic convolution Z on C_0([0,T],E) has full support for every T>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The reproducing kernel Hilbert space of the Gaussian law of Z on C_0([0,T],E) is the range of J, equipped with the infimum L^2-control norm. Therefore full support is equivalent to density of the range of J in C_0([0,T],E). The paper notes that each of D1 and D2 implies the needed density and continuity properties of J: D1 because instantaneous Q^{1/2}-directions are dense in E, and D2 because smoothing by S(t) places controlled paths in E with L^2 time integrability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4 and A6. Let P(t,x,.) be the transition probability of the mild solution X. Then P is irreducible in E: for every x∈H, every t>0, and every nonempty open set U⊂E, one has P(t,x,U)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First take x∈E and a ball U={v∈E:||v-z||<r}. Construct a deterministic E-continuous path Φ on [0,t] joining x to z, and define the control-like path ξ(s)=Φ(s)-S(s)x-∫_0^s S(s-u)f(Φ(u))du. By A4b, if an E-valued driving path e is close enough to ξ in C_0([0,t],E), then the deterministic solution u(t,x,e) lies within r of z. A6 gives P(||Z-ξ||<ε)>0, hence P(t,x,U)>0. For general x∈H, use that by A3 the solution is in E at every positive time, so for any 0<τ<t, P(t,x,U)=∫_E P(t-τ,y,U)P(τ,x,dy)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider dX(t)=(AX(t)+g(X(t)))dt+dW_t in H, where g:H→H is globally Lipschitz, and assume A1. Let v∈C^{1,2}((0,T)×H) be such that (v_x(t,x),Ax), initially for x∈D(A), extends to a continuous function h on [0,T]×H; v_{xx}(t,x) is nuclear; and for some k,p>0, |v_t(t,x)|+|v(t,x)|+|v_x(t,x)|+|v_{xx}(t,x)|_1+h(t,x)≤k(1+|x|^p). Then for 0<s<t<T, v(t,X(t))-v(s,X(s)) equals ∫_s^t [v_t(u,X(u))+h(u,X(u))+(g(X(u)),v_x(u,X(u)))+0.5 Tr(v_{xx}(u,X(u))Q)]du + ∫_s^t (v_x(u,X(u)),dW_u).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is an infinite-dimensional Itô formula adapted to possibly non-nuclear driving covariance Q. The usual trace term is still meaningful because v_{xx}(t,x) is nuclear. The polynomial growth bounds give integrability. The paper cites Ichikawa's Itô formula, noting that the non-nuclearity of Q is compensated by nuclearity of v_{xx}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 2.1, suppose v∈C^{1,2}(R_+×H) satisfies the same hypotheses on every finite interval. Let U be an open domain in H and x∈U. Assume v>0 on R_+×U and that Lv(s,y)≤-a(s) for (s,y)∈(0,T)×(D(A)∩U), where a>0 and b(t)=∫_0^t a(s)ds tends to ∞ as t→∞. Here Lv(s,y)=(Ay,v_x(s,y))+(g(y),v_x(s,y))+v_t(s,y)+0.5 Tr(v_{xx}(s,y)Q). If τ is the exit time of X from U, then P_x(τ<∞)=1 and E_x b(τ)≤v(0,x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Stop X at τ, at the exit time τ_n from the H-ball of radius n, and at deterministic time t. Apply Lemma 2.1 to v at the bounded stopping time min(τ,τ_n,t). The local martingale has zero expectation and the inequality Lv≤-a gives E_x b(min(τ,τ_n,t))≤v(0,x). Let n→∞ and then t→∞, using monotone convergence and Fatou, to get E_x b(τ)≤v(0,x). Since b(t)→∞, τ must be finite almost surely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A3. If U is a bounded open subset of E and τ_U is the exit time of X from U, then sup_{x∈U} E_x τ_U < ∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a globally Lipschitz map g:H→H agreeing with f on U, and choose a bounded open H-domain W containing U. The exit time from U for the original equation is bounded by the exit time from W for the globally Lipschitz equation dX=(AX+g(X))dt+dW. Apply Lemma 2.2 with a one-dimensional polynomial-exponential Lyapunov function built from a vector z∈D(A*) with z≠0. Pick d so that (x,z)+d>0 on W, choose a high polynomial power n so that the second-order noise contribution in the z-direction dominates the drift term on W, choose a positive constant c making the Lyapunov function positive on W, and then choose the exponential parameter small enough to obtain Lv≤-k e^{γt}. Lemma 2.2 then yields a uniform exponential-moment bound for the exit time from W, hence a uniform first-moment bound for τ_U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A4. For every bounded set A⊂E there exists a centered open ball W in E containing A such that lim_{t↓0} sup_{x∈A} P_x(τ_W<t)=0, where τ_W is the exit time of X from W.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose R with A contained in the E-ball U_R. By A4a there is M>R such that the deterministic solution u(t,x,Φ) remains in the E-ball U_M for t∈[0,R] whenever ||x||≤R and ||Φ||_{C([0,R],E)}≤R. The stochastic convolution Z is E-continuous and Z(0)=0, so P(sup_{0≤s≤t}||Z(s)||>R)→0 as t↓0. Since X(t)=u(t,x,Z), taking W=U_M gives the claimed uniform small-time exit estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.5 and recurrence convention
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For A⊂E, τ^A denotes the first hitting time of the E-closure of A by X. If U⊂E is open and nonempty, X is called U-recurrent if P_x(τ^U<∞)=1 for every x∈E. It is called recurrent if it is U-recurrent for every nonempty open U⊂E. Under A1-A3, if X is U-recurrent in this sense, then the same hitting probability identity holds for every initial x∈H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For x∈H and any t>0, A3 gives P(t,x,E)=1. Applying the Markov property at time t gives P_x(τ^U<∞)≥∫_E P_y(τ^U<∞)P(t,x,dy)=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Embedded-cycle Markov chain construction before Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Choose an open E-ball W containing U with the small-time property of Lemma 2.4. Let B be the E-closure of U. Define τ_0 as the first hitting time of B; define σ_1=inf{t>τ_0:X(t)∉W}; define τ_1=inf{t>σ_1:X(t)∈B}; and inductively σ_{n+1}=inf{t>τ_n:X(t)∉W}, τ_{n+1}=inf{t>σ_{n+1}:X(t)∈B}. Then τ_{n-1}<σ_n<τ_n almost surely and Y_n=X(τ_n), n∈N, is a homogeneous Markov chain. Its state space is the E-boundary ∂U=Cl_E(U)∖U after excursions from outside W back to Cl_E(U).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Continuity of paths gives σ_n>τ_{n-1}. Lemma 2.3 gives finiteness of exits from W, and U-recurrence gives finiteness of returns to Cl_E(U). The strong Markov property at the stopping times τ_n and σ_n, together with Maruyama's theorem on stopped strong Markov processes, yields a homogeneous Markov chain.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Let Y_n=X(τ_n) be the embedded Markov chain from the cycle construction, and let p(x,A)=P_x(Y_1∈A) be its transition kernel. Then the chain is strongly Feller in H: for every n∈N and every Borel A⊂H, p^n(.,A) is continuous on H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a bounded measurable φ on H, set Φ(x)=E_x φ(X(τ_n)). Decompose Φ according to whether τ_n≤t or τ_n>t. The part with τ_n≤t is uniformly small as t↓0 by Lemma 2.4 and the construction of the cycles. For the part with τ_n>t, fix 0<u<t and write it as E_x κ_{t,u}(X(u))=∫κ_{t,u}(z)P(u,x,dz), where κ_{t,u}(z)=E_z[φ(X(τ_n))1_{τ_n>t-u}] is bounded and measurable. A5 makes x↦∫κ_{t,u}dP(u,x,.) continuous. Letting t↓0 shows Φ is a uniform limit of continuous functions, hence continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A5 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Then the embedded Markov chain Y_n=X(τ_n) has a stationary distribution μ_hat supported on ∂U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.7 the chain is Feller, indeed strong Feller. Its values lie in ∂U, and the H-closure of ∂U is compact because U is bounded in E and E embeds compactly into H. A Feller Markov chain on a compact state space has an invariant probability measure. Since the chain actually takes values in ∂U, this invariant measure is supported on ∂U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Then X has a σ-finite invariant measure μ on B(H). More explicitly, if μ_hat is an invariant probability measure for the embedded chain on ∂U, and τ(A)=∫_0^{τ_1} 1_A(X(t))dt is the time spent in A during one cycle starting from ∂U, then μ(A)=∫_{∂U} E_x τ(A) dμ_hat(x), A∈B(H), defines a σ-finite invariant measure for X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition gives a measure by monotone convergence. To prove σ-finiteness, it is enough to show μ(A)<∞ for sets A=Cl_H(G) with G bounded in E. Proposition 1.5 gives positive hitting probabilities of U from boundary points, and A5 plus compactness of relevant H-closures upgrades these to positive uniform lower bounds on compact boundary pieces. Strong Markov arguments then yield a time t>0 and s<1 such that sup_{x∈A}P_x(τ(A)>t)≤s, where τ(A) denotes occupation time of A in a cycle. Define the stopping time κ(t)=inf{v>τ_0:∫_{τ_0}^v 1_A(X(u))du=t}; on {τ(A)>t}, X(κ(t))∈A. Iterating the strong Markov property at κ(t) gives sup_{x∈A}P_x(τ(A)>nt)≤s^n, hence sup_{x∈A}E_xτ(A)<∞. This proves finite mass on bounded E-pieces, hence σ-finiteness. Invariance is the standard regenerative-cycle calculation of Khasminskii: the expected occupation measure over one cycle is invariant because the distribution of the chain at successive return times is μ_hat and μ_hat is stationary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. Suppose there exists a bounded open set U⊂E such that, for every bounded set G⊂E, sup_{x∈G}E_x τ^U<∞, where τ^U is the first hitting time of Cl_E(U). Then X has an invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 2.9 constructs a σ-finite invariant measure as an expected occupation measure over cycles. The additional positive-recurrence condition gives finite expected return time to U uniformly on bounded E-sets. Together with Lemma 2.3, this implies that the total mass μ(H) of the occupation measure is finite. Normalizing μ by μ(H) gives an invariant probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U_R-recurrent for the E-ball U_R={x∈E:||x||<R}. Let Y_n be the embedded Markov chain associated with U_R. Then for every n∈N, every x∈∂U_R, and every nonempty set V⊂∂U_R open in the topology induced from E, one has p^n(x,V)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Chapman-Kolmogorov it suffices to prove n=1. Choose y∈V and a time T>0. Let r be so large that the auxiliary ball W used in the cycle construction is contained in U_r. Build a continuous E-path φ on [0,T] such that φ(0)=x, it moves out beyond W, then approaches y, and its final boundary hitting lies in V; explicitly the paper uses points x_1=(2r/R)x and y_1=(2r/R)y and a piecewise linear curve with ||φ(t)||>r on the middle third of the interval. Define ξ(t)=φ(t)-S(t)x-∫_0^t S(t-u)f(φ(u))du. By A4b, any driving path γ close to ξ forces u(.,x,γ) to follow φ closely, hence to realize the desired excursion and return to V. A6 gives positive probability that Z is that close to ξ, so p(x,V)>0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is U-recurrent for a bounded open set U⊂E. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure constructed in Theorem 2.9. If φ,ψ:H→R are μ-integrable Borel functions and ∫ψ dμ≠0, then for every x∈H, P_x[lim_{T→∞} (∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt)/(∫_0^T ψ(X(t))dt) = (∫φ dμ)/(∫ψ dμ)] = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Step 1 proves ergodicity of the embedded chain Y. Lemma 2.7 gives strong Feller and Lemma 3.1 gives irreducibility, so Doob's theorem yields the strong law (1/n)∑_{i=1}^n ξ(Y_i)→∫_{∂U}ξ dμ_hat for μ_hat-a.e. initial point and every μ_hat-integrable ξ; equivalence of p^n(x,.) and μ_hat extends it to all starting boundary points, and also gives p^n(x,A)→μ_hat(A). Step 2 starts X with initial distribution μ_hat on ∂U and considers cycle occupation variables β_i=∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}φ(X(t))dt. These form a stationary ergodic sequence, so n^{-1}∑β_i→∫φ dμ. If k(T) is the number of completed cycles before T, then k(T)^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt→∫φdμ, and the same for ψ; taking the ratio gives the assertion for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point. Step 3 transfers the result to arbitrary x∈H by conditioning on Y_m and using the equivalence of p^m(x,.) with μ_hat.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. If X is U-recurrent for one bounded open set U⊂E, then X is recurrent, meaning it is G-recurrent for every nonempty open set G⊂E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let μ be the invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. For any nonempty bounded open G⊂E, irreducibility from Proposition 1.5 and invariance imply μ(G)>0. Applying the cycle ergodic theorem to 1_G shows that, for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point, the total occupation time of G is infinite, so G is hit almost surely. Equivalence of embedded-chain transition probabilities transfers this hitting property to all x∈H. General nonempty open sets contain bounded nonempty open subsets, so the conclusion follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is recurrent. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. If μ(H)<∞, then μ*=μ/μ(H) is an invariant probability measure and for every x∈H and every μ*-integrable φ, P_x[lim_{T→∞} T^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt = ∫φ dμ*]=1; moreover P(T,x,A)→μ*(A) for every x∈H and every Borel A⊂H. If μ(H)=∞, then for every μ-integrable φ, every x∈H, and every ε>0, P_x[|T^{-1}∫_0^T φ(X(t))dt|>ε]→0; in particular, for every bounded measurable D⊂E, T^{-1}∫_0^T P(t,x,D)dt→0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the finite-mass case, apply Theorem 3.2 with ψ=1 to get the strong law of large numbers. Strong Feller plus irreducibility imply equivalence of P(t,x,.) and μ* for t>0, and Doob's convergence theorem yields P(T,x,A)→μ*(A). In the infinite-mass case, argue by contradiction: if time averages of a nonnegative integrable φ stayed above a fixed level with positive probability along T_n→∞, choose measurable sets M_k with μ(M_k)<∞ and μ(M_k)→∞. The ratio theorem applied to φ and 1_{M_k} contradicts the assumed lower bound for k large. The convergence of averaged transition probabilities for bounded D follows by applying the preceding assertion to φ=1_D and using the σ-finiteness estimate from Theorem 2.9 that μ(D)<∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is not recurrent. Then for every x∈H and every bounded measurable D⊂E, ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,D)dt<∞ and P(t,x,D)→0 as t→∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a bounded open U⊂E containing D that witnesses nonrecurrence. There exists z with positive probability never to hit U. By A5 this nonhitting probability is continuous on E in the needed sense, so there is a nonempty open V⊂E and r>0 such that starting from V the probability of never hitting U is at least r. By relative compactness of U in H, Proposition 1.5 and A5 give a uniform positive lower bound v for hitting V from Cl_E(U) in a fixed time. Strong Markov arguments then show that successive returns to U are dominated by a geometric tail with parameter rv, implying finite expected occupation time of U and hence of D. To prove P(t,x,D)→0, suppose instead P(t_n,x,U_L)>δ for some E-ball U_L and t_n→∞. A4a gives a larger ball U_M such that solutions starting in U_L stay in U_M for times s∈[0,1] when the noise path is small, and A6 gives a positive probability of such a small noise path. Thus P(t_n+s,x,U_M) is bounded below uniformly for s∈[0,1], contradicting the already proved integrability of t↦P(t,x,U_M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6 and suppose X is recurrent. Let μ be the σ-finite invariant measure from Theorem 2.9. Then for every x∈H and every Borel U⊂H with μ(U)>0, ∫_0^∞ P(t,x,U)dt=∞. In fact the proof gives P_x[∫_0^∞1_U(X(t))dt=∞]=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For 0<μ(U)<∞, start X with the embedded-chain invariant distribution μ_hat. The cycle strong law gives n^{-1}∑_{i=0}^{n-1}∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}1_U(X(t))dt→μ(U)>0, so the total occupation time of U is infinite almost surely. This implies the same assertion for μ_hat-a.e. boundary starting point. Equivalence of μ_hat with p^2(x,.) for arbitrary x transfers the almost sure infinite occupation statement to all x∈H. Sets of infinite μ-measure are handled by applying the argument to a positive finite-measure subset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume A1-A6. If X is recurrent, then there exists a unique σ-finite invariant measure for X, up to multiplication by a positive constant. If X is not recurrent, then there is no finite invariant measure. Consequently, within this abstract framework, the Markov process has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the recurrent case, Proposition 3.6 gives the Harris-type infinite-potential condition for all sets of positive μ-measure. Strong Feller and irreducibility imply equivalence of transition probabilities, hence equivalence of resolvents, so the abstract uniqueness theorem in the Appendix applies and gives uniqueness up to scalar. For the nonrecurrent case, Proposition 3.5 gives T^{-1}∫_0^T P(t,x,D)dt→0 for each bounded D⊂E. If ν were a finite invariant measure, dominated convergence would give ν(D)=lim_{T→∞}T^{-1}∫_0^T∫P(t,x,D)dν(x)dt=0 for all bounded D⊂E. Since E is the union of bounded balls, this forces ν=0, contradicting that ν is a finite invariant measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition A.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (E,Sigma) be a measurable space and let P_t(x,.) be Markov transition kernels satisfying measurability in (t,x), Chapman-Kolmogorov, and the usual kernel properties. Define T_t f(x)=∫f(y)P_t(x,dy) on bounded measurable f and the resolvent U(x,A)=∫_0^∞ e^{-t}P_t(x,A)dt. Assume all measures U(x,.) are equivalent. Let μ be a σ-finite invariant measure. Suppose there exists a strictly positive bounded measurable f with ∫f dμ<∞ and ∫_0^∞ T_t f(x)dt=∞ for every x∈E. Then every σ-finite invariant measure is a constant multiple of μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix x_0 and put m=U(x_0,.). By equivalence of resolvents, every σ-finite invariant measure is equivalent to m. Let ρ be another σ-finite invariant measure. Choose a strictly positive bounded h with finite integrals under μ and ρ, and choose c>0 so that ν=cρ satisfies ∫h dν=∫h dμ. Write μ=um and ν=vm with finite densities. Let η=(μ-ν)^+=(u-v)^+m. Minimality of the Jordan decomposition implies T_t^*η≥η. A telescoping estimate using invariance of μ and ν shows that for each fixed t>0, ∫_0^T <T_t^*η-η,T_s f>ds stays bounded uniformly in T. Since T_t^*η-η is nonnegative and ∫_0^∞T_s f(x)ds=∞ at every point, Tonelli forces T_t^*η=η. Thus η is invariant. If η=0, then μ≤ν and the equality of h-integrals gives μ=ν. If η≠0, invariance and resolvent equivalence make η equivalent to m; hence the set {u>v} has full m-measure, contradicting ∫h dμ=∫h dν. Therefore μ=ν, up to the scalar c.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H have orthonormal basis {e_i}, let Ae_i=-α_i e_i with α_i→∞ and α_i>0, and let Qe_i=λ_i e_i with 0<λ_i≤λ_0. Then A1 is equivalent to ∑_i λ_i/α_i<∞. For E_a=D((-A)^a) with graph norm, if ∑_i λ_i α_i^{2ε-1}<∞ for some ε>0, then A2 holds for 0<a<ε. The support assumption A6 holds if either a<1/2 or the sequence α_i^a λ_i^{1/2} is bounded. A strong Feller sufficient condition is that sup_i sqrt(α_i)/sqrt(λ_i(e^{2α_i t}-1)) ≤ c t^{-β} on small t for some β∈(0,1). If f:E_a→H satisfies the local bounded-set Lipschitz condition A3a and the global E_a-Lipschitz bound |f(x)-f(y)|≤C||x-y||_{E_a}, with 0<a<min(1/2,ε), then all assumptions A1-A6 hold and the paper's invariant-measure and ergodic conclusions apply.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The diagonal form makes the Hilbert-Schmidt stochastic convolution condition and smoothing estimates explicit. A1 follows by summing ∫_0^T e^{-2α_i t}λ_i dt. Regularity in E_a is obtained by inserting α_i^{2a} into the same series, yielding the stated stronger summability. The support condition follows from Proposition 1.4, and strong Feller from Proposition 1.3 after computing Q_t^{-1/2}S(t) on basis elements. Proposition 1.2 supplies A3b and A4 for globally E_a-Lipschitz f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the system ∂_t u_i(t,x)=Δu_i(t,x)+F_i(u_1(t,x),...,u_n(t,x))+η_i(t,x), i=1,...,n, on (0,π) with Dirichlet boundary conditions and globally Lipschitz F:R^n→R^n, driven by independent space-time white noises. In the abstract formulation H=(L^2(0,π))^n, A is the Dirichlet Laplacian componentwise, Q=I, and f is the Nemytskii map induced by F. The paper verifies A1-A6 for E=E_a=D((-A)^a) with 0<a<1/4. It also verifies A1-A6 for E=V_δ=[h_0^δ[0,π]]^n, 0<δ<1/4, where h_0^δ is the separable little Hölder space with zero boundary condition. Therefore all main conclusions, including at most one stationary distribution and the recurrence dichotomy, apply to this stochastic reaction-diffusion system.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the sine basis e_{i,j}; the Dirichlet Laplacian eigenvalues grow like i^2 and Q=I. The series ∑_i i^{4ε}∫_0^T e^{-2i^2t}dt is finite for ε<1/4, giving the stochastic convolution regularity needed for E_a. The strong Feller bound follows from the explicit estimate 1/sqrt(e^{2i^2t}-1)≤C t^{-1/2} after optimizing over i. The Nemytskii drift is globally Lipschitz on H because F is globally Lipschitz. For the Hölder choice E=V_δ, the paper uses separability of little Hölder spaces, strong continuity of the heat semigroup on V_δ, smoothing estimates S(t):L^2→C_0^μ with t-singularity integrable for μ<1/4, and Proposition 1.4 to get full support.

proof_techniques.name: Compactly embedded path space to recover compactness
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not work solely in the Hilbert state space H. It assumes solutions immediately live in a separable Banach space E compactly embedded into H and have continuous E-valued paths. This compact embedding is used when bounded sets in E have compact closure in H, which allows the embedded return chain to be treated as a Feller chain on a compact H-state space and guarantees existence of an invariant distribution for that chain.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a Dirichlet stochastic heat equation, one can try to use smoothing to place the process in a compactly embedded subspace such as a little Hölder space or a fractional Sobolev domain. This can replace local compactness of the full infinite-dimensional state space.

proof_techniques.name: Support-control proof of irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: Irreducibility is obtained by deterministic steering. Given initial point x, target open ball around z, and time t, construct a continuous path Φ from x to z and define a forcing path ξ that would make Φ solve the deterministic mild equation. Continuity of the deterministic solution map says that noise paths close to ξ reach the target, and full support of the Gaussian stochastic convolution makes this event have positive probability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For additive space-time white noise with Dirichlet heat kernel, the Gaussian convolution often has full support in suitable path spaces. If the solution map for the singular drift can be approximated or shown continuous in a weak enough topology, this gives a concrete route to topological irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Khasminskii embedded-cycle construction
proof_techniques.description: Starting from a bounded recurrent open set U, the paper chooses a larger ball W and defines alternating stopping times: returns to Cl_E(U) and exits from W. The boundary return points form a Markov chain Y_n. An invariant measure for the continuous-time process is then built as the expected occupation measure during one cycle, averaged over a stationary distribution of Y_n.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful when an invariant measure is hard to identify explicitly. If one can establish recurrence of some bounded set for the SPDE, the cycle construction can produce and analyze invariant measures without solving a stationary equation.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller property transferred to hitting chains
proof_techniques.description: The embedded chain's transition kernel involves random hitting times, not deterministic times. The paper proves it is strong Feller by decomposing expectations according to whether the hitting time occurs before a small deterministic time. The early-hit term is uniformly small by a small-time exit estimate; the remaining term is a deterministic-time semigroup applied to a bounded measurable function and is continuous by the strong Feller property of the original process.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If strong Feller estimates are available for the SPDE or its approximations, this technique can extend them to return-time chains used in recurrence arguments. It avoids differentiating or estimating the hitting distribution directly.

proof_techniques.name: One-cycle occupation measure as invariant measure
proof_techniques.description: Given a stationary law μ_hat for the embedded boundary chain, define μ(A)=∫E_x∫_0^{τ_1}1_A(X(t))dt dμ_hat(x). The regenerative structure implies invariance: after one complete cycle, the boundary distribution is again μ_hat, so time-shifted occupations have the same averaged law. σ-finiteness is proved by showing that occupation times of bounded E-sets have geometric tails, using irreducibility, strong Feller continuity, and compactness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For uniqueness questions, such a constructed measure can serve as the canonical σ-finite invariant measure to compare all others against. The geometric occupation estimate may be adaptable if bounded subsets of the solution space are compact in a weaker topology.

proof_techniques.name: Ratio ergodic theorem via cycle occupations
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves a Hopf-type ratio theorem by applying the strong law of large numbers to the embedded Markov chain and to cycle occupation variables β_i=∫_{τ_i}^{τ_{i+1}}φ(X(t))dt. For two μ-integrable observables φ and ψ, the ratio of their long-time integrals converges almost surely to the ratio of their μ-integrals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Once a σ-finite invariant measure and recurrent cycle structure are available, this gives ergodic consequences without requiring the invariant measure to be finite. It can be used to prove Harris recurrence and uniqueness of invariant probabilities.

proof_techniques.name: Recurrent/nonrecurrent dichotomy for invariant probabilities
proof_techniques.description: Under A1-A6, the paper proves that recurrence implies uniqueness of the σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar, while nonrecurrence implies all bounded E-sets have finite potential and transition probabilities to them vanish; as a consequence, no finite invariant measure can exist in the nonrecurrent case. Thus the existence of any invariant probability forces the recurrent side of the dichotomy, where uniqueness holds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the most direct strategic use for the original problem: to prove at most one invariant probability, it may suffice to verify the paper's structural assumptions rather than prove recurrence separately, because nonrecurrence would rule out invariant probabilities altogether.

proof_techniques.name: Appendix Harris uniqueness through equivalent resolvents
proof_techniques.description: The Appendix gives an abstract uniqueness criterion: if all resolvent measures U(x,.) are equivalent and one invariant σ-finite measure μ satisfies a Harris-type infinite-potential condition ∫_0^∞T_tf(x)dt=∞ for a strictly positive μ-integrable f, then μ is the only σ-finite invariant measure up to scalar. The proof compares two invariant measures by their densities with respect to a common resolvent measure and shows the positive part of their difference would itself be invariant, forcing a contradiction after normalization.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This criterion can be used independently of the full cycle construction. For the SPDE, proving equivalence of transition or resolvent laws plus a Harris recurrence condition for one invariant measure would directly imply uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Lyapunov exit-time estimate from a bounded domain
proof_techniques.description: To control cycles, the paper proves that expected exit times from bounded E-open sets are uniformly finite. The key is to replace the drift by a globally Lipschitz extension on a larger H-domain and apply an infinite-dimensional Itô formula to a Lyapunov function depending on one nondegenerate linear functional (x,z), with a high polynomial power chosen so the noise term forces exit.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For SPDEs with additive nondegenerate noise, analogous one-dimensional projection Lyapunov functions may provide uniform control of excursions from bounded sets, even when the full drift is difficult.

proof_techniques.name: Explicit verification for one-dimensional reaction-diffusion noise
proof_techniques.description: Example 3.10 checks the abstract assumptions for a Dirichlet heat operator on an interval with space-time white noise and globally Lipschitz Nemytskii drift. It uses sine eigenfunctions, heat semigroup smoothing into fractional domains or little Hölder spaces, full support of the stochastic convolution, and strong Feller estimates from the explicit covariance.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original SPDE has the same linear Dirichlet heat operator and additive space-time white noise. The example identifies natural compactly embedded spaces and the regularity thresholds that are likely relevant, though the singular drift requires additional justification beyond the paper.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly emphasizes that infinite-dimensional systems can have multiple stationary distributions or fail strong laws without additional compactness and nondegeneracy assumptions, unlike nonsingular finite-dimensional diffusions. Remark 3.9 shows in a diagonal linear example that if the strong Feller range condition fails, a unique stationary distribution may still exist while the strong law of large numbers fails; this underlines the role of equivalence/strong Feller hypotheses in the uniqueness and ergodicity theory. The cited sources most directly connected to the paper's tools are Da Prato and Zabczyk on smoothing and strong Feller properties, Khasminskii and Harris on recurrence and invariant measures, and Funaki/Manthey-Maslowski for stochastic reaction-diffusion invariant-measure results. For applying the paper to a singular-drift SPDE, the critical hypotheses to establish in the paper's language would be an H-strong Feller property, full support of the stochastic convolution or solution law in a compactly embedded E, and enough path regularity/continuity to run the cycle and irreducibility arguments.
why_relevant: This paper develops recurrence-based ergodic results for stochastic evolution equations. Such methods can help prove uniqueness of invariant measures when direct strong Feller estimates are hard to pass to a singular drift limit.

title: Analytically weak and mild solutions to stochastic heat equation with irregular drift
overall_summary: The paper studies the stochastic heat equation ∂t u = 1/2 ∂xx u + b(u) + space-time white noise when the scalar drift b is a generalized function in a Besov/Hölder-Zygmund space, on D=R or on [0,1] with periodic or Neumann boundary conditions. Its main result is an equivalence theorem: in the regularity regime α > -3/2, weak regularized solutions and mild regularized solutions coincide for processes in the class V(5/8), and for b in Lp all standard analytically weak, analytically mild, weak regularized, and mild regularized notions are equivalent. The core technique is stochastic sewing with random controls, used to obtain robust occupation-type bounds for integrals of singular functions of V+ψ, where V is the stochastic convolution and ψ is a controlled perturbation. The paper is useful for the original invariant-measure problem mainly because it justifies passing between weak and mild formulations and between distributional drifts and smooth drift approximations, assuming the same theory extends to Dirichlet boundary conditions. It does not prove invariant-measure uniqueness or ergodicity, but it supplies the solution-concept and approximation infrastructure needed before such arguments can be made rigorous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Convention 2.1 and stochastic convolution setup
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper fixes one of three spatial settings. Either D=R with heat kernel g_t(x-y) and test functions S(D) equal to the Schwartz space, or D=[0,1] with periodic boundary conditions, periodic heat kernel p_t^per, and smooth periodic test functions, or D=[0,1] with Neumann boundary conditions, Neumann heat kernel p_t^Neu, and smooth test functions satisfying Neumann boundary conditions. The corresponding heat semigroup is denoted P. If W is space-time white noise on [0,T]×D, the stochastic convolution is V_t(x)=∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y) W(dr,dy), for t≥0 and x∈D. The scalar drift b is a distribution in a Besov/Hölder-Zygmund space; C^α denotes B^α_{∞,∞}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is notation and setup rather than a result. The three kernels are explicitly defined or named, and all later definitions and estimates are stated uniformly for the triple (D,p,S(D)). The paper does not include Dirichlet boundary conditions in its formal statements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 2.2 and 2.5: analytically mild and analytically weak solutions for measurable drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings D∈{R, periodic [0,1], Neumann [0,1]}, let P be the corresponding heat semigroup, and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let b:R→R be measurable and u0∈B(D), the bounded measurable functions on D. A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]×D×Ω→R is an analytically mild solution if, for each t∈[0,T] and x∈D, ∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))| dy dr <∞ almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y)) dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely. A jointly continuous adapted process u is an analytically weak solution if, for every test function ϕ∈S(D), almost surely ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨u0,ϕ⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,1/2 Δϕ⟩ ds+∫_0^t ⟨b(u_s),ϕ⟩ ds+W_t(ϕ), for all t∈[0,T], with all integrals well defined.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions. The paper notes that for unbounded b, the full-probability set in the analytically mild formulation may depend on t and x, following the classical random-field definition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.4: mild regularized solution for distributional drift
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three heat-kernel settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let β∈R, q∈[1,∞], b∈B^β_q(R), and u0∈B(D). A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]×D×Ω→R is a mild regularized solution of ∂t u=1/2 Δu+b(u)+white noise if there exists a jointly continuous process K:[0,T]×D×Ω→R such that: (1) almost surely u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+K_t(x)+V_t(x) for all x∈D and t∈(0,T]; and (2) for every sequence b_n∈C∞_b(R) such that b_n→b in B^{β−}_q, meaning sup_n ||b_n||_{B^β_q}<∞ and ||b_n-b||_{B^{β′}_q}→0 for every β′<β, one has, for every N>0, sup_{t∈[0,T]} sup_{x∈D, |x|≤N} |∫_0^t ∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y)) dy dr - K_t(x)| →0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. The paper observes that every Besov distribution f can be approximated in this sense by smooth bounded functions, for example by heat-kernel mollification G_{1/n}f. When b is sufficiently regular and b_n converges uniformly to b, the definition reduces to the usual analytically mild formulation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.6: weak regularized solution for distributional drift
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let S(D) be the corresponding test-function space. Let β∈R, q∈[1,∞], b∈B^β_q(R), and u0∈B(D). A jointly continuous adapted process u:(0,T]×D×Ω→R is a weak regularized solution if there exists a continuous-in-time process K:[0,T]×S(D)×Ω→R such that: (1) for every ϕ∈S(D), almost surely ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨u0,ϕ⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,1/2 Δϕ⟩ ds+K_t(ϕ)+W_t(ϕ), for all t∈[0,T]; and (2) for every b_n∈C∞_b(R) with b_n→b in B^{β−}_q and every ϕ∈S(D), sup_{t∈[0,T]} |∫_0^t ∫_D ϕ(x)b_n(u_r(x)) dx dr - K_t(ϕ)| →0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. The paper notes that if β>0, one may choose b_n converging uniformly to b, and the weak regularized notion agrees with the usual analytically weak solution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.8: the regularity class V(κ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. For κ∈[0,1], an adapted measurable process u:[0,T]×D×Ω→R belongs to V(κ) if for every m≥2, sup_{(t,x)∈(0,T]×D} ||u_t(x)||_{L^m(Ω)}<∞ and sup_{0<s≤t≤T} sup_{x∈D} ||u_t(x)-V_t(x)-P_{t-s}[u_s-V_s](x)||_{L^m(Ω)} / |t-s|^κ <∞. If u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t is a mild regularized solution, the numerator is exactly ||K_t(x)-P_{t-s}K_s(x)||_{L^m}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. Its role is to quantify heat-semigroup time regularity of the drift component of u after subtracting the stochastic convolution.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.10: equivalence of weak and mild regularized solutions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings D=R, periodic [0,1], or Neumann [0,1]. Let α>-3/2, b∈C^α(R), u0∈B(D), and let u:[0,T]×D×Ω→R be a measurable adapted process. Assume u∈V(5/8). Then u is a weak regularized solution of ∂t u=1/2 Δu+b(u)+white noise with initial condition u0 if and only if u is a mild regularized solution of the same equation with initial condition u0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is split into two directions. For weak regularized implies mild regularized, the paper first proves Lemma 4.1: for any controlled perturbation u∈V(τ) and f∈C^γ with γ+4τ>1, the smoothed drift integrals against smooth approximations f_n converge to canonical limiting processes in both mild and weak forms. Applying this to f=b produces a candidate mild drift H^b. Lemmas 4.3 and 4.4 convert an abstract weak identity with a continuous distributional drift term K(ϕ) into a mild-type identity u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+R^K(t,x)+V_t(x), where R^K is obtained as the limit of ∫_0^{t-ε} K(dr,p_{t-r}(x,·)). Lemma 4.5 then identifies R^K with the same limit H^b by comparing ∫_0^t p_{t-r}b_n(u_r) with R^K, using time discretization, the weak regularized approximation property, and stochastic sewing estimates. Since both processes are continuous, equality on rational times and positions extends everywhere. For mild regularized implies weak regularized, Lemma 4.8 starts from u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t and uses a telescoping sum over the time grid i/n to show that for each test function ϕ there is a continuous process R_ϕ satisfying the weak identity and represented as the limit of sums ⟨K_{i/n}-P_{1/n}K_{(i-1)/n},ϕ⟩. Lemma 4.9 then identifies R_ϕ as the limit of ∫ϕ b_n(u) for every approximating sequence b_n→b in C^{α−}, again by time discretization and Proposition 3.4. This proves the two defining conditions of weak regularized solutions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.11: equivalence of four solution notions for Lp drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p≥1, b∈L^p(R), u0∈B(D), and let u:[0,T]×D×Ω→R be a measurable adapted process. Then the following are equivalent: (a) u is an analytically weak solution; (b) u is a weak regularized solution in V(5/8); (c) u is an analytically mild solution; and (d) u is a mild regularized solution in V(5/8). Moreover, if any of these conditions holds, then u∈V(1-1/(4p)), with the natural convention that 1/(4p)=0 when p=∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Theorem 2.10 to identify weak and mild regularized solutions. The main additional step is to prove that any classical analytically weak or mild solution automatically lies in V(1-1/(4p)). For mild-type identities, this is Lemma 4.10, whose proof uses Lemma 4.11: applying the stochastic sewing bound of Lemma 3.3 to f=|b|, ψ=u−V, and the random control w_{s,t}(x)=∫_s^t∫p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr yields an L^m bound of order |t-s|^{1-1/(4p)} for the drift increment; continuity and Fatou extend the estimate from almost every x to every x. For analytically weak solutions, Lemma 4.12 and Corollary 4.13 first show that the mild formulation holds for Lebesgue-a.e. x, after testing against time-dependent heat-evolved test functions and passing to heat-kernel approximations. Once u∈V(5/8), Corollary 3.5 shows that approximating b by smooth bounded b_n in the appropriate negative Besov topology yields convergence of the drift integrals in L^2, so the regularized and classical drift terms coincide. The reverse implications use the same Corollary 3.5 with b_n=G_{1/n}b to identify the regularized drift K with the actual Lebesgue integral involving b.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.12: existence and uniqueness for Lp drifts
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p≥1, b∈L^p(R), and u0∈B(D). Then the stochastic heat equation ∂t u=1/2 Δu+b(u)+white noise has a unique analytically mild solution and a unique analytically weak solution, and these two solutions coincide. The solutions are strong in the probabilistic sense.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes the earlier existence and uniqueness theorem for mild regularized solutions from Athreya-Butkovsky-Lê-Mytnik, cited as [1, Theorem 2.6], which gives a strong probabilistic mild regularized solution and uniqueness in the class V(3/4). By Theorem 2.11, that solution is both analytically mild and analytically weak. Conversely, any analytically mild or analytically weak solution is a mild regularized solution and belongs to V(1-1/(4p))⊂V(3/4), so the cited uniqueness theorem forces all such solutions to coincide.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2: stochastic sewing lemma with random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m∈[2,∞). Let λ be a random control on the simplex Δ_{[0,T]}, meaning λ(s,u)+λ(u,t)≤λ(s,t) almost surely whenever 0≤s≤u≤t≤T. Let A_{s,t} be a two-parameter adapted family and write δA_{s,u,t}=A_{s,t}-A_{s,u}-A_{u,t}. Suppose there are constants Γ1,Γ2,α1,α2,β2≥0 with α1>1/2 and α2+β2>1 such that, for every (s,t) and midpoint u=(s+t)/2, ||δA_{s,u,t}||_{L^m}≤Γ1|t-s|^{α1} and |E[δA_{s,u,t} | F_u]|≤Γ2|t-s|^{α2}λ(s,t)^{β2} almost surely. Suppose also that there is a process A_t such that for every t and every sequence of partitions of [0,t] with mesh tending to zero, A_t is the in-probability limit of the Riemann sums ∑ A_{t_i,t_{i+1}}. Then there exists a measurable B_{s,t} and a constant C depending only on α1,α2,β2,m such that |A_t-A_s-A_{s,t}|≤CΓ2|t-s|^{α2}λ(s,t)^{β2}+B_{s,t} almost surely and ||B_{s,t}||_{L^m}≤CΓ1|t-s|^{α1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This result is quoted from the earlier ABLM paper as [1, Theorem 4.7]. In the present paper it is used as a black box. The key novelty relative to deterministic sewing is that the conditional expectation error is controlled by a random superadditive quantity λ, while the martingale part is controlled in L^m; this allows one to sew nonlinear drift integrals even when the perturbation ψ has only random pathwise control.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: integral bound with a random control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let γ∈(-2,0), T∈(0,1], and let f:R→R be measurable, either bounded or nonnegative. Let ψ:[0,T]×D×Ω→R be adapted and measurable. Assume there is a nonnegative process w_{s,t}(x) on the time simplex and space such that for every t there is D_t⊂D of full Lebesgue measure with, for all (s,u)∈Δ_{[0,t]} and x∈D_t, |ψ_t(x)-P_{t-s}ψ_s(x)|≤w_{s,t}(x) almost surely and P_{t-u}w_{s,u}(x)+w_{u,t}(x)≤w_{s,t}(x) almost surely. Then there exists a nonnegative process L_{s,t}(x) such that for all (s,t)∈Δ_{[0,T]} and x∈D_T, |∫_s^t ∫_D p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+ψ_r(y)) dy dr| ≤ C||f||_{C^γ}(t-s)^{(3+γ)/4} P_{T-t}w_{s,t}(x)+L_{s,t}(x), where C depends only on γ. Moreover, for every m∈[2,∞), ||L_{s,t}(x)||_{L^m}≤C||f||_{C^γ}|t-s|^{1+γ/4}, with C depending only on γ and m.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For f∈C^1, the proof applies Proposition 3.2. Define A_{s,t}(x)=∫_s^t∫p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+P_{r-s}ψ_s(y))dy dr and A_t(x)=∫_0^t∫p_{T-r}(x,y) f(V_r(y)+ψ_r(y))dy dr. The random control is λ_{s,t}(x)=P_{T-t}w_{s,t}(x); the assumed inequality on w implies λ is a random control. For u=(s+t)/2, δA_{s,u,t} is the integral over [u,t] of the difference between f evaluated at V_r+P_{r-s}ψ_s and at V_r+P_{r-u}ψ_u. Conditional expectation at time u is estimated using Gaussian smoothing of the future stochastic convolution: the derivative norm of the heat-smoothed f is bounded by C||f||_{C^γ}(r-u)^{(γ-1)/4}, and the difference of the deterministic shifts is bounded by P_{r-u}w_{s,u}. This gives the conditional sewing estimate with exponent (3+γ)/4 and λ. The L^m sewing estimate follows from an existing integral estimate for V plus a bounded perturbation, after truncating ψ and passing to the limit. The reconstruction condition is verified by comparing A_t with Riemann sums and using Lipschitz continuity of f together with the random control. After sewing, f∈C^1 is extended to bounded continuous f by heat mollification, to indicators of open sets by Urysohn's lemma and the embedding L^1⊂C^{-1}, to bounded measurable f by Lusin approximation, and to nonnegative measurable f by truncation and Fatou's lemma.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4: deterministic L1-kernel integral bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let τ∈(0,1], ν∈[0,1/2), 0<T≤1, γ∈(-2,0), and Δ>0. Let f:R→R be measurable, either bounded or nonnegative, let X:[0,T)×D→R be measurable, and let ψ:[0,T]×D×Ω→R be adapted and measurable. Assume γ+4τ>1 and ∫_D |X_t(y)|dy≤Δ(T-t)^{-ν} for t∈[0,T). Then, for every m≥2, there is C=C(γ,ν,τ,m) such that for every (s,t)∈Δ_{[0,T]}, ||∫_s^t∫_D X_r(y) f(V_r(y)+ψ_r(y))dy dr||_{L^m} ≤ C||f||_{C^γ}Δ(t-s)^{1+γ/4-ν}(1+[ψ]_{C^τ L^m([s,t])}(t-s)^{τ-1/4}), where [ψ]_{C^τ L^m([s,t])}=sup_{a≤b in [s,t]} sup_x ||ψ_b(x)-P_{b-a}ψ_a(x)||_{L^m}/|b-a|^τ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For continuous f this is quoted from [1, Lemma 6.3]. The present paper extends it to bounded measurable or nonnegative f by the same approximation procedure used in Lemma 3.3: heat mollify bounded continuous approximants, use Lusin approximation for bounded measurable functions, use the embedding of L^1 into negative Hölder/Besov spaces to control exceptional sets, and use truncation plus Fatou for nonnegative f. The estimate exploits the smoothing and decorrelation supplied by the stochastic convolution V and the assumption that ψ is controlled in the semigroup Hölder norm.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5: Lp approximation bound for drift differences
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings and let V be the stochastic convolution. Let γ∈(-3/2,-1), p∈[1,∞], let g,h:R→R be measurable with g bounded and h∈L^p(R), let X:[0,1]×D→R be measurable, and let u:[0,1]×D×Ω→R be adapted and measurable. Then there is C=C(γ,p) such that for every t∈[0,1], ||∫_0^t∫_D X_r(y)(h-g)(u_r(y))dy dr||_{L^2} ≤ C||h-g||_{C^γ}(1+[u−V]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])}) sup_{r∈[0,1]} ||X_r||_{L^1(D)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix N and split h−g=(h1_{|h|≤N}−g)+h1_{|h|>N}. Apply Proposition 3.4 with τ=5/8, ν=0, T=1, ψ=u−V, and f equal to the bounded first term, then apply it again to the nonnegative tail |h|1_{|h|>N}. Since γ<-1, L^p(R) embeds into C^γ, so the tail C^γ norm is controlled by the L^p tail norm. Let N→∞ to remove the tail, using h∈L^p. The result is uniform in X through only sup_r||X_r||_{L^1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.6: Kolmogorov criterion for convergence of continuous processes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let γ1,γ2>0. Let X_n:[0,1]×D×Ω→R be jointly continuous processes with X_n(0,x)=0. Suppose that for every m≥1 there is C=C(m) such that, for all s,t∈[0,1], x,y∈D, and n, ||X_n(t,x)-X_n(s,y)||_{L^m}≤C|t-s|^{γ1}+C|x-y|^{γ2}; and suppose lim_{n,k→∞} sup_{t∈[0,1]} sup_{x∈D} ||X_n(t,x)-X_k(t,x)||_{L^m}=0. Then there is a jointly continuous process X such that for every N>0, sup_{s,t∈[0,1]} sup_{x∈D, |x|≤N} |X(t,x)-X_n(t,x)|→0 in probability. Moreover, for every ρ∈(0,1) and λ>γ2−γ2ρ, sup_{s,t∈[0,1]} sup_{x,y∈D} |X(t,x)-X(s,y)| / ((|t-s|^{γ1}+|x-y|^{γ2})^ρ(1+|x|+|y|)^λ) <∞ almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof applies a Kolmogorov continuity theorem with spatial weights, cited as [3, Proposition C.1], to obtain a jointly continuous limit and the weighted Hölder bound. Fatou's lemma transfers the moment increment estimates from X_n to the limiting process X. Local uniform convergence in probability follows from the assumed uniform L^m Cauchy property plus the common Kolmogorov modulus.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1: canonical limits of regularized drift integrals for u∈V(τ)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let γ∈(-2,0), τ∈(0,1), f∈C^γ(R), and let u:(0,1]×D×Ω→R be a jointly continuous adapted process with u∈V(τ). Assume γ+4τ>1. Then: (i) there exists a jointly continuous adapted process H^f:[0,1]×D×Ω→R such that for every sequence f_n∈C∞_b(R) with f_n→f in C^{γ−} and every N>0, sup_{t∈[0,1]} sup_{x∈D, |x|≤N} |∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f_n(u_r(y))dy dr - H^f_t(x)| →0 in probability. In addition, for every ε>0, sup_{s,t∈[0,1]} sup_{x,y∈D} |H^f_t(x)-H^f_s(y)| / ((|t-s|^{1/2−ε}+|x-y|^{1−ε})(1+|x|+|y|)^{2ε}) <∞ almost surely. (ii) For every ϕ∈S(D), there exists a continuous process H^f(ϕ):[0,1]×Ω→R such that for every f_n∈C∞_b with f_n→f in C^{γ−}, sup_{t∈[0,1]} |∫_0^t∫_D ϕ(x)f_n(u_r(x))dx dr - H^f_t(ϕ)| →0 in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the mild limit, set H^{f,n}_t(x)=∫_0^t∫p_{t-r}(x,z)f_n(u_r(z))dz dr. Proposition 3.4 is applied with ψ=u−V. Choosing γ′<γ with γ′+4τ>1 gives uniform L^m Hölder bounds for H^{f,n}_t(x) in t and x: the time-increment term uses X_r=p_{t-r}(x,·), and the spatial/time kernel-difference term uses X_r=p_{t-r}(x,·)−p_{s-r}(y,·), whose L^1 norm is bounded by heat-kernel estimates. Proposition 3.4 also gives uniform L^m Cauchy convergence because ||f_n−f_k||_{C^{γ′}}→0. Proposition 3.6 then yields a continuous limit H^f and local uniform convergence in probability, together with the weighted Hölder estimate. Independence of the approximating sequence is shown by interlacing two approximating sequences. The weak/test-function part is analogous, with H^{f,n}_t=∫_0^t∫ϕ f_n(u), using Proposition 3.4 with X=ϕ and again applying the one-dimensional version of the convergence criterion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: spatial growth and Hölder regularity of the drift component
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let α>-3/2, b∈C^α(R), u0∈B(D), and suppose u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+K_t(x)+V_t(x) is a mild regularized solution of the equation with drift b and u∈V(5/8). Then for every ε>0, sup_{s,t∈[0,1]} sup_{x,y∈D} |K_t(x)-K_s(y)| / ((|t-s|^{1/2−ε}+|x-y|^{1−ε})(1+|x|+|y|)^{2ε}) <∞ almost surely. Also, for every ε>0, sup_{t∈[0,1]} sup_{x∈D} |u_t(x)|/(1+|x|^ε)<∞ almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The mild regularized definition identifies K with the canonical limit H^b from Lemma 4.1, so the weighted Hölder bound for K follows from the Hölder conclusion there, using an admissible negative regularity below α when needed. For V, standard moment estimates for the stochastic convolution give ||V(t,x)-V(s,y)||_{L^m}≤C|t-s|^{1/4}+C|x-y|^{1/2}; Kolmogorov's theorem yields subpolynomial weighted growth. Since P_tu0 is uniformly bounded for bounded u0, the same subpolynomial growth holds for u=P_tu0+K+V.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3: time-dependent test functions for weak-type identities
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let u0∈B(D), let u:(0,1]×D×Ω→R be jointly continuous, and let H:[0,1]×S(D)×Ω→R be continuous in time. Assume that for every ϕ∈S(D), almost surely ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨u0,ϕ⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,1/2 Δϕ⟩ds+H_t(ϕ)+W_t(ϕ), for all t∈[0,1], with finite integrals. Then: (i) there is a full-probability set on which, for each ω, there are m(ω)∈Z_+ and Γ(ω)>0 such that sup_{t∈[0,1]} |⟨u_t(ω),ϕ⟩|≤Γ(ω)||ϕ||_{S(D),m(ω)} for all ϕ∈S(D), where ||·||_{S(D),m} is the Hilbertian Schwartz-type seminorm defined in the paper; (ii) for every t∈(0,1] and x∈D, P_εu_{t-ε}(x)→u_t(x) almost surely as ε→0; (iii) for every f∈C([0,1],S(D)), there is a continuous adapted process denoted ∫_0^. H(ds,f_s) such that the Riemann sums ∑_{i=1}^{⌊nt⌋}(H_{i/n}(f_{(i-1)/n})−H_{(i-1)/n}(f_{(i-1)/n})) converge in probability to ∫_0^t H(ds,f_s), and almost surely ⟨u_t,f_t⟩=⟨u0,f0⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,∂_s f_s+1/2 Δf_s⟩ds+∫_0^t H(ds,f_s)+∫_0^t∫_D f_s(x)W(ds,dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Part (i) uses the assumed weak identity to see that t↦⟨u_t,ϕ⟩ is almost surely continuous for each ϕ, then applies Mitoma's theorem for S(D)-valued distributions to obtain a random Schwartz-seminorm bound uniformly in time. For (ii), decompose P_εu_{t-ε}(x)=⟨u_{t-ε},p_ε(x,·)χ(x−·)⟩+⟨u_{t-ε},p_ε(x,·)(1−χ(x−·))⟩, with χ compactly supported near zero. The first term converges by local continuity of u, and the second tends to zero using the random seminorm bound from (i) because the heat kernel tail is rapidly small in S(D). For (iii), apply the weak identity on each interval [(i−1)/n,i/n] with the frozen test function f_{(i−1)/n}, sum over i, and telescope. The deterministic time-derivative term is identified through ∑⟨u_{i/n},f_{i/n}−f_{(i−1)/n}⟩; convergence follows from (i), continuity of f in the Schwartz topology, dominated convergence, and Itô isometry for the noise term. The remaining sums define the nonlinear integral against H.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.4: weak-type identity implies a mild-type identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.3, there exists a stochastic process R^H:[0,1]×D×Ω→R such that almost surely u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+R^H(t,x)+V_t(x) for all t∈[0,1] and x∈D. The process R^H is almost surely continuous on (0,1]×D, and for every fixed t>0 and x∈D, ∫_0^{t−ε} H(dr,p_{t-r}(x,·)) → R^H(t,x) in probability as ε→0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First take a test function ϕ∈S(D) and, for fixed terminal time t, insert f_s=P_{t-s}ϕ into Lemma 4.3(iii). Since ∂_s f_s+1/2 Δf_s=0, this gives ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨P_tu0,ϕ⟩+∫_0^t H(dr,P_{t-r}ϕ)+∫_0^t∫P_{t-r}ϕ W. Next choose ϕ=p_ε(x,·) and apply the identity at time t−ε. This yields P_εu_{t−ε}(x)=P_tu0(x)+∫_0^{t−ε}H(dr,p_{t-r}(x,·))+P_εV_{t−ε}(x). Lemma 4.3(ii) gives P_εu_{t−ε}(x)→u_t(x), while Itô isometry gives P_εV_{t−ε}(x)→V_t(x). Defining R^H(t,x)=u_t(x)−P_tu0(x)−V_t(x) proves both the mild identity and the convergence of the H-integral.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.5: identification of the mild drift obtained from a weak regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let α>-3/2, b∈C^α(R), u0∈B(D), and let u be a weak regularized solution with initial condition u0. Assume u∈V(5/8). Let R^K be the process obtained from Lemma 4.4 by applying it to the weak regularized drift K. Then, for every sequence b_n∈C∞_b(R) with b_n→b in C^{α−}, every t∈[0,1], and every x∈D, ∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b_n(u_r(y))dy dr → R^K(t,x) in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a smooth f, t, x, and ε∈(0,t). Decompose the difference between ∫_0^t p_{t-r}f(u_r) and R^K(t,x) into three terms: I1 over [0,t−ε], comparing ∫p f(u) with ∫K(dr,p_{t-r}); I2 over the short terminal interval [t−ε,t]; and I3 comparing ∫_0^{t−ε}K(dr,p_{t-r}) with R^K(t,x). Lemma 4.6 controls I1 in probability by ||f−b||_{C^{α′}} for α′∈(-3/2,α), using a time-discretization κ_n, the weak regularized approximation property of K, and Proposition 3.4. Lemma 4.7 controls I2 by ||f||_{C^α}ε^{1/2}. Lemma 4.4 gives I3→0 as ε→0. Taking f=b_n, first ε→0 and then n→∞, and using b_n→b in C^{α′}, gives the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.6: estimate comparing weak regularized drift and smooth drift before the terminal singularity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.5, fix α′∈(-3/2,α). For f∈C∞_b(R), t∈[0,1], x∈D, ε∈(0,t), define I_1^ε(t,x)=|∫_0^{t−ε}∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f(u_r(y))dy dr − ∫_0^{t−ε} K(dr,p_{t-r}(x,·))|. Then there is C=C(α,α′) such that, for every δ>0, P(I_1^ε(t,x)>δ)≤Cδ^{-1}(1+[u−V]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])})||f−b||_{C^{α′}}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof introduces the grid projection κ_n(r)=⌊nr⌋/n and splits I_1^ε into three errors. The first replaces p_{t-r} by p_{t−κ_n(r)} and is bounded by Proposition 3.4 using the heat-kernel L^1 difference estimate ||p_{t-r}−p_{t−κ_n(r)}||_{L^1}≤Cn^{-1}ε^{-1/2}, hence vanishes as n→∞. The second compares the frozen-kernel f-integral with the corresponding increments of K; by the weak regularized definition, K increments are limits of b_M-integrals, and Proposition 3.4 bounds the difference by ||f−b||_{C^{α′}} plus a vanishing n^{-1/2} term. The third is the difference between the grid sum defining the nonlinear integral ∫K(dr,p_{t-r}) and that integral itself; Lemma 4.3(iii) makes it vanish as n→∞. Combining and letting n→∞ yields the probability bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.7: short-time terminal drift bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.5, there is C=C(α) such that for every δ>0, every (s,t)∈Δ_{[0,1]}, every x∈D, and every f∈C∞_b(R), P(|∫_s^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)f(u_r(y))dy dr|>δ) ≤ Cδ^{-1}||f||_{C^α}(t-s)^{1/2}(1+[u−V]_{C^{5/8}L^2([0,1])}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Proposition 3.4 with ψ=u−V, τ=5/8, X_r(y)=p_{t-r}(x,y), ν=0, and a negative regularity exponent controlled by the C^α norm of f. Since ∫p_{t-r}(x,y)dy=1 and α>-3/2 gives the condition γ+4τ>1 at an admissible γ, the L^2 norm of the integral is bounded by C||f|| times (t-s)^{1/2}. Chebyshev's inequality gives the probability estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.8: weak identity obtained from a mild regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let α>-3/2, b∈C^α(R), u0∈B(D), and let u be a mild regularized solution with initial condition u0. Assume u∈V(5/8). Then for every ϕ∈S(D) and t∈[0,1], there is a continuous process R_ϕ(t) such that ∑_{i=1}^{⌊nt⌋} ⟨K_{i/n}−P_{1/n}K_{(i−1)/n},ϕ⟩ → R_ϕ(t) in probability as n→∞, and almost surely ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨u0,ϕ⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,1/2 Δϕ⟩ds+R_ϕ(t)+∫_0^t∫_D ϕ(x)W(ds,dx), for all t∈[0,1].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define R_ϕ(t) directly by subtracting the initial, heat-Laplacian, and noise terms from ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩; Corollary 4.2 ensures the needed integrability and continuity. To identify R_ϕ as a limit of drift increments, use the mild decomposition u=P_tu0+K+V at grid times. The identity ⟨u_{i/n},ϕ⟩−⟨u_{(i−1)/n},P_{1/n}ϕ⟩=⟨K_{i/n}−P_{1/n}K_{(i−1)/n},ϕ⟩+⟨V_{i/n}−P_{1/n}V_{(i−1)/n},ϕ⟩ is summed over i. The terms involving u and P_{1/n}ϕ−ϕ converge to the Laplacian integral by local continuity plus weighted growth; the stochastic convolution telescoping sum converges to the stochastic integral by Itô isometry. The remaining term is the desired limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.9: identification of the weak drift obtained from a mild regularized solution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.8, for every sequence b_m∈C∞_b(R) with b_m→b in C^{α−}, every ϕ∈S(D), and every t∈[0,1], ∫_0^t∫_D ϕ(x)b_m(u_r(x))dx dr → R_ϕ(t) in probability.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof compares ∫ϕ b_m(u) with the grid drift-increment sum from Lemma 4.8.
