The difference is split into four terms: replacing ϕ by a heat-evolved kernel average over each grid interval; comparing the resulting expression with the K increments using the mild regularized approximation property; the difference between the K-increment sum and R_ϕ(t); and the final short interval [κ_n(t),t]. Proposition 3.4 controls the first and last terms by powers of n^{-1}. For the K-comparison term, the definition of mild regularized solution expresses K as a limit of b_M-integrals, and Proposition 3.4 bounds the resulting b_M−b_m difference by ||b_M−b_m||_{C^{α′}} for α′<α. Letting M→∞ yields a bound involving ||b−b_m||_{C^{α′}}. Finally take m→∞ and n→∞, using Lemma 4.8 for the grid-sum convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10: classical mild-a.e. solutions with Lp drift belong to V(1−1/(4p))
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let p∈[1,∞], b∈L^p(R), u0∈B(D), and let u:(0,1]×D×Ω→R be jointly continuous and adapted. Suppose that for every t∈[0,1], for Lebesgue-a.e. x∈D, ∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr<∞ almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely. Then u∈V(1−1/(4p)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the full-measure set D_t of spatial points where the mild identity holds, Lemma 4.11 gives ||∫_s^t∫p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr||_{L^m}≤C|t-s|^{1−1/(4p)} for x∈D_t. Therefore for x∈D_t, the semigroup increment u_t−V_t−P_{t-s}(u_s−V_s) equals the drift integral over [s,t] and satisfies the same bound. Since u and V are continuous in x, Fatou's lemma extends the estimate from x∈D_t to all x∈D. This is exactly the defining V(1−1/(4p)) estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.11: Lp drift-increment estimate via random controls
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.10, let D_T be the full-measure set of spatial points x for which the assumed mild integrability and identity hold at time T. Then for every m≥2, every (s,T)∈Δ_{[0,1]}, and every x∈D_T, ||∫_s^T∫_D p_{T-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr||_{L^m} ≤ C|T-s|^{1−1/(4p)}, where C depends on m, p, and ||b||_{L^p(R)} but not on s,T,x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For p<∞, apply Lemma 3.3 with γ=−1/p, f=|b|, ψ=u−V, and w_{s,t}(x)=1_{x∈D_t}∫_s^t∫p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr. The mild identity verifies |ψ_t−P_{t-s}ψ_s|≤w_{s,t}, and the semigroup property gives P_{t-u}w_{s,u}+w_{u,t}=w_{s,t}. The embedding L^p⊂C^{−1/p} gives the needed C^γ norm. Lemma 3.3 yields an inequality in which the desired quantity is bounded by C||b||_{L^p}(t-s)^{3/4−1/(4p)} times itself plus a remainder of order |t-s|^{1−1/(4p)}. On sufficiently short intervals, the self-term is absorbed; longer intervals are handled by partitioning into short subintervals. For p=∞, the paper takes M=||b||∞ and observes that the part |b|1_{|b|>M} vanishes a.e.; the remaining bounded part gives the linear bound M|T-s|, which is stronger.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.12: analytically weak solutions tested against time-dependent functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix one of the paper's three settings. Let b:R→R be measurable, u0∈B(D), and let u be an analytically weak solution. Let f∈C([0,1],S(D)) satisfy sup_{t∈[0,1]} sup_{x∈D} |f_t(x)|e^{|x|}<∞. Then for every t∈[0,1], almost surely ⟨u_t,f_t⟩=⟨u0,f0⟩+∫_0^t ⟨u_r,∂_r f_r+1/2 Δf_r⟩dr+∫_0^t∫_D b(u_r(x))f_r(x)dx dr+∫_0^t∫_D f_r(x)W(dr,dx). Moreover, if ϕ∈S(D) and sup_x |ϕ(x)|e^{|x|}<∞, then for every t∈[0,1], almost surely ⟨u_t,ϕ⟩=⟨u0,P_tϕ⟩+∫_0^t∫_D P_{t-r}ϕ(x)b(u_r(x))dx dr+∫_0^t∫_D P_{t-r}ϕ(x)W(dr,dx).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First, the analytically weak definition implies ∫_0^1∫_D |b(u_r(y))|e^{−|y|}dy dr<∞ almost surely, because one can choose a nonnegative test function dominating e^{−|x|}. Apply Lemma 4.3 with H_t(ϕ)=∫_0^t∫b(u_r(y))ϕ(y)dy dr. The Riemann sums defining ∫H(ds,f_s) are ∫_0^{κ_n(t)}∫b(u_r(y))f_{κ_n(r)}(y)dy dr, which converge by dominated convergence to ∫_0^t∫b(u_r(y))f_r(y)dy dr, using the exponential decay assumption. This identifies the H-integral in Lemma 4.3(iii) and gives the time-dependent weak identity. Taking f_r=P_{t-r}ϕ makes ∂_r f_r+1/2 Δf_r=0 and yields the heat-evolved identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.13: analytically weak solutions satisfy the mild identity for a.e. spatial point
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses of Lemma 4.12, for every t∈[0,1], for Lebesgue-a.e. x∈D one has ∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)|b(u_r(y))|dy dr<∞ almost surely and u_t(x)=P_tu0(x)+∫_0^t∫_D p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+V_t(x) almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The integrability follows by Fubini from ∫|b(u)|e^{−|y|}<∞ and the elementary inequality −|x|≤|x−y|−|y|, which controls the heat-kernel convolution weighted by e^{−|x|}. To identify the mild identity, apply the heat-evolved identity of Lemma 4.12 at time t−ε with the test function ϕ=p_ε(x,·). This gives P_εu_{t−ε}(x)=P_tu0(x)+∫_0^{t−ε}∫p_{t-r}(x,y)b(u_r(y))dy dr+P_εV_{t−ε}(x). Lemma 4.3(ii) gives P_εu_{t−ε}(x)→u_t(x), Itô isometry gives P_εV_{t−ε}(x)→V_t(x), and for the a.e. x where the absolute drift integral is finite the drift integral converges by ordinary dominated convergence as ε→0.

proof_techniques.name: Regularized solution via universal smooth drift approximation
proof_techniques.description: The paper defines solutions with distributional drift by requiring convergence against every smooth bounded approximating sequence b_n→b in the appropriate Besov topology. In the mild version, the convolutions ∫p_{t-r}b_n(u_r) converge locally uniformly in probability to a process K_t(x). In the weak version, the test-function integrals ∫ϕ b_n(u_r) converge uniformly in time in probability to K_t(ϕ). This makes the distributional term b(u) meaningful without assigning pointwise values to b(u_t(x)).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirac drift in the original problem, this is the formal mechanism that supports replacing δ0 by heat-kernel mollifiers G_{1/n} and interpreting the limiting drift as a canonical additive term. Under the problem's assumed Dirichlet extension, it justifies using approximation limits when reasoning about the Markov semigroup.

proof_techniques.name: Stochastic sewing with random controls
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly controls singular nonlinear integrals by comparing f(V_r+ψ_r) with f(V_r+P_{r-s}ψ_s), where V is the stochastic convolution and ψ is a perturbation with semigroup increments controlled by a random superadditive quantity w. Conditional expectations are estimated after Gaussian smoothing by the future part of the stochastic convolution, while martingale errors are handled in L^m. Proposition 3.2 then sews local estimates into global integral bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is relevant for controlling occupation of the solution near the singular level 0, which is exactly where a δ0 drift concentrates. It may help establish tightness or uniform estimates for approximating equations with drifts G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Besov regularization by the stochastic convolution
proof_techniques.description: The estimates exploit that, conditionally on the past, increments of the stochastic convolution introduce Gaussian smoothing in the scalar variable. This converts a negative Besov norm ||f||_{C^γ}, γ<0, into usable bounds for integrals of f(V+ψ), with time exponents such as 1+γ/4. The condition γ+4τ>1 balances the singularity of f against the semigroup Hölder control of ψ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A Dirac mass is a negative-regularity distribution, so this is the analytical mechanism behind making ∫δ0(u) meaningful. It suggests looking for estimates that depend on the C^γ norm of the approximating drifts rather than on their sup norms, which blow up for G_{1/n}.

proof_techniques.name: Canonical drift limits independent of the approximation
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 4.1 constructs limits H^f for drift integrals whenever u∈V(τ) and f∈C^γ with γ+4τ>1. The proof first proves uniform moment Hölder bounds and Cauchy estimates for the approximating integral processes, then applies a Kolmogorov convergence criterion to obtain a jointly continuous limit. Interlacing two approximating sequences proves that the limit is intrinsic and does not depend on the chosen mollifier.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If two invariant-measure arguments use different smooth approximations to δ0, this result is the kind of tool that prevents approximation-dependence. In the original setting, the assumed convergence u^n(t)→u(t) can be paired with such intrinsic-limit ideas.

proof_techniques.name: Weak-to-mild conversion by time-dependent heat tests
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts a weak identity into a mild one by testing against f_s=P_{t-s}ϕ, for which ∂_s f_s+1/2 Δf_s=0. To justify time-dependent tests, it proves a partition/telescoping formula and defines ∫H(ds,f_s) as a limit of Riemann sums. It then takes ϕ=p_ε(x,·) and lets ε↓0, using a Mitoma-type distributional continuity bound and Itô isometry.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly useful if the Dirichlet solution is defined weakly or probabilistically but an invariant-measure proof needs the mild heat-kernel representation. The original problem explicitly assumes a probabilistically weak PDE mild solution; this technique explains how weak and mild formulations can be reconciled.

proof_techniques.name: Mild-to-weak conversion by grid telescoping
proof_techniques.description: Starting from u=P_tu0+K_t+V_t, the paper compares ⟨u_{i/n},ϕ⟩ and ⟨u_{(i−1)/n},P_{1/n}ϕ⟩ over a time grid. The heat semigroup cancels the initial condition, the stochastic convolution increments converge to the Itô integral, and the K increments define the weak drift. A second approximation argument identifies this weak drift with the limit of ∫ϕ b_n(u).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If an argument establishes properties of the mild Dirichlet solution but needs martingale-problem or weak-form identities, this telescoping method is the portable bridge, assuming analogous Dirichlet heat-kernel estimates.

proof_techniques.name: Automatic V-regularity for Lp drifts
proof_techniques.description: For integrable drifts, the paper proves that any analytically weak or mild solution automatically belongs to V(1−1/(4p)). The proof uses the drift integral itself as the random control w in Lemma 3.3, absorbs a small multiple of the desired quantity on short intervals, and patches short intervals to obtain a global estimate. This removes the need to assume V-regularity separately in the Lp case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although δ0 is not an Lp function, the absorption strategy suggests how one might prove V-type bounds for measure drifts by using occupation/local-time controls. Such bounds are often prerequisites for approximation and uniqueness arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Approximation identification through negative Besov norms
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.5 shows that for h∈Lp and bounded smooth g, the integral of (h−g)(u) against a kernel X is controlled by ||h−g||_{C^γ}, γ∈(-3/2,-1), times a V-type norm of u and an L1 norm of X. This lets the paper identify regularized drift limits with classical Lebesgue drift integrals when b∈Lp.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For invariant measures obtained as limits from smooth approximating drifts, analogous estimates could be used to pass semigroup or occupation identities to the singular limit. For δ0, one would need the corresponding distributional version rather than the Lp tail argument.

other_useful_info: The paper's main theorems are stated only for R, periodic [0,1], and Neumann [0,1] settings; Dirichlet boundary conditions are not part of the formal results, so the original problem's assumption that ABLM-type results extend to Dirichlet is essential for direct use. The paper cites Athreya-Butkovsky-Lê-Mytnik [1] for existence and uniqueness of mild regularized solutions in certain Besov regimes and specifically invokes [1, Theorem 2.6] for strong probabilistic existence and uniqueness in V(3/4) in the proof of Corollary 2.12. The introduction also states that weak probabilistic existence and uniqueness of mild regularized solutions for b∈C^α, α>-3/2, was obtained in a very recent cited work [5]. The paper contains no invariant-measure, strong Feller, irreducibility, coupling, or ergodicity theorem; its direct contribution to the original problem is solution-notion compatibility and approximation/occupation estimates for singular drifts.
why_relevant: This recent paper clarifies weak and mild solution notions for stochastic heat equations with irregular/distributional drift. It is useful for checking that the Markov semigroup and approximation limits used in invariant-measure arguments are compatible with the ABLM solution concept.

title: On a Unique Ergodicity of Some Markov Processes
overall_summary: The paper proves that a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup on a Polish space has at most one invariant probability measure if it satisfies the e-property and a weak topological irreducibility condition. The core argument is not strong Feller smoothing: it shows that, for e-processes, distinct ergodic invariant measures must have disjoint supports, while weak topological irreducibility forces supports of ergodic invariant measures to intersect. A central technical device is the notion that a point generates an invariant measure through weak limits of Cesaro averages Q_tδ_x, together with tightness, Arzela-Ascoli, and equicontinuity from the e-property. The paper also gives an SPDE criterion: for additive-noise evolution equations, weak dissipativity of the deterministic part implies the e-property via synchronous coupling, so uniqueness follows once weak irreducibility is verified. For the original SPDE, the main possible use is to reduce uniqueness of invariant measures to proving the e-property on the chosen state space and a weak support/irreducibility statement for the transition probabilities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1: tight Cesaro averages of an e-process have a unique weak limit
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space and let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Markov semigroup on bounded Borel functions on X. Assume throughout that (P_t) is Feller, meaning P_t maps bounded continuous functions to bounded continuous functions for every t>0, and stochastically continuous, meaning P_tψ(x)→ψ(x) as t↓0 for every bounded continuous ψ and x∈X. Let π_t(x,A)=P_t1_A(x) be its transition probabilities and P_t^* its dual action on probability measures. For t>0 define Q_tμ:=t^{-1}∫_0^t P_s^*μ ds, set Q_0μ:=μ, and write Q_t(x,·):=Q_tδ_x. Let T be the set of points x∈X for which the family {Q_t(x,·):t≥0} is tight. Suppose (P_t) has the e-property: for every bounded Lipschitz function φ:X→R and every x∈X, lim_{y→x} sup_{t≥0}|P_tφ(x)-P_tφ(y)|=0. Then for every x∈T, the net/sequence Q_t(x,·), t→∞, converges weakly to a probability measure, and that limit is invariant for (P_t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix x∈T. By tightness and Prokhorov, every sequence t_n→∞ has weakly convergent subsequences; standard Krylov-Bogoliubov arguments using Feller and stochastic continuity make every weak subsequential limit invariant. To prove uniqueness of the subsequential limit, argue by contradiction. Suppose Q_{s_n}(x,·)→μ and Q_{t_n}(x,·)→ν weakly along s_n,t_n→∞ with μ≠ν. Choose f∈L_b(X), bounded Lipschitz, separating μ and ν, and choose ε>0 with |∫f dμ−∫f dν|>ε. Tightness gives a compact K with Q_t(x,K)>1−ε/(6∥f∥) for every t≥0; by the Alexandrov/Portmanteau theorem, μ(K) and ν(K) are also close to 1. Define averaged functions g_n:=s_n^{-1}∫_0^{s_n}P_s f ds. The e-property implies that {g_n} is equicontinuous on K and uniformly bounded, so Arzela-Ascoli gives a uniformly convergent subsequence on K, with limit ~f∈C(K). Extend ~f to f_*∈C(X) with ∥f_*∥≤∥f∥. The mass estimate outside K and uniform convergence on K give |∫f_* dμ−∫g_n dμ|<ε/3 for large n; since μ is invariant, ∫g_n dμ=∫f dμ. Thus ∫f_*dμ is close to ∫f dμ. Next choose N with ∫f_*dQ_{t_N}(x,·) close to ∫f_*dν. Applying the same compact/uniform convergence estimate to the fixed measure Q_{t_N}(x,·) gives ∫f_*dQ_{t_N}(x,·) close to ∫g_n dQ_{t_N}(x,·)=∫f dQ_{s_n,t_N}(x,·), where Q_{s,t}=Q_s∘Q_t. The proof then invokes Lemma 2 of Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek as cited in the paper, which says that for fixed t_N the total variation distance between Q_{s_n,t_N}(x,·) and Q_{s_n}(x,·) tends to 0 as n→∞. Hence this last integral is close to ∫f dμ. Combining the three estimates by the triangle inequality contradicts |∫f dμ−∫f dν|>ε. Therefore all subsequential weak limits coincide, and tightness gives full weak convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1: generated measures have the same Cesaro limit as the generating point
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same setting as Lemma 1, assume (P_t) has the e-property. Say that a probability measure μ on X is generated by a point x∈X if μ belongs to the weak closure of the convex hull of {Q_tδ_x:t≥0}. If x∈T and μ is generated by x, then Q_tμ has the same weak limit as Q_t(x,·) as t→∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix f∈L_b(X) with ∥f∥≤1. The paper uses Lemma 2 of Szarek-Slęczka-Urbański together with the e-property to obtain a uniform-in-time continuity statement: if μ_n→μ weakly, then sup_{t≥0}|∫f dQ_tμ_n−∫f dQ_tμ|→0. Since μ is generated by x, for any ε>0 one can choose finitely many times t_1,...,t_N and weights α_i≥0 with sum 1 such that the convex combination Σ_i α_i Q_{t_i}(x,·) approximates μ well enough that sup_{t≥0}|∫f dQ_tμ−∫f dQ_t(Σ_i α_iQ_{t_i}(x,·))|<ε/2. For each fixed t_i, Lemma 2 of Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek gives ∥Q_tQ_{t_i}(x,·)−Q_t(x,·)∥_{TV}→0 as t→∞. Thus for all large t, each term Q_tQ_{t_i}(x,·) is within ε/2 in total variation of Q_t(x,·), uniformly over the finite set i=1,...,N. Hence |∫f dQ_tμ−∫f dQ_t(x,·)|<ε for all large t. Lemma 1 supplies convergence of Q_t(x,·), so Q_tμ converges to the same limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 1: local characterization of generation
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the same Polish-space Markov semigroup setting, assume the e-property. A probability measure μ is generated by x, meaning μ∈cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}, if and only if for every ε>0, μ belongs to the weak closure of the convex hull of all measures Q_tν where t≥0, ν is a probability measure, and supp ν⊂B(x,ε). Here B(x,ε) is an open ball for any compatible metric on the Polish space X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward direction is immediate by taking ν=δ_x. For the reverse direction, the key observation is the uniform-in-time consequence of the e-property: for each bounded Lipschitz f and tolerance η>0 there is a radius r>0 such that |∫f dQ_tν−∫f dQ_t(x,·)|<η for every t≥0 whenever ν is a probability measure supported in B(x,r). Therefore convex combinations of Q_tν with ν supported sufficiently close to x are weakly indistinguishable, on bounded Lipschitz test functions and uniformly in t, from corresponding convex combinations of Q_tδ_x. This transfers membership in all the local closed convex hulls to membership in cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2: every support point of an ergodic invariant measure generates it
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with the e-property. Let μ be an ergodic invariant probability measure, where ergodic means that every μ-invariant measurable set has μ-measure 0 or 1. Then every point x∈supp μ generates μ, i.e. μ∈cl_w conv{Q_tδ_x:t≥0}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has three steps. Step I is an accessibility fact from support points. Fix x∈supp μ and an open neighbourhood A of x. Define B={y∈X:π_t(y,A)=0 for all t≥0}. Stochastic continuity permits B to be written as an intersection over rational times: B=∩_{q∈Q_+}{y:π_q(y,A)=0}. If μ(B)>0, then for z∈B and any t≥0 one must have π_t(z,B)=1: otherwise π_t(z,X\B)>0, so for some rational q_m the set C_m={y:π_{q_m}(y,A)>0} has positive π_t(z,·)-mass, implying π_{t+q_m}(z,A)>0, contrary to z∈B. Thus B is μ-invariant. Ergodicity would force μ(B)=1, but B⊂X\A and μ(A)>0 because x∈supp μ, contradiction. Hence μ(B)=0. Step II fixes ε>0 and A=B(x,ε), and proves that μ can be approximated in mass from below by finite sums of images of probability measures supported in A. More precisely, let γ be the supremum of ν(X) over all finite measures ν≤μ of the form ν=Σ_{i=1}^n α_i P_{t_i}^*ν_i, where ν_i are probability measures supported in A and α_i,t_i≥0. If γ<1, choose ν_n of this form with ν_n(X)→γ and let μ_n=μ−ν_n. A subsequence converges weakly to a nonzero μ_*. By Step I, for some z∈supp μ_*⊂supp μ and some t≥0, η:=π_t(z,A)>0. Feller implies lower semicontinuity of y↦π_t(y,A) for open A, so there is θ>0 with π_t(y,A)≥η/2 on B(z,θ). If α:=μ_*(B(z,θ))>0 and N is large, then μ_N(B(z,θ))>α/2 and γ−ν_N(X)<αη/4, so P_t^*μ_N(A)≥αη/4. Since μ=P_t^*μ≥P_t^*ν_N plus a nonzero normalized piece supported on A, one constructs a competitor in the definition of γ with mass at least ν_N(X)+αη/4, contradiction. Therefore γ=1. Step III converts the mass approximation into generation. For arbitrary ε>0 choose ν_i supported in B(x,ε), times t_i, and weights α_i with Σα_i>1−ε/2 and μ≥Σα_iP_{t_i}^*ν_i. Choose T large enough that ∥Q_Tν_i−Q_TP_{t_i}^*ν_i∥_{TV}<ε/2 for all i, using the fixed-shift Cesaro averaging lemma cited from Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek. Since μ is invariant, Q_Tμ=μ, and a total-variation triangle inequality gives ∥μ−Σα_iQ_Tν_i∥_{TV}<ε. Remark 1 then implies that μ is generated by x.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1: distinct ergodic invariant measures have disjoint supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with the e-property. If μ and ν are two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for (P_t), then supp μ∩supp ν=∅.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Suppose x∈supp μ∩supp ν. The paper recalls from earlier work that the support of every invariant measure is contained in T, the set of points whose Cesaro averages {Q_t(x,·)} are tight; hence x∈T. By Proposition 2, because μ and ν are ergodic invariant measures and x lies in both supports, x generates both μ and ν. Proposition 1 then says that Q_tμ and Q_tν have the same weak limit as Q_t(x,·). But μ and ν are invariant, so Q_tμ=μ and Q_tν=ν for every t. Hence μ=ν, contradicting distinctness. Therefore the supports cannot intersect.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2: weak topological irreducibility plus e-property implies at most one invariant measure
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be Polish and let (P_t) be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with transition probabilities π_t. Assume the e-property. Assume also weak topological irreducibility: for every pair x_1,x_2∈X there exists a point y∈X such that, for every open neighbourhood A of y, there exist times t_1,t_2>0 with π_{t_i}(x_i,A)>0 for i=1,2. Then (P_t) has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If there were two distinct invariant probability measures, standard ergodic-decomposition reasoning gives two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures μ and ν. Pick x_1∈supp μ and x_2∈supp ν. By weak topological irreducibility, there is y∈X such that every neighbourhood A of y is reachable with positive probability from x_1 and from x_2 at possibly different positive times. The Feller property implies lower semicontinuity of z↦π_t(z,A) for open A. Thus if π_t(x_1,A)>0 and x_1∈supp μ, then π_t(z,A)>0 on a neighbourhood of x_1 of positive μ-measure, and invariance gives μ(A)=∫π_t(z,A)μ(dz)>0. Hence y∈supp μ; similarly y∈supp ν. Therefore supp μ∩supp ν≠∅, contradicting Theorem 1. Thus at most one invariant probability measure exists.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2: weak dissipativity gives the e-property for additive-noise evolution equations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Hilbert space with norm ∥·∥ and inner product ⟨·,·⟩. Consider a Markov process {Y_x(t)}_{t≥0}, x∈H, given as the unique mild solution of dY=(AY+F(Y))dt+dZ(t), Y_x(0)=x, where Z is an additive noise on H. Assume the process is Feller. Define G(y):=Ay+F(y). If G is weakly dissipative, meaning ⟨G(x)−G(y),x−y⟩≤0 for all x,y∈H, then the associated semigroup P_tf(x):=E f(Y_x(t)) has the e-property. In fact, for every Lipschitz f:H→R with Lipschitz constant L_f, |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤L_f∥x−y∥ for all x,y∈H and t≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Couple the two solutions Y_x and Y_y by driving them with the same additive noise Z. The noise cancels in the difference, so formally d(Y_x−Y_y)=(A(Y_x−Y_y)+F(Y_x)−F(Y_y))dt. Taking the squared norm and expectation gives (1/2)d/dt E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²≤E⟨G(Y_x(t))−G(Y_y(t)),Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)⟩≤0 by weak dissipativity. Therefore E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²≤∥x−y∥² for every t. For a Lipschitz f, |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤E|f(Y_x(t))−f(Y_y(t))|≤L_f(E∥Y_x(t)−Y_y(t)∥²)^{1/2}≤L_f∥x−y∥. This bound is uniform in t, which is exactly the e-property for bounded Lipschitz f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3: SPDE uniqueness criterion from weak dissipativity and weak irreducibility
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Hilbert-space evolution-equation setting of Lemma 2, suppose the semigroup associated with dY=(AY+F(Y))dt+dZ(t) is weakly topologically irreducible in the sense that for every x_1,x_2∈H there is y∈H such that every open neighbourhood of y is hit with positive probability from both x_1 and x_2 at some positive times. If the deterministic part G= A+F is weakly dissipative, then the semigroup admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Weak dissipativity implies the e-property by Lemma 2. The weak irreducibility assumption is exactly the irreducibility hypothesis of Theorem 2, and the process is assumed Feller in the Section 3 setup. Applying Theorem 2 yields at most one invariant probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Estimate (3.4): isometry for the impulsive-noise stochastic integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the paper's impulsive-noise setup, let ν be a finite Borel measure on R with supp ν=R and a_ν:=∫_R σ²ν(dσ)<∞. A measure-valued impulsive white noise Z is constructed from independent Poissonian impulse times, spatial locations in a partition of [0,1], and jump sizes with law ν restricted to pieces U_n satisfying ∫_{U_n}σν(dσ)=0. For one coordinate copy Z_k and a predictable L²([0,1])-valued integrand X, the stochastic integral satisfies, for 0≤t_1≤t_2<T, E|∫_{t_1}^{t_2} X(s)dZ_k(s)|² = a_ν E∫_{t_1}^{t_2}∥X(s)∥² ds. For predictable Hilbert-Schmidt-operator-valued X, the Hilbert-valued integral is defined by ∫_{t_1}^{t_2}X(s)dZ(s):=Σ_{n≥1}(∫_{t_1}^{t_2}(X(s)^*e_n)dZ_n(s))e_n, where {e_n} is the Dirichlet sine basis of L²([0,1]); the series converges in L²(Ω;L²) when E∫∥X(s)∥_{HS}²ds<∞ and yields a square-integrable cadlag martingale.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality is first checked for simple predictable random fields X(ω,s,·)=ξ(ω)1_{(a,b]}(s)1_A(·), with ξ bounded and F_a-measurable. The construction of the impulse process gives independent increments, the centering condition ∫_{U_n}σν(dσ)=0 removes cross terms, and the second moment of the compound Poisson sum contributes exactly ∫σ²ν(dσ) times the spacetime L² norm. Density of simple predictable fields in the predictable L² space extends the isometry to all integrands. For Hilbert-Schmidt X, independence of the coordinate noises Z_n and the isometry give Σ_n E(∫(X^*e_n)dZ_n)² = a_ν E∫Σ_n∥X^*e_n∥²ds = a_ν E∫∥X∥_{HS}²ds, proving convergence of the defining series. Stochastic continuity and Doob's theorem give a cadlag modification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3: positive-probability control of one impulsive-noise integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Z be the scalar measure-valued impulsive white noise constructed in Section 3 from a finite full-support jump-size measure ν on R with finite second moment, spatial partition {O_m}, jump-size partition {U_n}, exponential impulse times, uniform spatial locations on O_m, and jump sizes distributed as ν conditioned on U_n. Let X∈L²([0,T];L²([0,1])) be such that ∫_s^t∫_0^1 |X(u)(x)| dx du>0 for some 0≤s<t≤T. Then for every a∈R and ε>0, P(|∫_s^t X(u)dZ(u)−a|<ε and sup_{s≤τ≤t}|∫_s^τ X(u)dZ(u)|<|a|+ε)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to s=0 and a≠0. Since X is nonzero on a set of positive spacetime measure, choose a spatial partition cell O_m, a number v≠0, and a measurable set Γ⊂[0,t]×O_m of positive Lebesgue measure such that |X(u)(x)−v|<ε|v|/(3|a|) on Γ. Set γ=a/v and choose n with γ∈U_n. Choose η>0 smaller than both ε/(3|v|) and |a/v|. Consider the event Ω_0 that: no impulse from any pair (k,l)≠(n,m) occurs before t; the first impulse of type (n,m) has time and location in Γ; the second impulse of type (n,m) occurs after t; and the first jump size σ_1^{(n,m)} lies in (γ−η,γ+η). These four requirements concern independent random variables, so their probabilities multiply. The no-other-jump event has probability at least exp(−ν(R)t)>0; the time-location event has positive probability because Γ has positive Lebesgue measure and the density is positive on [0,∞)×O_m; the second waiting-time event is positive; and the jump-size event is positive by the full-support assumption on ν. On Ω_0 there is exactly one relevant jump in [0,t], located where X is close to v and with size close to γ, so the final integral is within ε of a, while all partial integrals are bounded by |a|+ε. Hence the desired event has positive probability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4: support property for the impulsive stochastic convolution
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S(t) be the Dirichlet heat semigroup on L²([0,1]) with eigenbasis e_k(x)=√2 sin(πkx), and let Z be the Hilbert-valued impulsive noise built from independent scalar copies of the measure-valued impulsive noise. Define the stochastic convolution Z_S(t):=∫_0^t S(t−s)dZ(s). For every f∈L²([0,T];L²([0,1])), every g∈L²([0,1]), and every ε>0, P(∫_0^T∥f(t)−Z_S(t)∥²dt<ε and ∥Z_S(T)−g∥<ε)>0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First reduce by density to the case where f is a step function f=Σ_{j=1}^N f_j1_{[t_{j−1},t_j)} with 0=t_0<...<t_N=T, f_1=0, f_N=g, and all f_j lie in a fixed finite-dimensional span of sine modes. The stochastic-convolution isometry implies E∫_0^T∥Z_S(t)∥²dt<∞, hence its high Fourier-mode tail can be made smaller than ε with positive probability. Thus it suffices to show, for each fixed finite coordinate i, that the coordinate process ⟨Z_S(t),e_i⟩ can track the prescribed coordinate path with positive probability and end near the desired coordinate. For a fixed i, set a small tolerance ~ε=ε/T and refine the time partition so that the heat-semigroup decay over a mesh interval changes the target coordinate by at most ~ε/8, i.e. max_j |(1−e^{−π²i²δ})⟨f_j,e_i⟩|<~ε/8. The proof proceeds by induction over intervals [t_l,t_{l+1}]. Assuming a positive-probability event on which the past coordinate error up to t_l is at most ~ε t_l and the coordinate at t_l is within ~ε of target, choose γ in the support of the conditional law of ⟨Z_S(t_l),e_i⟩ on that event. Let h=⟨f_{l+1},e_i⟩ and η=h−γ. Choose s∈(t_l,t_{l+1}) close to t_l so that the contribution of the short interval [t_l,s] to the L²-in-time error is small. Lemma 3 gives a positive-probability event on which the impulsive integral over [t_l,s] produces approximately η with controlled partial supremum. A second application of Lemma 3 gives a positive-probability event on which the subsequent noise over [s,t_{l+1}] remains small. These events are independent of the past and of each other, so their intersection has positive probability. On this intersection, explicit estimates using the semigroup factor e^{−π²i²(t−u)} show that the coordinate stays within the required tolerance on [t_l,t_{l+1}] and ends within tolerance at t_{l+1}. Induction proves the coordinate tracking statement. Independence across coordinate noises and the positive-probability high-mode tail event yield the full L² path and terminal approximation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4: heat equation with impulsive noise has e-property, weak irreducibility, and uniqueness when L_g≤π²
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider on L²([0,1]) the stochastic heat equation with Dirichlet boundary conditions driven by the impulsive noise of Section 3: ∂_t u(t,x)=Δu(t,x)−g(u(t,x))+∂_tZ(t,x), u(t,0)=u(t,1)=0, u(0)=ξ. Here g:R→R is continuously differentiable with bounded derivative and Lipschitz constant L_g, and the associated mild solution defines a Feller semigroup P_tf(x)=E f(u_x(t)). If L_g≤π², then (P_t) has the e-property and is weakly topologically irreducible. Consequently it admits at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the e-property, note that G=Δ−g is weakly dissipative when L_g≤π². Indeed, the Dirichlet Laplacian gives ⟨Δw,w⟩≤−π²∥w∥² by the Poincare inequality, while the Lipschitz bound gives |⟨g(u)−g(v),u−v⟩|≤L_g∥u−v∥²; hence ⟨G(u)−G(v),u−v⟩≤(L_g−π²)∥u−v∥²≤0. Lemma 2 therefore yields the e-property. For irreducibility, write u_ξ(t)=v_ξ(t)+Z_S(t), where Z_S is the stochastic convolution and v_ξ is the adapted solution of the random deterministic equation v_ξ(t)=S(t)ξ+∫_0^tS(t−s)g(v_ξ(s)+Z_S(s))ds, as displayed in the paper. It is enough to show supp Law(u_ξ(T))=L² for arbitrary T>0 and ξ∈L². Fix a target υ∈L² and ε>0. By Proposition 2.11 of Maslowski cited in the paper, there exists f∈L²([0,T];L²) such that the deterministic solution v^f_ξ driven by f satisfies ∥v^f_ξ(T)∥<ε/2. Since g is Lipschitz, the map f↦v^f_ξ(T) is continuous from L²([0,T];L²) to L², so there exists r>0 such that ∫_0^T∥f(t)−h(t)∥²dt<r implies ∥v^h_ξ(T)∥<ε/2. Lemma 4 gives positive probability that Z_S is within r of f in L²([0,T];L²) and that Z_S(T) is within ε/2 of υ. On this event, u_ξ(T)=v^{Z_S}_ξ(T)+Z_S(T) lies within ε of υ. Thus every open ball in L² has positive transition probability from every initial condition, which is stronger than weak topological irreducibility. Proposition 3 then gives uniqueness of the invariant measure if one exists.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness via e-property plus weak topological irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces strong Feller smoothing by the e-property, a uniform-in-time equicontinuity condition for P_tφ when φ is bounded Lipschitz. The abstract route is: prove that distinct ergodic invariant measures of an e-process have disjoint supports; prove that weak topological irreducibility and Feller lower semicontinuity force any two ergodic invariant measures to have a common support point; conclude uniqueness. This is weaker than requiring every P_t to map all bounded measurable functions to continuous functions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, a direct strategy is to verify the e-property of its Markov semigroup on C_0([0,1]) and a weak topological irreducibility/support property. Theorem 2 would then prove at most one invariant probability measure without needing a full strong Feller theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Generated-measure argument from support points
proof_techniques.description: A point x generates a measure μ if μ lies in the weak closed convex hull of the Cesaro averages Q_tδ_x. The paper proves that every support point of an ergodic invariant measure generates that measure. The proof combines an accessibility lemma from support points, a submeasure approximation of the invariant measure by images of measures localized near x, and a local characterization of generation using the e-property.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If two candidate invariant measures for the original SPDE can be shown to share an accessible point, this mechanism can identify both as limits generated by the same initial condition. It is useful when direct comparison of invariant measures is hard but support/accessibility information is available.

proof_techniques.name: Tight Cesaro averages plus Arzela-Ascoli from e-property
proof_techniques.description: For x in the tightness set T, the paper proves convergence of Q_tδ_x by contradiction. Tightness provides a compact set carrying almost all mass for all times, while the e-property makes time-averaged observables t^{-1}∫_0^tP_s f ds equicontinuous on that compact set. Arzela-Ascoli then gives uniform convergence along subsequences, and invariance of subsequential limits plus an averaging-shift estimate rule out two different limits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This suggests a way to analyze long-time averages from a fixed initial condition of the original SPDE if tightness can be proved. Even if existence of invariant measures is assumed separately, the same compact/equicontinuity logic underlies uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Synchronous additive-noise coupling for the e-property
proof_techniques.description: For additive-noise evolution equations, the paper couples two solutions with different initial data by using the same noise realization. The difference then solves a deterministic evolution equation. If the deterministic drift is weakly dissipative, the squared Hilbert norm of the difference is nonincreasing in expectation, yielding a uniform Lipschitz bound |P_tf(x)−P_tf(y)|≤L_f∥x−y∥ for Lipschitz f.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original stochastic heat equation, one can try to establish an analogous contraction or nonexpansion estimate for two solutions driven by the same space-time white noise, perhaps first for smooth drift approximations and then by passage to the singular drift. Such a bound would imply the e-property.

proof_techniques.name: Support/irreducibility through controllability of the stochastic convolution
proof_techniques.description: In the heat-equation application, weak irreducibility is proved by showing that the stochastic convolution has sufficiently large support: with positive probability it approximates any prescribed L² time path and terminal point. Continuity of the deterministic solution map with respect to the forcing path then transfers this support property to the full nonlinear equation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original equation has additive space-time white noise, whose stochastic convolution is expected to have rich support in suitable path spaces. A solver could combine a support theorem for the stochastic convolution with continuity of the solution map or approximation by regular drifts to prove weak topological irreducibility on C_0([0,1]).

proof_techniques.name: Feller lower semicontinuity transfers pointwise reachability to invariant-measure support
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 2 uses the fact that for an open set A, Feller continuity implies lower semicontinuity of x↦π_t(x,A). Thus if π_t(x,A)>0 at a support point x of an invariant measure μ, then π_t(z,A)>0 on a neighbourhood of x with positive μ-mass; invariance gives μ(A)>0. This is how weak topological irreducibility produces an actual common support point.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When applying the criterion to the original SPDE, it is not enough to show that each initial condition can reach neighbourhoods with positive probability; one also needs the Feller property, or an equivalent lower-semicontinuity mechanism, to move from pointwise reachability to support intersection of invariant measures.

proof_techniques.name: Borderline dissipativity via the Poincare inequality
proof_techniques.description: In the heat-equation application, the deterministic part Δ−g is weakly dissipative when the Lipschitz constant of g is at most the first Dirichlet eigenvalue π². The estimate is ⟨Δw,w⟩≤−π²∥w∥² and |⟨g(u)−g(v),w⟩|≤L_g∥w∥². This handles the borderline case L_g=π², where strict dissipativity is unavailable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the singular drift or its approximations can be interpreted as having a one-sided Lipschitz bound compatible with the Dirichlet Laplacian, this technique could yield the e-property even without strict contraction. The original drift is distributional, so this would likely require an approximation or monotonicity argument.

other_useful_info: Throughout the abstract uniqueness theory the paper assumes the semigroup is Feller and stochastically continuous; these are not optional in the stated proofs. The paper recalls from earlier work that T={x:{Q_t(x,·)} is tight} is nonempty if and only if an invariant measure exists, and that the support of any invariant measure is contained in T. The main theorem proves uniqueness only, not existence. The introduction cites Hairer-Mattingly for asymptotic strong Feller uniqueness methods and Komorowski-Peszat-Szarek/Lasota-Szarek for e-process criteria; it also notes a gradient-type sufficient condition for the e-property from prior work: bounds of the form |∇P_tφ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(sup|φ|+sup∥∇φ∥), uniformly in t, imply the e-property. In the SPDE application, full support of transition laws is used as a stronger condition than weak topological irreducibility.
why_relevant: This paper refines uniqueness criteria based on asymptotic strong Feller/e-property ideas and applies them to nonlinear heat equations with noise. It is useful for proving at most one invariant measure from weaker continuity plus irreducibility assumptions.

title: Ergodicity of the 2D Navier-Stokes equations with degenerate stochastic forcing
overall_summary: Hairer and Mattingly introduce the asymptotic strong Feller property for Markov semigroups on Polish spaces and prove that it rules out two distinct ergodic invariant measures whose supports meet at a point. Their abstract consequence is that asymptotic strong Fellerness plus a weak irreducibility condition, namely the existence of a point lying in the support of every invariant probability measure, implies uniqueness of the invariant measure. They then prove this property for degenerate-noise 2D stochastic Navier-Stokes equations using high-low mode splitting, Malliavin calculus, and an approximate integration by parts formula. For the original SPDE, the most directly reusable content is the abstract uniqueness criterion, the gradient-estimate criterion for asymptotic strong Fellerness, and the support-at-a-common-point argument based on a priori bounds plus accessibility of a small ball around 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.1 and 3.8: totally separating metrics and asymptotic strong Feller property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space. A pseudo-metric d on X is a continuous function d:X×X→R_+ with d(x,x)=0 and satisfying the triangle inequality. An increasing sequence {d_n}_{n≥0} of pseudo-metrics is called totally separating if d_n(x,y)→1 for every pair x≠y. For a pseudo-metric d, define the Lipschitz seminorm ∥φ∥_d=sup_{x≠y}|φ(x)-φ(y)|/d(x,y), and for two positive finite Borel measures µ_1,µ_2 of equal mass define ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d as the infimum of ∫_{X×X} d(x,y) π(dx,dy) over all couplings π of µ_1 and µ_2. A Markov semigroup P_t on X is asymptotically strong Feller at x∈X if there exist a totally separating system {d_n} and times t_n>0 such that inf_{U open, x∈U} limsup_{n→∞} sup_{y∈U} ∥P_{t_n}(x,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}=0. It is asymptotically strong Feller if this holds at every x∈X. If B(x,γ) denotes balls for any metric inducing the topology on X, the condition is equivalently lim_{γ→0} limsup_{n→∞} sup_{y∈B(x,γ)} ∥P_{t_n}(x,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is definitional. The point of the definition is that the distances d_n become increasingly close to the discrete metric 1_{x≠y}, so closeness in ∥·∥_{d_n} for large n is an asymptotic substitute for continuity of transition probabilities in total variation. The equivalent ball formulation follows because neighborhoods of x and metric balls around x form the same local filter in a Polish topology.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: Monge-Kantorovich duality for continuous pseudo-metrics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let d be a continuous pseudo-metric on a Polish space X, and let µ_1 and µ_2 be positive finite Borel measures on X with equal mass. For a finite signed measure ν with total mass zero, define |||ν|||_d=sup_{∥φ∥_d=1} ∫_X φ dν. Then ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d=|||µ_1−µ_2|||_d, where ∥·∥_d is the coupling distance associated with d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define an equivalence relation x~y iff d(x,y)=0, and let X_d=X/~ with quotient map π:X→X_d. The pseudo-metric d descends to a genuine separable metric on X_d, though X_d need not be complete. Standard Monge-Kantorovich duality on the separable metric space X_d gives equality between the coupling formulation and the Lipschitz-dual formulation for π_*µ_1 and π_*µ_2. Both the coupling cost and the dual seminorm are unchanged by passing from X to X_d, since d vanishes exactly on equivalence classes and d-Lipschitz functions are precisely pullbacks of Lipschitz functions on the quotient, up to irrelevant constants.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: monotone convergence of transport distances
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let {d_n}_{n≥0} be a bounded increasing family of continuous pseudo-metrics on a Polish space X, and define d(x,y)=lim_{n→∞} d_n(x,y). If µ_1 and µ_2 are positive measures on X with equal mass, then lim_{n→∞} ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n}=∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since d_n increases and is bounded, the limit L=lim_n ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n} exists and L≤∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d is immediate. For the reverse inequality, choose for each n an optimal coupling π_n for d_n. The family {π_n} is tight because all π_n have the same fixed marginals µ_1 and µ_2; take a weakly convergent subsequence with limit π_∞. For m≥n, optimality gives ∫d_n dπ_m≤∫d_m dπ_m≤L. Passing m→∞ using continuity of d_n yields ∫d_n dπ_∞≤L for every fixed n. Dominated convergence then gives ∫d dπ_∞≤L, so ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_d≤L.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.5: total variation as the limit along totally separating metrics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a Polish space and let {d_n} be a totally separating system of pseudo-metrics for X. If µ_1 and µ_2 are positive measures of equal mass on X, then ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{TV}=lim_{n→∞} ∥µ_1−µ_2∥_{d_n}, where the paper uses the normalized total variation norm ∥ν∥_{TV}=1/2(ν^+(X)+ν^−(X)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a totally separating system, d_n(x,y) increases pointwise to the discrete cost d(x,y)=1 for x≠y and d(x,x)=0. The optimal coupling cost for this limiting d equals the minimum possible mass off the diagonal, which is exactly the normalized total variation distance. Lemma 3.4 applies because the limiting pseudo-metric need not be continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.10: strong Feller implies asymptotic strong Feller
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a Markov semigroup P_t on a Polish space is strong Feller at some time t>0, then it is asymptotically strong Feller. More precisely, if P_t maps bounded measurable functions to continuous functions, then P_{2t}=P_tP_t has transition probabilities continuous in total variation, and this total-variation continuity implies the asymptotic strong Feller condition with a constant time sequence t_n=2t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites the fact that if P and Q are two strong Feller Markov operators on the same Polish space, then the product PQ has transition probabilities continuous in total variation. Applying this with P=Q=P_t gives total-variation continuity of x↦P_{2t}(x,·). Since ∥·∥_{d_n}≤∥·∥_{TV} for the bounded totally separating metrics used in the definition, total-variation continuity immediately yields the required local limsup condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11: gradient criterion for the strong Feller property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Hilbert space H. Suppose that for some fixed time t and every function φ:H→R with finite ∥φ∥_∞ and finite ∥∇φ∥_∞ one has |∇P_tφ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)∥φ∥_∞, where C:R_+→R is a fixed nondecreasing function independent of φ. Then P_t is strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is quoted as a standard criterion from Da Prato and Zabczyk. The essential mechanism is that the derivative estimate depends only on the sup norm of the test function and not on its derivative. One first obtains local Lipschitz continuity of P_tφ for smooth bounded φ with a bound uniform under bounded pointwise approximations. Then bounded measurable functions are approximated by smooth bounded functions, and the Markov property plus dominated convergence pass continuity to P_tφ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.12: gradient criterion for asymptotic strong Feller
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a Hilbert space. Let {t_n} be a nondecreasing sequence of positive times and let δ_n>0 with δ_n→0. Suppose that for every φ:H→R with finite ∥φ∥_∞ and ∥∇φ∥_∞, the semigroup satisfies |∇P_{t_n}φ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(∥φ∥_∞+δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞) for all n and all x∈H, where C:R_+→R is a fixed nondecreasing function. Then P_t is asymptotically strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For ε>0 define d_ε(w_1,w_2)=1∧ε^{-1}∥w_1−w_2∥, and let ∥·∥_ε be the corresponding Lipschitz and measure seminorm. If δ_n is decreasing, then {d_{δ_n}} is totally separating; otherwise one can pass to a decreasing subsequence without changing the asymptotic conclusion. For a Fréchet differentiable φ with ∥φ∥_ε≤1, constants may be subtracted so that ∥φ∥_∞ is controlled by 1, and ∥∇φ∥_∞≤ε^{-1}. The assumed gradient bound gives |P_{t_n}φ(w_1)−P_{t_n}φ(w_2)|≤∥w_1−w_2∥ C(∥w_1∥∨∥w_2∥)(1+δ_n/ε). General d_ε-Lipschitz φ are approximated pointwise by Fréchet differentiable functions using a linear strong Feller diffusion, preserving the d_ε-Lipschitz seminorm, and dominated convergence passes the estimate to all such φ. Therefore ∥P_{t_n}(w_1,·)−P_{t_n}(w_2,·)∥_ε≤∥w_1−w_2∥ C(∥w_1∥∨∥w_2∥)(1+δ_n/ε). Taking ε=a_n=√δ_n gives a totally separating scale with right side bounded by a local constant times ∥w_1−w_2∥(1+a_n), which tends to 0 as w_2→w_1 uniformly in the required limsup sense.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.16: asymptotic strong Feller separates ergodic invariant supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be a Markov semigroup on a Polish space X, and let µ and ν be two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures for P_t. If P_t is asymptotically strong Feller at x∈X, then x∉supp(µ)∩supp(ν).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Distinct ergodic invariant probability measures are mutually singular, so their normalized total variation distance is 1. The proof first establishes the key inequality: for any measurable A⊂X, any t>0, and any pseudo-metric d≤1, ∥µ−ν∥_d≤1−α(1−M), where α=min{µ(A),ν(A)} and M=max_{y,z∈A}∥P_t(z,·)−P_t(y,·)∥_d. To prove this, decompose µ=(1−α)µ_bar+αµ_A and ν=(1−α)ν_bar+αν_A with µ_A,ν_A supported on A; use invariance P_t^*µ=µ and P_t^*ν=ν; then apply the triangle inequality and the trivial bound ∥·∥_d≤1 to the first part and integrate transition-distance bounds over A×A for the second part. Now assume x lies in both supports. By asymptotic strong Fellerness, choose an open U containing x and N such that for all n>N and all y,z∈U, ∥P_{t_n}(z,·)−P_{t_n}(y,·)∥_{d_n}≤1/2. Since x is in both supports, α=min{µ(U),ν(U)}>0. The inequality gives ∥µ−ν∥_{d_n}≤1−α/2 for all n>N. Corollary 3.5 then implies ∥µ−ν∥_{TV}≤1−α/2, contradicting mutual singularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.17: asymptotic strong Feller plus a common support point gives uniqueness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be an asymptotically strong Feller Markov semigroup on a Polish space X. If there exists a point x∈X such that x∈supp(µ) for every invariant probability measure µ of P_t, then P_t has at most one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If there were two distinct invariant probability measures, their ergodic decompositions would yield at least two distinct ergodic invariant probability measures. The assumed common-support condition applies in particular to all ergodic invariant measures, so both would have x in their supports. This contradicts Theorem 3.16.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equation 4.9: approximate integration by parts with a residual error
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes setting of the paper, let w_t be the solution, J_{0,t}ξ the derivative at time t with respect to an initial perturbation ξ, and A_{0,t}v the Malliavin derivative at time t induced by an infinitesimal Cameron-Martin perturbation v of the Wiener path over [0,t]. For a possibly nonadapted process v, set ρ_t=J_{0,t}ξ−A_{0,t}v. Then for smooth φ and P_tφ(w)=E_w φ(w_t), ⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩=E_w[φ(w_t)∫_0^t v(s)dW(s)]+E_w[(∇φ)(w_t)ρ_t], where the stochastic integral is Itô if v is adapted and Skorokhod if v is nonadapted. Consequently |⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩|≤∥φ∥_∞ E_w|∫_0^t v(s)dW(s)|+∥∇φ∥_∞ E_w∥ρ_t∥.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from ⟨∇P_tφ(w),ξ⟩=E[(∇φ)(w_t)J_{0,t}ξ]. Decompose J_{0,t}ξ=A_{0,t}v+ρ_t. By the Malliavin chain rule, E[(∇φ)(w_t)A_{0,t}v]=E[D^v φ(w_t)], where D^v is the Malliavin derivative in direction v. Malliavin integration by parts gives E[D^v φ(w_t)]=E[φ(w_t)∫v dW], with Skorokhod interpretation when v is nonadapted. The final inequality is immediate from the sup norms. The estimate is the paper's central replacement for exact Bismut-Elworthy-Li formulas when the chosen noise perturbation only cancels the initial perturbation asymptotically.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1: asymptotic strong Feller property for the restricted 2D Navier-Stokes dynamics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the two-dimensional stochastic Navier-Stokes equation in vorticity form on the torus, driven by a finite-dimensional additive noise acting on Fourier modes Z_0, let H_tilde be the closed subspace spanned by the Fourier modes in the nonlinear closure Z_tilde_∞ of Z_0. Then the Markov transition semigroup on H_tilde generated by the solutions is asymptotically strong Feller.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces Theorem 4.1 to Proposition 4.3. Proposition 4.3 gives, for integer times n, a derivative estimate of the form ∥∇P_nφ(w)∥≤C exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+e^{−δ n}∥∇φ∥_∞). This is exactly the hypothesis of Proposition 3.12 with t_n=n and δ_n=e^{−δ n}, after absorbing exp(η∥w∥^2) into the local nondecreasing coefficient C(∥w∥). Therefore the semigroup is asymptotically strong Feller on H_tilde.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.3: derivative estimate implying asymptotic strong Feller for Navier-Stokes
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes setting restricted to H_tilde, for every η>0 there exist constants C>0 and δ>0 such that every Fréchet differentiable φ:H_tilde→R satisfies ∥∇P_nφ(w)∥≤C exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+∥∇φ∥_∞ e^{−δ n}) for every w∈H_tilde and every integer n≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a unit direction ξ∈H_tilde. The proof constructs an infinitesimal perturbation v of the Wiener path so that the residual ρ_n=J_{0,n}ξ−A_{0,n}v_{0,n} decays exponentially in moments, while the Skorokhod cost of v remains bounded. Time is split into unit intervals, and on the first half of each interval [n,n+1/2] the control is chosen as v_n=A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1} J_hat_n ρ_n, where A_n is the Malliavin map on that half interval, M_n=A_nA_n^* is the Malliavin matrix, J_hat_n is the derivative flow over the first half interval, and β>0 regularizes the inverse. On [n+1/2,n+1] the control is zero, allowing parabolic dissipation to damp high modes. This gives the recursion ρ_{n+1}=J_check_n β(β+M_n)^{−1} J_hat_n ρ_n. The Malliavin lower-tail estimate of Theorem 4.12, high-mode damping, and a small choice of β yield Proposition 4.13: E∥ρ_N∥^{10}≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2)2^{−N}. A Skorokhod-isometry calculation using Malliavin derivatives of A_n and J_hat_n yields Proposition 4.14: the square cost of ∫v dW is controlled by a convergent series in the residual moments. Plugging these two bounds into the approximate integration by parts estimate, with N=n, gives the displayed derivative bound uniformly over ∥ξ∥=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.2: uniqueness of the invariant measure on H_tilde for the Navier-Stokes application
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation considered in the paper, restricted to the invariant subspace H_tilde generated by the forced modes and the nonlinearity, there exists exactly one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence is standard for this dissipative SPDE. Uniqueness follows from Corollary 3.17. Theorem 4.1 supplies asymptotic strong Fellerness. It remains to show that every invariant measure has 0 in its support. Applying Itô's formula to the enstrophy ∥w∥^2 and using invariance gives the uniform a priori bound ∫_H ∥w∥^2 µ(dw)≤C E_0/ν for every invariant measure µ, where E_0=tr QQ^*. Hence there is a radius C_tilde such that µ(B(C_tilde))>1/2 for every invariant µ. A controllability or small-ball lemma from E and Mattingly, whose proof also works for the full solution, states that for every γ>0 there is T_γ such that inf_{w∈B(C_tilde)} P_{T_γ}(w,B(γ))>0. Invariance then gives µ(B(γ))≥∫_{B(C_tilde)}P_{T_γ}(w,B(γ))µ(dw)>0 for every γ>0. Thus every neighborhood of 0 has positive µ-mass, so 0∈supp(µ) for every invariant µ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11: essentially elliptic gradient estimate via adapted low-mode control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_t be the semigroup generated by the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation on H. There exists N_* depending on the energy injection E_0 and viscosity ν such that, if the forced set Z_0 contains every Fourier mode k with 0<|k|≤N_*, then for every η>0 there are constants c,γ>0 such that |∇P_tφ(w)|≤c exp(η∥w∥^2)(∥φ∥_∞+e^{−γt}∥∇φ∥_∞) for all suitable φ and all t≥0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a low-mode projection π_l onto modes below a cutoff N and high-mode projection π_h=1−π_l, with N large enough that high modes are strongly dissipative and with π_lH contained in the range of the noise operator Q. Given an initial derivative direction ξ, define an auxiliary process ζ_t by ∂_tζ_t=−(1/2)π_lζ_t/∥π_lζ_t∥+νΔπ_hζ_t+π_h B_tilde(w_t,ζ_t), ζ_0=ξ, with 0/0 interpreted as 0. Then the low part ζ_t^l reaches 0 by time at most 2, while the high part obeys the linearized dissipative dynamics. Define an adapted control v(t)=Q_l^{−1}F_t with F_t=(1/2)ζ_t^l/∥ζ_t^l∥+νΔζ_t^l+π_l B_tilde(w_t,ζ_t), so that the residual ρ_t in the approximate integration by parts formula equals ζ_t. Energy estimates for the Jacobian and the Navier-Stokes solution give E∥ζ_t^h∥^p≤C e^{η∥w_0∥^2}e^{−γt}. Because v is adapted, the cost E|∫v dW| is bounded by Itô isometry and estimates on F_t. Substitution into Equation 4.9 yields the derivative bound. The construction shows how direct forcing of sufficiently many low modes plus dissipation of high modes can replace full ellipticity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.10: a priori bounds for the Navier-Stokes solution, Jacobian, and Hessian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the 2D stochastic Navier-Stokes vorticity equation in the paper, let w_t be the solution, E_0=tr QQ^*, ∥·∥ the L^2 norm, and ∥·∥_1 the H^1 norm. The paper proves: (1) There are C,η_0>0 depending only on Q and ν such that for every s≥0 and η≤η_0, E exp(η sup_{t≥s}(∥w_t∥^2+ν∫_s^t∥w_r∥_1^2 dr−E_0(t−s)))≤C exp(η e^{−νs}∥w_0∥^2). (2) There are η_1,a,γ>0 depending only on E_0 and ν such that E exp(η Σ_{n=0}^N∥w_n∥^2−γN)≤exp(aη∥w_0∥^2) for every N and η≤η_1. (3) For every η>0 there is C such that the derivative flow J_{0,t} satisfies ∥J_{0,t}∥≤exp(η∫_0^t∥w_s∥_1^2 ds+Ct) almost surely. (4) For every η>0 and p>0 there is C such that the second derivative flow K_{s,t} satisfies E∥K_{s,t}∥^p≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2) for all s>0 and t∈(s,s+1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The solution estimates start from Itô's formula for ∥w_t∥^2. The Navier-Stokes nonlinearity is energy-conserving in vorticity form, so the drift contributes the dissipative term −ν∥w_t∥_1^2, while the noise contributes a martingale ∫⟨w,QdW⟩ and trace E_0t. An exponential martingale estimate gives the pathwise exponential bound and, by iteration over integer times plus the one-time exponential moment estimate, the discrete exponential moment bound. For the Jacobian, write the linearized equation ∂_tξ_t=νΔξ_t+B_tilde(w_t,ξ_t). Sobolev and interpolation inequalities bound the nonlinear term by ν∥ξ_t∥_1^2 plus η∥w_t∥_1^2∥ξ_t∥^2 and constants, after which Gronwall gives the displayed bound. For the Hessian, use the variation-of-constants formula K_{s,t}(ξ,ξ')=∫_s^t J_{r,t}B_tilde(J_{s,r}ξ,J_{s,r}ξ')dr, Sobolev estimates on B_tilde, smoothing estimates for J, and the previous exponential integrability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.12: Malliavin matrix lower-tail estimate quoted from Mattingly-Pardoux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the Malliavin matrix for the stochastic 2D Navier-Stokes equation over the time interval [0,1/2], and let H_tilde be the invariant subspace generated by the forced modes. For every α>0, η>0, p>0, and every orthogonal projection π_l onto finitely many Fourier modes, there exists C_tilde such that P(⟨Mφ,φ⟩<ε∥φ∥_1^2)≤C_tilde ε^p exp(η∥w_0∥^2) for every random vector φ∈H_tilde satisfying ∥π_lφ∥≥α∥φ∥_1 almost surely, every ε∈(0,1), and every initial condition w_0∈H_tilde.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this result from Mattingly and Pardoux rather than proving it. It says that, with high probability, the Malliavin covariance is nondegenerate in directions that have a nontrivial low-mode component. In the proof of Proposition 4.3 it is used to show that applying β(β+M)^{−1} to such a direction either makes it very small or pushes its mass into high modes, where deterministic dissipation can contract it.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.17: high-mode damping for the Jacobian
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the Navier-Stokes derivative flow J_{0,T}, for every p≥1, T>0, and γ,η>0, there exists an orthogonal projection π_l onto finitely many Fourier modes such that E∥(1−π_l)J_{0,T}∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2) and E∥J_{0,T}(1−π_l)∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first estimate, the paper proves a smoothing bound for the Jacobian: ∥J_tξ∥_1^2≤C t^{−1} exp(η∫_0^1∥w_s∥_1^2ds)∥ξ∥^2 for t∈(0,1]. A high-mode projection π_N satisfies ∥π_Nξ∥≤N^{−1}∥ξ∥_1, so choosing N large and using the exponential integrability of ∫∥w_s∥_1^2 gives the bound. For the second estimate, decompose ξ_t into low and high parts. The high part has a term −νN^2∥ξ_t^h∥^2 from the Laplacian, plus lower-order couplings controlled by ∥w_t∥_{1/2}; the low part starts at zero and is generated only through coupling to the high part. Gronwall, interpolation, exponential energy estimates, and a sufficiently large cutoff N make both contributions arbitrarily small in L^p up to the factor exp(η∥w_0∥^2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.18: regularized Malliavin inverse kills low-mode components
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix ξ∈H_tilde and define ζ=β(β+M_0)^{−1}J_hat_0ξ, where M_0 is the Malliavin matrix on [0,1/2], J_hat_0 is the derivative flow over [0,1/2], and β>0. For every γ,η>0, every p≥1, and every finite-dimensional low-mode projection π_l, there exists β>0 such that E∥π_lζ∥^p≤γ exp(η∥w_0∥^2)∥ξ∥^p.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let A_α be the event ∥π_lζ∥>α∥ζ∥_1, and split ζ=ζ_α+ζ_bar_α according to A_α and its complement. On the complement, ∥π_lζ_bar_α∥≤α∥ζ∥_1, and the Jacobian bounds allow α to be chosen so small that this part has the required expectation. On A_α, the vector ζ_α has a significant low-mode component, so Theorem 4.12 applies. The identity ⟨ζ,M_0ζ⟩≤β∥J_hat_0ξ∥^2 follows from ζ=β(β+M_0)^{−1}J_hat_0ξ. Combining this with the Malliavin lower-tail estimate gives high inverse-moment bounds of the form E[(∥ζ_α∥_1/∥J_hat_0ξ∥)^p]≤Cβ^q exp(η∥w_0∥^2). Choosing β small then makes E∥π_lζ_α∥^p arbitrarily small, and the two pieces together give the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.16: one-step contraction of the controlled residual
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the residual recursion ρ_{n+1}=J_check_n β(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n used in the proof of Proposition 4.3, for every γ,η>0 and every p≥1 there exists β_0>0 such that, whenever β≤β_0, E(∥ρ_{n+1}∥^p | F_n)≤γ exp(η∥w_n∥^2)∥ρ_n∥^p almost surely.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Set ζ_n=β(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n, so ρ_{n+1}=J_check_nζ_n. Basic Jacobian estimates give E(∥ζ_n∥^p|F_n)≤C exp((η/2)∥w_n∥^2)∥ρ_n∥^p uniformly in β. Choose a finite-dimensional projection π_l using Lemma 4.17 so that J_check_n(1−π_l) is small in conditional L^p; this controls the high-mode part of ζ_n after the second half-step. Then Lemma 4.18, with β small, makes the low-mode part π_lζ_n small before applying J_check_n. A final Jacobian estimate propagates the low part through J_check_n. Combining the two contributions yields the stated conditional contraction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.13: exponential decay of the residual error
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the construction of Proposition 4.3, for any η>0 there exist β>0 and C>0 such that E∥ρ_N∥^{10}≤C exp(η∥w_0∥^2)2^{−N} for every integer N≥0. The paper notes that by decreasing β further, the factor 2^N can be replaced by K^N for any fixed K≥2, without changing C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let C_n=∥ρ_{n+1}∥^{10}/∥ρ_n∥^{10}, with C_n=0 if ρ_n=0. Since ρ_0 has unit norm, ∥ρ_N∥^{10}=Π_{n=0}^{N−1}C_n. A crude Jacobian bound gives C_n≤exp(η∫_n^{n+1}∥w_s∥_1^2ds+C_η), uniformly in β. For a large R define a truncated-renormalized variable C_{n,R}=e^{−ηR} if ∥w_n∥^2≥2R and C_{n,R}=e^{ηR}C_n otherwise. Lemma 4.16 allows β to be chosen so that E(C_{n,R}^2|F_n)≤1/2. The crude bound also implies C_n≤C_{n,R} exp(η∫_n^{n+1}∥w_s∥_1^2ds+η∥w_n∥^2+C_η−ηR). Multiplying over n and using the inequality that a product is controlled by the product of C_{n,R}^2 plus exponential energy terms, the first contribution decays by the conditional second-moment bound, while the remaining energy contributions are controlled by Lemma 4.10. Choosing R large and then β small yields the exponential decay.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14: Skorokhod cost bound for the nonadapted control
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the nonadapted control v constructed in Proposition 4.3 and any η>0, there is C such that E|∫_0^N v(s)dW(s)|^2≤(C/β^2) exp(η∥w_0∥^2) Σ_{n=0}^∞ (E∥ρ_n∥^{10})^{1/5}, uniformly in N. The integral is a Skorokhod integral because v is generally nonadapted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Skorokhod isometry: E|∫_0^N v dW|^2 is bounded by E∥v∥_{L^2(0,N)}^2 plus the double integral over Malliavin derivatives D_s v_n(t) on each controlled half-interval [n,n+1/2]. The control is v_n=A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1}J_hat_nρ_n. Since ρ_n is F_n-measurable, its Malliavin derivative vanishes for s≥n. Differentiating v_n requires differentiating A_n, A_n^*, M_n=A_nA_n^*, and J_hat_n. The derivative of the inverse is D(β+M_n)^{−1}=−(β+M_n)^{−1}(DM_n)(β+M_n)^{−1}. The operator inequalities ∥A_n^*(β+M_n)^{−1/2}∥≤1, ∥(β+M_n)^{−1/2}A_n∥≤1, and ∥(β+M_n)^{−1/2}∥≤β^{−1/2} yield ∥D_s v_n∥≤3β^{−1}∥D_sA_n∥∥J_hat_n∥∥ρ_n∥+β^{−1/2}∥D_sJ_hat_n∥∥ρ_n∥. Malliavin derivative formulas express D_sA_n and D_sJ_hat_n through the Hessian K and Jacobian J. Lemma 4.10 bounds the resulting moments, and Hölder gives E∥D_s v_n∥^2≤C e^{η∥w_0∥^2}β^{−2}(E∥ρ_n∥^{10})^{1/5}. The same type of bound controls E∥v_n∥^2, and summing over n gives the result.

proof_techniques.name: Asymptotic strong Feller uniqueness criterion
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the classical strong Feller plus irreducibility route by a weaker asymptotic condition. The key is to measure transition probabilities using a sequence of Wasserstein-type distances associated with pseudo-metrics d_n that increase to the discrete metric. If the semigroup asymptotically makes transition laws from nearby initial points close in these increasingly separating distances, then two distinct ergodic invariant measures cannot both have the same point in their supports, because Corollary 3.5 would force their total variation distance to be strictly less than 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, it would be enough to prove that P_t is asymptotically strong Feller and that all invariant measures have a common support point, such as 0. This bypasses the need to prove full topological irreducibility or ordinary strong Feller if the singular Dirac drift makes those difficult.

proof_techniques.name: Gradient estimate with a decaying derivative-loss term
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.12 shows that a bound of the form |∇P_{t_n}φ(x)|≤C(∥x∥)(∥φ∥_∞+δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞), with δ_n→0, is sufficient for asymptotic strong Fellerness. The ∥φ∥_∞ term represents genuine probabilistic smoothing, while the δ_n∥∇φ∥_∞ term represents a residual unsmoothed component that is damped by long-time dynamics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the stochastic heat equation with additive noise, one can try to prove this estimate directly, or first for smooth approximating drifts G_{1/n} and then pass to the limiting semigroup. The heat operator's high-mode dissipation is naturally suited to making the derivative-loss term decay.

proof_techniques.name: Common support point from Lyapunov bounds plus small-ball accessibility
proof_techniques.description: In Corollary 4.2, the authors prove that 0 belongs to the support of every invariant measure by combining a uniform invariant-measure moment bound with a positive-probability reachability estimate. The moment bound ensures that every invariant measure assigns uniformly positive mass to a fixed bounded ball. A separate controllability/small-ball result says that from any point in that ball, the process has positive probability to enter any prescribed neighborhood of 0 after some time. Invariance then forces every invariant measure to charge every neighborhood of 0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, an analogous plan is to establish a Lyapunov or moment bound under invariant measures and a support or small-noise argument showing positive probability of reaching a neighborhood of 0 from bounded initial data. This would verify the weak irreducibility hypothesis of Corollary 3.17.

proof_techniques.name: Approximate integration by parts instead of exact Bismut formula
proof_techniques.description: The paper derives an identity in which an initial-condition derivative is partially represented by a Malliavin derivative in the noise direction, leaving a residual ρ_t. The derivative of P_tφ is bounded by a noise-shift cost involving ∫v dW and by ∥∇φ∥∞ times E∥ρ_t∥. If v is chosen so that the residual decays as t→∞ and the noise-shift cost remains bounded, the estimate has exactly the structure needed for asymptotic strong Fellerness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the singular drift prevents a clean strong Feller proof, one can try to construct an approximate control v for the regularized equations and obtain residual decay from the heat semigroup. The assumed convergence of regularized solutions may then help transfer the resulting smoothing or ASF bounds to the limiting δ_0 drift equation.

proof_techniques.name: High-low mode splitting
proof_techniques.description: The Navier-Stokes proof separates finitely many low modes, where randomness or Malliavin covariance must provide smoothing, from high modes, where the Laplacian gives strong deterministic contraction. This splitting appears both in the adapted essentially elliptic argument and in the hypoelliptic regularized-Malliavin-inverse construction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The stochastic heat equation has an even more explicit spectral gap and high-mode damping under Dirichlet boundary conditions. A proof for the original problem may only need to control finitely many low Dirichlet modes probabilistically while using heat dissipation for the rest.

proof_techniques.name: Adapted control in the essentially elliptic case
proof_techniques.description: When all sufficiently many low modes are directly forced, Proposition 4.11 constructs an adapted Cameron-Martin shift that kills the low-mode part of an initial perturbation in finite time. Since the control is adapted, the cost is estimated by the ordinary Itô isometry rather than by Skorokhod calculus. High modes then decay exponentially by dissipation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Space-time white noise is nondegenerate in all Dirichlet modes, so an adapted-control or Bismut-type argument may be feasible for smooth approximations of the original SPDE. This could yield strong Feller or at least the gradient estimate in Proposition 3.12.

proof_techniques.name: Regularized Malliavin inverse for degenerate or nonadapted smoothing
proof_techniques.description: In the fully hypoelliptic Navier-Stokes argument, the authors avoid assuming that the Malliavin matrix is globally invertible. They use (β+M)^{−1} with β>0 on short time intervals, accepting an error β(β+M)^{−1} that is then shown to be either small in low modes or shifted to high modes. The construction is nonadapted, so Skorokhod integral estimates and Malliavin derivatives of the control are required.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the limiting δ_0 drift equation lacks a tractable global Malliavin inverse, a regularized inverse on finite-dimensional projections may still be enough. This is most relevant if one proves uniform estimates for the smooth G_{1/n} approximations and cannot pass exact strong Feller estimates to the limit.

proof_techniques.name: Strong Feller as a sufficient route through ASF
proof_techniques.description: The paper explicitly notes that strong Feller semigroups on Polish spaces are asymptotically strong Feller after composing the transition operator with itself. Therefore all ASF uniqueness results can be used even if one proves ordinary strong Feller by more classical methods.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the ABLM24 framework or the G_{1/n} approximations can be used to prove strong Feller for P_t, then the paper's Corollary 3.17 reduces uniqueness to the common-support-point condition.

other_useful_info: The paper's weak irreducibility condition is much weaker than full topological irreducibility: it only requires a point belonging to the support of every invariant measure. Examples 3.13-3.15 show that asymptotic strong Fellerness can hold even when strong Fellerness fails, including an infinite-dimensional Ornstein-Uhlenbeck example where transition laws from different initial data can be singular at finite times but the deterministic contraction still gives ASF. The proof of Corollary 4.2 uses a small-ball/accessibility lemma from E and Mattingly 2001 and standard invariant-measure energy estimates; this pattern is likely more transferable to dissipative SPDEs than the paper's Fourier-geometry analysis for Navier-Stokes. The paper cites Da Prato-Zabczyk for classical strong Feller/ergodicity tools, Hairer 2002 for asymptotic coupling, and Mattingly-Pardoux 2006 for the Malliavin matrix lower-tail estimate used in the hypoelliptic proof.
why_relevant: This paper introduces the asymptotic strong Feller property and proves that it plus weak irreducibility yields uniqueness of the invariant measure. The abstract criterion is useful if the singular drift prevents proving ordinary strong Feller for Pt.

title: Approximation and Support Theorem in Holder Norm for Parabolic Stochastic Partial Differential Equations
why_relevant: This classical support theorem for parabolic SPDEs in Hölder norms gives the control/Wong-Zakai machinery typically used to prove topological irreducibility. It is useful for showing that transition probabilities from different initial data have overlapping support.

title: Strong Feller Property and Irreducibility for Diffusions on Hilbert Spaces
why_relevant: This is a foundational result proving strong Feller and irreducibility for nondegenerate stochastic evolution equations, implying uniqueness of invariant measures. It is directly applicable to smooth drift approximations and provides the standard Hilbert-space strategy.

title: Coupling and Invariant Measures for the Heat Equation with Noise
why_relevant: Mueller’s paper is an early direct coupling proof of uniqueness/mixing for stochastic heat equations with noise. Its coupling ideas are highly relevant if one tries to prove uniqueness of the invariant measure without relying solely on strong Feller theory.

title: Irreducibility and strong Feller property for stochastic evolution equations in Banach spaces
why_relevant: The state space in the problem is C0([0,1]), a Banach space rather than just a Hilbert space. This paper gives Banach-space strong Feller/irreducibility criteria and applies them to stochastic nonlinear heat equations.

title: The support of singular stochastic partial differential equations
overall_summary: Hairer and Schönbauer prove a Stroock-Varadhan type support theorem for a broad class of subcritical singular SPDEs formulated via regularity structures, including equations where renormalisation is unavoidable. The core result identifies the support of a renormalised limiting model, and hence of the SPDE solution under a continuous solution map, as the closure of deterministic controlled equations with smooth controls and renormalisation constants lying in a specific coset of a subgroup H of the renormalisation group. The proof combines analytic BPHZ convergence, a continuous shift operator on model space, Cameron-Martin support arguments, and a delicate construction of high-frequency random shifts that realise prescribed finite renormalisation constants. For the original invariant-measure problem, the most useful extracted content is the paper’s mechanism for proving irreducibility/full support from controlled equations, together with its application showing uniqueness of invariant measures once full support is combined with strong Feller and a compactness or coming-down-from-infinity property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4, Shift operator on admissible models
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T be the regularity structure associated in the paper to a subcritical singular SPDE of the form ∂ₜuᵢ = Lᵢuᵢ + Fᵢ(u,∇u,...) + Σⱼ Fᵢʲ(u,∇u,...) ξⱼ, with reduced admissible model space M₀. Let Ω∞ be the space of smooth deterministic noises indexed by the noise types. For every smooth noise h in Ω∞ there exists a continuous operator T_h: M₀ → M₀ such that for every smooth noise f in Ω∞ and every renormalisation character g in the renormalisation group G₋, one has R_g Z_c(f+h) = T_h R_g Z_c(f) = R_g T_h Z_c(f), where Z_c(f) is the canonical lift of f to an admissible model and R_g is the action of G₋ on M₀. Moreover, the map (h,Z) ↦ T_h Z is continuous from Ω∞ × M₀ to M₀.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper constructs an enlarged regularity structure by adding, for each original noise type Ξ, a shifted noise type Ξ-bar. The shifted noise types are assigned sufficiently large positive homogeneity so that the negative renormalisation sector is unchanged.
