Given an admissible model Z, represented by a linear map Π, and a smooth function h, one first extends Z uniquely to a model Y(h,Π) on the enlarged structure by requiring that the original symbols are represented as in Z and that each shifted noise symbol Ξ-bar is represented by the corresponding smooth component h_Ξ. This extension uses the deterministic reconstruction and integration maps of regularity structures and is continuous in (h,Z). One then applies the canonical contraction map that replaces each occurrence of an original noise plus shifted noise by their sum, thereby obtaining a model on the original structure. This defines T_h. For canonical smooth models, this construction is exactly the identity Z_c(f+h) = T_h Z_c(f). Since renormalisation acts on the negative sector and the shift construction is natural with respect to the coproducts, T_h commutes with R_g, giving the displayed identities. Continuity follows from the continuity of the extension and contraction maps.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.13, Support under continuous maps
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X and Y be Polish spaces, let T: X → Y be continuous, and let μ be a probability measure on X. Then the topological support of the push-forward T_*μ is exactly the closure of T(supp μ), that is supp(T_*μ) = closure(T(supp μ)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For μ-almost every x, x lies in supp μ, hence T(x) lies in closure(T(supp μ)); this gives supp(T_*μ) contained in closure(T(supp μ)). Conversely, if y = T(x) with x in supp μ and U is an open neighbourhood of y, then T^{-1}(U) is an open neighbourhood of x, so μ(T^{-1}(U)) > 0. Therefore T_*μ(U) > 0, proving y is in supp(T_*μ); taking closures gives the reverse inclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.12, Smooth shifts preserve the support of the limiting model
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ξ be the underlying Gaussian noise and let Z-hat be the limiting BPHZ-renormalised random model. For every deterministic smooth noise h, one has T_h Z-hat[ξ] = Z-hat[ξ+h] almost surely, where T_h is the shift operator of Theorem 2.4. Moreover, if h is any smooth random noise, then supp(T_h Z-hat) is contained in supp(Z-hat).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For deterministic smooth h, Cameron-Martin equivalence gives that the laws of ξ and ξ+h are mutually absolutely continuous, so Z-hat[ξ+h] is well-defined on a full-measure event. For mollified approximations ξ^ε and h^ε, the identity T_{h^ε} Z-hat^ε[ξ] = Z-hat^ε[ξ+h] follows from the smooth canonical model identity in Theorem 2.4. Letting ε → 0 and using convergence of BPHZ models and continuity of T_h yields T_h Z-hat[ξ] = Z-hat[ξ+h]. Since ξ and ξ+h have equivalent laws, Z-hat and T_h Z-hat have the same support for deterministic h. For random smooth h, choose a full-probability set on which Z-hat(ω) belongs to supp Z-hat. For each fixed value h(ω), the deterministic shift T_{h(ω)} maps supp Z-hat into itself. Hence T_{h(ω)}Z-hat(ω) lies in supp Z-hat almost surely, proving the support inclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8, Deterministic renormalised zero models lie in the support
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be the Lie subgroup of the renormalisation group G₋ defined as the annihilator of the ideal J of linear constraints among renormalisation constants, and let f_ξ be the character appearing in Assumption 8 of the paper. For every character g in the left coset f_ξ ∘ H, the deterministic model 𝒵(g) := R_g Z_c(0), obtained by applying the renormalisation action R_g to the canonical model over the zero noise, belongs to the support of the limiting BPHZ random model Z-hat.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proposition is proved by constructing, for a fixed h in f_ξ ∘ H, random smooth shifts ζ^δ of the original noise such that T_{ζ^δ} Z-hat converges in probability to 𝒵(h). Lemma 3.12 shows that every shifted model T_{ζ^δ} Z-hat has support contained in supp Z-hat; since supp Z-hat is closed, the limit 𝒵(h) lies in supp Z-hat. The construction of ζ^δ is split into Proposition 3.20 and Proposition 3.21. Proposition 3.20 builds shifts that cancel the original noise in the rough-noise topology and tune the expected values of a basis of negative trees to match h. Proposition 3.21 upgrades these moment identities to convergence of the shifted models to R_h Z_c(0), using the algebraic structure of the ideal J and the BPHZ theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.20, Construction of shifts with prescribed renormalisation constants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a character h in the coset f_ξ ∘ H. There exists a family of smooth random noises ζ^δ, δ > 0, in the paper’s space of shifted smooth noises such that ξ + ζ^δ converges to 0 in the rough-noise space M₀ as δ → 0, and such that for every tree τ in the selected finite set 𝔗₋ of negative trees complementary to the ideal J, lim_{δ→0} lim_{ε→0} Υ_{ξ^ε+ζ^δ} M_{g^ε} τ = h(τ). Here ξ^ε is a smooth approximation of ξ, g^ε is its BPHZ character, M_{g^ε} is the corresponding renormalisation map on trees, and Υ_η σ denotes E[(Π^η σ)(0)] for the canonical model over the smooth noise η.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is carried out in Section 5. The noise type set is enlarged so that, besides the original noise Ξ and a cancelling copy Ξ-tilde representing -ξ^δ, there are extra types (Ξ,τ) indexed by negative trees τ in 𝔗₋. The shift is ζ^δ = -ξ^δ plus high-frequency random perturbations k^δ_{Ξ,τ}; each perturbation is placed in a homogeneous Wiener chaos of order equal to the number of leaves of τ minus one. Its stochastic integral kernel is a rescaled compactly supported kernel Φ_{Ξ,τ}, with scale λ^δ_τ much smaller than δ and amplitude a^δ_τ. Lemma 5.10 chooses Φ_{Ξ,τ} so that the dominant contribution to Υ M_{g^ε} 𝒮τ is diagonal: it equals a_τ λ_τ^{-κ} plus lower-order terms. The remaining non-dominant contributions are estimated in Lemma 5.17 by using separated scales ordered by homogeneity and number of leaves. A Schauder fixed point argument in Proposition 5.19 then chooses amplitudes a^δ_τ and scales λ^δ_τ so that the prescribed values h(τ) are attained while a^δ_τ → 0. The smallness of amplitudes and weak/high-frequency nature of the perturbations give ξ+ζ^δ → 0 in M₀.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.21, Shift moment conditions imply convergence to a deterministic model
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ζ^δ be a family of smooth random shifts satisfying the two conclusions of Proposition 3.20 for a character h in f_ξ ∘ H. Then T_{ζ^δ} Z-hat converges in probability, in the admissible model topology, to 𝒵(h) = R_h Z_c(0) as δ → 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let g^ε be the BPHZ character of ξ^ε and let g^{ε,δ} be the BPHZ character of ξ^ε+ζ^δ. Define h^{ε,δ} in G₋ by h^{ε,δ} ∘ g^{ε,δ} = g^ε. The key induction, ordered by the paper’s total order on negative trees, proves lim_{δ→0} lim_{ε→0} h^{ε,δ}(τ) = h(τ) for every negative tree τ. For τ in 𝔗₋, this follows directly from the prescribed identities in Proposition 3.20 and from the fact that the renormalised expectation of a nontrivial tree for a noise converging to 0 vanishes. For τ not in 𝔗₋, by construction there is a lower-order linear combination σ with τ+σ in J. Assumption 8 and the Hopf-ideal property of J imply that a suitable conjugate of h^{ε,δ} annihilates J; hence the limiting value on τ is forced by the already known lower-order values. Finally, the shifted model satisfies T_{ζ^δ} Z-hat^ε = R_{h^{ε,δ}} R_{g^{ε,δ}} Z_c(ξ^ε+ζ^δ). Since ξ^ε+ζ^δ tends to 0 in the noise topology, the analytic BPHZ theorem gives R_{g^{ε,δ}} Z_c(ξ^ε+ζ^δ) → Z_c(0), while h^{ε,δ} → h and the renormalisation action is continuous. Passing ε → 0 and then δ → 0 yields the convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.14, Support theorem for random BPHZ models
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Z-hat^ε be the BPHZ-renormalised admissible model associated to a smooth approximation ξ^ε of the Gaussian noise ξ, and let Z-hat be its limiting BPHZ model. For every renormalisation character k in G₋, supp(R_k Z-hat) = ⋂_{ε>0} closure(⋃_{δ<ε} supp(R_k Z-hat^δ)) in the model topology. Moreover, if f_ε := f_{ξ^ε} is the family of characters from Assumption 8 and f_ε → f_ξ, then the stronger identity supp(R_k Z-hat) = closure(⋃_{ε∈(0,1)} supp(R_{k ∘ f_ξ ∘ (f_ε)^{-1}} Z-hat^ε)) holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion of supp(R_k Z-hat) in the right-hand side follows from weak convergence of the approximating models, together with closedness of supports. For the reverse inclusion, write the support of a smooth renormalised model using Lemma 3.11 as smooth shifts of deterministic renormalised zero models: Z-hat^ε = T_{ξ^ε}𝒵(g^ε). By Assumption 8, g^ε = f_ε ∘ ĝ^ε with ĝ^ε in H. Therefore R_{k ∘ f_ξ ∘ (f_ε)^{-1}} Z-hat^ε is supported on models of the form T_h 𝒵(k ∘ f_ξ ∘ ĝ^ε), with h smooth. Proposition 3.8 says 𝒵(k ∘ f_ξ ∘ ĝ^ε) lies in supp(R_k Z-hat), and Lemma 3.12 says smooth shifts preserve that support. This proves the stronger union identity. The first identity follows because f_ξ ∘ (f_ε)^{-1} tends to the identity and the renormalisation action is continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.6, Support of renormalised SPDE solutions as a limit of approximate supports
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under Assumption 1 of the paper, namely the structural assumptions of Bruned-Chandra-Chevyrev-Hairer for the subcritical SPDE class plus the paper’s Assumptions 2–5, 7 and 8, let u^ε be the classical solution to the regularised and renormalised equation ∂ₜuᵢ^ε = Lᵢuᵢ^ε + Fᵢ(u^ε,∇u^ε,...) + Σⱼ Fᵢʲ(u^ε,∇u^ε,...) ξⱼ^ε + [Υ(h ∘ g^ε_BPHZ,F)]ᵢ(u^ε,∇u^ε,...) for a fixed h in G₋, and let u be the limit as ε → 0 in a solution space X for which the model-to-solution map is continuous. Then supp u = ⋂_{ε>0} closure(⋃_{δ<ε} supp u^δ) in X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The solution u^ε is obtained by applying the continuous solution map of the regularity-structure fixed point problem to the renormalised model R_h Z-hat^ε, and u is the corresponding image of R_h Z-hat. Theorem 3.14 gives the analogous support identity at the level of models. Lemma 3.13 states that the support of the push-forward of a measure under a continuous map is the closure of the image of the original support. Applying this to the solution map and using its continuity gives the displayed identity for solutions. The paper also notes that a slightly tweaked choice of finite counterterms gives approximating supports already contained in supp u for each ε.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.7, Stroock-Varadhan type support theorem for singular SPDE solutions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under Assumption 1, let u be the limiting solution obtained from a renormalisation procedure with fixed h in G₋, as in Theorem 1.6. Then there exist a Lie subgroup H of the renormalisation group G₋ and a character f in G₋, independent of h, such that supp u is the closure of all deterministic controlled solutions φ of ∂ₜφᵢ = Lᵢφᵢ + Fᵢ(φ,∇φ,...) + Σ_{j≤n} Fᵢʲ(φ,∇φ,...) ψⱼ + (Υᵢ k)(φ,∇φ,...), where k ranges over the coset h ∘ f ∘ H, the controls ψⱼ are smooth deterministic functions, depending only on space when ξⱼ is purely spatial white noise, and the initial condition φ(0,·) ranges over the set Φ₀^k specified in Remark 1.8 of the paper. When the SPDE admits deterministic fixed initial data without a rough stationary correction, Φ₀^k is just that fixed initial datum.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The model support theorem identifies the support of the renormalised model with the closure of renormalised canonical lifts R_k Z_c(ψ) where ψ is a smooth deterministic control and k lies in the coset h ∘ f ∘ H. For a smooth model R_k Z_c(ψ), the reconstruction of the abstract fixed point is exactly the classical deterministic PDE displayed in the statement, with ψ replacing the noise and Υ k supplying the deterministic counterterm induced by renormalisation. The continuity of the solution map transfers the model support to solution support via Lemma 3.13. The only extra point is the initial condition: in theories where solutions are decomposed into a rough stationary part plus a remainder, the admissible controlled initial values Φ₀^k are obtained by applying the same model-dependent stationary rough-part map to smooth canonical models; when no such rough correction is needed, the controlled problem starts from the original deterministic initial datum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.12, Full support for Φᵖ_d equations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the Φᵖ_d equation ∂ₜu = Δu + Σ_{1≤k≤p−1} a_k u^k + ξ on a finite-volume torus, in the cases d = 2 with p even, d = 3 with p = 4, or formal dimension d = 4−ε with p = 4 as treated in the paper, with a_{p−1} < 0 and with the BPHZ solution theory. Let u₀ have regularity η > −2/3 for d = 2,3 and η > −(ε∧2/3) for d = 4−ε, and let u be the solution with initial condition u₀ plus the stationary rough part S⁻(ξ)(0,·) when that formulation is used. Then for every T > 0, u has full support in the parabolic Hölder space C^α_s((0,T)×T^d), where α = (2−d)/2 − κ for arbitrary κ > 0. For d = 2,3, when the equation is started from a fixed deterministic initial condition u₀ in C^η(T^d) with η in (−2/3, α], the support in C([0,T],C^η(T^d)) is exactly the set of functions whose value at time 0 is u₀.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Global existence for the equations is taken from known results cited in the paper. Theorem 1.7 gives a controlled deterministic description of the support. Because the noise is additive, smooth controls enter additively, and one can realise arbitrary smooth target trajectories by choosing the control to match the residual ∂ₜv − Δv − polynomial drift along the target v; smooth trajectories are dense in the stated Hölder spaces. Hence the support is full in the interior-time topology. For the fixed deterministic initial-condition statement, one must work with a slightly stronger model topology ensuring continuity of the solution map at time zero; Appendix C.1 verifies that the support theorem remains valid in that stronger topology for Φ⁴_3 and analogous cases, and the controlled paths then preserve exactly the initial value u₀.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.13, Uniqueness and exponential ergodicity for Φᵖ_d
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the Φᵖ_d setting of Theorem 1.12 with d ≤ 3 and a_{p−1} < 0, the equation admits a unique invariant probability measure μ on C^α(T^d). Moreover, if p ≥ 4, then the transition law from any initial condition u₀ in C^α(T^d) converges uniformly exponentially fast to μ in total variation: ||(u_t)_*P − μ||_{TV} ≤ 1 ∧ C exp(−λt) for some C,λ > 0 independent of t and u₀.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Doeblin’s theorem. It suffices to prove that for some fixed time t and some δ > 0, the total variation distance between the transition laws from any two initial conditions v,w is at most 1−δ. A coming-down-from-infinity estimate gives a compact set K in C^α(T^d) such that inf_v P(u^v_1 ∈ K) ≥ 1/2. The strong Feller property, cited from Hairer-Mattingly and Tsatsoulis-Weber, gives continuity of transition probabilities in total variation; hence there is an ε-ball B_ε around 0 such that transition laws after time 1 from any two points in B_ε differ in total variation by at most 1/2. By the full-support theorem, P(u^v_1 ∈ B_ε) > 0 for every v in K, and by compactness and total-variation continuity the infimum ρ := inf_{v∈K} P(u^v_1 ∈ B_ε) is positive. Combining these three steps at times 1+1+1 yields a uniform overlap of transition probabilities and hence the Doeblin minorisation with δ proportional to ρ. Doeblin’s theorem then gives uniqueness of the invariant measure and exponential total-variation convergence.

proof_techniques.name: Reduce solution support to model support via continuity
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses the principle that if a random model Z has a known support and the SPDE solution is obtained as a continuous function Φ(Z) of the model, then supp Φ(Z) is the closure of Φ(supp Z). This is Lemma 3.13 applied to the regularity-structure solution map. The model-level support theorem is therefore the fundamental object; once it is known, solution-level support and controlled PDE descriptions follow by deterministic continuity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original SPDE, an analogous route would be to identify a topology and a continuous map from the driving white noise, or from an enhanced object if needed, to the mild solution. Then irreducibility or support properties can be proved at the noise/enhanced-noise level and pushed forward to the solution.

proof_techniques.name: Cameron-Martin shifts plus a continuous shift operator
proof_techniques.description: The paper constructs a deterministic shift operator T_h on the space of admissible models, satisfying T_h Z_c(f) = Z_c(f+h) and commuting with renormalisation. For deterministic Cameron-Martin shifts h, the laws of ξ and ξ+h are equivalent, so T_h preserves the model support. For random smooth shifts h(ω), the same pointwise support preservation holds because every deterministic smooth shift maps the support into itself.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an additive-noise heat equation, Cameron-Martin shifts are the classical mechanism for showing that the support contains solutions driven by smooth deterministic controls. This is a direct route to irreducibility if the deterministic controlled equation can reach neighbourhoods of arbitrary target states.

proof_techniques.name: Support through controlled deterministic equations
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 1.7 turns support questions into deterministic control questions. The support is the closure of solutions obtained by replacing the stochastic noises by smooth controls and allowing finitely many renormalisation constants in a specified coset. In additive-noise examples such as Φᵖ_d, this immediately yields full support because the control can be chosen to force a smooth target trajectory.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original SPDE has additive space-time white noise and a singular drift in the state variable. If a support theorem can be established for its weak mild solution, the corresponding controlled equation would be the heat equation with the same drift term and an added smooth control; showing approximate controllability would imply topological irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Renormalisation-coset support rather than a single deterministic equation
proof_techniques.description: A central point of the paper is that for singular SPDEs, the naive support obtained by simply replacing noise with smooth controls is usually wrong because renormalisation constants can affect the deterministic equation. The correct support is described using a subgroup H of the renormalisation group and a coset fH. The subgroup H is defined as the annihilator of an ideal J encoding linear constraints among renormalisation constants, and the paper proves under its assumptions that J is a Hopf ideal and that BPHZ characters lie in a controlled coset of H.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If approximating the δ₀ drift by smooth drifts creates correction terms or local-time-like constants in a limiting support theorem, this paper suggests not fixing one deterministic limiting control equation prematurely; one should identify all finite constants that can be generated by approximations or high-frequency controls.

proof_techniques.name: High-frequency random shifts to realise finite counterterms
proof_techniques.description: To prove lower support bounds, the paper constructs random smooth shifts ζ^δ that both cancel the original noise in a rough topology and produce prescribed limiting renormalisation constants. These shifts are made from high-frequency perturbations in fixed Wiener chaoses. Their kernels are scaled so that selected negative trees give dominant diagonal contributions, while all remaining contributions are controlled by separating the scales according to homogeneity and using a fixed point argument for the amplitudes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the singular drift equation is approached through smooth approximations and one needs to show that the limiting process has at least as large a support as controlled approximations, a simplified version of this high-frequency-shift idea may help construct controls or perturbations that survive the singular limit.

proof_techniques.name: Doeblin argument from strong Feller plus full support plus compact return
proof_techniques.description: In Corollary 1.13, uniqueness and exponential convergence of an invariant measure are obtained by combining three ingredients: a compact set K reached with uniformly positive probability from every initial state, strong Feller continuity of transition probabilities in total variation, and full support/irreducibility guaranteeing that from every point of K there is positive probability to enter a fixed small ball. Compactness upgrades pointwise positivity to a uniform lower bound, yielding a Doeblin minorisation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original invariant-measure question, this is a direct template: prove strong Feller or an asymptotic strong Feller property, prove irreducibility/full support, and prove tight return to a compact set or at least use an existing invariant measure with full support. Even without exponential convergence, strong Feller plus irreducibility is a standard path to at most one invariant measure.

proof_techniques.name: Full support in additive-noise equations via exact control of smooth paths
proof_techniques.description: In the Φᵖ_d application, additive noise means the deterministic controlled equation contains a free smooth forcing term. For any smooth desired trajectory v, the control can be chosen as ∂ₜv − Δv minus the deterministic drift evaluated at v, so v itself solves the controlled equation. Density of smooth trajectories then gives full support in the target Hölder topology.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original equation is also additively forced. If the drift term δ₀(u) can be interpreted along sufficiently smooth controlled trajectories or via the same weak-solution framework, a similar residual-control argument may yield dense reachability.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that many support theorems for non-renormalised SPDEs already existed, citing Bally-Millet-Sanz-Solé 1995, Cardon-Weber-Millet 2001, Chueshov-Millet 2011, and Delgado-Vences-Sanz-Solé 2014. For singular SPDEs, it cites Chouk-Friz 2018 for a support theorem for the 2D generalized parabolic Anderson model and Tsatsoulis-Weber 2018 for full support of Φ⁴_2. The strong Feller input used in the ergodicity application is cited from Hairer-Mattingly 2018, and the Doeblin theorem reference is Hairer’s 2016 lecture notes on convergence of Markov processes. The paper’s main theorems are formulated for locally subcritical regularity-structure SPDEs with singular driving noise and renormalisation; the δ₀(u) drift in the original problem is not itself treated in this paper, so its content is most directly useful as a support/irreducibility and ergodicity strategy rather than as a directly applicable theorem.
why_relevant: This gives a modern support-theorem framework for singular SPDEs. Although aimed at regularity-structures settings, its support/controlled-equation methods are relevant to proving irreducibility for singular limits rather than only for smooth approximations.

title: Uniqueness of the Invariant Measure for a Stochastic PDE Driven by Degenerate Noise
overall_summary: The paper proves uniqueness of the invariant Borel probability measure for a stochastic Ginzburg-Landau equation on a one-dimensional periodic domain when the additive noise is highly degenerate: it may vanish on all low Fourier modes but acts on every sufficiently high Fourier mode with polynomially decaying amplitudes. The proof follows the classical route strong Feller plus irreducibility implies uniqueness, but both ingredients are nontrivial because the noise is degenerate and the state space is infinite-dimensional. Irreducibility is obtained from an approximate controllability theorem using the cubic nonlinearity to transfer high-frequency controls into low frequencies. Strong Feller is proved by localizing with a cutoff equation, proving a smoothing estimate for the cutoff semigroup via a low/high frequency splitting, a partial Malliavin calculus, a restricted Hörmander bracket condition, and Norris-type lower-tail estimates for a partial Malliavin matrix, then removing the cutoff by moment bounds. For the original problem, the most transferable content is the uniqueness criterion strong Feller plus irreducibility, the localization argument from cutoff to original dynamics, and the use of approximation/smoothing estimates to get strong Feller for singular or degenerate SPDEs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S be the real Hilbert space of even H^1-periodic functions on the interval [-π,π], represented in the cosine Fourier basis. Let A be the positive diagonal operator with eigenvalues 1+k^2, let F be the Nemytskii polynomial nonlinearity associated with an odd polynomial of negative leading coefficient, and let B be a diagonal noise operator with coefficients b_k. Assume the noise may vanish on finitely many low modes but satisfies the paper's high-frequency hypotheses: for all sufficiently large k, b_k is nonzero, b_k^{-1} k^{-2α} is bounded for some α, and A^{α-3/4}B is Hilbert-Schmidt. Consider the Markov process ψ_t solving the stochastic PDE dψ_t = -Aψ_t dt + F(ψ_t)dt + B dW_t, where W is cylindrical Brownian motion on S. Then this process has a unique invariant Borel probability measure on S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is assembled in Section 4. Existence of at least one invariant measure is obtained from standard compactness criteria for dissipative SPDEs, quoted from Da Prato-Zabczyk: on a finite spatial domain the semigroup e^{-At} is compact, and the moment bounds of Proposition 2.3 provide tightness of time averages. Irreducibility is deduced from the approximate controllability theorem, Theorem 3.1, together with the support/controllability criterion quoted as DPZ96, Theorem 7.4.1: from every initial state the transition probability of every nonempty open set is positive. Strong Feller is Theorem 4.1. Once strong Feller and irreducibility are known, the paper invokes Khasminskii regularity and Doob's theorem, quoted from DPZ96, pp.42-43, to conclude that an invariant measure, if it exists, is unique. The proof uses no explicit formula for the invariant measure; the uniqueness mechanism is purely Markov-semigroup theoretic.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the deterministic real Ginzburg-Landau equation ∂_t u = Δu + u - u^3 on the periodic line, restricted to the even H^1-periodic space S, every initial condition u_0 in S becomes analytic for positive times. More precisely, for every u_0 in S there exist a positive time τ and a constant C such that, for 0<t≤τ, the solution u(t,u_0) belongs to a Banach space of functions analytic in a strip around the real axis, with the strip width proportional to t and with analytic norm bounded by C.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper does not reprove the analytic smoothing estimate. It cites Collet 1994 for the corresponding result on the infinite line. Periodic functions form an invariant subspace for the deterministic evolution, so the cited estimate applies to the periodic setting. This smoothing is used in the controllability proof: after a short uncontrolled evolution, the state is analytic, which allows one to apply controls involving B^{-1} on high modes despite polynomially decaying noise amplitudes.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the uncut stochastic equation dψ_t = -Aψ_t dt + F(ψ_t)dt + B dW_t on S, under the paper's noise assumptions and with polynomial drift F of odd degree and negative leading coefficient, the solution exists in S up to every finite time and defines a Markov transition semigroup. For every T>0 and every p≥1 there is a constant C_{T,p} such that E sup_{0≤t≤T} ||ψ_t(x)||^p ≤ C_{T,p}(1+||x||)^p for all initial data x in S. Moreover the process has at least one invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives only references and a sketch in Section 8.6. It uses known a priori bounds for stochastic Ginzburg-Landau equations, specifically Cerrai 1999 for L^∞-type estimates, then obtains S=H^1 bounds from the mild formulation by estimating e^{-At}x, the deterministic convolution with F(ψ), and the stochastic convolution using the stochastic-convolution estimate later formalized as Proposition 8.4. Existence of an invariant measure is then quoted from DPZ96, Theorem 6.3.5, using compactness of the semigroup generated by A on a bounded spatial domain and the moment bounds.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Consider the deterministic controlled Ginzburg-Landau equation on S, ∂_t u = Δu + u - u^3 + Bv(t), with initial condition u(0)=u_i. The control v is square-integrable in time and acts only through the directions where the diagonal noise operator B is nonzero; in particular the low Fourier modes may be uncontrolled. Under the same high-frequency nondegeneracy assumptions on B as in the stochastic problem, for every final time T>0, every initial state u_i in S, every target state u_f in S, and every ε>0, there exists a control v in L^2([0,T],S) such that the corresponding solution satisfies ||u(T)-u_f||<ε.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof splits the time interval into four phases. First, choose zero control for a short time; Lemma 2.2 makes the state analytic. Second, drive all high-frequency modes to zero while allowing the finite-dimensional low part to evolve: prescribe a smooth interpolation for the high part from its current value to zero, solve the finite-dimensional ODE for the low modes, and define the high-mode control by substituting the prescribed path into the PDE. Analyticity and the polynomial lower bound on the high-frequency noise amplitudes ensure that applying B^{-1} to the required high-frequency forcing gives an admissible L^2 control. Third, steer the low-frequency modes. For each low mode k, choose a disjoint triple of very high modes A_k. All modes in A_k are forced to be equal to a scalar function z_k(t), all other high modes are kept zero, and the combinatorial Lemma 3.3 ensures that the cubic interaction of that triple contributes to the k-th low mode and produces no unwanted low-mode interactions except simple damping terms. The low-mode equations reduce to a finite system where the cubic quantities z_k^3 can be chosen to shadow any prescribed smooth low-mode path; a Gröbner-basis argument shows the associated real polynomial equations have real solutions, and the chosen functions are then smoothed without losing the required accuracy. Fourth, after the low part is close to the target and the special high modes vanish, use high-mode controls again to match an analytic approximation of the target high-frequency component in a very short time; the low-mode displacement during this final adjustment is made smaller than ε by choosing the interval short.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each low Fourier mode k with 0≤k<k_0, the paper constructs a triple A_k of large, mutually separated high frequencies. The triples A_k and the low set A_0^low={0,...,k_0-1} are pairwise disjoint and have the following cubic-interaction property: if A_k={k_1,k_2,k_3}, then among the signed sums ±k_1±k_2±k_3 exactly one equals k and exactly one equals -k, while all other signed sums have modulus larger than k_0. Moreover, if three indices are chosen from the union of all these triples and the low set and a signed sum has modulus below k_0, then either the three indices are precisely one of the triples A_k, or all are low, or the collection is of the form {k,k',k'}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The triples are chosen at very widely separated scales, for instance using multiples of a large number such as 10k_0 plus the low index k. With this spacing, the only cancellation of the large parts in a signed sum of the three elements of one triple is the intended cancellation producing ±k. If indices come from two different triples, or from one high triple and unrelated low indices, the large parts cannot cancel sufficiently to produce a low frequency. The final exceptional case {k,k',k'} corresponds to the usual contribution of |z_{k'}|^2 z_k-type terms in the cubic nonlinearity. This lemma is the combinatorial backbone of the controllability proof.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The Markov transition semigroup P_tφ(x)=E φ(ψ_t(x)) associated with the original uncut stochastic Ginzburg-Landau equation is strong Feller on S: for every t>0 and every bounded Borel measurable φ:S→R, the function x↦P_tφ(x) is continuous on S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses cutoff dynamics. For a cutoff parameter R, define a modified equation that agrees with the original equation inside the ball ||x||≤R, truncates the polynomial drift at large amplitudes, and adds noise to the finitely many low modes at large amplitudes so that the cutoff equation satisfies a global Hörmander-type condition. Theorem 4.3 gives differentiability, hence continuity, of the cutoff semigroup P_t^R on bounded measurable functions. Fix x, a bounded φ with ||φ||∞≤1, and ε>0. By the moment bound of Proposition 2.3, choose R so large that, uniformly for y in a fixed neighborhood of x, the probability that the original solution exits the ball of radius R before time t is at most O(ε). The original and cutoff solutions coincide up to this exit time, hence |P_tφ(y)-P_t^Rφ(y)|≤O(ε) for y near x. Therefore |P_tφ(y)-P_tφ(x)| is bounded by two cutoff-error terms plus |P_t^Rφ(y)-P_t^Rφ(x)|. The cutoff term tends to zero as y→x by Theorem 4.3, and ε is arbitrary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff equation with cutoff parameter R, let P_t^R be its transition semigroup. There exist positive exponents r and s, and for every R a constant C_R, such that for every bounded Borel φ:S→R, every t>0, and every x in S, P_t^Rφ is Fréchet differentiable at x and ||D P_t^Rφ(x)|| ≤ C_R(1+t^{-r})(1+||x||^s)||φ||∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first establishes Proposition 5.2 for φ in C_b^2 and initial data in a smoother interpolation space S^α, with a derivative bound depending on ||x||_α. To pass from S^α initial data to arbitrary x in S, use the Markov property: P_{2t}^Rφ(x)=E[P_t^Rφ(X_t^R(x))]. Differentiate this expression with respect to x. Proposition 5.1 gives moment bounds for the derivative flow DX_t^R(x) and also the smoothing estimate that X_t^R(x) belongs to S^α for t>0 with ||X_t^R(x)||_α growing like a power of t^{-1}. Combining these estimates yields the stated bound for smooth φ and arbitrary x. Finally, a standard approximation lemma from DPZ96 extends the result from C_b^2 functions to all bounded Borel functions without changing the bound.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff stochastic Ginzburg-Landau equation, assume the paper's high-frequency noise condition. Let S^γ denote the interpolation/Sobolev scale defined by the graph norm of A^γ. For every p≥1 and every finite time T, the cutoff stochastic flow X_t^R has the following properties. If x belongs to S^γ with 0≤γ≤α, then X_t^R(x) stays in S^γ and E sup_{0≤t≤T} ||X_t^R(x)||_γ^p ≤ C_{T,p,R}(1+||x||_γ)^p. For arbitrary x in S, X_t^R(x) belongs to S^α for every positive time, and E sup_{0≤t≤T} t^{αp}||X_t^R(x)||_α^p ≤ C_{T,p,R}(1+||x||)^p. The map x↦X_t^R(x) has almost surely bounded derivatives, and E sup_{0≤t≤T} ||DX_t^R(x)h||^p ≤ C_{T,p,R}||h||^p. If x is in S^α, then DX_t^R(x)h also lies in S^α for positive times and satisfies a corresponding t^{αp}-weighted moment bound. Finally, for small times the nonlinear stochastic solution is close to the linear evolution e^{-At}x in S^γ in the sense that E sup_{0≤t≤δ} ||X_t^R(x)-e^{-At}x||_γ^p is bounded by C(1+||x||_γ)^p times a positive power of δ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is deferred to Section 8.4. It writes the cutoff equation in mild form and separates the stochastic convolution Z_t=∫_0^t e^{-A(t-s)}B_R(X_s)dW_s. Proposition 8.4 gives S^α regularity and moment bounds for Z. Subtracting Z from the solution leaves a deterministic mild equation with random forcing; Proposition 8.7 gives pathwise bounds in S^γ and smoothing from S to S^α. Differentiability of the flow is obtained by applying the general Hilbert-space SDE theorem, Theorem 8.9, to the linearized equation; global boundedness of derivatives of the cutoff coefficients is essential here. The S^α estimates for the derivative flow combine stochastic-convolution bounds for the differentiated noise term with deterministic smoothing estimates and an iteration in the Sobolev exponent. The small-time estimate follows directly from the mild formula by bounding the deterministic and stochastic convolution terms on [0,δ].

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff transition semigroup P_t^R there exist positive exponents r_0 and s_0 such that, for every φ in C_b^2(S), every x in S^α, and every t>0, ||D P_t^Rφ(x)|| ≤ C_R(1+t^{-r_0})(1+||x||_α^{s_0})||φ||∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is in Section 5.3 and combines high-frequency smoothing by a Bismut-Elworthy argument with low-frequency smoothing by the partial Malliavin calculus. The state space is split as S=S_low⊕S_high, where S_low is finite-dimensional and contains the low modes plus finitely many high modes needed for the bracket construction. For high-frequency derivatives, Itô's formula is applied to P_{t-s}^Rφ(X_s), then multiplied by a stochastic integral involving B_high^{-1}D_high X_s^high h over the middle portion of the interval. Taking expectations gives D_high P_t^Rφ plus a low-frequency correction. The high-frequency part is controlled using the lower bound on the high-mode noise amplitudes and derivative-flow estimates. The low-frequency correction is controlled by Proposition 5.3, while off-diagonal derivatives between low and high modes are controlled by Lemma 5.4. For low-frequency derivatives directly, Proposition 5.3 gives the required bound. The estimates are first obtained on a small time interval, where terms involving the unknown derivative norm can be absorbed, and then extended to arbitrary positive time by the semigroup property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3 / Proposition 7.12
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff equation, let X_t^R=(X_t^{low},X_t^{high}) be the low/high splitting of the stochastic flow. There exist positive exponents r and s such that, for every bounded C^2 function φ:S→R, every initial condition x in S^α, every finite T, and every 0<t≤T, the low-frequency derivative estimate |E[(D_low φ)(X_t^R(x)) · (D_low X_t^{low})(x)]| ≤ C_{T,R} t^{-r}(1+||x||_α^s)||φ||∞ holds, with the natural operator contraction over the finite-dimensional low variables.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is proved in Section 7.4 using the Bismut integration-by-parts formula of Proposition 6.1 and the partial Malliavin matrix estimate of Theorem 7.1. The argument augments the Markov process by adding the low-frequency Jacobian, the approximate inverse Jacobian, and the Malliavin derivative generated by the specially constructed adapted direction v from Theorem 6.6. One chooses an auxiliary test function equal to φ(X_t) times suitable matrix entries of the inverse partial Malliavin derivative and the low Jacobian. Applying Proposition 6.1 transfers D_low φ from the left-hand side to φ multiplied by a stochastic integral involving v, plus terms from differentiating the inverse Malliavin matrix and the Jacobian. The inverse moments of the partial Malliavin matrix are controlled by Theorem 7.1, and all remaining factors are controlled by the moment estimates for the flow and derivative flow. This yields a bound depending only on ||φ||∞, not on derivatives of φ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff flow X_t^R split as S_low⊕S_high, and for every p≥1 and finite T, there is a constant C_{T,p,R} such that for all initial data x in S, all h_low in S_low, all h_high in S_high, and all 0<t≤T, E sup_{0≤s≤t} ||D_low X_s^{high}(x)h_low||^p ≤ C_{T,p,R} t^p ||h_low||^p and E sup_{0≤s≤t} ||D_high X_s^{low}(x)h_high||^p ≤ C_{T,p,R} t^{p/4} ||h_high||^p.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is in Section 8.5. For D_high X^{low}, the low equation differentiated in a high direction has no initial component and is driven by integrals involving the bounded derivative of the cutoff drift and the differentiated full flow. The deterministic convolution yields a factor of t, while the stochastic term is estimated as in the derivative-flow stochastic convolution estimate, giving the weaker t^{1/4} power in L^p. For D_low X^{high}, the high noise coefficient is constant by construction of the cutoff, so no stochastic differentiated noise term appears; only the bounded drift derivative contributes, yielding the stronger t factor. The global boundedness of cutoff derivatives is essential for constants independent of the initial condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let ξ_t solve a Hilbert-space SDE dξ_t = (-Aξ_t+F(ξ_t))dt + B(ξ_t)dW_t on a Hilbert space S, where B maps the noise Hilbert space U into S and the coefficients have at most linear growth with bounded first and second derivatives. Let v_t be a predictable U^m-valued process with finite moments of all sup norms on compact time intervals. Define the directional Malliavin derivative D_v ξ_t in the directions v by the linearized equation obtained by adding B(ξ_t)v_t dt to the noise variation. If O is an open subset of S containing ξ_t almost surely and φ:O→R is differentiable with E|φ(ξ_t)|^2+E||Dφ(ξ_t)||^2 finite, then for every η in R^m, E[Dφ(ξ_t) D_vξ_t η] = E[φ(ξ_t) ∫_0^t ⟨v_s η,dW_s⟩_U].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the Bismut integration-by-parts formula in the form used by Norris. The paper notes that the finite-dimensional proof is classical and that the Hilbert-space extension is routine under the stated assumptions. The key idea is to perturb the Brownian path by an adapted Cameron-Martin drift ε∫_0^. v_sη ds. Girsanov's theorem gives the change of measure and differentiating at ε=0 yields the stochastic integral on the right-hand side. Differentiating the solution map with respect to ε gives precisely the directional derivative D_vξ_t on the left-hand side. The moment and bounded-derivative assumptions ensure all differentiations and expectations are justified.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the finite-dimensional low-frequency approximate Jacobian J_t^{low} and its inverse K_t^{low}, defined by the linear SDEs in Section 6.1 along the cutoff flow, every p≥1 and finite T have a constant C_{T,p,R} such that E sup_{0≤t≤T}(||J_t^{low}||^p+||K_t^{low}||^p)≤C_{T,p,R}. Moreover, for small δ, E sup_{0≤t≤δ} ||K_t^{low}-I||^p ≤ C_{T,p,R} δ^{p/2}. Almost surely K_t^{low}=(J_t^{low})^{-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equations for J_t^{low} and K_t^{low} are finite-dimensional linear SDEs with bounded coefficients because the cutoff coefficients and their derivatives are bounded. Standard finite-dimensional SDE estimates give the uniform moment bounds. The small-time estimate follows by writing K_t^{low}-I in integral form and applying Burkholder-Davis-Gundy to the martingale term and elementary bounds to the drift term. The inverse relation is verified by applying Itô's product rule to K_t^{low}J_t^{low}; the correction terms in the definition of K_t^{low} are chosen exactly so that all drift and martingale terms cancel.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the cutoff equation and an initial condition x in S^α, there is a predictable noise direction v_t=(v_t^{low},v_t^{high}) with finite moments such that the Malliavin derivative D_vX_t^R has zero high-frequency component and a controlled low-frequency component. More precisely, if J_t^{low} is the approximate low Jacobian, K_t^{low} its inverse, and M_t^{low}=∫_0^t K_s^{low} B_low(X_s)B_low(X_s)^* (K_s^{low})^* ds, then D_vX_t^{high}=0 and D_vX_t^{low}=J_t^{low} M_t^{low}. The matrix M_t^{low} is called the partial Malliavin matrix.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The construction chooses v_t^{low}=(K_t^{low}B_low(X_t))^*. A preliminary low process Z_t is defined as the solution of the low linearized equation driven by the deterministic forcing B_low(X_t)v_t^{low}. The high direction is then chosen as v_t^{high}=-B_high^{-1}(D_low F_high)(X_t)Z_t. The lower bound on the high-mode noise amplitudes and the smoothing estimates ensure this belongs to the high noise Hilbert space and has finite moments. Substitution into the full Malliavin derivative equation shows that the high component satisfies a homogeneous equation with zero initial condition, hence is identically zero. The low component equals Z_t. Applying Itô's formula to K_t^{low}Z_t cancels all terms except K_t^{low}B_lowB_low^*(K_t^{low})^*dt, yielding K_t^{low}Z_t=M_t^{low} and hence Z_t=J_t^{low}M_t^{low}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Φ:S→S_low be a sufficiently smooth polynomially bounded vector field, and let X_t be the cutoff flow started from an initial condition regular enough that the strong form of the equation is valid. Let K_t^{low} be the inverse approximate low Jacobian. Then K_t^{low}Φ(X_t) satisfies an Itô identity whose martingale terms are K_t^{low}[B_i,Φ]_low(X_t)dβ_i(t), and whose drift terms are K_t^{low}(-[A,Φ]_low+[Y,Φ]_low)(X_t)dt plus one half of K_t^{low}Σ_i [B_i,[B_i,Φ]_low]_low(X_t)dt, where [.,.]_low denotes the projected Lie bracket and Y is the drift corrected by the Itô terms coming from low-mode state-dependent noise. The identity remains valid for slightly less regular initial data when Φ is defined on the corresponding smoother space.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The formula is obtained by applying Itô's rule to the product K_t^{low}Φ(X_t). The SDE for K_t^{low} contributes terms involving derivatives of the low noise coefficients and the low drift; Itô's rule for Φ(X_t) contributes DΦ applied to the drift and noise plus second derivative terms. Rearranging terms gives projected Lie brackets [B_i,Φ]_low and the iterated brackets [B_i,[B_i,Φ]_low]_low. In infinite dimensions the term involving A is handled by requiring enough regularity of X_t or of Φ; Proposition 5.1 supplies this regularity for positive times. The identity is the algebraic link between small Malliavin quadratic variation and small bracket directions in the Norris argument.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 7.5 and Theorem 7.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite collection A of smooth vector fields Φ:S→S_low satisfies the paper's restricted Hörmander condition if there exist constants δ>0 and R_0>0 such that, uniformly near every point of S, the vectors Φ(q), Φ in A, span the finite-dimensional low space S_low in the quantitative sense that every low vector η has some bracket direction Φ whose inner product with η is at least δ||η|| after taking the prescribed local infimum over a ball of radius R_0. For the cutoff Ginzburg-Landau equation with cutoff parameter R sufficiently large, the collection consisting of the low noise vector fields and finitely many projected Lie brackets generated by the corrected drift and the forced high-mode noise vector fields, up to bracket depth three, satisfies this restricted Hörmander condition. The constant δ can be chosen bounded away from zero for all sufficiently large R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two regimes. At large amplitudes, the cutoff noise operator has been modified to add nondegenerate noise directly in all low modes, so the original noise vector fields themselves span S_low. At moderate amplitudes, where the cutoff dynamics agrees with the original equation, the cubic nonlinearity generates low directions from high forced directions. For each low mode k, the combinatorial triples from Lemma 3.3 are used. Taking three projected Lie brackets of the cubic drift with the constant high-mode noise vector fields in the chosen triple produces a nonzero multiple of the low basis vector e_k; lower-degree polynomial terms disappear after three brackets, and the spacing of the triples prevents unwanted low-frequency contributions. Terms introduced by the cutoff derivatives are O(1/R), so for R large they are a small perturbation of a spanning set. Since only finitely many low modes are involved, this yields a uniform quantitative spanning constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let W_t be a cylindrical Wiener process in a separable Hilbert space, and let a_t, b_t, u_t, v_t be predictable processes such that the scalar semimartingales Y_t=Y_0+∫_0^t a_s ds+∫_0^t ⟨u_s,dW_s⟩ and Z_t=Z_0+∫_0^t Y_s ds+∫_0^t ⟨v_s,dW_s⟩ are well defined up to a bounded stopping time τ. If |a_t|, |Y_t|, ||u_t||, and ||v_t|| are uniformly bounded by C_0 on [0,τ], then for every p>1 there is C_p such that P(∫_0^τ Z_s^2 ds ≤ ε^2 and ∫_0^τ (Y_s^2+||v_s||^2)ds ≥ ε) ≤ C_p(1+C_0)^p ε^p for 0<ε≤1, up to harmless changes of the powers exactly as stated in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is Norris' martingale estimate. It says that a semimartingale Z cannot have very small L^2 norm while its drift derivative Y or martingale coefficient v has a much larger L^2 norm, unless a low-probability cancellation occurs. The paper cites Norris' proof but tracks the polynomial dependence on the uniform bound C_0. In the Malliavin argument, Z is an inner product ⟨K_t^{low}Φ(X_t),η⟩, Y corresponds to a drift bracket such as ⟨K_t^{low}[A_0,Φ](X_t),η⟩, and v corresponds to noise brackets ⟨K_t^{low}[B_i,Φ](X_t),η⟩. Iterating the lemma propagates lower bounds from high-order brackets down to the original noise directions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1, Theorem 7.3, and Corollary 7.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_t^{low}=∫_0^t K_s^{low}B_low(X_s)B_low(X_s)^*(K_s^{low})^*ds be the partial Malliavin matrix of the cutoff flow. For every finite T and p≥1 there exist positive exponents r and s and a constant C_{T,p,R} such that, for all 0<t≤T and all sufficiently regular initial data x, E[λ_min(M_t^{low})^{-p}] ≤ C_{T,p,R} t^{-rp}(1+||x||_α)^{sp}. Equivalently, the inverse of the low Malliavin derivative constructed in Theorem 6.6 has finite moments with the same type of bound. The underlying lower-tail estimate states that P(inf_{||η||=1} ∫_0^t ||B_low(X_s)^*(K_s^{low})^*η||^2 ds ≤ ε) is bounded by C ε^p t^{-rp}(1+||x||_α)^{sp}, with the precise Sobolev exponent and constants as in the paper.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of the lower-tail estimate, Theorem 7.3, begins by stopping the process before it leaves a small ball around the initial point and before K_t^{low} leaves a small neighborhood of the identity; Proposition 5.1 and Lemma 6.3 show that the stopping time is unlikely to be very small, with polynomial bounds in t and ||x||. On the stopped interval, the restricted Hörmander condition of Theorem 7.8 guarantees that for every low unit vector η some bracket direction has a uniformly nonzero inner product with η. Proposition 7.4 expresses each such bracket direction as the drift or martingale derivative of a lower-order bracket process. Norris' lemma is then applied iteratively, at most three times because the cubic nonlinearity gives brackets of depth at most three, to show that if the original Malliavin quadratic form is small then all bracket inner products would have to be small, contradicting the restricted Hörmander condition except on a probability of order ε^p times a negative power of t. Integrating the lower-tail bound over ε yields the inverse-moment estimate of Theorem 7.1. Corollary 7.2 follows from D_vX_t^{low}=J_t^{low}M_t^{low} and the moment bounds on J_t^{low} and K_t^{low}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let z_t be a predictable S-valued process on [0,T], and define the stochastic convolution Z_t=∫_0^t e^{-A(t-s)}B_R(z_s)dW_s for the cutoff noise coefficient B_R. Under the Hilbert-Schmidt regularity assumption on B_R, for every p>1 the process Z belongs to L^p(Ω;C([0,T],S^α)) and satisfies ||Z||_{L^p(Ω;C([0,T],S^α))} ≤ C_{T,p,R} times a positive power of T; in the cutoff setting the bound is uniform in z because B_R has the required global Hilbert-Schmidt bound.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the factorization method for stochastic convolutions. For a parameter θ between 1/p and 1/2, write Z_t as a deterministic convolution of (t-s)^{θ-1}e^{-A(t-s)} against an auxiliary stochastic process Y_s=∫_0^s (s-r)^{-θ}e^{-A(s-r)}B_R(z_r)dW_r. Semigroup smoothing moves powers of A onto B_R, while the Hilbert-Schmidt assumption makes the resulting integrals finite. Burkholder-Davis-Gundy estimates Y_s in L^p, and Young/Hölder estimates for the deterministic convolution give continuity in time and the stated sup norm bound. This estimate is repeatedly used to obtain spatial smoothing from rough noise.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.8 and Proposition 8.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 8.8: For the deterministic convolution operator (Kf)(t)=∫_0^t e^{-A(t-s)}f(s)ds, the analytic semigroup generated by A gives a smoothing estimate from a time-weighted space of S^{γ-ε}-valued functions into a time-weighted space of S^γ-valued functions, with a constant proportional to a positive power of the time horizon. Proposition 8.7: For the deterministic mild equation y_t=e^{-At}x+∫_0^t e^{-A(t-s)}F_R(y_s+r_s)ds with a continuous forcing r_t, if x is in S^γ and r is continuous in S^γ then y is continuous in S^γ and ||y||_{C([0,T],S^γ)}≤C(1+||x||_γ+||r||_{C S^γ}); if x is only in S and r is in C([0,T],S^α), then y has the time-weighted smoothing bound sup_{0<t≤T} t^α||y_t||_α≤C(1+||x||+||r||_{C S^α}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 8.8 is a direct analytic-semigroup estimate: split the integral into early and late parts, use ||A^ρ e^{-A(t-s)}||≤C(t-s)^{-ρ}, and exploit the time weights to make the singularity integrable. Proposition 8.7 first uses local existence and the global Lipschitz/linear-growth bounds of the cutoff nonlinearity F_R in S^γ. Gronwall's lemma gives the uniform S^γ bound. For smoothing from S to S^α, rewrite the equation as y=e^{-At}x+K(F_R(y+r)); the semigroup already gives t^α||e^{-At}x||_α≤C||x||, and Lemma 8.8 is iterated to bootstrap the regularity of the convolution term until reaching S^α.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H and U be separable Hilbert spaces, let A generate a strongly continuous semigroup on H, and consider the stochastic evolution dX_t=(-AX_t+F(t,ω,X_t)+g_t)dt+G(t,ω,X_t)dW_t, where W is cylindrical Brownian motion on U. Assume F and G are predictable in (t,ω), globally Lipschitz in X with linear growth, G is Hilbert-Schmidt-valued, and g_t is predictable with continuous paths and finite moments on compact time intervals. Then for every initial condition x in H and every finite T there exists a unique mild solution with continuous paths, the solution is strongly Markov, and E sup_{0≤t≤T}||X_t||^p≤C_{T,p}(1+||x||^p). If in addition F and G are twice continuously differentiable in X with uniformly bounded derivatives, then x↦X_t(x) has almost surely bounded derivatives satisfying the formally differentiated SDEs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites standard Hilbert-space SDE theory from DPZ96 for the case g=0 and observes that the same proof works with the additional predictable forcing g_t under the stated moment assumptions. Existence and uniqueness are obtained by Picard iteration in the mild formulation using the Lipschitz bounds and stochastic convolution estimates. Moment estimates follow from Burkholder-Davis-Gundy, semigroup bounds, and Gronwall's lemma. Differentiability of the stochastic flow follows by differentiating the Picard iteration or the mild equation; bounded first and second derivatives of the coefficients give uniform estimates for the derivative process and justify passage to the limit.

proof_techniques.name: Uniqueness via strong Feller plus irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper's final uniqueness argument is the classical Khasminskii-Doob mechanism. One first proves that the Markov semigroup is strong Feller, so transition probabilities regularize bounded measurable observables into continuous functions. One also proves irreducibility, meaning that from every state every nonempty open set has positive transition probability at positive times. Together these imply regularity of the Markov semigroup and, by Doob's theorem, at most one invariant probability measure. Existence is handled separately by compactness and moment bounds.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the SPDE in the original problem, a natural strategy is to prove strong Feller and topological irreducibility of the solution semigroup on C_0([0,1]); the paper gives a precise template for turning those two properties into invariant-measure uniqueness.

proof_techniques.name: Approximate controllability implies irreducibility
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves approximate controllability for the deterministic controlled equation obtained by replacing Brownian forcing with deterministic controls in the noise directions. It then invokes a standard support theorem criterion from DPZ: if deterministic controls can steer any initial condition arbitrarily close to any target in any positive time, then the stochastic transition probabilities charge every open set. The proof is constructive and handles severe degeneracy of the noise by using nonlinear mode coupling.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original problem the noise is full space-time white noise, so deterministic controls in the Cameron-Martin directions should be much richer than in the paper; a simpler version of this controllability-to-irreducibility route may be available, possibly first for smooth drift approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Four-phase control construction
proof_techniques.description: The controllability proof uses a robust four-step maneuver: let the dissipative PDE smooth the initial condition; use high-frequency controls to reset the high modes; use carefully chosen high-frequency triples and the cubic nonlinearity to steer low modes; finally adjust high modes to match the target while keeping low modes nearly fixed. The construction separates the infinite-dimensional and finite-dimensional aspects of the control problem and uses analyticity to justify inversion of a noise operator with polynomially small coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the drift in the original problem is not cubic, the general lesson is useful: combine parabolic smoothing with dense deterministic controls to prove support/irreducibility. For additive nondegenerate noise, the third nonlinear transfer step may be unnecessary.

proof_techniques.name: Cutoff localization for strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves strong Feller first for a modified cutoff equation whose coefficients are globally regular and whose noise is artificially made nondegenerate where the cutoff alters the dynamics. Since the cutoff equation agrees with the original equation inside a large ball, moment bounds imply that on a fixed time interval the two solutions differ with small probability if the cutoff radius is large. Continuity of the cutoff semigroup plus the small exit probability yields continuity of the original semigroup.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is directly relevant when the original drift is singular or only distributional. One can try proving strong Feller for smooth, localized, or approximating equations and then use exit-probability/localization or convergence of semigroups to transfer continuity to the limiting SPDE.

proof_techniques.name: Low/high frequency splitting
proof_techniques.description: The state space is split into a finite-dimensional low-frequency part and an infinite-dimensional high-frequency part. The high part is treated by direct smoothing from the high-frequency noise and semigroup estimates, while the finite low part is treated by Malliavin calculus and Hörmander brackets. This avoids having to prove full infinite-dimensional nondegeneracy of the Malliavin matrix.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For full space-time white noise on [0,1], a spectral splitting can still be useful: finite-dimensional projections can be handled by nondegenerate Malliavin or support arguments, while high modes are controlled by heat-kernel smoothing.

proof_techniques.name: Partial Malliavin matrix
proof_techniques.description: Instead of constructing an invertible Malliavin matrix on the whole infinite-dimensional space, the paper constructs an adapted noise direction whose Malliavin derivative has zero high-frequency component and whose low-frequency component equals a low Jacobian times a finite-dimensional partial Malliavin matrix. The finite-dimensional matrix is then shown to have inverse moments. This yields integration-by-parts estimates for low derivatives only, which are enough after high derivatives are handled separately.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original SPDE's full noise makes the finite-dimensional Malliavin matrices directly nondegenerate, this technique may simplify to standard finite-dimensional projection estimates; if the δ_0 drift obstructs differentiability, the construction may still be useful at the level of smooth approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Bismut integration by parts for semigroup gradients
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses an adapted Cameron-Martin perturbation of the driving noise to convert an expectation involving Dφ(X_t) into an expectation involving φ(X_t) times a stochastic integral. This is the key step in proving gradient bounds that depend only on ||φ||∞ rather than on derivatives of φ. The construction is carefully adapted so that the Malliavin derivative targets only the low modes.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original problem, smooth approximating drifts G_{1/n} may permit Bismut-type formulas for P_t^n. Uniform gradient or asymptotic strong Feller estimates could then be sought and passed to the singular limit.

proof_techniques.name: Restricted Hörmander condition
proof_techniques.description: The paper formulates a Hörmander condition only for the finite-dimensional low subspace. It builds a finite family of projected Lie brackets from the low noise fields, high forced noise fields, and the corrected drift. Quantitative spanning of the low subspace is established uniformly on local balls. This restricted condition is sufficient because the Malliavin calculus is only used to control low derivatives.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original problem, if additive noise is nondegenerate in every Fourier direction, the Hörmander condition should be immediate for finite-dimensional projections. If one uses approximations or transformations that degenerate in some directions, the paper's restricted condition gives a model for what must be checked.

proof_techniques.name: Norris lemma for lower tails of Malliavin matrices
proof_techniques.description: The proof of inverse moments for the partial Malliavin matrix uses Norris' lemma iteratively. If the quadratic variation in the original noise directions is small, then semimartingale identities force the first-level brackets to be small; iterating forces all bracket directions to be small. This contradicts the restricted Hörmander spanning condition except on a probability with polynomially small upper bound.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If strong Feller for the original SPDE is approached through Malliavin calculus for smooth approximations, Norris-type estimates are a route to uniform nondegeneracy of finite-dimensional densities.

proof_techniques.name: Semigroup factorization for stochastic convolution regularity
proof_techniques.description: The stochastic convolution is estimated by the factorization method: rewrite it as a deterministic singular convolution of an auxiliary stochastic process, estimate the auxiliary process by Burkholder-Davis-Gundy, and use analytic semigroup smoothing to obtain spatial regularity and time continuity. This provides the smoothing estimates needed for the flow, derivative flow, and cutoff-removal argument.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original equation is driven by space-time white noise and the Dirichlet heat semigroup; analogous factorization estimates with the Dirichlet heat kernel are likely central for moment bounds, tightness, and continuity of transition probabilities.

proof_techniques.name: Semigroup bootstrapping for deterministic convolutions
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly uses deterministic analytic-semigroup estimates to bootstrap regularity of mild solutions. If the forcing has a certain time-weighted Sobolev regularity, convolution with e^{-A(t-s)} gains additional powers of A, and the gain can be iterated until the desired regularity exponent is reached.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the Dirichlet heat equation in the original problem, the same bootstrapping principle can be used for smooth drift approximations and for estimating differences between approximations and the limiting singular-drift solution.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly quotes three external Markov-process facts from Da Prato-Zabczyk that are useful as black boxes: compactness plus moment bounds gives existence of invariant measures; approximate controllability gives irreducibility via a support theorem; strong Feller plus irreducibility gives uniqueness by Khasminskii/Doob. The cutoff noise is deliberately modified so that at large amplitudes all low modes are directly forced; this is not part of the original equation but is only a localization device, removed by exit-probability estimates. The proof is designed for noise that acts on all sufficiently high Fourier modes but possibly on none of the low modes; hence many arguments are stronger than needed for fully nondegenerate additive noise. The paper cites Cerrai 1999 for smoothing properties of transition semigroups in Banach spaces, DPZ92 for stochastic evolution equations and stochastic convolution estimates, DPZ96 for ergodicity criteria, and Norris 1986 for the Malliavin calculus lemma used in the inverse Malliavin matrix estimate.
why_relevant: This paper proves invariant-measure uniqueness for a stochastic Ginzburg-Landau-type SPDE using smoothing and controllability ideas. It is relevant as a model proof of uniqueness for nonlinear stochastic heat-type equations.
